title: Development of Vestibular Organs in Microgravity
objectives: Many species sense gravity, and therefore linear acceleration, by way of otoliths (dense calcium carbonate crystals attached to hair cells and associated nerve fibers). As the mass moves with acceleration, a signal is sent through each hair cell to the central nervous system. The knowledge of the factors that control development of these otoliths (and their analogs in some species) is limited. Previous studies suggest that altered gravity does not substantially affect the volume of the otoliths or their analogs in adult animals. This study was designed to test the hypothesis that during development the mass of the otolith is regulated to achieve a desired weight; as such, one would expect to reference larger-than-normal otoliths in animals reared in microgravity.
approach: The statoconia (analogous to otoliths) of the pond snail, Biomphalaria glabrata, and the otoliths of the swordtail fish, Xiphophorus helleri, were the focus of this study. Thirty-five adult snails, four pre-mated adult female swordtail fish, and 50 to 200 juvenile swordtail fish were flown in the Closed Equilibrated Biological Aquatic System (CEBAS), which contained seven liters of water and 50 grams of the hornweed, Ceratophylum. The specimen container was videotaped for later analysis. Many of the snails mated in flight, and produced the juvenile snails used in the experimental analysis. The pregnant adult swordtail fish were selected at a stage that they would produce developing fry but not have any hatch by the end of the mission. On landing day, approximately half the juvenile snails were fixed; the other half were fixed five days postflight. Adult fish ovaries and juvenile fish were fixed in alcohol on landing day. Fixed snails were removed from their shell and sectioned at 1-2 micrometer; serial sections were examined by transmission electron microscopy.
results:
Results were obtained from both the Neurolab flight (16-day) and a similar experiment of nine days duration, using the same organisms, flown on STS-89. Total statoconia volume in the snails varied considerably within both the flight and ground control groups. However, the difference in mean values for the two groups was significant at p < 0.05, with the average total volume greater in the flight group. In most of the groups of flight snails (divided into groups by shell diameter), the increase in statoconia volume increased as the animals developed another five days on Earth. Overall, the flight-reared snails had approximately 50 percent more statoconia than the ground controls. This indicated that rearing in microgravity causes the supporting cells to produce more statoconia, of approximately the same size as in ground-reared animals. 

Otoliths of the embryonic fish from STS-90 (Neurolab) exhibited a greater growth, with increased body length in the flight-reared animals, compared to ground controls. Although there was a trend for larger otoliths in the flight-reared juvenile swordtails, this difference did not reach statistical significance. The embryos from STS-89 were much smaller that those retrieved after STS-90, since this was a shorter mission. In this case, the growth of otoliths of the ground-reared fish embryos was actually larger than that of the flight-reared animals. These results suggest a critical period, in the late embryonic stages, where rearing in microgravity produces larger otoliths.

title: CNS Control of Rhythms and Homeostasis During Space Flight
objectives: Research has shown that the static gravitational field of the Earth and the dynamic cyclic changes caused by the Earth's rotation are two important selective pressures that shaped the evolution of biological organisms. Many features of an animal's physiology and behavior are a consequence of both static and dynamic geophysical influences. The circadian timing system (CTS) is one important temporal organizer controlling both physiology and behavior. Animals (including humans) exposed to microgravity exhibit alterations in both CTS function and homeostasis. These experiments examined the effects of space flight on the physiology of the CTS and the homeostatic control system of animals, specifically: 1) circadian rhythms; 2) neural responses of the circadian pacemaker and the sensory pathway for light information from the retina to the CTS; 3) adaptations in homeostatic regulation; and 4) neural changes in hypothalamic nuclei that regulate specific homeostatic functions.
approach: Six flight rats and ground control rats (six flight simulated and six vivarium controls) were utilized in one set of experiments. Twelve flight rats and two sets of ground control rats (12 simulated and 12 vivarium) were used in another set of experiments. The rodents were implanted with biotelemetry units to monitor circadian rhythms of body temperature, heart rate, and activity. In addition, eating and drinking was monitored for all rats. To examine the space flight effect on circadian and homeostatic regulation of physiological and behavioral functions, rats were exposed to 24-hour light/dark cycles (LD) consisting of 12 hours of light (approximately 30 lux) followed by 12 hours of darkness. The circadian rhythms and daily mean of rat body temperature, heart rate, feeding, drinking and activity (as a measure of homeostasis) were compared. To examine the space flight effect on free-running circadian rhythm characteristics, a subset of rats was maintained in a constant light condition (LL). Data were analyzed to determine circadian rhythms and results were compared to those of rats exposed to 24 -hour light/dark cycles. To examine the space flight effect on the pattern of c-Fos synthesis in hypothalamic nuclei, and whether it alters the effect of light pulses to induce c-Fos expression in the central nervous system, one group of rats received a one-hour pulse of light during the dark cycle. Another group was not exposed to a pulse of light. The brains and eyes from both groups were then removed and sections of the hypothalamus were histochemically stained for c-Fos reactive neurons.
results: The LL flight rats exhibited an increase (p<0.001) in free-running period of body temperature and heart rate relative to controls. The periods returned to preflight values after landing. The LL flight animals maintained internal phase angle relationships between rhythms compared with controls. The LD flight rats remained entrained to the LD cycle; however, they evidenced a pronounced phase delay in body temperature, suggesting an increase in period, compared to controls. The LD flight rats also demonstrated a decrease in body temperature and a change in the daily waveform compared to controls. Both the LD and LL flight rats and controls exhibited an increase in heart rate, suggesting a possible caging effect. Finally, the Flight Day 2 flight animals demonstrated a reduced sensitivity to light as evidenced by highly attenuated c-Fos immunoreactivity in the SCN compared to controls. The sensitivity to light of the flight animals returned to preflight and control levels by Flight Day 14. These findings demonstrate that microgravity affects the circadian clock, including the clock's ability to maintain temporal organization and to properly entrain to an external LD cycle.

title: Alterations In Skeletal Muscle Gene Expression In Relation To The Muscle Atrophy Associated With Exposure To Microgravity
objectives: This experiment investigated whether muscle loss associated with space flight is accompanied by increased levels of myostatin and a reduction in insulin like growth factor IGF-I and -II levels in the muscle, and whether these changes correlate with an increase in muscle proteolysis and apoptosis.
approach: Rats were divided upon return to Earth into two groups, and sacrificed either 1 day later (R1) or after 13 days of acclimatization (R13). Ground-based control rats were maintained for the same periods in either vivarium (R3 and R15, respectively), or flight-simulated cages (R5 and R17, respectively). RNA and protein were isolated from the tibialis anterior, biceps femoris, quadriceps, and gastrocnemius muscles. Myostatin, IGF-I, IGF-II and proteasome 2c mRNA concentrations were determined by reverse transcription/PCR; myostatin and ubiquitin mRNA were also measured by Northern blot analysis; myostatin protein was estimated by immunohistochemistry; the apoptotic index and the release of 3 methylhistidine were determined respectively by the TUNEL assay and by HPLC.
results: Muscle weights were 19–24% lower in the R1 rats compared with the control R3 and R5 rats, but were not significantly different after the recovery period. The myostatin/b-actin mRNA ratios were higher in the muscles of the R1 rats compared with the control R5 rats: 5-fold higher in tibialis, 3-fold in biceps, 1.9-fold in quadriceps, and 2.2-fold in gastrocnemius. These values also normalized upon acclimatization. Western blotting showed that myostatin immuno-staining was increased in muscle sections from R1 rats, compared with control R3 rats, and normalized upon acclimatization. In contrast, IGF-II mRNA concentrations in the muscles from R1 rats were 64–89% lower than those in R3 animals. With exception of the gastrocnemius, IGF-II was also decreased in R5 animals and normalized upon acclimatization. The intramuscular IGF-I mRNA levels were not significantly different between the flight rats and the controls. No increase was found in the proteolysis markers 3-methyl histidine, ubiquitin mRNA, and proteasome 2C mRNA. In conclusion, the loss of skeletal muscle mass that occurs during space flight is associated with increased myostatin mRNA and protein levels in the skeletal muscle, and a decrease in IGF-II mRNA levels. These alterations are normalized upon restoration of normal gravity and caging conditions.

title: Effect of Space Flight on Adrenal Medullary Function
objectives: It is hypothesized that microgravity conditions during space flight alter the expression and specific activities of the adrenal medullary catecholamine synthesizing enzymes (CASE). Previous examination of adrenals from six rats flown for 6 days aboard STS-54 showed that microgravity induced a decrease in the expression and specific activity of rat adrenal medullary tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate limiting enzyme of catecholamine (CA) synthesis, without affecting the expression of other CASE. Analyzing the catecholamine contents and the enzymatic activities of catecholamine synthesizing enzymes in adrenals from the STS-90 Neurolab mission was intended to confirm and extend previous findings.
approach: Adrenals from 3 Space Shuttle missions (PARE.03, SLS-2 and Neurolab) were examined as follows: Tissue catecholamine content was determined by HPLC and the levels of immunoreactive CASE were evaluated using Western blotting and quantitative RT-PCR. In addition, the enzymatic activities of two critical enzymes in this cascadetyrosine hydroxylase (TH), the rate limiting enzyme of the catecholamine synthesis cascade) and phenylethanolamine-N-methyl transferase (PNMT), the enzyme catalyzing the production of epinephrine from norepinephrine - were analyzed by radioenzymatic assay.
results: Exposure to microgravity during space flight led to a decrease in the levels of tissue catecholamines and CASE expression, evident in the samples from PARE.03, SLS-2, and Neurolab, collected at 24 h after return to earth. For example, the levels of norepinephrine (NE) are decreased by 15 ± 7 %. (p < 0.05) , while the levels of epinephrine (E) decreased by 34 ± 13 %. (p < 0.05). The levels of immunoreactive TH decreased by 28 ± 11 % (p < 0.01), while PNMT levels decreased by 12 ± 5 %. (p < 0.05). Similar data were obtained for the expression of the genes for TH and PNMT. These findings seem to support a recent theory that gravitational alterations in circulating catecholamines levels might, in part, be responsible for physiological consequence of weightlessness and cardiovascular abnormalities upon return from space. Thus, the findings are consequential for space flight in general and might provide new pharmacological approaches for the management of physiological imbalances during space flight and upon re-adaptation to 1 G.

title: Reduction in Density and Expression of Vasopressin Receptors in the Kidneys of Rats Following Spaceflight
objectives: A number of studies have demonstrated that renal function is altered during and immediately following space flight. It has been suggested there is a decreased renal responsiveness to vasopressin following space flight and that this may be the mechanism for the increased urine flow that is observed following return to normal gravity. This experiment measured vasopressin receptor expression and activity in kidneys taken from rats 1 and 14 days following space flight of 15 days duration.
approach: Twelve male Fischer 344 rats were individually housed in the RAHF during space flight. Ground controls consisted of rats similarly kept in the RAHF and another 12 rats maintained in standard vivarium cages. Flight animals were killed within 5 hours of landing and real-time quantitative RT-PCR was used to determine mRNA levels for the vasopressin V2 and V1a receptors. The proportion of V2 and V1a receptors was determined by measuring 3H-labeled arginine vasopressin binding to kidney membranes.
results: Measurements of renal vasopressin V2 and V1a receptor mRNA expression by quantitative RT-PCR demonstrated little difference at either 1 day or at 14 days following return from space. Evaluation of 3 H-labeled arginine vasopressin binding to membranes prepared from kidneys indicated that the majority of the vasopressin receptors were V2 receptors. Furthermore, the data suggested that binding to vasopressin V2 or V1a receptors was unaltered at 1 day and 14 days following space flight. Similarly, the ability of vasopressin to stimulate adenylate cyclase suggested no change in vasopressin V2 receptor activity in these animals. These data suggest that, whatever changes in fluid and electrolyte metabolism are observed following space flight, they are not mediated by changes in vasopressin receptor number or vasopressin-induced stimulation of adenylate cyclase.

title: Morphological and Molecular Markers of Skeletal Muscle Denervation during Recovery from Spaceflight
publication_titles: The Neurolab Spacelab Mission: Neuroscience Research in Space; Results from the STS-90, Neurolab Spacelab Mission | Recovery of neuromuscular junction morphology following 16 days of spaceflight | Neurolab Postflight Science Report ; Appendices

title: Mechanisms Underlying Loss of Proprioception
objectives: Adaptations of skeletal muscle to exposure to 0 G have been well documented. It has previously been established that space flight elicits alterations in the morphology of the neuromuscular system that includes expansion of the neuromuscular junction (NMJ) and myofiber atrophy. The purpose of this study was to determine the capacity of the neuromuscular system to recover from space flight-induced modifications upon return to normal gravity.
approach: Soleus muscles were obtained from rats participating in the 16-day Neurolab Space Shuttle mission at 1 day and 14 days after returning to Earth: solei were also taken at the same time points from ground-based control rats. Cytofluorescent techniques, coupled with confocal microscopy, were used to assess NMJ morphology. Histochemistry, in conjunction with phase contrast microscopy, was employed to examine myofiber size and type.
results: One day post landing, the neuromuscular junction were significantly larger in the spaceflight group compared to control. Muscle fiber atrophy was present. By 14 days post landing, neuromuscular area and muscle fiber size were not different from control, suggesting a robust capacity to recover from spaceflight-induced perturbations upon return to normal gravitation influences.

title: Morphological and Functional Adaptations of Muscle Fibers, Muscle-Tendon, and Nerve- Muscle Junctions to Spaceflights
objectives: Most myofilament contractile forces transmitted from the skeletal muscle fibers to the tendon collagen fibers is transmitted via the myotendinous junction (MTJ), a specialized region at the endings of the muscle fibers. The structure of the MTJ is dependent on the chronic loading forces experienced by the muscle. Previous studies have shown ultrastructural MTJ modifications caused by an increase or decrease in loading. This study compares the effects on MJT ultrastructure of 8, 18, and 29 days of hindlimb suspension to the effects of 14 days of microgravity exposure and 14 days of confinement.
approach: Animals were suspended by their tails, making the hindlimbs neither active nor weight bearing. After different lengths of suspension, the region of the distal MTJ was removed from the both the soleus and plantaris muscles in the hindlimb suspension controls, confinement controls, and spaceflight animals. All muscles were fixed in a glutaraldehyde solution, then post-fixed, dehydrated, and embedded in epoxy resin. Ultrathin sections were cut, stained with uranyle acetate and lead citrate, and examined using an electron microscope.
results: The first morphological modifications in the hindlimb suspension group were seen after 18 days, on the soleus muscle, an antigravity postural muscle. Twenty-nine days of hindlimb suspension showed profound morphological  and cytoarchitectural modifications. The animals that experienced 14 days of microgravity showed greater morphological and cytoarchitectural modifications that did the 18-day hindlimb suspension group. All the changes observed in the spaceflight group appeared to have occurred as if the observed ultrastructural disorganization was caused by a profound decrease in intensity of the applied force upon the axis of the MTJ tissular structure. The morphological modifications of the plantaris MTJ were found to be much less profound than those observed in the soleus MTJ, under all experimental conditions.
publication_titles: Spacelab Life Sciences 2 (SLS-2) 180 - Day Report | Respective effects of hindlimb suspension, confinement and spaceflight on myotendinous junction ultrastructure

title: Molecular Pharmacology of Alpha-1 Adrenoreceptors and Calcium Channels in Rat Vascular Myocytes
objectives: The cardiovascular system adapts successfully to upper body fluid shifts by increasing heart rate, blood pressure and total peripheral vascular resistance and by decreasing venous pressure.  These adaptive responses to fluid shift in spaceflight leads to a severe increase in heart rate and low blood pressure upon return to Earth.  There have been few studies examining microgravity's effect on contractile properties of smooth muscles and the cellular and molecular alterations that control vascular tone.  The objectives of this experiment were to look at changes in contractile response to norepinephrine of the vena cava, especially the  alpha 1B adrenoreceptors and to determine the mechanism by which the adrenoreceptors are altered.
approach: Vena cava were removed from flight rats and cut into longitudinal strips.  Specific binding to adrenoceptors was measured by incubating the strips in various concentrations of (3H) prazosin and determining radioactivity after dissolving strips in NaOH.  Isometric contraction was measured in an experimental chamber using circular strips taken from the venae cavae.  The maximum contractile response was determined using 30 micromolar norepinephrine.  All other contractions were expressed as a percentage of maximal contraction.  The inhibition of contractile response due to the binding of prazosin was also determined.
results: A decrease in contractile strength in response to norepinephrine was found in flight rats.  (3H) prazosin binding affinity was reduced, indicating a reduction in specific affinity to alpha 1 adrenoreceptors.  These data indicate that the reduction in contractile strength is due to a decrease in sensitivity of adrenoreceptors rather than a decrease in the number of adrenoreceptors.  Ground control studies show a similar decrease in sensitivity through sustained activation of protein kinase C.  This effect was not seen in the presence of an inhibitor of protein kinase C.  This implies that  desensitation of adrenoreceptors due to microgravity may be dependent on increased protein kinase C activity.
publication_titles: Spacelab Life Sciences 2 (SLS-2) 180 - Day Report | Influence of spaceflight, hindlimb suspension, and venous occlusion on alpha 1-adrenoceptors in rat vena cava | Effect of a 14-day hindlimb suspension on cytosolic Ca2+ concentration in rat portal vein myocytes

title: ANP, Pro-ANP and mRNA Distribution in Rat Heart During a Space Flight
objectives: Previous studies show that spaceflight induces a wide variety of changes in biological systems, including the cardiovascular and neuroendocrine systems. Cardiovascular deconditioning occurs upon return to earth. The regulation of blood pressure is partially carried out by the noradrenergic cells groups of the brain. However, the involvement of these systems is still unknown.  Data provided by SLS-1 experiments on central and peripheral catecholamines could be the result of acute stress occuring in the animals. The purpose of this experiment is to re-evaluate the data retrieved from SLS-1 and compare these data with those obtained in simulated microgravity experiments (hindlimb-suspended rats).
approach: The flight and control groups were divided into two groups, one group examined upon recovery (R+0) and the other nine days later (R+9). For the ground-based simulation experiment, the tail-suspended and control animals were divided into analogous groups. Rats were anesthetized with metofane and blood was taken through cardiocentesis. The animals were then sacrificed by decapitation. The brain, kidneys, and heart were removed and prepared for analysis. Thick serial frontal sections were taken to analyze the noradrenergic cell groups (A1, A2, A5, and A6) of the central nervous system. Brain and peripheral tissue samples were treated with perchloric acid.  The brain and tissue extracts were analyzed with liquid chromatography for norepinephrine (NE) content. The protein content of peripheral tissues was measured with the Bradford method.
results: There was no significant difference of the norepinephrine (NE) contents in the A1, A2, A5, and A6 cell groups between the flight and vivarium control groups. In the peripheral noradrenergic system, neither the cardiac atria and ventricles nor kidneys showed significant differences after spaceflight.  The tail suspended group, when compared to controls, showed similar results.  In the central nervous system, there was no significant difference between the NE levels in the A1, A2, A5, and A6 groups. In the peripheral noradrenergic system, neither the cardiac atria and ventricles nor kidneys showed significant differences after tail-suspension.
publication_titles: Spacelab Life Sciences 2 (SLS-2) 180 - Day Report | Central and Peripheral Noradrenergic Responses to 14 Days of Spaceflight (SLS-2) or Hindlimb Suspension in Rats

title: Bone Cell Activity During Spaceflight and Recovery
objectives: Microgravity associated with spaceflight has been shown in numerous experiments to be associated with marked skeletal changes, ranging from decrease in bone volume to alterations in biomechanical properties.  However, little attention has been given to bone tissue recovery that follows spaceflight after return to Earth's gravity.  In addition, how microgravity affects bone metabolism at the cell level has only been assessed by histological techniques: studies of osteoblastic cell number and differentiation in spaceflight animals have not been performed.  This study investigated the effects of microgravity  and subsequent recovery on trabecular bone morphology and compared histomorphometric parameters on caudal vertebrae with the behavior of vertebral osteoblastic cells in culture.
approach: Two groups of five rats were flown on SLS-2, one group was sacrificed after landing, the other after a recovery time equal to the flight (14 days).  Histomorphometric measurements were made on caudal vertebrae, thoracic vertebrae and the left humeri.  Lengths, volumes and wet weights were measured.  Trabecular volume, thickness, and spacing was measured using vertebral bodies and humeral metaphyses. Osteoid thickness was measured.  Osteoid, osteoblast and osteoclast surfaces were measured. Caudal vertebral bone cells were isolated and cultured. Cultures were evaluated for cell growth and phenotype.  The proliferation rate was determined by cell number. Cell alkaline phosphatase activity (ALP) and osteocalcin production, two parameters of osteoblast differentiation, were determined in confluent cells isolated from the caudal vertebrae.
results: Humeral weight and length showed normal growth during the experiment and were unaffected by spaceflight or recovery from spaceflight. However, spaceflight resulted in inhibition of static indexes of bone formation in humeral proximal metaphyses and thoracic vertebral bodies. This was associated with a decrease in bone volume in humeral metaphyses. After 14 days of recovery on Earth, osteoblastic and osteoid surfaces returned toward normal and bone volume was normalized in humeri, whereas the static bone formation parameters were not restored on thoracic vertebrae. In addition, histological indexes of bone formation and osteoblastic cell growth in vitro were not affected by spaceflight in caudal vertebrae. The rat humeri and thoracic and caudal vertebrae exhibit different patterns of response to spaceflight and subsequent on-Earth recovery, which could be due, at least in part, to the different loading pattern of these bones, and to differences in bone turnover rate.

title: Influence of Microgravity on Rat Digestive Physiology and Xenobiotic Metabolizing System - Interactions with Intestinal Microflora Alterations
objectives: Intestinal microflora possess an extremely versatile enzymatic potential that can interact directly or via its products with the overall body physiology. Spaceflight conditions, which are known to generate modifications of the gastrointestinal and hepatic functions, may also alter functions of the digestive microflora. The objective of this work was to assess the influence of a 14-day spaceflight on several parameters of digestive physiology and microbial fermentation. For this purpose, rats flown aboard the space shuttle were studied just after landing (RF group, n=6) and after a recovery period equal to the mission length (RFM group, n=6). They were compared to age-matched ground-based controls (RF-synchronous and RFM-synchronous groups), which were maintained in the same conditions as flight animals with respect to flight operations, timelines, hardware (RAHF) and environment. The following parameters were investigated microbial fermentation products  in the cecal content, profile of mucus-containing cells (MCC) in the colonic mucosa, xenobiotic metabolizing enzymes in the small intestinal mucosa and the liver.
approach: Rats were sacrified by decapitation. The cecal content, the mucosa of the small intestine and the colon, and the right lobe of the liver were immediately collected and stored under appropriate frozen conditions until analyses. In the cecum, pH was measured, short chain fatty acids (SCFA) and ammonia were analyzed as described by Guérin-Danan et al., urea and histamine were assayed according to a spectrophotometric method and a continuous-flow fluorometric method, respectively. The concentration of total CYP450 was determined in liver microsomes according to the method of Omura and Sato.  Glutathione-S-transferase (GST) activity was assayed in both microsomal and cytosolic fractions of the liver and the small intestine according to the method of Habig et al.  In the colonic mucosa, variations of the number of neutral, acid and sulfated MCC were investigated as described by Meslin et al.
results:
The 14-day spaceflight induced a slight acidification of the cecal content (6.96 in RF group vs 7.32 in control group; p<0.05) and a 60% decrease of cecal SCFA concentration (168 vs 287 µmol/g dry matter; p<0.05). In SCFA, acetate greatly increased (+14%) at the expense of butyrate (-7%) and cumulated valerate, caproate and isoacids (-7%) whereas propionate remained stable. Cecal ammonia, urea and histamine were not modified. Spaceflight did not alter GST activity either in the small intestine or in the liver, whereas concentration of hepatic CYP450 was significantly lowered (0.52 vs 0.59 nmol/mg proteins; p<0.05).  In the colon, spaceflight led to a 20 % reduction of the number of neutral MCC (15 vs 19 cells/crypt section; p<0.05). Microgravity temporarily affected the microbial fermentation and the histochemical structure of the mucosa in the large intestine, since modifications occurring in the RF group were not observed in the RFM group (at the end of the 14-day recovery period). On the contrary, the decrease of hepatic CYP450 observed in RF rats persisted in the RFM group.
These findings, together with those obtained in a previous flight (SLS-1), should help to predict the alterations of digestive physiology likely to occur in astronauts and suggest that microgravity may durably disturb host responses to toxics and drugs.
publication_titles: Spacelab Life Sciences 2 (SLS-2) 180 - Day Report

title: Effects of Spaceflight on beta-Adrenoceptors in Rat Hindlimb Muscles
objectives: Gravitational unloading has been observed to cause changes in the slow twitch muscle fibers of the antigravity soleus muscle.  The density of beta-adrenoceptors (beta-AR) is greater in slow-twitch red muscle fibers than fast-twitch white muscle fibers. The density of beta-ARs in the rat soleus decreases in response to gravitational unloading which has lead to the hypothesis that the same metabolic adaptation would occur in the rat plantaris muscle.
approach: Plantaris muscles were taken from the right limb of rats approximately 5 hours after 14 days of spaceflight and were cut into 20 µm consecutive cross sections.  Quantitative autoradiographic analysis was performed determining the maximum binding capacity (Bmax) and the dislocation constant of beta-1 and beta-2 ARs.  Qualitative histochemical analysis was performed, after staining for myosin adenosine triphosphatase.  Fibers were categorized as slow, intermediate or fast.  The activities of beta-hydroxyacyl CoA dehydrogenase (HAD) and succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) were measured spectrophotometrically.
results: The Bmax of beta-AR was significantly lower after flight and did not normalize after nine days of recovery.  The dissociation constant remained unchanged, suggesting the changes in Bmax were caused by a change in the number of receptors.  SDH activity was approximately 24 percent subnormal, but normalized after nine days of recovery.  No significant responses were seen in HAD activity or in fiber type percentages of flight animals.  The decrease in Bmax seems to be associated with a decrease in the inner membrane enzymes of the mitochondria rather than with the matrix enzyme HAD.

title: Effects of Spaceflight on Enzyme Activities and Ultrastructure of Fast-Type Skeletal Muscles of Rats
objectives: There is little information available about the characteristics of fast muscles exposed to space microgravity. Calcium (Ca2+) release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) is an important step in the excitation-contraction (E-C) coupling of skeletal muscle. It is generally accepted that transverse (T)-tubule is identified as a signal pathway from sarcolemma to the SR. However, there is no observation regarding the ultrastructure of these architectures in skeletal muscles exposed to actual microgravity. This study was designed to investigate structural changes in the myofilaments, the T-tubules, and the SR, as well as changes in the volume fraction of mitochondria and the activities of oxidative and glycolytic enzymes in fast-type skeletal muscles after spaceflight.
approach: The skeletal muscles examined in this study were the tibialis anterior (TA), the plantaris (PLN), the extensor digitorum longus (EDL), the medial gastrocnemius (MGAS), and the lateral gastrocnemius (LGAS) sampled from the right limb. These muscles are generally classified as fast-type muscles. Several small sample blocks were dissected from the muscles, and were fixed. These blocks were stained by modified Golgi's staining. The fraction was analyzed by point counting and a digitizer from electron micrographs of transverse sections for each muscle. A portion of muscle for biochemical analyses, except for EDL, was homogenized after which the supernatant was collected. The SDH and PFK activities were determined at 20 degrees C.
results: Activity of succinate dehydrogenase in medial gastrocnemius muscles of rats was significantly increased following 2-week spaceflight (P< 0.05). That of phosphofructokinase in plantaris muscles was lowered after flight (P < 0.05). Overall activities of both enzymes were effectively maintained during flight. No structural alterations in the mitochondria and other organelles were observed in response to spaceflight. However, a myofilament disordering and central nucleus were often seen in the fast muscle during recovery after landing, but not immediately after landing. These observations indicated that spaceflight increases susceptibility to sarcomere damage and metabolic activity in a specific muscle during reloading.

title: Characterization of the Retinal Glial Response to Spaceflight
objectives: Determining how spaceflight affects the eyes of astronauts is critical if humans are to attempt long-duration space missions. We aim to determine if reactive processes occur in the eye following spaceflight and if these processes are dependent on inflammatory regulators such as iNOS and COX-2. If so, anti-inflammatory determines how spaceflight affects the eyes of astronauts is critical if humans are to attempt long-duration space missions. We aim to determine if reactive processes occur in the eye following spaceflight and if these processes are dependent on inflammatory regulators such as iNOS and COX-2. If so, anti-inflammatory drugs could play a role in protecting astronauts from the biological hazards associated with spaceflight could play a role in protecting astronauts from the biological hazards associated with spaceflight.

title: Individual Variations in Measures of Muscle and Bone in Ambulatory Subjects
objectives: Muscle strength has been shown to predict bone mineral density (BMD) in women, but there is limited data in healthy non-athletic men.  The purpose of this study was to determine the relationship of muscle strength and bone mineral density in men, and to define the effect of exercise patterns in non-athletes on BMD and muscle strength.  A secondary objective was to test a newly developed instrument, the Mechanical Response Tissue Analyzer, to determine a mechanical property of bone related to its strength (EI or bending stiffness) non-invasively.
approach: Fifty healthy men, aged 28 to 51 years were recruited for an ambulatory study and gave informed consent.  Volunteers were instructed in the daily recording of physical activities and outfitted with a step meter to be worn at all times except during bathing, sleeping and recreational exercise (softball games etc.). The exercise log was reviewed each week for accuracy with an exercise physiologist.  A five-day diet record was also provided with instructions to enter all foods eaten for four week-days and at least one weekend day.  Each subject had a series of bone density measurements (Hologic QDR 1000) and a series of strength tests using isotonic weight equipment (Universal and Nautilus).  Bending stiffness of the right ulna and right tibia was estimated with a new instrument (MRTA) on 3 separate occasions and on the left ulna and tibia, on one occasion. A single measurement of bone density of the wrist was carried out with a Norland densitometer and the calcaneal bone density by a single energy densitometer, (Osteon, Inc).  The right calcaneus was measured three times and the left, twice.
results:
We obtained the first data on bending stiffness in the ulna and tibia of adult males with our non-invasive instrument and found the left tibia to be stronger than the right.  The ulnas of right-handed men showed lower values for EI in the left ulna than in left-handed men whose left and right ulnas were equal.  EI was related to the mineral density of the right ulna, and both right and left tibias. EI was also related to the width of the ulna and the strength of the biceps.  We concluded that the measurement of EI was reproducible, and was influenced by arm strength. 

We found that BMD at all sites was related to the muscle strength in the back and the biceps (recorded in pounds).  Body weight correlated with tibia and whole body BMD.  Comparison of exercisers and non-exercisers walking mileage showed no difference in miles per day (3.9 miles a day), age, body weight, dietary protein or fat intakes in these two groups. Kcal/d were greater in exercisers than non-exercises due to intakes of carbohydrate.  BMD and muscle strength were significantly greater in the exercisers than in the non-exercisers, indicating that hours spent doing recreational exercise is a better predictor of BMD than daily walking. Calcaneal BMD was not related to walking steps, but could be correlated with the amount of time engaged in high load exercise  (i.e. loads of more than two body weights).

title: The Effect of Bed Rest on Bone Histology and the Calcium Endocrine System in Adult Men
objectives: It is known that small animals show decreases in bone formation when the skeleton is unloaded and that humans develop acute disuse osteoporosis (bone loss) after prolonged periods of unloading.  The purpose of this experiment was to determine the response of human bone to a brief period of unloading in a bed rest model for space flight.
approach: Eight healthy men, similar in age to astronauts, were recruited for a 7-day bed rest study.  Their bones were labeled with antibiotics that have an affinity for bone tissue 7 days before bed rest, the first day of bed rest and the last day of bed rest.  An iliac crest bone biopsy was obtained 2-3 days after the last bed rest day and examined for rates of bone formation and resorption. Newer assays that serve as markers of bone metabolism (formation and resorption) were carried out on urine and serial blood specimens.   Diets were controlled and a metabolic study was conducted for a few days before, during 7 days bed rest and for up to 5 days after bed rest.  The urinary excretion of calcium, phosphorus, sodium and potassium was monitored and calcium regulating hormones in the circulation were monitored before, during and after bed rest. Statistical analyses estimated the changes in parameters measured by repeated measures ANOVA primarily and comparisons of bone histomorphometry with data from healthy normal volunteers the same age as the bed rest subjects. The concentration of calcium and phosphorus in sublingual cells before and after the bed rest period was measured.
results:
The response of bone, as estimated by markers, revealed prompt increases in markers of bone resorption with significant increases by the 4th day of bed rest.  A marker of bone turnover in blood, osteocalcin, showed an increase within one day of bed rest, remained high for the 7 days of bed rest and returned to pre-bed rest values within 5 days of reambulatiion. This non-collagenous protein originating in bone proved to be the most sensitive indicator of the bed rest response.  We found average rates of bone formation were reduced in 6, unchanged in 1 and increased in 1.  The decreased was greatest in the most active subjects and the increase occurred in a swimmer.

There was an increase in the urinary excretion of calcium and sodium evident the 2nd bed rest day and continuing for the entire week.  Reduced excretion of phosphorus and 3', 5' cyclic adenosine monophosphate on the first and second bed rest days was followed by an increase in serum phosphorus on the 6th bed rest day.  Serum ionized calcium; parathyroid hormone and 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D were decreased by the 6th and 7th bed rest day.  We concluded that the response of the bone and the calcium endocrine system of healthy men after only 7 days was similar to results in longer bed rest studies and emphasized the responsiveness of the adult human skeleton to the biomechanical stimuli induced by changes in activity and/or position.  

The concentration of calcium and phosphorus in sublingual cells, representative of epithelial soft tissue, showed significant increases from 81±13 to 109±13 for calcium and 1180±98 to 1577±187 Exa units after 7 days bed rest, a phenomenon that was later shown to be prevented by exercise in a longer 30 day bed rest study.

title: Acute Cutaneous Microvascular Flow Responses to Whole Body Tilting in Humans
objectives: On Earth, upright posture establishes a linear gradient of blood pressure along the length of the body.  The change from upright to supine body position is followed by an increase in local pressure from 100 to 200 mmHg in the feet and a decrease at the head level from 100 to 70 mmHg. Considering the combined effect of central and local controls for arteriolar tone, these mechanisms may work cooperatively in the lower body and antagonistically in the upper body, i.e. above the heart level, baroreceptor-induced changes in arteriolar tone may be negated by local myogenic factors, as suggested by cutaneous micro-vascular responses in a study by Aratow et al. that compared responses in the forehead and foot to two tilt angles.  This study proposed to further document the different responses in cutaneous microvascular flow to graded postural changes in humans.
approach: Ten healthy subjects (7 male and 3 female) ranging in age from 26 to 47 years (median 27.5), with average weight of 67 kg and height of 171 cm agreed to participate in the study.  Flow meters were attached with adhesive tape to the neck and to the leg of each subject.  Instrumented subjects were placed upright on a motorized tilt table against a footplate and mildly restrained with a canvas strap across the chest.  After stabilization of cutaneous flows, subjects underwent the following stepwise protocol: (Each angle was maintained for 30 seconds with 10 minute transitions between angles) 90o , 54o, 30o, 12o, 0o, -6o,   -12o, -6o, 0o, 12o, 30o, 54o, 90o. In 8 subjects, Ga/ln-in-silastic strain gauge plethysmography measured percent changes in leg and neck volumes.
results:
Cutaneous flows (avg±SE) at the calf and neck increased significantly from standing to 12o head-up-tilt (247±55% and 161±15%).  From 12o to –12o tilt, flows continued to increase at the calf to 479±91% of control, but decreased at the neck to near baseline (119±13% of control, NS).  Tilting from head-up to head-down produced significant increments in mean neck volume of 3.8±0.5% above standing control and significant decrements in mean leg volume of 1.8±0.2% below control.  Responses of body segment volumes to tilt were practically instantaneous, indicating that blood volume translocation accounted for most of the change.

We concluded that flow increases from standing to supine posture can be attributed to baroreceptor-mediated vasodilation at both sites with the added effect of increased pressure at the neck.  The subsequent decrease in flow at the neck with head-down-tilt may be due to a myogenic response to increased local arteriolar pressure.  The great dissimilarity of flow responses at the two sites suggests that the cutaneous microcirculation of the calf is more sensitive to gravitational stresses than that of the neck.
publication_titles: Acute cutaneous micro-vascular flow responses to whole-body tilting in humans | Postural responses of head and foot cutaneous microvascular flow and their sensitivity to bed rest

title: Comparison of Bone Bending Stiffness in Elite Female Synchronized Swimmers and Judo Athletes (N.B. Gymnasts studied in lieu of Judo athletes)
objectives: There is a great deal of information on the bone densities of athletes with different loading histories, but limited information on the effect of sports activities on the structure and mechanical properties of bone.  It is known that gymnasts have high density bone because of their exposure to high-impact loads.  It is also known that swimmers have lower density bone because of their exposure to a buoyant environment. We asked the question: Are the bones of synchronized swimmers, stimulated primarily by non-impact loads and muscular contractions, weaker than those of gymnasts?
approach: Thirty seven young (20-22 years) women were recruited from Olympic level synchronized swimming (n=13) and gymnastic (n=8) clubs as well as from an untrained population (n=16) of college students.  All gave informed consent for the study.  Historical information was acquired before the tests for muscle in the athletes and the non-invasive test of length and bone strength in the ulnas and tibias of all subjects. The MRTA delivers a "bzz" and then detects the response of the bone on which the probe is centered on the skin surface. The response curve to the vibratory stimulus is analyzed by software that generates a measurement for E (the modulus of elasticity) and for I (the moment of inertia) for the ulna and the tibia.  The instrument had been previously validated in the bones of monkeys (correlation of non-invasive measurement with a three-point bending test in the same bone extracted at necropsy was found to be 0.95, highly significant).
results:
The synchronized swimmers (SYN) were taller than the gymnasts (GYM) and the untrained women (UNT) partly due to a longer tibia in SYN. The SYN also had, higher body weights and lower BMI measurements then GYM or UNT groups.  Years of training in GYM exceeded those in SYN by 3.5 years. The BMD in the wrist was highest in GYM, lowest in SYN and intermediate in UNT. In contrast, the ulnar EI was similar in GYM and SYN and 50% higher than in UNT.  Ulnar EI was related to Ulnar width, but not to wrist density.  EI in the tibia was similar in both athletic groups, and twice the value of the UNT.  The knee flexor strength correlated with tibial EI.  

We concluded that in spite of different loading histories that could be related to bone mineral density in the wrist, the bending stiffness of synchronized swimmers and gymnasts was similar and in the athletes, much higher than in the untrained women.  EI (bending stiffness) in the ulna could be related to the width of the bone and in the tibia, to muscle strength.  It seems clear that vigorous physical training of the type used in synchronized swimming can increase bone strength without necessarily increasing bone density.
researchAreas:
Bone and calcium physiology

title: Pilot Study for the Life and Microgravity Spacelab Mission (LMS)
objectives: The Life Sciences, Microgravity Science and Spacelab Mission contains a number of human experiments directed toward identifying the functional, metabolic and neurological characteristics of muscle weakness and atrophy during space flight. To ensure the successful completion of the flight experiments, a ground-based pilot study, designed to mimic the flight protocols, was carried out in the head-down tilt bed rest model.
approach: Eight healthy male volunteers the same age as the payload specialists were recruited to perform12 of the experiments planned for four individuals on the mission.  Critical to the study was an integrated schedule of muscle experiments for two groups of four that allowed the experiments to be repeated at the same time of day while maintaining a normal diet schedule. Scientists with experiments accepted for the mission traveled to ARC and conducted their experiments with equipment that was similar to that to be used in space as well as before and after the 17 days of bed rest.
results:
The intensity and frequency of exercise from the muscle testing protocols did not appear to prevent muscle atrophy and weakness. Two experiments testing calf muscle strength and fatigue characteristics with the torque velocity dynamometer showed surprisingly little effect of bed rest. At the whole muscle level, the work capacity of the calf muscle was not compromised. However, there was a decrease in the capacity of individual muscle fibers in the soleus to produce peak force, a phenomenon that could be attributed to the decrease in fiber size. The thigh muscle (quadriceps) showed an 8% decrease in the strength of a maximum voluntary contraction accompanied by a decrease in neural drive. The loss was restored by the 2nd day of recovery.
Enzymes involved in glucose and glycogen metabolism as well as glycogen itself were increased, illustrating the increased dependence of type I fibers on carbohydrate metabolism. The response of a bioassayable tibial growth factor to exercise was reduced by the 2nd bed rest day and restored 3 days after the end of bed rest. Testosterone and thyroid hormones were unaffected by bed rest. 
Weight loss averaging 1% occurred during the first 3 days of bed rest and was promptly regained during the first two recovery days.  This may have been directly related to reduced fluid intakes (250 ml/d average) and an increase in urine volume on the first bed rests day. Other elements in the diet that were spontaneously reduced by the subjects during the bed rest period were calories, protein, carbohydrate, sodium, calcium and phosphorus intakes. Total body water was reduced, but the value was not significant. The legs lost 7% lean mass with 5% regained during the first 2 days ambulation. Fluid loss during bed rest came primarily from the legs. Nitrogen balance was negative during bed rest, consistent with the decrease in lean body mass estimated by DEXA. Bone mineral density, content and body fat were unchanged. A bone marker in urine showed increased activity of bone cells during bed rest, a phenomena that continued during the recovery period. The length of the vertebral column increased by 3.5 mm at the end of 14 days bed rest, greater than the 2.7mm increase after one night and less than the 4.4 mm after long duration bed rest.  
Cardiopulmonary function tests revealed changes in pulmonary tissue volume (an increase) and a small but significant decrease in residual volume that appeared to be due to lung compression from the weight of the abdominal contents pressing against the diaphragm.  Cardiac output, stroke volume, and heart rate were all affected by the bed test tilt and returned to standing and supine values during 1 week of recovery. Later comparison with changes in microgravity revealed that the head-down-tilt bed rest model proved to be a good model of the cardiovascular response to microgravity, but a poor model for microgravity effects on pulmonary ventilation and gas exchange.   
The circadian rhythm of body temperature was disrupted during head-down tilt with phase delay and a progressive flattening in a manner similar to those observed in one individual aboard the Space Station Mir. There was both subjective and objective evidence of insomnia during early bed rest and a slight increase and early appearance in REM (dreaming) sleep.  The effect of bed rest on cognitive performance based on the 20 minute NASA Performance Assessment Workstation (PAWS) was minimal.

title: Comparison of Bending Stiffness in the Dominant and Non-dominant Tibias of Women
objectives: There is limited information on the mechanical properties of the long bones of healthy women compared to the amount of data on the density of bone. We were interested in determining the mechanical strength of a long bone and evaluating it in reference to the strength of the muscles of the same bone. We were also interested in determining gender differences in the laterality of tibial strength since previous data in normal men revealed the left tibia was stronger than the right.
approach: Twenty women (age 44±11 yrs) were recruited from the working staff of Ames Research Center.  All gave informed consent for the study and provided brief   historical information on general health and physical activity. The tests for muscle strength were carried out by quantifying the forces required to extend and flex the leg with a LIDO instrument. A non-invasive test of bone strength was performed in the tibia of a relaxed recumbent subject with the probe of instrument positioned on the center of the 'shin' bone.  The MRTA delivers a "buzz", detects the response of the bone to a brief vibration at the same site on which the probe is centered and then analyzes the response curve by software that generates a measurement for bending stiffness or EI (E is the modulus of elasticity and I the moment of inertia) for the tibia.  The instrument had been previously validated in the bones of monkeys by correlating the non-invasive measurement with three-point bending in a mechanical instrument in the same bone (0.95 was the significance of the results of the in vivo test vs. the extracted bone tested with a mechanical instrument). Gender comparisons were done on a population of 22 normal men, aged 39±7 yrs studied earlier (ref #4).
results: In contrast to the findings in men, there was no difference in the bending stiffness of the right and left tibias (150±71 vs. 151±47Nm2).  Values in women averaged 28% lower than in men, appropriate for the 24% difference in body weight (men weighed 80±10 vs. women 61±10 kg).  Women were shorter than the men but had the same body mass index (BMI = wt/ht2 = 23) and tibial length.  Flexion and extension of the right and left legs were also similar (83±22 vs. 84±31 in Nm for flexion and 177±38 vs. 168±51 Nm for extension) and unrelated to EI.  We concluded that tibial strength is appropriate for its load carrying function in both sexes, symmetrical in women, but not in men.

title: Exercise Countermeasure for Bedrest Deconditioning
objectives: The aim of this 30-day bed rest study was to investigate the effects of short-term, high-intensity isotonic and isokinetic exercise training on the working capacity, muscular strength and on orthostatic tolerance, posture and gait. Other data on muscle atrophy, bone mineralization and density, endocrine analyses concerning vasoactivity, fluid and electrolyte balance, muscle intermediary metabolism, performance and mood of the subjects were also acquired.
approach:
The protocol consisted of a 7-day ambulatory control period, 30 days of –6o head-down bed rest and a 4.5-day ambulatory recovery period. 
Nineteen 32-42 year old male volunteers were divided into three groups:  no-exercise controls (NE, n=5), isotonic exercise with an electronic Quinton ergometer, (IE, n=7), and isokinetic exercise on the Lido ergometer (KE, n=7).  The exercise regimens were conducted near peak levels for 30 min in the morning and 30 min in the afternoon 5 days/wk. Diets were controlled to provide 2,800 kcal/d for the NE group and 3,100 kcal/d for the two exercise groups.
results:
Average caloric consumption of 2,678 kcal/d in NE, 2,833 kcal/d in IE and 2,890 kcal in KE resulted in mean losses of 1.01, 0.85, and 0.0 kg during bed rest in each group, respectively.  
(1)  Working capacity, based on VO2 max, was maintained during bed rest in the cycle ergometer group but not in either of the other two groups. Plasma volume was maintained only in the IE group and significantly reduced in NE and KE groups. (2) In general, there was no significant decrease in strength or endurance of arm of leg muscles during bed rest in spite of some reduction in the size of some leg muscles (atrophy). (3) There was no effect of IE training on orthostasis, since tilt-table tolerance was reduced similarly in all three groups following bed rest with average tolerance range of 42-53 min before and 30 to 34 min after bed rest and similar increases in heart rate responses to tilt. (4) Bed rest resulted in significant decreases in postural stability, self-selected step length, stride length and walking velocity, none of which were influenced by the exercise training regimens.

title: Calcium Metabolism and Correlated Endocrine Measurements in Primates
objectives: The problem of calcium loss from the body during space flight had been long recognized; however, little is known about the initiating mechanism of this loss.  Two hypotheses have been suggested in explanation: 1) a primary increase in bone resorption due to decreased mechanical stress on the bone or 2) a primary renal calcium leak.  This experiment was to measure the initial responses of the calcium homeostatic system in nonhuman primates subjected to the space flight environment.
approach: The basis for this study was the "tracer" technique, in which direct measurements of changes in bone resorption are possible through isotopically labeled calcium.  Fifteen days prior to launch, flight and control subjects were started on a diet laced with the isotopically labeled calcium.  Excretion samples were collected pre- and postflight, and amounts of labeled calcium were detected by means of irradiation.  Single lateral x-rays were obtained for the right and left arms and legs of one flight, two synchronous, and one laboratory monkey to provide high contrast and good radiographic detail for bone.  No serum samples were received for the flight experiment, so the planned endocrine studies were not done.
results: Results indicate that after five days of flight, the fraction of circulating calcium that comes from the bone was increased.  A major point to be noted is that, even with a wide fluctuation in dietary calcium intake, urinary calcium output remained fairly constant over the two to three week period.  Similar results have been noted in immobilized monkeys.  However, the very low variation when expressed as calcium/creatinine suggests a technical explanation having to do with collection.  Gross skeletal changes were not observed, but there was a suggestion of subtle changes as determined from the high-quality radiographs.  Qualitative assessment of the films for cortical porosity suggests that one of the flight monkeys may have experienced a state of slightly increased bone turnover because of the presence of some intracortical striations in the radial and ulnar cortex.  Data indicate that the methods used to study calcium metabolism during space flight were sufficient to answer the questions asked.

title: Commercial Biomedical Test Module - 2 (CBTM-2)
objectives: Commercial Biomedical Test Module - 2 (CBTM-2) will use a validated mouse model to examine the effectiveness of an experimental therapeutic as a possible countermeasure for muscle atrophy. Combined with exercise, this experimental therapeutic developed by Amgen could one day form the basis for a treatment that will help maintain a high level of physical fitness in future flight crews. Also, through an extensive tissue sharing program several additional investigations were performed to determine the effects of microgravity on the skeletal, cardiovascular, immune system, liver and kidney function as well as other physiological systems.
approach: Nine week old female C57/B6 mice were launched on this 13-day flight on the Space shuttle, for  a total of 11-12 days in microgravity. Flight mice were treated once with a placebo vehicle or therapeutic agent approximately 24 hours before launch. Ground control mice were treated similarly but with a 48 hour offst and were housed under the same environmental conditions (temperature, light/dark cycle, humidity, oxygen levels and carbon dioxide levels) as the flight mice. All mice received the same full access to food and water. Upon return to Earth, bone marrow cells were isolated from the humerii (long bones of the upper limb or forelimb) of the mice and counted. The expression of several molecules (Ly6C, CD11b, CD31 (PECAM-1), Ly6G (Gr-1), F4/80, CD44 and c-Fos) that defines the maturation state of cells in the granulocytic lineage on three bone marrow cell subpopulations ( R1, R2 and R3) were defined by their size and light-scattering properties. Body weight of the mice was also measured pre and post flight.
results: There were no observable characteristic differences between total bone marrow cells isolated from flight and ground-control mice. Nevertheless, there were subpopulation differences observed that suggested neutrophil activation in response to landing. Decreases were noticed in Ly6C, c-Fos, CD44high and Ly6G. An increase in F4/80 suggested that the cells in the bone marrow R3 subpopulation of the mice flown on Shuttle were more differentiated compared to the ground controls. A loss in body weight was also noticed in the mice that flew in space which suggest that they were subjected to chronic stress beyond that what was endured during landing. Therefore, it is not unreasonable to suggest that there are significant changes in bone marrow phenotype in response to the stress of the space flight experience.

title: Dietary Sodium Effects on Bone and Calcium Metabolism
objectives: There is a predictable relationship between the amount of sodium in the diet and excretion of calcium in the urine.  It is not known whether the same relationship between sodium and calcium occurs in space or during bed rest.  Urinary calcium increases during bed rest and space flight, a phenomenon that is thought to reflect increased resorption of bone and not the amount of dietary salt.  We recently observed no increase in the excretion of urinary calcium during a 30-day bed rest study when subjects were fed sodium-restricted diets.  The purpose of this research was to compare this 30-day study with restricted dietary salt to a 7-day bed rest study of volunteers fed normal salt diets.
approach: There were 8 men, aged 30-50 years in the 7-day and 11 in the 30-day study. The 30 day study was carried out 4 years before the 7 day study. Daily dietary sodium averaged 193 mm in the 7-day and 114 mmol in the 30-day study.  Dietary calcium was the same at 20 mmol/d. Circulating levels of parathyroid hormone as well as indicators of the hormone action on kidney and bone were evaluated    The excretion of urinary calcium, sodium, and creatinine was monitored carefully. The renal excretion of urinary cyclic adenosine-monophosphate, an indicator of the action of parathyroid hormone on the kidney was measured.  The excretion of hydroxyproline, a product of collagen metabolism in bone excreted as a reflection of the level of bone resorption in the urine, was also measured.
results:
Within the first 3 bed rest days, subjects fed normal sodium diets showed increases in fractional excretion of calcium, increased serum ionized calcium and decreases in circulating parathyroid hormone.  In contrast, subjects fed restricted salt diets showed no early increase in ionized calcium or decrease in parathyroid hormone.  Urinary hydroxyproline increased during the first few days of bed rest in subjects fed normal salt diets and remained elevated for one week, the duration of the study.  This product of collagen metabolism did not increase in the 8 subjects in whom it was measured in the salt restricted study.  

We concluded that the level of dietary salt has a significant effect on the excretion of urinary calcium and on bone resorption during bed rest.  The results suggest that low normal (restricted) dietary salt may reduce the early increase in urinary calcium during bed rest and possibly also, space flight.

title: Intramuscular Pressure During Concentric and Eccentric Contraction in Isotonic and Isokinetic Exercise
objectives: The purpose of this study was to determine whether electromyography (EMG) or intramuscular pressure (IMP) correlates better with ankle joint torque (force) during eccentric and concentric exercise of the tibialis anterior and soleus.
approach: Ten healthy male volunteers, aged 28 – 54 years, consented to the study after familiarization of the experimental exercise protocol.  The foot was secured to a Lido active dynamometer (for force measurement) with the knee and hip joints flexed at 90o to prevent the gastrocnemius muscle from being activated during plantar and dorsiflexion of the ankle joint, the exercises used to test the muscles.  Under local anaesthesia, a catheter, connected to a pressure transducer, was inserted into the anterior tibialis and a second into the soleus muscle.  EMG recordings were obtained by both surface and intramuscular electrodes.  The exercise protocol consisted of 5 maximum plantar flexion contractions (soleus) followed by 5 maximum dorsiflexions (tibialis anterior muscle). The contractions were performed at 25, 50, 75, and 100% of maximal voluntary contractions (MVC) for both plantarflexion and dorsiflexion.  Five MVC were performed concentrically, and five, eccentrically with 3 minute rests between contractions.
results:
Soleus and tibialis anterior IMP and EMG correlated with ankle joint torque, or muscle contraction force in all 9 subjects.  For analysis, the IMP and EMG data were normalized to percent of maximum for each dynamic exercise.

Linear regression analysis of concentric contractions, normalized soleus plantarflexion against force revealed high correlations (r2=0.937 for IMP and r2=0.716 for EMG; and for the tibialis anterior, during dorsiflexion, r2=0.948 for IMP and r2=0.802 for EMG.  During eccentric plantarflexion IMP correlated well with force (r2=0.883), but EMG did not (r2=0.489).  Relationships of eccentric dorsiflexion and force were better with IMP, but good for both IMP and EMG (r2=0.904 and r2=. 702).  

Conclusions:  IMP values reflect joint forces during concentric and eccentric exercises while EMG did not appear to correlate as well as IMP with force output during eccentric contractions.  IMP is theoretically dependent on the tension of muscle fibers at the site of measurement while EMG depends of the size and number of depolarized motor units that remain constant with changes in the length or tension of the muscle fibers.  Although more invasive than EMG and also characterized by considerable intersubject variability, IMP provides a more direct and accurate index of muscle contraction force for both concentric and eccentric exercise.

title: Pathophysiology and Treatment of Spinal Pain Associated with Microgravity
objectives: The purpose of this first back pain study was to determine whether the head-down tilt (HDT) bed rest model would elicit back pain and spinal lengthening similar to that in Astronauts during space flight.
approach: Eight male subjects, aged 38±6 years with an average weight of 80.8±10.4 kg and height of 182.6±5.5 cm volunteered for a 21 day study.  There was a 4-day control period, 16 days of HDT to simulate microgravity and 1 day of recovery standing upright.  Subjects heights were measured on 2 control days, the first 8 and last two days of bed rest.  All subjects completed a pain questionnaire daily.  The questionnaire asked the subjects to rate the back pain on a scale of 1-5, note any activity that eased or worsened the back pain and to describe sleep patterns.
results:
There was an increase of 2.1±0.5 cm by the 3rd day of HDT and no further increase.  An increase in height during the night after a day of normal ambulation was less than the increase on the 3rd day of HDT. The intensity of back pain in the lower back increased to 2.5 (dull pain) and then decreased to basal levels by the 9th day.   

Conclusions: Although spinal lengthening in space is twice that experienced during HDT, the type, level, distribution and time course of back pain during HDT was similar to that experienced during space flight. The pain subsided when the spinal lengthening stopped. Astronauts report dull back pain between days 1 and 6 of space flight and it is noted that a knees to chest position reduces the pain in astronauts as well as in volunteers in this study.

title: The use of Flurocortisone or Salt Load to Expand Plasma Volume in Ambulatory Subjects
objectives: It is known that a fall in blood pressure occurs after space flight on return to Earth's gravity.  This is associated with a reduced volume of plasma, an adaptation made for the weightless environment, and currently restored by the ingestion of large volumes of water and salt tablets on landing. Earlier work revealed that the synthetic corticosteroid, flurocortisone, clinically useful in patients with low blood pressure, could expand plasma volume in subjects exposed to the bed rest model for space flight.  The purpose of this study was to compare the effectiveness of salt ingestion with the administration of a synthetic corticosteroid to prevent orthostatic hypotension.
approach: A previous study (HR77) had tested 4 subjects with flurocortisone (Florinef) and 4 subjects with a saline load after 7 days of – 6o  bed rest.  Florinef restored plasma volume to pre-bedrest levels, but a saline load did not.  The response in ambulatory subjects was opposite to this post bed rest response.  This study plans to repeat the earlier study in the same subjects, reversing the protocol (giving Florinef to subjects who were treated with a saline load before and giving a saline load to subjects treated with Florinef earlier). A second group of tests will be done at night to understand if the lack of effect of a salt load in some astronauts was related to a difference in the regulation of salt excretion during the evening. The saline load was 8 g NaCl, (1 g/tablet) given with 960 ml water and the dose of Florinef was 0.2 mg every 8 hrs for 3 doses.
results:
Four of 5 subjects in the saline group experience syncope and could  not complete 15 minutes of standing after bed rest while 5 of the  6 subjects given Florinef showed no syncope.  After 7 days of bed rest, the increase in heart rate was similar in Florinef and saline treated subjects (to 24 bpm) and greater than the heart rate response before bed rest (to 13 bpm, p<0.05).  However the saline load failed to maintain mean arterial pressure during standing post bed rest while Florinef treatment did maintain blood pressure. Plasma volume decreased approximately 12% after 7 days bed rest.  Following treatments, Florinef returned plasma volume to within 4% of baseline whereas saline failed to restore plasma volume.  Urine volume increased during 2 hours of standing after the saline load and decreased after Florinef compared with the pre-bed rest response.  Urine Na and the Na/K ratio was similar before and after bed rest following the salt load, but urine Na was decreased and the Na/K ratio reduced after bed rest following Florinef.  

Conclusions: Acute treatment with Florinef appears to have distinct advantages over the administration of a saline load as a countermeasure for post-bed rest orthostatic intolerance.  Florinef restored plasma volume, maintained mean arterial pressure, and reduced the renal excretion of Na and the Na/K ratio whereas a saline load did not.

title: Minimum Intermittent Gz Requirements With or Without Activity for Preventing Fluid and Electrolyte Regulating Hormone Changes After 4 Hours –6 Degrees Head Down Bed Rest
objectives: The purpose of this study was to determine the minimum G requirements of exposure to G forces to prevent the deconditioning effects of microgravity in a ground-based model.  This consisted of evaluating the effectiveness of intermittent exposure to passive and active +1 Gz force on   deconditioning manifested as orthostatic intolerance, changes in oxygen uptake, reduced plasma volume, increases in urinary volume, sodium and calcium excretion during a period of 4 days –6o head down bed rest.
approach: Nine healthy men of average age (38±4.5) yrs were recruited to participate in a four day bed rest study with each individual tested across five treatment conditions: Each group consisted of 4 days bed rest with a daily treatment of 1) no intervention (OG) 2) 4 hours intermittent standing (S4), 3) 4 hours intermittent walking (W4), 4) 2 hours intermittent standing (S2), 5) 2 hours intermittent walking (W2).  Walking was at 3 mph on a level treadmill. Recovery time in between each treatment was a minimum of one month.  Measurements of body weight, Tilt tolerance, Peak VO2, plasma volume and urinary calcium were carried out just prior to and on the 4th bed rest day.  Statistical analysis of the five treatments was done by repeated measures ANOVA with missing estimates adjusted by least squares except for measurements of Heart rate and Blood Pressure precluded by the large amount of data.
results:
Of interest is that the responses of the physiologic variable to each treatment differed as a function of the variable measured.  Orthostatic intolerance was mostly compensated for by an increase in HR.  Standing for 4 hours was most effective with standing for 2 hours more effective than walking for 2 or 4 hours.  Peak oxygen consumption was best preserved by walking either 2 or 4 hours and minimally maintained by standing for 4 or 2 hours. Either standing or walking for 4 hours prevented the decrease in plasma volume.  Walking for either 2 or 4 hours prevented the loss of 4 days urinary calcium excretion. Absolute values for all data are printed in NASA TM 108800.

Conclusions: No single treatment was most effective across all dependent variables. Preventive effects of different treatments are specific to each system. Calcium balance responded favorably to activity whereas orthostatic tolerance and plasma volume were more dependent on the stimulus provided by the gravitational field.  In most cases, differences between 2 and 4-hour exposures were small and it was surprising to find out how little exposure the human body needs to maintain normal health.

title: Etiology of Back Pain in Microgravity
objectives: The purpose of ARCbedrest117 was to develop a balanced traction to elongate the spine to the degree experienced by astronauts. The effects of traction on the spine exposed to the 6 degree head down tilt (HDT) model is compared to effects on the spine with traction and horizontal bed rest (HBR).  The second part of this study (ARCbedrest129) was designed to determine the electrical activity and intramuscular pressures of the muscles along the lumbar spine while upright and while in 6o HDT or HBR position.
approach:
Healthy volunteers were tested in the MRI before and after 3 days exposure to a balanced traction device that placed a load on the spine by suspending a load corresponding to 5% of the body weight from each leg with a cable-pulley system.  The volunteer was at bed rest in the 6o HDT or in HBR position.  Activity was not restricted.  HDT with traction was compared with HBR with traction. The second part of the study conducted a few days after ARCbedrest117 estimated the electrical activity of lumbar spine muscle through surface electrodes for specified hours of normal activity.  Through a catheter inserted into the muscle on either side of the lumbar spine, the pressures were recorded during upright and then at three time intervals during of HDT or HBR.  Electrical activity was recorded concurrent with intramuscular pressure.

Subjects were randomized into 2 groups of three.  Group 1 underwent HBR followed by HDT in balanced traction.  Group II underwent HDT in balanced traction followed by HBR.  There was a two-week recovery period between the two forms of 3d-bed rest.
results:
Repeated measures ANOVA indicated that total body length increased more (mean±SE) during HDT with balanced traction (22±8 mm) than during HBR (16±4 mm).  Spine length between L5 and C7 also increased more during HDT with traction  (25±8 and 14±9 mm).  Ultrasonography showed increases in lumbar disc height during HDT and HBR (2.9±0.8 mm vs. 3.3±0.6 mm), but there were no differences among groups.  Seventy percent of the increase in disc height occurred during the first 5 hours of bed rest.  Within the first hour of standing, there was a 10 mm decrease in body height, about 80% of the normal diurnal variation in our subjects.  The intensity of pain in the back, abdomen and head was greater during HDT with traction than during HBR with traction.  Reflexes were normal in both groups.  Intramuscular pressure in erector spinae muscles that averaged 8.4±0.7 mm Hg did not change during the study and was similar in HBR and HDT.  Ankle joint torque during plantar- and dorsi-flexion did not change during the study and was similar in HBR and HDT with balanced traction.

Conclusions: Results suggest that HDT with balanced traction may combine the advantages of both models to simulate total body and spinal lengthening, as well as back pain associated with microgravity.

Note:  Both studies were carried out on the same group of subjects.  The measurements of intra muscular pressure in ARCbedrest129 were reported separately, but the protocols for each study were the same.  Therefore, it is more complete to have both ARCbedrest117 and ARCbedrest129 reported together as the first part and the second part of the study as described.

title: Etiology and Countermeasure for Back Pain Associated with Microgravity
objectives: The purpose of ARCbedrest147 was to compare the effect of spine compression with a harness to the physiologic condition of an upright position on the lumbar spine.
approach:
Twelve men and three women (aged 24.4 ±4.8 yrs, mean±SD)  
With no prior history of back pain volunteered for the 2-day study.  On day 1, the baseline scan of the lumbar spine in a supine subject was obtained followed by a scan after 30 minutes exposure to a compression load of 50% of body weight applied by a harness.   On the second day, supine subjects were scanned followed by a scan after 30 minutes in an upright position (kneeling in lieu of standing).
results:
Both spinal compression with a harness and upright posture increased the anterior disk height and decreased the posterior disk heights.  This resulted in a curvature of the spine that was similar after 30 minutes in both loading conditions (14.1±5.3o  after harness and 11.5±6.0o after upright posture).

Spinal compression with a specially designed harness has the same effect as the physiologic loading of the spine in the kneeling upright position within the first hour after loading when 80% of the changes occur.  Further studies will be required to evaluate the effect of the harness in space flight.
