title: The Effect of Daily Activity and Age on Bone Density
objectives: The purpose of this study was to 1) develop a new method of quantifying daily activity level in terms of the daily history of ground reaction forces and 2) to clarify the relationship between musculoskeletal tissue stress (strain) histories and the material and structural properties of bone during normal daily activity and 3) to establish equivalent loading histories in space compared to baseline activity levels on Earth.
approach: Male subjects, aged 30-55 years of average weight and similar shoe size were selected. An insole sensor (1/8th inch thick) was fitted to a foot of each subject.  Pressure between the foot and shoe sole caused the sensor to deform and output a voltage (2.5 Volts maximum) proportional to the applied force.  A shielding wire threaded through the pant leg connects the insole sensor with a microprocessor unit that is attached to the waist for data acquisition.  The sensor is calibrated to zero and one body weight by lifting the foot off the ground for 2.5 seconds and then a second reading is taken after placing the foot on the ground for 2.5 seconds.  These static data points are stored to obtain a linear force voltage output calibration and compared with a set of dynamic points from a swaying subject to ensure no difference in readings greater than 5% that would indicate a defective sensor.
results:
Peak cyclic ground reaction forces (GRFz), normalized to subject body weight were recorded for three consecutive weekdays. Average daily histograms of the number of loading cycles vs cycle-magnitudes (loading histories) were obtained by averaging 24 hour periods.  The total number of cycles for men were 4526 ±1187 and for women, 4744 ±1383.  The total number of cycles greater than 0.8 BW were 3649 ±1139 for men and 3909 ±1259 for women.  Mean cycle magnitude for all cycles greater than 0.8 BW were 1.11 ±0.07 BW for men and 1.13 ±0.09 for women.  The number of cycles at the higher >0.2 BW for men were 7.3±12.5 and for women 5.4±9.5.  A trend toward reduced high load activity was observed with age.   

These results demonstrate for the first time objective force measurements of human activity in men and women.  The range in frequency and magnitude of daily loading may be an important contributor to variation in bone density in lower limb skeletal sites loaded down by internal forces supporting the ground reaction force.

title: 6 Degree Headdown Tilt with Balance Traction to Emulate Spinal Lengthening Experienced by Astronauts during Microgravity
objectives: The purpose of ARCbedrest117 was to develop a balanced traction to elongate the spine to the degree experienced by astronauts. The effects of traction on the spine exposed to the 6o head down tilt (HDT) model is compared to effects on the spine with traction and horizontal bed rest (HBR).  The second part of this study (ARCbedrest129) was designed to determine the electrical activity and intramuscular pressures of the muscles along the lumbar spine while upright and while in 6o HDT or HBR position.
approach:
Healthy volunteers were tested in the MRI before and after 3 days exposure to a balanced traction device that placed a load on the spine by suspending a load corresponding to 5% of the body weight from each leg with a cable-pulley system.  The volunteer was at bed rest in the 6 degree HDT or in HBR position.  Activity was not restricted.  HDT with traction was compared with HBR with traction. The second part of the study conducted a few days after ARCbedrest117 estimated the electrical activity of lumbar spine muscle through surface electrodes for specified hours of normal activity.  Through a catheter inserted into the muscle on either side of the lumbar spine, the pressures were recorded during upright and then at three time intervals during of HDT or HBR.  Electrical activity was recorded concurrent with intramuscular pressure.

Subjects were randomized into 2 groups of three.  Group 1 underwent HBR followed by HDT in balanced traction.  Group II underwent HDT in balanced traction followed by HBR.  There was a two-week recovery period between the two forms of 3d-bed rest.
results:
Repeated measures ANOVA indicated that total body length increased more (mean±SE) during HDT with balanced traction (22±8 mm) than during HBR (16±4 mm).  Spine length between L5 and C7 also increased more during HDT with traction  (25±8 and 14±9 mm).  Ultrasonography showed increases in lumbar disc height during HDT and HBR (2.9±0.8 mm vs. 3.3±0.6 mm), but there were no differences among groups.  Seventy percent of the increase in disc height occurred during the first 5 hours of bed rest.  Within the first hour of standing, there was a 10 mm decrease in body height, about 80% of the normal diurnal variation in our subjects.  The intensity of pain in the back, abdomen and head was greater during HDT with traction than during HBR with traction.  Reflexes were normal in both groups.  Intramuscular pressure in erector spinae muscles that averaged 8.4±0.7 mm Hg did not change during the study and was similar in HBR and HDT.  Ankle joint torque during plantar- and dorsi-flexion did not change during the study and was similar in HBR and HDT with balanced traction.

Conclusions: Results suggest that HDT with balanced traction may combine the advantages of both models to simulate total body and spinal lengthening, as well as back pain associated with microgravity.

Note:  Both studies were carried out on the same group of subjects.  The measurements of intra muscular pressure in ARCbedrest129 were reported separately, but the protocols for each study were the same.  Therefore, it is more complete to have both ARCbedrest117 and ARCbedrest129 reported together as the first part and the second part of the study as described.

title: The Effect of Daily Activity and Age on Bone Density in Women
objectives: The purpose of this study was to investigate the relationship between daily physical activity and bone density in young and elderly women. It is known that the rate of bone loss in the calcaneus (heel bone) is 0.2-0.3% per year beginning at age 35-40 years.  The hypothesis of his study is that declining physical activity is a contributor to age-related bone loss. We have developed a device (in-sole sensor) that quantifies the activity level in an objective manner so that we can determine the loading history of a bone through the daily exposure to ground reaction forces. This data is then applied to mathematical models for bone adaptation in response to loading histories.
approach: Forty-two female subjects, ranging in age from 25 to 74 years were placed into two self-selected activity groups (18 runners and 24 non-loading type of activities, i.e. swimming, cycling or no sport). Our ground reaction force monitor was fitted to the right foot of each subject and worn for five consecutive days.  The monitor measured average daily loading histories (GRFz). The density of the right heel was measured as close to the monitoring of activity as possible. A bone density index (BDI) was estimated from the average daily loading history and correlated with the heel density in each subject. The BDI was determine from [  Σni  ( β ⋅ GRFzi  ) m ]1/(2m)  where activity is determined from i  loading conditions with n cycles of load per day, β is a subject specific scale factor to account for differences in body weight (BW) and GRF is the ith ground reaction force cycle peak load magnitude normalized to BW.
results:
The observed number of gait related steps (>0.8 or 0.95 BW) was similar to data obtained by others with stepmeters or pedometers.
Histograms of cycles of activity (load vs. frequency) could be made in terms of the subjects actual BW ('relative' load) or in terms of a BW scaled factor (absolute load). 'Relative' loading history is used when the intensity of the activity is compared among groups or individuals whereas 'absolute' loading histories are used to describe the actual force history. The histograms of higher body weight subjects showed a shift to higher GRFz loads when expressed in terms of β, the scaled factor, a more accurate description of actual applied forces. One goal of this study was to determine a value for m, a stress exponent relating GRFz history to Calcaneal Bone Mineral Density. The Bone Density Index was significantly correlated with the Calcaneal bone density (r = 0.437, p = 0.004).  Through least squares fitting, for all subjects, a stress component of 6.07 was determined as a value for m.

Female volunteers were divided into high loaders (HL) and low loaders (LL) based on self-reported activity.  There were 18 HL with an average age of 42 yrs, height of 1.66 m, BW of 61.5 kg and BMI of 22.3.  There were 24 LL with an average age of 48, height of 1.63, BW of 73 and BMI of 27.3.  Both BW and BMI were greater in the LL than in the HL in both the young and elderly age groups.  The number of cycles of activity for more than 0.8 BW or 0.95 BW were higher in both elderly and young HL. However, for most activity parameters, elderly and young did not differ.  Activity did not appear to decrease with age.

In contrast, calcaneal bone density (CBMD) was lower in the elderly than young (383 vs 446 mg/cm2).  In 20 elderly women, BW was a determinant for CBMD.  BW was not a determinant for CBMD in LL, but was in HL and only In 18 HL subjects, could activity be related to the CBMD through the Bone Density Index (r  = 0.59, p<0.009).  

Conclusions: This study was inconclusive in terms of finding a relationship between activity grouping and bone density.  Nevertheless, we did find a significance to activity grouping when the BW is accounted for in the bone density measurement.  A Bone Density Index, determined from daily ground reaction force loading history is correlated with the calcaneal density or the CBMD/BW Scale Factor (m=6).

title: The Effect of Daily Activity and Age on Bone  Density in Men
objectives: The purpose of these studies was to determine the relationship between declining activity level with age and calcaneal density as well as in vivo calcaneal stresses from external and internal forces during walking and running
approach:
The first study: A mathematical model previously derived using experimental data of exercise effects on bone mass was used to calculate the sensitivity of calcaneal bone density to age-related changes in body weight, walking speed, and daily walking duration over the age range of 30-70 years. Changes in body weight and walking speed with age were obtained from the literature.  The assumption was made that walking provides the primary mechanical load stimulus to the calcaneus.

The second study towards a simple Ground Reaction Force (GRF) measurement from external forces was guided by the hypothesis that the reciprocal of foot-ground contact time or the reciprocal of the stride-period-normalized contact time would predict the peak horizontal and vertical GRF during gait. GRF data was collected from 24 subjects while they walked or ran at a variety of speeds.

The third study was directed toward a more detailed study of the internal forces from joints, muscles, and tissues during walking and running.  It utilized force plate measurements and   hi-speed cine-radiography data collected at the Herrick-Davis Motion Analysis Laboratory, Henry Ford Hospital, Detroit, MI.  A finite element model coupled with contact of the foot was constructed to determine the forces acting on the calcaneus during walking and running.  The GRF data (units in BW) was transformed to a reference system fixed on the foot with the forefoot and the Achilles tendon as the 2 points of contact.
results:
A decline in daily walking duration with age may be a major factor in the decrease in bone density up to the age of 50 years.  A 39% reduction in daily walking from 30 to 50 years of age can account for a bone loss of 0.2% per year and a reduction of 79% over the same period can account for a bone loss of 0.8% per year. Past age 55, individuals tend to walk more slowly and lose  BW, two factors that combined with reduced duration in walking, may accelerate bone loss.

Collectively, the second and third studies showed that all parameters were significantly correlated to the reciprocal of foot-ground contact time and the reciprocal of stride-period-normalized contact time.  The calculated force-time profiles of the joint contact, ligament, and Achilles tendon forces varied with the time history curve of the moment about the ankle joint.  The forces acting on the calcaneus at peak loading during walking occur at 70-80% of stance and during running, earlier or at 60% of stance. The model predicted peak talocalcaneal and calcaneocuboid joint loads of 5.4 and 4.2 BW during walking and 11.1 and 7.9 BW during running.  The maximum predicted Achilles tendon forces were 3.9 BW for walking and 7.7 BW for running.

Conclusions: Temporal measures can predict force parameters of gait and may be an alternative to direct GRF measurements for determining daily histories of habitual lower limb loading quantities needed for a bone-remodeling stimulus.  The bone-remodeling algorithm predicts that running can substantially increase calcaneal bone density.

Large magnitude forces and calcaneal stresses are generated late in the stance phase, with maximum loads occurring at 70% of the stance phase during walking and at 60% of the stance phase during running, for the gait velocities analyzed. The trajectories of the principal stresses, during both walking and running, corresponded to each other and qualitatively to the trabecular architecture of the calcaneus.

title: Effects of Age and Activity Level on Tibia Mass and Distribution
objectives: The purpose of this study is to gain a better understanding of the relationship between age, activity level (runners vs non-runners) and site specific bone mass and long bone structure.
approach: Three studies were performed because nonbone soft issue in the path of the x-ray beam bone is comprised of materials that have different attenuation properties than mineralized bone.  The first accuracy study involved the construction of a large plexiglass tube with a bladder filled with saline to emulate soft tissues for the leg. An aluminum phantom was used to estimate in vivo setup accuracy at 3 different angles and two energies by two sets of three scans. The second study determined the in vivo precision of cross-sectional structural parameters in the long bones of 15 female human non-runners on the same day repeated measures for 60o and 90o.  The third study compared the parameters in the legs of 14 25-35 year old women with the same in 12 women >age 60 and at two  activity levels (recreational runners and non-runners).  Scans were taken of the middle third of the lower right leg; structural parameters for the tibia and fibula were determined on each scan line, averaged over the section and adjusted by factors accounting for body size variations.
results:
Study one: Parameters for the eight rectangular sections of the aluminum phantom were averaged over four lines per section and errors computed as differences from expected values.  Principal moments were estimated within 2.3% and principal angles were estimated within 2.3o. 
Study two:  Low energy yielded slightly better precision than high with the area measurement the highest precision (CV <1%).  Principal and polar moment CV were 2-6% and the precision of the principal angle was 3-6o.  
Study three:  Low loaders had higher body weights than high loaders, emphasizing the need to account for body size in estimates of bone structure.  All differences in the tibia were related to load level, not age.  Imax was higher by 46%, Imin by 30%, J by 41% and A by27% and  along the midshaft, the principle angle for runners was consistently aligned closer to the AP direction than in nonrunners.  Fibula results did not show the same pattern of results as in the tibia.  There was no dependence on age or load group in any parameter for the fibula.    

Conclusions:  A three-scan densitometric method for measuring cross-sectional structural parameters in long bones in vivo was validated; accuracy and precision measurements established confidence limits. 
Structural parameters related to bone stiffness and strength are increased in female recreational runners compared with nonrunners and these increases are maintained with age. These measures that incorporate bone mass distribution all had larger percent differences than area (A) and were therefore more sensitive measures in detecting differences between runners and nonrunners than simple bone mass measurements (A) alone or I in a single, nonprincipal plane.  From the activity study results, it is postulated that higher loads associated with running lead to increased cross-sectional parameters to support axial loads, bending and torsion in the tibia.

title: The Effect of Daily Physical Activity and Age on Bone Density and Bone Structure in Men
objectives: The purpose of the first study is to investigate the differences in the bone density of the skeleton as well as the structural properties of the lower leg bones in three groups of age-matched men: soccer players, runners, and sedentary controls.
approach: Methodology used the same tools developed for a previous study in women (HR 202). Three groups of 20-30 year old men were recruited: 15 elite soccer players (S) 15 elite long distance runners (R) and 15 sedentary controls (C).
results:
Age, body weight, height and tibia length were similar in all 3 groups. Average values age were S 21, R 24.2 and C 24.3 years; for body weight were S 79.4, R 67.5, C 75.7 Kg; for height were S 181.6, R 179.8, C 180.5 cm; and for tibial length were S 40.4, R 41.4, C 40.4 cm respectively.  Area was scaled by normalized body weight while tibial structural parameters in midshaft and distal third were higher in S and R than C  (S 3.03, R 3.36. C 2.68 cm2 for A, 
S 2.37, R 2.80, C 1.95 cm4 for Imax.  S had higher whole body, spine, right hip, right leg and calcaneal BMD than C (p = 0.008, 0.041, <0.001, 0.019, <0.001 respectively).  S also had  higher right hip and spine BMD than R (p = 0.012 and p = 0.009, respectively).  R had higher calcaneal BMD than C  (p = 0.002). Forty percent of R had t-scores of the lumbar spine between –1 and –2.5, similar to C whose T-scores were either below –1 (34%) or lower than –2.5 (7%). 
  Structural parameters (scaled values of A, Imax, Imin, and J) of the tibia were increased in S and R, indicating increased stiffness and strength.  The tibial cross-sections of S were generally more symmetric than in R, indicative of their wider variety of loading conditions compared to the repetitive loading in R.  There was also a relationship between the tibia and the fibula and the relative distance between the two bones indicating the two bones work together as structural elements in the lower leg.

Conclusions:  Playing soccer is associated with higher BMD of the skeleton at all sites measured while running is associated with higher BMD at directly loaded sites (calcaneus), but not at relatively unloaded sites (spine).  Specific loading conditions, seen in ball sports or in running, play a pivotal role in skeletal adaptation.

title: Serial Changes in Site Specific Bone Density and Structure with Paralysis and Fibular Resection
objectives:
Study 1: The purpose of the first study is to investigate the effect of paralysis on bone density and structural parameters of the tibia.

Study 2: The purpose of the second study was to investigate bone mass and structure in the tibia after resection of the fibula.
approach:
Study 1:  were two male and  two female adults aged 17-32 years who had sustained a spinal cord injury at least 4 years prior to the study.  Cross-sectional structural parameters including area (A), principal area moments of inertia (Imax, Imin), polar area moments of inertia (J), and major principal axis orientation of the tibia and fibula were computed every millimeter along the scan length for the three scans of the leg using the lower energy for its larger dynamic range.

Study 2: Subjects who had undergone a fibular resection at least 2 years earlier were recruited (n=3).  Fibular resection was carried out in all 3 men to remedy avascular necrosis in the hip.  In addition to anthropomorphic data (BW, Ht, tibial length) a set of 3 scans of the tibia on right and left sides as above was done as well as a DXA scan of the right and left calcaneus to measure areal bone density, an indication of loading symmetry. Structural parameters of the tibia and fibula as enumerated above were performed on one leg and on the tibia of the other leg.
results:
Study 1: Body weights in paraplegics were greater than in controls.  Two male subjects had lower values for cross-sectional areas and density as well as lower structural parameters in the midshaft, distal tibia and femoral midshaft.  Data supporting bone loss in the two female paraplegics was not convincing except at the tibial midshaft where the data was not scaled to body size.  In one male subject, cross sectional areas and principal moments of inertia of the tibia were not symmetrical. 
Conclusions of study 1: The large variation in results of just a few subjects point to the need for longitudinal studies and more subjects to definitively measure changes in structural parameters due to paralysis and unloading.

Study 2:
Subject 1. The cross-sectional area of the tibia on the side of the fibular resection (rt) was increased 2% at the midshaft and 33% at the distal third compared to the unaffected tibia.  The principal and polar moments of inertia on the right tibia were increased 51% and 89% at the midshaft and distal third, respectively.  The midshaft was also more symmetric, but not the distal section.  

Subject 2: The tibia on the resected side (left) showed an increase in cross-sectional area (8% at midshaft and 10% at distal third) and an increased minimum moment of inertia of 22 % in the mid and 47% in the distal third.  Imax increased by 9% in both sites.

Subject 3:  The increases in the cross-sectional area were 32% at the mid and 27% at the distal third, compared to the unaffected left side.  Imin increased by 74% and 95% at the distal third and Imax increased 15 and 24 % in the same respective regions, an indication the tibia is more symmetric.

Calcaneal density was not consistent in the 3 subjects and showed R and L differences outside the 0.6% observed in normal men. Calcaneal density was less (by 5.7 and 8 %) in the affected than the unaffected limb in two subjects who also had functional problems in gait.  The lower heel density may be an indication of less load on the affected limb and was not present in the subject who reported normal gait and skiing activity post surgery.

Conclusions: All three subjects with fibular resection showed an increase in cross-sectional area as well as structural parameters related to stiffness and strength, an indication of increased loading of the tibia from the ipsilateral side of the fibular resection resulting in increased bone deposition.  This points to a role of the fibula in driving the shape of the tibia cross-section and in load bearing.

title: Stone Risk Factors in a study of Exercise Countermeasures for Bed Rest Deconditioning
objectives: The aim of this 30-day bed rest study was to investigate the effects of short-term, high-intensity isotonic and isokinetic exercise training on working capacity, muscular strength and on orthostatic tolerance, posture and gait. Other data on muscle atrophy, bone mineralization and density, endocrine analyses concerning vasoactivity, fluid and electrolyte balance, muscle intermediary metabolism, performance and mood of the subjects were also acquired and are enumerated in summary ARCbedrest063.  This report summarizes the effort to determine the effect of each exercise on the risk of developing kidney stones.
approach:
The protocol consisted of a 7-day ambulatory control period, 30 days of –6o head-down bed rest and a 4.5-day ambulatory recovery period. 
Nineteen 32-42 year old male volunteers were divided into three groups:  no-exercise controls (NE, n=5), isotonic exercise with an electronic Quinton ergometer, (IE, n=7), and isokinetic exercise on the Lido ergometer (KE, n=7).  The exercise regimens were conducted near peak levels for 30 min in the morning and 30 min in the afternoon 5 days/wk. Diets were controlled to provide 2,800 kcal/d for the NE group and 3,100 kcal/d for the two exercise groups. Twenty-four hour urine specimens were collected during the ambulatory pre-bed rest phase of the study and on the 8th and 28th days of bed rest.  The specimens were sent to Mission Pharmacal Co. in Dallas, Texas for analysis.
results:
Average caloric consumption of 2,678 kcal/d in NE, 2,833 kcal/d in IE and 2,890 kcal in KE resulted in mean losses of 1.01, 0.85, and 0.0 kg during bed rest in each group, respectively.  Plasma volume was maintained only in the IE group and significantly reduced in NE and KE groups. 

There were significant increases in urinary calcium in only 5 bed rest subjects who represented 1-2 from each exercise group.  Urinary calcium increased 20% in control, 16% in IE and 2% in KE.  The increases in urine calcium were not statistically significant probably because of small group size.  However, the activity product of the stable form of calcium phosphate in urine increased in both exercising groups, especially the cyclists (IE).  Brushite increased non-significantly from 2.1 to 4.5 in control and significantly from 2.7 to 6.4 in the cyclists and from 3.2 to 6.2 in the isokinetic group. These increases would favor stone formation and can be related to lower urine volumes or higher urinary pH (6.1 to 7.2 in control, 6.2 to 6.6 in IE and 6.5 to 7.2 in KE). Of interest is that differences in Brushite present on the 8th bed rest day persisted to the 28th bed rest day in both exercise groups (significant only in the IE group because of a large variance in the KE group).

title: Effect of Spaceflight on Microbial Gene Expression and Virulence (MDRV)
objectives: Determine the effect of spaceflight on the virulence potential of the pathogenic microorganisms: S. typhimurium, P. aeruginosa, and C. albicans, immediately following their return from spaceflight Determine the effect of spaceflight on cellular morphology of the pathogenic microorganisms: S. typhimurium, P. aeruginosa, and C. albicans, immediately following their return from spaceflight Compare S. typhimurium cultures grown in nutrient limited media to those grown in nutrient rich media to determine the potential effects of nutritional status on changes in gene expression, morphology, and virulence properties during spaceflight.
approach: Three GAPs were used for each of 4 projects:  S. typhimurium in rich media; S. typhimurium in minimal media; P. aeruginosa in rich media; C. albicans in rich media.  Temperature loggers and radiation area monitors (RAMs) were used to collect the thermal profile and radiation profile experienced by the flight samples, respectively.  Synchronous ground controls (12 GAPs) were loaded at the same time as the flight.  Asynchronous ground controls were performed at the PI lab post-flight utilizing the Rotating Wall Vessel and the temperature data recorded from the flight experiment temperature logging devices.  The FPAs (flight and synchronous) were loaded with the flight reagents (terminators and growth media) and static cultures and installed into the GAPs at approximately launch minus L-5 days.  On-orbit, the cultures were activated with growth media on Flight Day 10 and allowed to grow for 24 hours +/- 2 hours. The experiment was terminated with addition of either RNA Later II, for post flight microarray studies, or fresh media for post flight LD50 studies.  Postflight, microarray gene expression was performed on the fixed samples.  A mouse (465 BALB/c female) LD-50 study was also performed at KSC to assess changes in virulence potential.
results: Confirmed the effects of microgravity observed in the STS-115 Microbe experiments and homed in on the importance of the growth medium for gene expression and virulence of the microbe Samonella during spaceflight. A large number of Hfq-regulated genes were differentially expressed (167).  Hfq is known to regulate one third of the 167 differentially expressed genes. Hfq may serve to globally modify bacterial responses to microgravity.

title: Streptococcus Pneumoniae Expression of Genes in Space (SPEGIS)
objectives: To investigate the alteration of virulence of Streptococcus pneumoniae ATCC 6304 after growth in spaceflight compared to cultures grown on earth.  This bacterium was investigated on a previous flight (STS-118, SPEGIS I) for alterations in gene and protein expression and is an opportunistic respiratory pathogen. It has been isolated previously from a crewmember's nasopharynx preflight indicating that this normally commensal bacterium could become a risk during flight.
approach: Streptococcus pneumoniae ATCC 6304 was cultured in spaceflight or in parallel ground controls using Group Activation Pack (GAP) hardware.  MDRV flew on STS-123 (March, 2008). The Flight FPAs in GAP8 were activated on MET day 11.  The Ground Control (GC) samples were activated   one hour later.  FPAs were received ~2.5 hrs after landing.  The culture media from each sample was collected and aliquots diluted for serial dilution and plate counting on Blood Agar plates.  There was minimal growth in the Flight samples.  The optical density at 600nm of flight cultures with high initial loading was similar with the ground control with low loading, which showed a slightly higher value. Because of similarity of optical density which is indicative of bacterial cell numbers, the Flight high load and Ground Control low pools were selected for the post-flight mouse virulence study.
results: There was an obvious and clear difference in mouse virulence between the flight and ground control cultures in terms of LD50 (16-fold lower for the flight grown cultures).  However, the LD50 of these suboptimal cultures were much higher (FLT 35-fold; GC  566-fold) than that observed for strain ATCC 6304 under optimal culture conditions (37C). The differences between flight and ground cultures may reflect virulence differences arising from changes in bacterial gene expression or altered physiology/metabolism in the space environment. Alternatively, the differences observed could arise from the differential growth temperature between the flight and ground controls.  This seems unlikely as the ground cultures were at a higher temperature closer to the growth optimal where maximal virulence is observed.  However, this remains to be fully established with additional flight experimentation and ground controls.

title: Bacterial Physiology and Virulence on Earth and in Microgravity
objectives: The focus of this research is to characterize the effects of microgravity on cell proliferation and production of Exotoxin A (ETA) in both P. aeruginosa PA01 and PA103.  P. aeruginosa PA01 is a stock strain that has historically been used for virulence and other studies; t is known to have a low level of ETA production, while strain PA103 produces much higher amounts of ETA under similar conditions.
approach: Both P. aeruginosa PA01 and PA103 were cultured in defined medium (SDM1) that limits the production of Exotoxin A.  The experiment was contained in Fluid Processing Apparatus (FPA) tubes that were placed into Group Activation Packs (GAP); each GAP held 8 FPAs. The pseudomonas exotoxin experiment occupied 2 GAPs, including 4 replicates of each of the Time O (T0) cultures.  The first chamber of each FPA tube contained Modified Simple Defined Medium 2 (MSDM2), a growth medium which stimulates ETA production; the second chamber contained the culture suspension; and the third chamber contained a fixative (MSDM2 containing 0.4% formalin).  The experiment was activated on-orbit (10 days after launch and 14 days after preparation) by introducing the culture suspension into the growth medium (TO). Ground controls were activated 30 minutes later.   After 51.5 hours (ca. 2 days, T2D) incubating at ambient temperature, the experiment was terminated by adding fixative.  Post-flight, the cells were counted and ETA measured by ELISA.
results: Both ETA and cell count results at the start of incubation (T0) were similar for flight and ground cultures. Results after 51.5 h incubation (2 days) indicate that PA103 produced, as expected, more ETA than PA01 in both flight samples and ground controls. However, cell count numbers for both cultures at 2 Days, either after Ground or Flight incubation, were all very close to 109 cells/ml. This shows that on-orbit incubation did not affect growth of these cultures within 2 days at ambient temperatures. PA103 produced about 10% more ETA in flight samples, both at T0 and after 2 days incubation, while ETA results for PA01 were about the same for flight vs. ground conditions. However, these results are probably not statistically significant (yet to be determined) because of the variability of results after incubation.

title: Drug Resistance and Virulence (MDRV): Saccharomyces cerevisiae Gene Expression and Susceptibility to Voriconazole During Spaceflight
objectives: The objective is to investigate the alteration in antifungal drug response of Saccharomyces cerevisiae after growth in spaceflight compared to growth on Earth. 1) Evaluate the single drug dilution of voriconazole selected to be flown on PharmaSat. 2) Investigate genomic expression as influenced by voriconazole during spaceflight. Saccharomyces cerevisiae has been investigated for susceptibility to voriconazole and alterations in gene expression using the High Aspect Ratio Vessel (HARV) bioreactor and Gene Chip Yeast Genome S98 Arrays in our laboratory. Modeled microgravity data indicate that S. cerevisiae has an increased resistance to voriconazole and that gene expression involving the ergosterol biosynthetic pathway is altered. GAP experiments will allow us to confirm these modeled microgravity data. Susceptibility data during the spaceflight will validate the concentration of voriconazole selected to be used in the PharmaSat experiment planned for 2008.
approach: Yeast was grown under various concentrations of the antifungal compound.
results: Microgravity and modeled microgravity data suggest that resistance to azoles is increased, which can have an important effect upon management of fungal infections during space habitation.

title: Antigen-Specific CD4+ T Cell Priming and Memory Response During Spaceflight (Mouse Immunology)
objectives: Spaceflight immunosuppression is a significant obstacle to long-term human space travel. Of foremost concern is whether astronauts/cosmonauts may be able to generate effective protective immune responses against infections while in spaceflight. Over 50% of the Apollo astronauts had bacterial or viral infections during flight or within 1 week of landing. Subsequent studies demonstrated that T-cells had a blunted activation response in vivo and in vitro after spaceflight. Studies from this lab have shown that early T-cell activation changes in microgravity.  Using an innovative mouse experimental model, this set of experiments that will test 1) whether initial specific activation of T cells is intact and 2) whether memory T cell function is maintained during spaceflight. Memory T cells are the mediators of immune protection after vaccinations and whether they function properly during spaceflight will determine if pre-flight vaccinations may be useful interventions. These experiments directly address the research priorities of the Bion-M1 mission to investigate resistance to infections and acquired immune responses without using infectious agents onboard the spacecraft.
approach:
C57BL/6J female mice were exposed to microgravity for 15 days on STS-131. Splenocytes were harvested within 2-3 hrs post-flight, cells isolated and activated (~9 hrs after landing) with Concanavalin A and antiCD28 for 2.5 hrs. Cells were harvested (11.5 hrs post landing) and stored in RNAprotect (Qiagen) at -80°C. Total RNA was isolated from the activated samples with RNeasy (Qiagen). 

Transgenic T-cells specific for a model protein were transferred into recipient mice with implanted mini-pumps that released the model protein along with noninfectious purified bacterial cell wall components.  This model allowed T-cell stimulation to be delayed until after mice acclimated to spaceflight to ensure that T-cell activation was initiated in microgravity. Naive (previously unactivated) T-cells were transferred to determine whether responses to new immunological challenges (e.g., new infections) were intact. Also, previously activated T-cells that developed into memory cells were transferred to determine whether robust secondary responses could be elicited during spaceflight.
results:
Task Book 8/2015 update:
We found that T-cell activation markers Interleukin-2 (IL-2), IL-2R alpha, interferon gamma (IFN gamma), TNF alpha, and IL-17 using quantitative real time RT-PCR. Expression of IL-2, IL-2R alpha, IFN gamma, CCL-3, TNF alpha, and IL-17 mRNA levels were significantly decreased 2-10 fold in the activated flight group as compared to ground controls. Expression of cyclophilin, and VEGF was unchanged. Protein synthesis of IL-2, IL-2R alpha, IFN gamma, CCL-3, TNF alpha, and IL-17 was decreased in the flight cell supernatants when compared to ground controls. The observed downregulation of induction of T-cell activation genes combined with changes of synthesis of these proteins show that animal immune cells require gravity and that the spaceflight induced changes in T-cell function remains after landing. More importantly, these data correspond with previous findings of human immune function in astronauts after return to Earth and support the conclusion that there is a physiological dependence of the immune system on gravity. This suggests that at least one mammalian system developed on this planet is dependent on gravity for full function.

Immune function in mice splenocytes was significantly depressed, similar to the immunosuppressive changes seen in spaceflown T cells. Additionally, gene array data from spaceflown animals showed a similar pattern of immunosuppression as compared to animals on the ground with down regulation of several immune genes of interest. Expression of these genes was confirmed with qRTPCR. Activation was shown to be reduced in flown animals.  Finally, the chemokines and cytokines associated with T cell activation were significantly increased with activation in ground animals and were suppressed in the flown animals. 

Impaired induction of T-cell activation genes as well as the altered synthesis of immune chemokines and cytokines show that gravity is required for optimal immune function in mice. The spaceflight induced changes in T cell function remained for many hours after landing, suggesting that µg induced changes in the splenocytes require time to repair. These data suggest that at least one mammalian system developed on this planet is dependent on gravity for full function. More importantly, these data correspond well with our previous findings of inhibited human immune function in astronauts after return to Earth and support the conclusion that there is a physical dependence of the immune system on gravity.
publication_titles: Spaceflight impairs antigen-specific tolerance induction in vivo and increases inflammatory cytokines | Spaceflight alters expression of microRNA during T-cell activation | Spaceflight and simulated microgravity cause a significant reduction of key gene expression in early T-cell activation

title: A Graviresponsive Time-keeping Protein of the Plant and Animal Surface
objectives: A graviresponsive protein with a potential time-keeping function located at the external surface of plant and animal cells will be described. The protein functions as a hydroquinone (NADH) oxidase (NOX) with protein disulfide-thiol interchange activity with an essential role in driving cell enlargement. Both plant and animal cells must enlarge following division to some minimum size in order to divide again. If NOX activity is inhibited, cells do not enlarge, division ceases and, after several days, the inhibited cells undergo apoptosis. The NOX protein has been characterized extensively from both plant (soybean) and animal (HeLa = human cervial carcinoma) sources. One of several unique properties of the protein is that its activity is stimulated both by unit gravity and by centrifugal forces of up to 400 g. An engineered form of the protein expressed by HeLa cells and produced in bacteria also in graviresponsive. Additionally, enzymatic activities catalyzed by the protein oscillate with a temperature-compensated period of 24 min (60 times per 24 h day) and is implicated as an ultradian (period less that 24 h) driver of cell growth. In both plant and animal cells, rates of cell enlargement also oscillate with a period of 24 min and gravi-stimulation correlates with increased growth. As such, the findings may be relevant to plant production in space, astronaut health and well being and other applications. The structural basis for both oscillatory (time-keeping) for collaborative studies to both explore and exploit the unusual behavioral characteristics of the protein which may be of interest to NASA.

title: Effects of Microgravity on the Development of the Immune System
objectives:
Taskbook Description:

We have developed a microgravity organ culture dish system (MOCDS) that allows for the normal development of T cells from hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) progenitors. This system mimics the normal development of T cells outside of the body, away from stress hormones made during flight. The MOCDS can be used both on the clinostat and during spaceflight. The culture systems are easy to set up and manipulate, and they should be readily adaptable to the Group Activation Pack (GAP) for use in the Commercial Generic Bioprocessing Apparatus - Isothermal Containment Module (GBA-ICM) Payload.

Our work has revolved around studying the effects of vector-averaged gravity, as produced in the clinostat, on the development of T cells in organ culture. In our previous work utilizing the MOCDS we demonstrated the gravity-dependence of T cell development. The results from this work suggested that the full sequelae of pre-TCR signaling may not be taking place in vector-averaged gravity, as indicated by a block at the immature single positive stage of development. Since then, we have set out to determine what the underlying mechanism was for this block. Additionally, using the information obtained from the mechanistic studies, we aimed to reverse the effects that we observed after exposure to clinorotation.
results: In our current work, we were able to show that after 4 days of vector-averaged gravity exposure FTOC exhibited a significant reduction in CD4+CD8+ double positive (DP) cell production. Furthermore, CD4-CD8- double negative (DN) cells expressed lower levels of CD3 on their surface. DN, as well as immature single positive (ISP) cells, also expressed reduced levels of the IL-7 receptor ? chain (CD127). These reductions in CD3 and CD127 expression were concomitantly associated with an increased production of tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-?. We were also able to show that addition of an exogenous signal (anti-CD3? monoclonal antibody) to these cultures effectively mitigated the MMG-induced effects, therefore suggesting that MMG-exposure, does in fact, cause a signal dampening effect on developing thymocytes.

title: Streptococcus pneumoniae Gene Expression and Virulence Potential in the Space Environment (SPEGIS) Reflight
objectives: Streptococcus pneumoniae Expression of Gene in Space (SPEGIS) will examine the behavior and growth of bacteria in microgravity and investigate the effects of the space environment on the gene expression, protein production, and virulence of the bacteria Streptococcus pneumoniae. The data collected will also provide insight on what types of bacterial infections may occur during long-duration space missions and the risks to crew members. This is a follow-on study to the  MDRV-SPEGIS II study in which the PI discovered a profound increase in the virulence of Streptococcus pneumoniae grown during flight and injected into mice post-flight. Of particular interest is identification of S. Pneumoniae virulence factors that might account for the higher virulence seen in mice infected with space-grown bacteria.
approach: The experimental setup used for this flight utilized the Group Activation Pack (GAP,) which is essentially a cylinder with a crank in the tops. The BAPS held FPAs (Fluid Processig Apparatueses0. FPAs are basically test tubes with rubber septa fitted into them to make compartments which held the bacterial samples. The bacteria cultures were activated for growth when the astronauts cranked down the lid of the GAP which pushed the rubber septa together in the FPAs, mixing the different components of the cultures (media, inoculum, additives).
results:
A similarity of flight samples,regardless of which shuttle and which culturing method used was observed. Genes that were identified as differential on average were also reproducible across shuttle flights.  213 individual genes were identified as significantly altered as a result of growth in space.

Collectively, these results provide strong evidence that microgravity environments increase the virulence of this opportunistic pathogen. Understanding virulence enhancement after bacterial growth in the space environment is critical to the long-term goal to devise countermeasures to protect space travelers/residents from infectious diseases during long-term flight or space habitation

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.9802291

title: The Effects of Microgravity on the Tissue Regenerative Potential of Stem Cells - Space Tissue Loss Stem Cell Regeneration (STL-SCR)
objectives: The work proposed here was centered on the hypothesis that somatic stem cells responsible for tissue regeneration may require the mechanical stimulation of gravity to proliferate and differentiate, and to regenerate tissues at normal rates, and therefore exposure to microgravity may slow down tissue regenerative processes based on adult stem cell progenitors. Results from spaceflight experiments with amphibian (newt) tail regeneration during the Foton M2 and Foton M3 missions suggest that spaceflight significantly retards tissue regeneration by interfering with newt tail blastema stem cell progenitor proliferation and differentiation. These findings suggest that normal long-term homeostatic tissue regenerative processes in mammals may also be sensitive to the absence of gravity-generated forces in space, and that this may cause tissue degeneration. This study sought to gather further evidence about the role of gravity-generated forces in promoting tissue regeneration and the molecular mechanisms by which gravity in stem cells is translated into proliferation and differentiation.
approach: The Cell Culture Module (CCM) hardware was used to differentiate mouse embryonic stem cells in spaceflight and in ground controls, using an experimental model based on the formation of embryoid bodies containing various differentiated tissue lineages and cell types. Three bioreactors were recovered with cells fixed during spaceflight for messenger ribonucleic acid (mRNA)/gene expression analysis, and three maintained live cells for cell biology assays conducted postflight.
results: Experimental analysis showed that spaceflight cell cultures fixed in space did not express tissue lineage markers characteristic of differentiation and retained elevated stem cell markers, versus ground-control cultures that expressed tissue markers indicative of normally differentiated embryoid bodies and lacked stem cell markers. In ground controls, a variety of gene markers of tissue differentiation quantified using real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) appeared normally as differentiated embryoid bodies. These markers included specific genes for endoderm, mesoderm, ectoderm, and specific cell types within these tissues. Additionally, the disappearance of stem cell markers was observed in these controls. However in spaceflight samples, nearly of 80% of the tissue lineage markers characteristic of embryoid body differentiation were not detected, and stem cell markers remained elevated. Measurements of cell number, DNA content, and glucose consumption also revealed a slight decrease in initial cell proliferation in space, but no changes in cell viability or apoptosis. Finally, live cells recovered in adhesion outgrowth assays from postflight embryoid bodies in spaceflight bioreactors, when cultured, showed greater potential for de novo differentiation of contractile cardiomyocyte colonies, suggesting greater numbers of undifferentiated progenitors were present. The results suggest that spaceflight inhibited normal stem cell differentiation and maintained the stem cell potential of the differentiating cultures, and support the hypothesis that the tissue regenerative potential of stem cells may be decreased during spaceflight. Further gene expression analysis and cell biology studies are currently underway.
publication_titles: Stem Cell Health and Tissue Regeneration in Microgravity

title: Space Tissue Loss Innate Mammalian Mechanisms Universal Novel Expressions (STL-IMMUNE)
objectives:
The objectives of this flight experiment were directly related to and complemented ground-based work; the goal of which was to identify universally conserved molecular responses to ground-based spaceflight analogue culture across a variety of bacterial and mammalian cell types. The research focus for the flight portion was to investigate the effects of the space flight environment on the host-to-pathogen interaction by conducting the first-ever in-flight infection of human cells with a pathogenic bacteria.

The specific objectives were: a) determine the evolutionarily conserved role for space flight-responsive ribonucleic acid (RNA) binding proteins in human intestinal and lung epithelial cells before and after infection; b) determine the effect of space flight on select innate immune defense mechanisms and stress responses of human epithelial cells before and after infection; and, c) determine the effect of space flight on cellular differentiation and morphology of human intestinal epithelial cells before and after infection.

The work performed for this flight experiment, and all cell lines and bacterial strains used therein, are directly related to and complement on-going work in our ground-based grant - NRA NNH08ZTT003N; "RNA Binding Proteins as Evolutionarily Conserved Cellular Spaceflight Response Mechanisms"-
approach:
Sample analysis was finalized for alterations in protein expression (via global proteomic response profiling/iTRAQ), cellular differentiation, immune function (inflammatory response mediators),and stress responses associated with space flight in the presence and absence of infection. All flight sample comparisons were made to identical synchronous ground controls housed in duplicate Cell Culture Module (CCM) hardware as that used for flight.

Experimental conditions:
1) Twelve bioreactors: 10 with HT-29 cells, 2 with A549 cells.
2) Cells were adherent to the hollow fibers.
3) Five bioreactors were injected with bacteria in flight on mission Day 11.
4) Eight bioreactors were injected with RNALater 2, and four bioreactors were injected with 4% paraformaldehyde, in flight on mission Day 11.
5) All 12 bioreactors were sampled for conditioned medium.
6) Continuous medium circulation at 37°C with 5% CO2.
results:
Sample analysis is finalized for global alterations in both transcriptomic and proteomic profiling, cellular differentiation, immune function (inflammatory response mediators), and stress responses associated with space flight in the presence and absence of infection. All flight sample comparisons were made to identical synchronous ground controls housed in duplicate CCM hardware as that used for flight. Through iTRAQ analysis, we have identified 86 total unique human proteins (95% confidence, 1% FDR) that were expressed by the HT-29 intestinal epithelial cells cultured on orbit either before or after infection with Salmonella typhimurium. These proteins belonged to a range of different functional classes and categories, including those important for metabolism, apoptosis, cell communication, immune function, and others. Of the 89 proteins identified, a unique subset of these were found to be differentially regulated in response to space flight, and showed differences in response to infection between flight and ground cultures. Collectively, we found intriguing differences in how microgravity uniquely impacted the space flight infected intestinal cells as compared to the ground controls. We have also finalized global proteomic response profiling for the A549 lung epithelial cells as well. Moreover, immunohistochemical profiling has revealed differences in both distribution and expression patterns of targeted host cell proteins and alterations in cellular cytoarchitecture.


No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data figures are available in Characterization of the invasive, multidrug resistant non-typhoidal Salmonella strain D23580 in a murine model of infection.

title: The Role of Artificial Gravity in Promoting Tissue-Regenerative Matrix-Integrin-Kinase Cell Signaling
objectives:
The broad working hypothesis is that in space the mechanical unloading caused by microgravity can affect the growth and regenerative health of a broad range of mammalian tissues. Conversely, we hypothesize that gravity on earth activates a key, complex cell-signaling pathway responsible for maintaining normal regenerative tissue growth, the extracellular matrix(ECM)-integrin-kinase signaling pathway.  The work we proposed was centered on the specific hypothesis that somatic stem cells responsible for tissue regeneration may require the mechanical stimulation of gravity to proliferate and differentiate, and to regenerate tissues at normal rates, and that microgravity may slow down tissue regenerative processes. Specifically this experiment will investigate the role of gravity-generated forces in promoting tissue regeneration, and the molecular mechanisms by which gravity in stem cells is translated into proliferation and differentiation. Ground artificial gravity studies were expanded to microgravity during rapid turnaround spaceflight opportunities in the Space Tissue Loss (STL1) and (STL2) experiments with stem cell differentiation. This work was integrated in the ongoing grant research expanding the artificial gravity approach to the questions to also include microgravity.  

Specifically, the following aims are proposed to broadly test the hypotheses: 
1) Gravity-dependent mechanical loading regulates cell proliferation and differentiation in the in-vivo newt tail regeneration model 
2) Gravity-dependent mechanical-load signaling in somatic stem cell progenitors regulates their proliferation and differentiation in-vitro
approach:
The Space Tissue Loss 2 experiment on STS-135 studies microgravity effects on keratinocyte differentiation from embryonic stem cells. The choice of keratinocyte differentiation as a model to study the effects of microgravity versus 1g on stem cell differentiation addresses the key question in Aim 2 for a specific cell type, which was not possible with the mixed-lineage embryoid body model flown on STL1, and in addition addresses the important issue of wound closure by keratinocytes in microgravity. Between April and July 2011, development and ground verification of the spaceflight protocol for differentiation of mouse embryonic stem cells into keratinocytes was completed before the STL2 keratinocyte experiment was flown on the final space shuttle mission, STS-135.

Specifically, for the cell culture flight experiments, hollow fiber bioreactors seeded with stem cells were used and initiated differentiation immediately prior to flight with specific growth and transcription factors and cell culture medium to induce keratinocyte differentiation. After 15 days spaceflight and just prior to landing a set of flight bioreactors was fixed in space with RNA later to preserve mRNA, while another set was brought back to earth for live cell functional assays, such as cell migration in an vitro wound-healing assay. Following the flight, ground based sample preparation qPCR analysis of gene expression and of post-flight cell cultures were performed. The analyses also included migration quantification on wound healing assays and histocytochemistry. As a result of these studies we already determined that keratinocytes differentiated in microgravity with altered expression of keratins and reduced migratory ability, suggesting that differentiation and function of keratinocytes in wound closure would be diminished in microgravity.

Finally, to complete the studies on stem cell proliferation and gene expression in response to gravity mechanostimulation, cell culture centrifugation studies of mouse embryonic stem cells were conducted at 1g vs. 25g. In these studies, no alterations in proliferation were observed, as previously observed in differentiated osteoprecursors, suggesting that stem cells and later stage progenitor lineages respond to mechanical stimulation in different ways. Gene expression studies suggest that in stem cells gravity mechanostimulation affects differentiation and stemness markers while leaving proliferation regulators unchanged.
results:
Results for Aim 1 are reported in the publication "Gravity Mechanical Load Modulates Tissue Growth and Regeneration in the Newt Pleurodeles waltl."  Results from the Foton M3 spaceflight experiment were analyzed in the context of this experiment as part of obtaining results for tissue regeneration going from unloaded condition in aquaria, and microgravity, as well as under normal load at 1g and hypergravity at 2g. In total these results offer a very complete picture of responses to varying regenerative responses to increasing load, and of the importance and necessity of gravity for normal tissue regenerative processes. 

For Aim 2, the cell biology results clearly show a gravity-dependent switch between stem cells and their functional differentiated descendents, and suggest microgravity is a strong inhibitor of regenerative processes.  The results of from these studies are being prepared for publication.

This proposal has a companion spaceflight component entitled, "The Effects of Microgravity on the Tissue Regenerative Potential of Stem Cells--Flight." The spaceflight experiments are relevant to Aim 2.

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.
publication_titles: Microgravity reduces the differentiation and regenerative potential of embryonic stem cells

title: The Role of Artificial Gravity in Promoting Tissue-Regenerative Matrix-Integrin-Kinase Cell Signaling (STL-SCR)
objectives: As with STL, the experiments conducted on STL2 were centered on the specific hypothesis that somatic stem cells responsible for tissue regeneration may require the mechanical stimulation of gravity to proliferate and differentiate, and to regenerate tissues at normal rates, and that therefore, exposure to microgravity may slow down tissue regenerative processes based on adult stem cell progenitors. In STL  significant microgravity effects were found on the ability of stem cells to differentiate using a stem cell to embryoid body model of tissue regeneration. STL 2 sought to complement previous studies by differentiation of a single cell type, keratinocytes, from mouse embryonic stem cells, and to replicate results on differentiation markers and tissue regeneration.
approach: The Cell Culture Module (CCM) hardware was used to differentiate mouse embryonic stem cells in spaceflight and in ground controls, using an experimental model based on the differentiation of a single tissue cell type. As in STL, three bioreactors were recovered with cells fixed during spaceflight for ribonucleic acid (RNA) for gene expression analysis, and three maintained live cells for cell biology assays conducted postflight.
results:
Preliminary experimental analysis shows that keratinocytes differentiated in spaceflight expressing various keratins in the cytoskeleton, however cells in cultures recovered from flight did not migrate normally, as required in keratinocyte-based wound closure. Gene expression is currently being analyzed to determine if markers characteristic of differentiation appear normally. The results so far suggest that spaceflight inhibited the migratory ability of microgravity-differentiated cultures, and also support the hypothesis that the tissue regenerative potential of stem cells may be decreased during spaceflight.

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.

title: Identification of mechano-sensors to protect against skeletal muscle atrophy
objectives: Skeletal muscle mass is maintained by a dynamic equilibrium between signals driving muscle growth and muscle atrophy. Protection against loss of muscle mass in anti-gravity requires enhancement of pro-growth signals to counter the drive toward atrophy. The muscle membrane harbors two protein complexes - the focal adhesion complex and the dystrophin glycoprotein complex  that are important for sensing stress at the membrane, and are thought to help coordinate the balance between growth and atrophy. Whether these complexes mediate identical, complementary, or divergent mechanical signals has been difficult to ascertain, yet both appear to be important for promoting muscle cell survival and muscle growth. Identifying the specific pathways utilized by these mechano-sensors is critical to the development of strategies to maintain muscle mass even in the absence of environmental cues for growth. We will employ an in vitro system to map out the signal transduction pathways triggered by mechanical stress via the focal adhesion and dystrophin glycoprotein complexes, the resultant changes in gene expression, and muscle cell survival. Specific inhibition of each complex will be achieved through isolation of muscle cells from mice lacking members of each complex. These studies will establish a mechanistic understanding of the mechanical responses through each membrane complex, and provide the groundwork for developing novel strategies to prevent muscle atrophy in space.
approach: The description of the approach is integrated in the discussion of the results below.
results:
Task Book Update (7/29/2011):

The goals of this project are to understand the contribution of a protein complex in muscle cell membranes to sensing load. Using cultured cells from muscle of mice that lack specific members of this protein complex, called the sarcoglycan complex, enables us to test if the complex was important for normal load signaling. First, we grew muscle cells from the mice on flexible membranes so that we could stretch the cells after they matured, and thereby change dynamic load. We found that there were changes in the normal signaling pattern, not only at rest, but also in response to stretch when the sarcoglycan complex was missing. In short, it appeared that the muscles from the normal cells had a transient signaling response in all pathways we measured after a period of stretch. However, the cells lacking the complex had no increased responses in the classical load signaling pathways, but also had heightened responses in the pathways directly coupled to protein synthesis. We conclude that the sarcoglycan complex is a central component of load sensing in muscle.

Next we examined changes in gene expression in the two cultures. We found that the absence of the sarcoglycan complex caused increased expression of genes in the "atrogene" pathway, as well as increased expression of IGF-I. Thus, these cultures appear to drive both growth and atrophy pathways when the sarcoglycan complex is absent. At this point, we do not know if these changes result in changes at the protein level, or which pathway ultimately controls the state of the muscle growth. The responses did not change dramatically after a short stretching period, so longer stretching protocol will be used to determine if they evoke more dramatic changes in gene expression.

Finally, we were given the opportunity to dissect muscles from mice subjected to anti-gravity and age and sex matched ground controls on the last space shuttle mission, STS-135. These will be used to extend our understanding of how the sarcoglycan complex is modified by lack of load, including localization and post-translational modifications (phosphorylation). We obtained tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum longus, and masseter muscles from these mice. While much has been achieve in previous shuttle missions with mouse samples, the sarcoglycan complex involvement has not been addressed. From related projects, we have reported that there are clear differences between the masseters and TA muscles in terms of loading signals and expression differences. We hypothesize that the loading of masseter muscles comes in part from normal chewing activity, and so the mouse masseters may be spared from atrophy. However, this remains to be tested. 

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.

title: The role of mechanoperception in gravity responses of the model organism Drosophila melanogaster
objectives:
The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster offers unique opportunities for genetic and molecular studies of cellular processes common to all multicellular organisms. In order to learn more of the pathways activated by the force of gravity, we have isolated a series of Drosophila mutants that have altered behavioral responses to gravity. These and other Drosophila mutants will be used to explore the signaling pathways underlying one the most marked changes associated with exposure to microgravity - loss of muscle tissue. Specifically the studies will address the issue of whether mechanosensory neurons and associated central brain circuitry regulate muscles responses to altered gravitational force. 
These studies will provide unique information on the regulation of muscle metabolism in response to changes in gravity and significant insight into these adaptive processes.
approach: The experiments were performed in the model organism Drosophila melanogaster, a small fruit fly.  The project grew out of our previous work to identify genes that contribute to responses to gravitational force. During the course of this funding, we have pursued molecular and behavioral studies of two of these genes, which we have named yuri gagarin (yuri) and jim lovell (lov) in honor of our NASA funding and two heroic space pioneers. A powerful technique has been developed within Drosophila that allows conditional inactivation of targeted neurons. This technique will be used to inactivate the neurons under study and to determine whether this loss of neural input alters the responses of the muscles to hypergravity. Quantitative PCR will be used to examine muscle responses. A selection of genes indicated by other studies to show altered expression in response to altered gravitational force will be examined.
results:
We have established that the yuri gene encodes a protein, Tropomyosin, that interacts with a key component of the muscle contraction apparatus and that yuri is required for attachment of the centriole (basal body) to the spermatid nucleus.  We have also established that the Lov protein is expressed in a subset of chordotonal neurons associated with muscles involved in locomotion. 

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.

title: Modifying Protein-Lipid Interactions of a Microbial Mechanosensitive Channel: Functional Screens after Tethering Specific Residues to the Membrane
objectives: At the foundation of the ability of organisms to detect gravity is the capacity to detect mechanical forces. It is, for humans, the sheering force on stereocillia of vestibular hair cells, and for plants, the sedimentation of amyloplasts within a root cell. One of the primary molecules underlying the ability to detect mechanical forces is the mechanosensitive channel. To date, the best characterized is MscL, a microbial MechanoSensitive Channel of Large conductance. MscL opens a large pore in response to membrane tension; several studies now suggest sensing membrane tension is common among many mechanosensitive channels from microbes to plants and animals. Hence, the interactions between mechanosensitive channels and the lipid-membrane environment are of tremendous interest. Manipulating these interactions should have functional consequences that yield important clues of how the channel normally interacts with lipid. We have generated and characterized a library of mutated MscL channels in which each residue in and around the transmembrane domains has been sequentially mutated to cysteine. Treatment of these mutants with sulfhydryl reagents containing aromatic rings or alkyl chains should encourage hydrophobic interactions or tether the residue to the membrane. If the modified residue is normally within a hydrophobic environment primarily when in a specific state (closed, transition or open), then that state would be stabilized. We have developed an in vivo screen that allows us to rapidly assess functional changes in the MscL channel simply by monitoring bacterial growth; channels in which the open, closed or even various transition states have been stabilized are identified. Once manipulations that lead to functional consequences are selected, the exact functional changes of MscL will be assessed by electrophysiological assays. Preliminary experiments have demonstrated the feasibility of this approach. When completed, this study will yield critical insight into how mechanosensitive channels sense and respond to membrane tension.
approach:
Task Book Description (7/9/2012):
The project was basically to utilize an in vivo screen approach to determine areas that may be dynamic within the membrane. Basically, we utilized a library of individual cysteine mutations throughout the protein. Cysteine is unique in that it is reactive to sulfhydryl reagents. Thus, you can post-translationally modify any residue. Here we used modifiers that made the region more hydrophobic, i.e. wanting to go into the membrane. The assumption is that certain regions will normally go into the membrane upon activation of sensor; by forcing this region into the membrane, we may artificially cause the channel to be activated. We know from several other experiments that if this occurs in a living cell, it causes the cell to be less viable or die. Therefore, we can use this as a screen. The modifying reagents we used are: DMTS, which adds a fatty acid chain and would be very aggressive in burying the region within the membrane; MTSBn, which adds what equates to a phenylalanine to the site; and 4-HB-MTS, which is the same as previous but contains a polar hydroxyl group, thus adding what equates to a tyrosine.
results:
Task Book Update (7/9/2012):

The mechanosensitive channels of large conductance, MscL, and of smaller conductance, MscS, are the two major MS channels in bacteria. They effect a coordinated response to hypo-osmotic shock, serving as emergency release valves for the rapid flux of fluid and solutes. MscL from E. coli was the first to be identified, and is perhaps the best studied mechanosensor from any species. A crystal structure of the M. tuberculosis MscL has been solved, revealing a pentameric structure in a nearly-closed pore of about 4 Å. An extremely large conformational change of this protein must occur to achieve the open pore size of 30 Å estimated by conductance (the amount of ions that pass through the pore in a unit of time) and by molecular sieving experiments. Several models of the gating of MscL have been proposed to explain the drastic structural rearrangements to transition from the closed to open states. Although the models differ in details, all agree that a thinning of the channel in the plane of the membrane, accompanied by the tilting of the transmembrane domains, are necessary to allow the pentamer to achieve an open pore structure. Both of these arrangements require dynamic interactions between regions of the protein and lipids.

Since X-ray crystallography of integral membrane proteins typically provide little information on their interactions with the surrounding lipids, other techniques have been used to address this issue for MscL. Utilizing some of these approaches, the second transmembrane span (TM2) of E. coli MscL, which faces the lipid membrane, has been defined. Five amino acids after the predicted end of TM2 there is a cluster of charged residues (RKKEE), which appear to be important for channel function; one E. coli MscL study showed that a C-terminal deletion at this point leads to non-functional channels, while another suggested that it serves as a pH sensor by changing the conformation of the region, and it was also suggested to interact with anionic phospholipids. Interestingly, analogous structures with a series of charged amino acids distal to the cytoplasmic transmembrane helix has been noted in other MS channel families. Hence, this region, just cytoplasmic of TM2, is a prime candidate to be involved in the dynamic lipid-protein interactions that occur during MscL gating. 

We finished the screening of the newly generated cysteine mutants, evaluated the data from the entire screen, and performed secondary screens on the "hits" that allow us to determine if the modifications lead to an overactive (gain-of-function; GOF) or dysfunctional (loss-of-function; LOF) sensor.

The data have provided a glimpse at how a mechanosensor dynamically interacts with the lipid membrane. Notably, the results of this project led to the finding that one residue appears to enter the lipid membrane in a piston-like manner upon sensor activation. 

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.

Chimeras reveal a single lipid-interface residue that controls MscL channel kinetics as well as mechanosensitivity | The oligomeric state of the truncated
mechanosensitive channel of large
conductance shows no variance in vivo | Three Routes to Modulate the Pore Size of the MscL Channel/Nanovalve | Sensing and Responding to Membrane Tension: The Bacterial MscL Channel as a Model System

title: Gravitational Effects on Biofilm Formation During Spaceflight (Micro-2)
objectives: This experiment is designed to further our understanding of Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus biofilms. The specific objectives are: compare biofilms formed on mixed cellulose ester membranes in normal gravity and during spaceflight, and examine the influence of mutations affecting motility on biofilm formation and structure; assess the effects of newly developed, non-leaching, nanotechnology-based, antimicrobial surfaces on biofilm formation during spaceflight and assess their potential to reduce the impact of biolfims in future spacecraft designs; identify changes in gene expression related to biofilm formation and virulence that occur when cells are cultured during spaceflight.
approach: This experiment utilized Bioserve's Group Activation Packs (GAPs) and Commercial Generic Bioprocessing Apparatus (CGBA), a flight certified incubator capable of holding 16 GAPs and controlling the temperature between 4ºC and 37ºC. Each GAP holds eight Fluid Processing Apparatus (FPA) inserts, composed of a glass barrel divided into three chambers that are separated from one another by rubber septa.  Each FPA contained growth medium with membranes in chamber A, a microbial culture suspended in stasis medium in chamber B, and a termination reagent, or more growth media, in chamber C. The culture growth was initiated inflight by a crewmember.  After the growth period (48 +/- 2 hours), a crewmember terminated the experiment.
results:
Results demonstrated that biofilm formation by both of these model organisms, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus, can occur during spaceflight and demonstrated that these organisms respond differently to the spaceflight environment. Direct measurements of biomass showed P. aeruginosa PA14 formed 3 to 5-fold more biofilm during spaceflight compared to normal gravity. In addition, quantitative analysis of CLSM images showed increased biofilm biomass (p<0.001) and increased biofilm thickness (p<0.001) in samples grown during spaceflight. A clear effect of phosphate on biofilm biomass was not observed in any biofilm samples. Spaceflight was also observed to increase planktonic biomass by approximately 10-fold, but only in the presence of 5 mM phosphate.
 
Conclusions: Direct biomass measurement and image analysis demonstrated that biofilm formation by P. aeruginosa PA14 increased during spaceflight. Phosphate concentration did not appear to affect the amount of biofilm formed during spaceflight or in normal gravity. However, both phosphate and spaceflight were observed to affect planktonic biomass.

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.
publication_titles: Spaceflight Promotes Biofilm Formation by Pseudomonas aeruginosa | Effect of spaceflight on Pseudomonas aeruginosa final cell density is modulated by nutrient and oxygen availability
