title: Integrated Stress Response in Space
objectives:
The long term objective of the proposed project is to evaluate the effects of "integrated stress response (ISR) in space" using bone metabolism as a model system. It is known that diverse environmental stresses including radiation, oxidative stress, nutrient limitation, and stress to the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) induces ISR. ISR leads to a pro-survival pathway (rescue program) for alleviation of cellular injury or, conversely, an apoptotic pathway for removal of damaged cells. Unloading in space is a unique form of environmental stress, but its physiological responses in connection to ISR is poorly understood. There are no changes in the objective of the proposal.

To test the above hypothesis and examine a potential role of ISR-driven signaling under unloading, we are conducting the following three specific aims using a mouse hindlimb suspension model: 

Aim 1: Evaluate phosphorylation of eIF2α and its kinases (e.g., PERK and GCN2) as well as translational efficiency in response to transient unloading 

Aim 2: Evaluate gene regulatory mechanisms linked to oxidative stress and stress to the ER in response to continuous unloading 

Aim 3: Examine the effects of administration of salubrinal (inhibitor of eIF2α phosphatase) on pro-survival signaling and bone metabolism
approach: The project will employ various molecular and cellular techniques including real-time polymerase chain reactions, Western blot analysis, enzymatic activity assays as well as bone histology. To test specific signaling, we will use pharmacological stimulators and inhibitors, and eIF2a-linked knockout mouse models.
results:
This study demonstrates that unloading-reduced bone mass is significantly restored by salubrinal. The result is consistent with salubrinal's stimulatory role on osteoblasts. The study provides evidence that salubrinal can be a potential therapeutic agent for preventing bone loss in disuse osteoporosis, which is encountered by astronauts under microgravity. 

The result, obtained in this study period, was published in the following peer-reviewed journals:

Zhao, L., Tanjung, N., Swarnkar, G., Ledet, E., Yokota, H. (2012). Regulation of eIF2α phosphorylation in hindlimb-unloaded and STS-135 space-flown mice. Adv. Space Res. 50:576-583. 

Zhao, L., Dodge, T., Ledet, E., Yokota, H. (2011). Knee loading stimulates bone formation in tail-suspended mouse hindlimb. Biol. Sci. Space 25:77-82.
publication_titles: Regulation of eIF2a phosphorylation in hindlimb-unloaded and STS-135 space-flown mice | Knee loading stimulates bone formation in tail-suspended mouse hindlimb
disease_areas: Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases

title: Novel Explorations Into the Interactions Between Light and Gravity Sensing in Plants - Seedling Growth-1
objectives:
The major goals of this project are to determine how gravity and light responses influence each other in plants and to better understand the cellular signaling mechanisms involved in plant tropisms. This proposed project builds on our previous spaceflight experiments on the ISS (TROPI-1, 2006; TROPI-2, 2010) with the EMCS using the model plant Arabidopsis. In this new proposed project, we plan to confirm and extend our discovery during the recent TROPI-2 ISS experiment of a novel red-light-based positive phototropic response in plant shoots. 

Thus, the specific objectives of this proposed research are: (1) To confirm and characterize the novel red light-dependent phototropic response in flowering plants. (2) To investigate the relationship between light and gravity by measuring thresholds in fractional gravity. (3) To determine whether the red light effect on blue-light-based phototropism is a direct or indirect effect.
approach:
The Seedling Growth series of experiments (1, 2, 3 and possibly 4) is supported collaboratively by NASA and the European Space Agency (ESA). The experiment will be conducted in the European Modular Cultivation System (EMCS) on the International Space Station (ISS).  The EMCS is operated by ESA and is located in the Columbus Module of the ISS.

These experiments can be accomplished with telemetric science and therefore fit with the extremely limited down mass capabilities of current spaceflight opportunities. The EMCS is an automated facility and can be operated largely with telemetry. Experimental containers will be launched via Space-X, and during an experimental run, images of seedling growth responses can be downlinked to Earth. Seedlings will be returned to Earth in the GLACIER freezer.
results:
Project 1: Phototropic curvature

Utilizing the European Modular Cultivation System (EMCS) on board the International Space Station (ISS), we investigated the interaction between phototropic and gravitropic responses in three Arabidopsis thaliana genotypes, Landsberg wild type, as well as mutants of phytochrome A and phytochrome B. Onboard centrifuges were used to create a fractional gravity gradient ranging from reduced gravity up to 1 g. A novel positive blue-light phototropic response of roots was observed during conditions of microgravity, and this response was attenuated at 0.1 g. In addition, a red-light pretreatment of plants enhanced the magnitude of positive phototropic curvature of roots in response to blue illumination. Additionally, a positive phototropic response of roots was observed when exposed to red light, and a decrease in response was gradual and correlated with the increase in gravity. The positive red-light phototropic curvature of hypocotyls when exposed to red light was also confirmed. Both red-light and blue-light phototropic responses were also shown to be a product of directional light intensity. To our knowledge, this is the first characterization of a positive blue-light phototropic response in Arabidopsis roots, as well as the description of the relationship between these phototropic responses in fractional or reduced gravity.

title: Epigenetic Control of Radiogenic Damage Processing in C. elegans
objectives:
Task Book Description:

Levels of expression of specific genes in individuals are determined by the particular combination of alleles that they carry and the regulatory environment of cells expressing those genes. Molecular pathways and mechanisms that detect and respond to damage from ionizing radiation are thus subject to modulation by external processes acting upon the genes expressed in the irradiated cells. These mechanisms constrain and determine the final levels of gene activity. These epigenetic mechanisms may be cell autonomous or operate at the tissue and organism level by processing information from cytokines, growth factors, hormones, and extracellular matrix. The range of gene expression patterns driven by the combinatorial actions of these epigenetic regulatory mechanisms ultimately determines the inter-individual variability of responses to radiation exposure.
approach: We propose to address these issues using a simple animal model, the nematode C. elegans, in which genotoxicity (chromosome aberrations) can be quantified in vivo in individual intestine cells whose tissue location and cell lineage are invariant. We have shown that individual cells in this 20-cell tissue respond to genotoxic damage in their neighbors and that their radiosensitivities appear to be modulated by signals originating in an adjacent tissue, the immature gonad. To identify the mechanisms underlying these cell-cell and tissue-tissue interactions we will employ a targeted screening protocol based on RNA interference methods to determine which of approximately 250 candidate genes alter the tissue architecture, cell-cell communication properties, DNA repair capacity, and important regulatory signal cascades which we believe are likely to modulate the expression and transmission of the genotoxic damage. This will be complemented by targeted irradiations of individual cells and cell groups with microbeam and collimated alpha particles to determine the range and topology of the damage transmission between cells.
results:
Task Book Update (10/18/2012):

In the past we have used a distance-based method to identify positions of intestinal cell pairs relative to an anatomical landmark. This year we made important improvements in methodology which enabled precise targeting of individual cells in the intestine. The major methodological improvement required a combined genetic and optical approach. The concept was to construct a transgenic worm strain that expresses green fluorescent protein only in gut cells and then visualize them. We previously reported the construction of the appropriate GFP-expressing strain called JP2003 which expresses GFP constitutively in intestine cells int-1 through int-9 under control of the gut-specific elt-2 GATA-type promoter. Two additional strains (JP2004 and JP2006) incorporating radiosensitizing mutations in genes cku-70 and smk-1 have also been constructed. With respect to optics, we tested a number of high intensity LED illumination systems and filters to the microbeam system but eventually found a blue laser with enough intensity to obliquely illuminate the small larval stage animals for visualization through the microbeam optical system and camera. Now we are able to directly visualize the desired cell(s) to target the proton beam within 1 or 2 microns. From our microbeam experiments many pair-wise combinations of cell signal transmission (determined by targeting) and reception (observed as expression of anaphase bridges) have now been observed and we have been able to construct a map of the signaling patterns between cells in the 20-cell organ. Several important observations have emerged. (1) The signaling pattern does not simply reflect which cells are in direct contact or nearest each other as distal cells may respond while proximal ones do not. (2) The distance that a signal can travel can be as far as from cell pair 2 to cell pair 8, a distance of well over 100 microns. (3) There appears to be a functional compartment boundary at approximately the position of the pharynx/intestine valve through which signals generated in pharyngeal cells to not pass or, alternatively, pharyngeal muscle and nerve cells do not generate the proper type or intensity of signal to which intestine cells react. (4) The signal transmission and reception patterns have specificity such that not every cell-cell pair interacts whether from the transmission or reception perspective. (5) The frequency of anaphase bridges in bystander cells may exceed that in directly irradiated cells - a very unexpected result. (6) The overall sensitivity pattern resembles that for whole body irradiations which may reflect the dominance of the response by bystander cells - cf. #5.

Genetic screening for genes modifying the radiation-induced anaphase bridge response has identified 26 genes that modify the overall frequency of anaphase bridge expression or alter the covariant expression of bridges between cell pairs. This was established using an odds ratio-based statistical method in whole body irradiation experiments. Six of the genes exhibited strong effects. These include three DNA repair genes from the non-homologous end joining pathway for double strand breaks (cku-70, cku-80 and lig-4), a telomere structure gene (trt-1), a mitochondrial complex II gene (mev-1) and a MAP kinase interacting protein gene (smk-1). A limited number of GFP transgenic worms incorporating cku-70 and smk-1 have now been microbeam irradiated and these archived samples await further analysis.

title: Synaptic plasticity in mammalian vestibular maculae
objectives: The nervous systems of animals, including man, have been found to exhibit a remarkable ability to adapt to changes in the ambient gravity environment. An understanding of these adaptations is critical to managing human exploration and survivability during long-term spaceflight. Previous studies have shown that a gravity-sensing structure within the inner ear, the utricle, exhibited quantitative changes in components of its constituent hair cells during microgravity exposures up to 14 days. This structure is known as one of the vestibular maculae, inner ear structures with important load sensing capabilities. The changes that were observed involved modification in the number of synaptic ribbons, which are organelles involved in the transfer of sensory signals from the hair cells to neurons that communicate with the central nervous system. Such changes represent a form of adaptation that is more generally characterized as synaptic plasticity. At present, however, it is not known whether these changes represented a transient effect of short-term spaceflight, or whether they are more permanent and reflect a chronic adaptive modification.
approach: The present study is designed to test whether the approximately 30-day exposure to microgravity proposed for the BION-M1 satellite mission results in chronic modification in synapse number within hair cells of the utricle. This will be achieved through transmission electron microscopy and quantitative analyses to estimate the density of synaptic ribbons in different hair cell types and in different regions of utricles from flight and control animals. In addition, we will utilize new methods of conical tomography to visualize these ribbon synapses in three dimensions. This enables us to test whether synaptic plasticity is manifested in changes in subpopulations of ultrastructural elements of the synaptic ribbon. These results will be critical to a more comprehensive understanding of the long-term exposure to spaceflight in mammalian gravity-sensing systems.
results:
The investigator team continues to collect and analyze ultrastructural data for additional tomograms and serial reconstructions. Two publications are in preparation, the first one reporting the synapse distribution data and the second on the ultrastructural findings from spaceflight specimens. 

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.

title: The Response of Articular Cartilage to Microgravity
objectives:
The objective of this project is to determine whether the reduced mechanical forces associated with microgravity lead to osteoarthritis. 

Although the effects of microgravity on bone are well known and appear to be reversible, the effects reduced mechanical forces on the cartilage surfaces of articulating joints are less well understood. Studies in humans and animals clearly demonstrate that osteoarthritis results from abnormal (both increased and decreased) mechanical forces on cartilage. Based on these findings, we hypothesize that appropriate mechanical forces are critical for the health of articular cartilage. We further hypothesize that the absence of significant biomechanical forces affects that ability of the chondrocyte to maintain healthy functioning cartilage. This may lead to accelerated cartilage breakdown and osteoarthritis.

There have been no studies that specifically investigate whether microgravity results in the degeneration of articular cartilage. Articular cartilage breakdown could be a major consequence of long-term space travel because, unlike bone, once cartilage degradation begins it cannot be reversed, typically leading to osteoarthritis. The mobility of an individual affected by even mid-stage osteoarthritis would be compromised leading to a failure to complete the full range of mission duties.
approach:
Tissue samples were collected from the BION-M1 mission as part of the Biospecimen Sharing Program (BSP). Mice were exposed to microgravity for 30 days at an altitude of 575 kilometers on an unmanned Soyuz II spacecraft launched on April 19, 2013.  The availability of joint tissue exposed to an extended period of microgravity is an ideal opportunity to address these hypotheses. The joints of mice returned from microgravity were examined for for altered gene and protein expression and evidence of cartilage breakdown. This was achieved using histology for joint surface integrity, immunohistochemistry using antibodies against a range of cartilage proteins and gene expression analysis tools.  

Specifically, knees and elbows were dissected from 6 flight mice and a cohort of vivarium controls. The right elbows and knees from each mouse were placed in formalin in preparation for histological and immunohistochemical analyses. The left elbows and knees were placed in RNALater for RNA preservation for gene expression analysis.  Ground control dissections were also planned.
results:
Effects on cartilage of 30 days exposure to microgravity. There was significant proteoglycan loss, as shown by reduced toluidine staining in the knee cartilage of flight animals compared to the non-flight animals (p=0.008, students t-test). Interestingly, many of the chondrocytes within the middle zone remain blue. Despite this proteoglycan degradation, no clear structural changes to the articular cartilage surface of the flight mice were present suggesting that, with 30 days of unloading, the collagen II network remains intact.

Extensive literature exists in humans and in animals suggesting that a normal range of biomechanical forces is required for cartilage homeostasis.  Consistent with this is our finding that the reduced biomechanical environment of microgravity induces cartilage breakdown.  Although there is clear evidence of proteoglycan loss after 15 days of microgravity on STS-131, there is clear evidence of proteoglycan loss after 30 days on BION.  In addition, the mRNA changes in BION are consistent with reduced cartilage matrix synthesis and further support the finding that cartilage atrophy occurs in spaceflight.  

Based on these data we speculate that the maintenance of biomechanical loading during Spaceflight will reduce cartilage destruction.  Exercise is the countermeasure.  

Additional analysis and results are available in the NASA GeneLab repository, https://genelab.nasa.gov/data/.
publication_titles: Articular cartilage and sternal fibrocartilage respond differently to extended microgravity

title: Protein Expression in Salivary Glands: Effects of Extended Space Flight
objectives: The study was designed to determine effects of extended space travel on oral tissues using the salivary glands of mice. Rodent salivary glands are structurally and functionally similar to those of humans therefore useful for research on changes associated with travel in space. Salivary glands and oral fluid play an important role in maintaining the integrity of the mouth and teeth. Proteins present in secretion granules of salivary glands are released into saliva and can be used to monitor effects of environmental stress. Salivary proteins can serve as markers for physiologic processes such as digestive and immune systems and hormonal regulation. The proteins in saliva are reliable indicators of the environmental effects of travel in space. The physiologic processes for which they are markers are either slowed, accelerated or unaffected. Several of the proteins have previously been shown to respond to a variety of environmental stimuli, including space flight. Our previous studies on short duration (~ 1 week) space flight missions showed that specific secretory proteins were modified by exposure to both zero (ØG) and hyper G conditions. Proteins associated with hormonal signaling and gene expression are down-regulated. An enzyme that is associated with digestion seems unaffected while some cellular proteins appear increased. Employing saliva as a diagnostic test medium could have clinical applications and be useful in measuring human responses to space flight.
approach: The goal is to devise a clinical test for stress disorders in space and on Earth. Saliva contains substances (e.g. proteins) that change during travel in space. We can determine which salivary proteins have been changed by using biochemical tests and histologic analyses to study tissues and cells (salivary glands) that produce saliva in mice flown on the space shuttle Discovery STS-131, 133, and 135 missions. This information will be used to design a test kit to measure responses in astronauts so no blood will have to be drawn. Collecting saliva instead of blood and urine, will be easier during space flight and will make it possible to study hormone changes experienced by Astronauts and Cosmonauts at ØG. Understanding the effect of ØG will define changes due to the environment as the stressor. This information can then be adapted to devising specific tests of different conditions using saliva for general clinical applications in medicine in space or on Earth.
results:
The present results agree with previous findings that proteins in salivary glands are specifically altered during space flight and can therefore be used as markers for environmental effects on different physiological and behavioral responses. Microarray data showed that space travel affects a large number of salivary gland genes including those involved in cellular signaling. Results from Bion-M1 differ in some aspects from the findings of the U.S. space shuttle STS missions. Several of the salivary protein changes seen during the shorter STS missions returned to normal during a 30-day Bion flight.

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications
publication_titles: Localization of cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator signaling complexes in human salivary gland striated duct cells | Salivary gland protein expression after Bion-M1 and Space Shuttle STS-135 missions | Initial studies of salivary gland responses to travel in space | Responses to spaceflight of mouse mandibular bone and teeth | Response of the mouse sublingual gland to spaceflight | Oral Tissue Responses to Travel in Space | Effects of spaceflight on the mouse submandibular gland
disease_areas: Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Mental health

title: The Response of Articular Cartilage to Microgravity
objectives:
The objective was to investigate alterations in gene expression in articular cartilage, skeletal muscle, and skin exposed to microgravity.

The data from STS-131 animals demonstrate that in cartilage, muscle, and skin, genes that respond to different types of common stresses such as oxidative stress and genotoxic stress are also upregulated under conditions of microgravity. This is a completely novel finding and may explain some of the tissue-level effects of microgravity reported, such as muscle atrophy.

This experiment examined the hypothesis that one effect of microgravity in these tissues is the activation of stress response genetic networks, and this has effects on cell proliferation and tissue maintenance.
approach: The molecular data from STS-131 has generated new hypotheses to test. The availability of muscle samples from STS-133 and STS-135 animals was extremely useful for validating and extending  current data. The STS-131 samples were assessed for gene expression differences using defined microarrays for Affymetrix. The tissue samples stored in formalin were used to examine microgravity-related changes in protein levels and were guided by the gene expression analyses.
results: No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications. Additional analysis and results are available in the NASA GeneLab repository, https://genelab.nasa.gov/data/.
researchAreas:
Bone and calcium physiology
Muscle physiology
Skeletal physiology

title: Hindlimb suspension (HS) as an analog model of ocular alterations associated with cephalad fluid shifts: resveratrol as a countermeasure
objectives: Visual function and neuroanatomical ocular changes have been observed in some long-duration crewmembers. These alterations seem to be related to microgravity-induced fluid shifts and ensued ICP increase. The mechanisms by which these factors cause eye damage are not understood. Damage may extend beyond anatomical alterations of the eye. The hypothesis that these two pilot studies will test is that pathophysiological pathways at the molecular and cellular levels in the retina may also contribute to microgravity-induced vision risks. These pathways may be triggered by oxygenation perturbations and mechanical and oxidative stress associated to the fluid shifts in microgravity. In Part I, a mouse experiment aboard two shuttle missions, STS-133 and STS-135, will allow us to test the hypothesis that mouse eyes in microgravity (together with confounding space environment factors) exhibit alterations in the histological and gene expression profile that may be indicative of cellular stress pathways. To date, there have been limited flight animal studies aimed at studying the effects of microgravity on the retina (Tombran-Tink, Adv Exp Med Biol, 2006; Grigoryan, Adv Space Res, 2002). In Part II, a ground animal analog, the rat hindlimb suspension model (HS), will be used to test the same hypothesis using a model of simulated microgravity. A second hypothesis within Part II will test the efficacy of nutritional countermeasures. Resveratrol, a potent antioxidant and activator of the cellular stress response pathway, has been shown to ameliorate bone loss (Habold et al, J Bone Min Res 2010) and muscle atrophy in hindlimb suspended animals (Jackson et al, Am J Physiol Regul Integr Comp Physiol 2010). Likewise, resveratrol has been shown to prevent and ameliorate oxidative damage and retinal neurodegeneration in several experimentally induced ocular conditions in animals (Liu et al, IOVS 2007; Shindler et al, IOVS 2007; Kubota et al, IOVS 2009). This study will examine whether resveratrol has a cytoprotective effect in the retinas of rats subjected to the stress caused by cephalad fluid shifts. In addition, the protective effects of resveratrol will be compared to those of another reactive oxygen species scavenger, epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) found in green tea extract.
approach: This project aims at testing the overall hypothesis that cephalad fluid shifts elicited by either microgravity or hindlimb suspension (HS) represent a stress factor that induces optic disc neuroanatomical changes, as well as retinal cell deterioration and loss via oxidative stress. The first specific aim of this pilot project is to perform histological and gene expression analysis of retinas collected from mice flown in STS-133 and STS-135 and from their ground control counterparts. The second specific aim is to perform similar analysis in retinas from hindlimb suspended rats, focusing on pathways consistent with cellular and oxidative stress, thus validating this ground analog animal model for the study of retinal stress in microgravity. The third specific aim will explore the potential efficacy of reversatrol and EGCG as a nutritional countermeasures to mitigate the risks or retinal oxidative stress and degeneration in conditions of simulated microgravity.
results:
Upon completion of this task, we have expanded the immunohistopathologic analysis of the rodent retina as far as the effects of and responses to spaceflight observed in the eyes of mice aboard shuttle mission STS-133 and STS-135, focusing, for the first time, on molecular and cellular processes. While the results reported here represent pilot data due to the small sample size, these data provide for the first time, direct evidence suggesting that oxidative stress, neuronal damage, and mechanical injury take place in the retina and optic nerve of rodents flown in low-Earth orbit for a period under 2 weeks. Our work gives a first insight into the impact of space-associated factors on biological processes like cell death, oxidative stress, and probable mechanical injury in the rodent eye.

Moreover, comparative results obtained with two different mouse strains (albino versus pigmented mice) suggest that susceptibility to stress factors is determinant of the extent of the stressors effects. While individual variability is not regularly encountered with genetically homogenous mouse strains, we observed some individual susceptibility variation, which may be ascribed to a particular stress impact in spaceflight, part of which is stochastic in nature (radiation).

The present work also investigated the contribution of cephalad fluid shift alone in the rat retina by using the well-characterized hindlimb suspension (HS) model, and identified, for the first time, a gene expression response suggestive of mechanical stress imposed by cephalad fluid shift on the eye. In addition, an antioxidant nutritional countermeasure based on a green-tea enriched diet resulted in a decreased stress response, suggesting an association between the possible mechanical stress and oxidative stress genetic circuits.

title: Genotypic and phenotypic responses of Candida albicans to spaceflight
objectives: Modeling the microgravity environment, using rotating suspension culture bioreactors, has allowed investigation of eukaryotic cellular responses in a ground-based model and has provided insights into the impact of spaceflight on cellular physiology. Extrapolation of results from modeled systems to that expected in space is useful and informative, but evaluating the impact of actual space exposure strengthens the validity and interpretation of data from both environments. Using the Candida albicans (C. albicans) yeast system, it is demonstrated that simulated microgravity affects differential expression of several groups of genes, including those related to morphogenic transformation, environmental sensing and ergosterol biosynthesis; induces morphologic changes such as filamentation, biofilm formation and colony morphology; and results in an increased resistance to the antimicrobial agent Amphotericin B. The constellation of these physiological responses is consistent with a potential increase in the virulence of this opportunistic yeast pathogen. The focus of the current proposal is to characterize physiological outcome measures of C. albicans following exposure to flight on the International Space Station (ISS). The overall objective of this experiment is to evaluate the response of C. albicans to spaceflight in order to 1) predict the potential health risk posed by this opportunistic pathogen in the spaceflight environment, and 2) to further characterize the phenotypic and genotypic response of a eukaryotic system to conditions of microgravity.
approach: Please see description in related experiment Micro-8 (NNX12AH29G).
results:
A primary concern was the survival of the yeast stored in water until experimental processing and then, for the appropriate experiments, survival during return to Earth.  As predicted based on our ground-based assessment, the yeast survived the pre-experimental-processing storage in water as well as the post-experiment storage during the trip back to Earth.  The next basic analysis was to assess whether yeast grow more quickly when exposed to the microgravity environment, as has been documented for some prokaryotes.  Our data would suggest that there is not a significant difference in growth between ground and flight samples (data not shown) although design of appropriate ground control conditions is difficult due to sedimentation or employing a rotation device.  Perhaps more interesting is the suggestive, but not significant data, that the longer cells are exposed to flight, the slower they are likely to grow relative to ground. 

Our preliminary LSMMG studies demonstrated that cells acquired a complex and highly convoluted colony morphology following growth in the HARV.  In the Micro-6 study, colonies with similar morphology as was observed during LSMMG where not observed in the live samples when plated upon return to the Montana laboratory.  Actually, the colonies derived from flight cells were more normal than those derived from ground based samples.  Either exposure to microgravity was not long enough to induce similar changes (phenotype takes 5-8 days of continuous culture in HARV to observe, whereas cells in flight only grew 1.5-2 days), the phenotype reverted during the 4 days of gravity exposure before the analysis could be performed (although the phenotype was relatively irreversible when induced by LSMMG), or the response to microgravity is different than the response to LSMMG.  

Our ground-based studies demonstrated that yeast grown under LSMMG conditions were more resistant to the effects of the antifungal agent Amphotericin B.  For this reason, we evaluated the ability of cells to grow in the presence of the antifungal agent while on the ISS.  Cells were then fixed on station and cell growth was quantified upon sample return.  In addition, viable cells were exposed to AB upon sample return and cell viability was analyzed by flow cytometry. Flight samples were significantly more resistant to the highest dose (2.4 mg/L) of drug, similar to the results obtained in our LSMMG studies.  Although less conclusive, both in-flight and post-flight samples showed a trend toward increased resistance at lower concentrations of drug (1.2 mg/L) as compared to ground controls.
disease_areas: Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Rare inherited disorders

title: Novel Explorations Into the Interactions Between Light and Gravity Sensing in Plants - Seedling Growth-2
objectives:
The major goals of this project are to determine how gravity and light responses influence each other in plants and to better understand the cellular signaling mechanisms involved in plant tropisms. This proposed project builds on our previous spaceflight experiments on the ISS (TROPI-1, 2006; TROPI-2, 2010) with the EMCS using the model plant Arabidopsis. In this new proposed project, we plan to confirm and extend our discovery during the recent TROPI-2 ISS experiment of a novel red-light-based positive phototropic response in plant shoots. 

Thus, the specific objectives of this proposed research are: (1) To confirm and characterize the novel red light-dependent phototropic response in flowering plants. (2) To investigate the relationship between light and gravity by measuring thresholds in fractional gravity. (3) To determine whether the red light effect on blue-light-based phototropism is a direct or indirect effect.
approach:
The Seedling Growth series of experiments (1, 2, 3 and possibly 4) is supported collaboratively by NASA and the European Space Agency (ESA). The experiment will be conducted in the European Modular Cultivation System (EMCS) on the International Space Station (ISS).  The EMCS is operated by ESA and is located in the Columbus Module of the ISS.

These experiments can be accomplished with telemetric science and therefore fit with the extremely limited down mass capabilities of current spaceflight opportunities. The EMCS is an automated facility and can be operated largely with telemetry. Experimental containers will be launched via Space-X, and during an experimental run, images of seedling growth responses can be downlinked to Earth. Seedlings will be returned to Earth in the GLACIER freezer.
results: Results will be made available through Novel Explorations Into the Interactions Between Light and Gravity Sensing in Plants - Seedling Growth-3.

title: Mice Drawer System (MDS)
objectives:
Mice Drawer System (MDS) is an Italian Space Agency experiment that will use a validated mouse model to investigate the genetic mechanisms underlying bone mass loss and other microgravity effect on different tissues such as muscles, glands, brain. Research conducted with the MDS is an analog to the human research program, which has the objective to extend the human presence safely beyond low Earth orbit.

Mice Drawer System (MDS) reached the ISS on board Shuttle Discovery Flight 17A/STS-128 on August 28, 2009. MDS returned to Earth on November 27, 2009, on Shuttle Atlantis Flight ULF3/STS-129 after a 91-day stay, performing the longest duration of mice in space. MDS flew three wild-type (Wt) and three pleiotrophic transgenic (PTN) mice to determine the microgravity effects levied on each mouse. Unfortunately, during the investigation, one PTN mouse and two Wt mice died due to either health related or payload-related reasons. MDS participated in a Tissue Sharing Program with 20 different research groups in order to determine if microgravity induced any tissue modifications, with a primary focus on bone loss.
approach:
Operational Requirements
For the MDS experiment, three groups of mice were utilized. One group of mice has been sent to the ISS and was housed in an MDS enclosure. Two control groups of mice remained on Earth in Genoa, Italy; one in a MDS enclosure and the other in standard rodent housing.

On board the ISS, MDS is relatively self-sufficient; the PI team from Genoa checked the health status of the rodents on a daily basis by assessing them through the camera on the viewing window. Water levels were also assessed by the PI team from Genoa daily and refilled by crewmembers as needed. Replacement of the food bars and waste filters were conducted in flight by crewmembers every 20-days or as needed. After landing, the MDS was returned to the investigator for extensive analysis.

Operational Protocols
Once the rodents were in space, the PI team from Genoa checked the health status of the rodents on a daily basis by assessing them though the camera placed on the viewing window on each MDS. Water levels on the water boxes were checked daily by the PI team from Genoa and refilled by the crewmembers on the ISS as needed. Replacement of the food bars and replacement of the waste filters was conducted in flight by crewmembers every 20 days.
results: Analysis is in progress.
publication_titles: Skin physiology in microgravity: a 3-month stay aboard ISS induces dermal atrophy and affects cutaneous muscle and hair follicles cycling in mice

title: Cell Biology Tech Demo (CBTD)
objectives:
NASA's International Space Station Program Office is sponsoring the development of facilities enabling advanced biological research in space to fully utilize the scientific potential of the station.

To maximize research capability on the space station, specific pieces of hardware have been selected for upgrade following many years of successful research aboard the space shuttle. The Cell Culture Module, which flew successfully 21 times on the shuttle, was selected for upgrade to meet future needs for scientific research. The new Bioculture System will fulfill critical science needs to support a wide diversity of tissue and microbiological culture methods and cell types for long duration research. The new hardware is being designed by Tissue Genesis, Inc. of Honolulu, Hawaii. Tissue Genesis Inc. built the original space shuttle based system.
approach: The Bioculture System will be delivered to the space station aboard a SpaceX rocket in 2014. The Bioculture System will help to further the study of cell and tissue growth in microgravity. Cellular and bacterial microgravity experiments give us a better understanding of how to ensure the health of long duration space travelers and can help to improve treatments for injury and disease on Earth.
results: Station crew demonstrated that the tools and procedures used to transfer liquid samples in or out of special bags used to store nutrients for yeast and cells can be used without contamination. The crew also demonstrated use of a special tube crimper to seal experiment chambers while maintaining sterility. The crew also confirmed that the Cell Biology Tech Demo hardware interfaces with existing space station laboratory tools.
disease_areas: Cancer; Musculoskeletal diseases; Cardiovascular diseases; Endocrine and metabolic diseases

title: miRNA Expression Profiles in Cultured Human Fibroblasts in Space
objectives: The objectives for this research are: 1) Deternine the miRNA expression profiles in confluent human fibroblast cells in space and on the ground with PCR array, 2) Determine gene expression profiles in confluent human fibroblast cells in space and on the ground using Illumina gene array, 3) Determine changes in miRNA and gene expression profiles and pathways affected by the microgravity environment using statistical and bioinformatics methodologies to determine correlation between miRNA and RNA expression patterns, and 4) Determine the effects the space flight environment cell response to  DNA double stranded breaks induced by bleomycin.
approach: Pre-flight the cells are seeded into the biocells and are grown to confluence; the hardware is loaded with cells, fixative, preservative, and Bleomycin. The experiment is launched at +4C and once on orbit is activated by raising the temperature to +37C. At specific time points RNAlLater 2 or formaldehyde is automatically injected into a defined set of biocells and then the cultures are stored in MELFI. For the bleomycin time course study, a defined set of biocells are automatically injected with the chemical on-orbit and then at specific time points a defined set of cultures will be automatically injected with either RNALater 2 or formaldehyde and then stored in MELFI.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

disease_areas: Cancer; Musculoskeletal diseases; Cardiovascular diseases; Rare inherited disorders

title: Evaluation of Hindlimb Suspension as a Model to Study Ophthalmic Complications in Microgravity: Ocular Structure and Function and Association with Intracranial Pressure
objectives: An animal ground-analog is proposed for validation as a model to induce cephalid fluid shifts and evaluate ocular structural changes similar to those produced in humans after exposure to a microgravity environment. In vivo ocular and ICP measures and tissue analysis will be performed in hindlimb suspension (HS) and normal positive control rats.
approach: Intraocular pressure (IOP), intracranial pressure (ICP), fluorescein angiography (FA), optical coherence tomography (OCT) scans of the retina and ultrasound of the optic nerve will be evaluated before, during, and after HS. Retinal microvascular changes will be evaluated by computerized quantitative analysis of FA and retinal flat mounts. In order to study cellular responses that are possibly associated with the stress of variations in translaminar pressure in the retina due to cephalid fluid shift, marker of oxidative stress, hypoxia, and cellular death will be investigated by gene expression analysis and immunohistochemistry. This study will lead to better characterization and problem definition of the Visual Impairment and Intracranial Pressure risk, and in turn, it will evaluate the need for countermeasures to mitigate this risk.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
researchAreas:
Biomedical countermeasures
Ocular physiological phenomena
Neuroscience
publication_titles: Inflammatory gene expression signatures in idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Possible implications in microgravity-induced ICP elevation

title: Evaluation and Reduction of Centrifuge Rider Anxiety Through Progressive Familiarization
objectives: To investigate centrifuge rider anxiety
approach: Twelve volunteer subjects selected from a pool of 35 were supine and oriented radially to the axis of rotation with their feet at the 3.8 meter Human Performance Centrifuge (HPC) perimenter.  Three identical exposures (2 minutes of 1 Gz at feet) accompanied by three State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI) questionnaires that measure anxiety were administered on consecutive days followed by a control (no centrifuge motion).  Rider anxiety was assessed with the 6-item STAI scale and analyzed.  Electrocardiogram (ECG), Heart Rate, and HRV (heart rate variability) were measured with Imeds Cardiax 12-lead ECG while continuous digital blood pressure was measured with Ohmega's Finapres.
results: Data collection and analysis is not yet complete, but inital results show peak subject anxiety occurs either during spin-up to or spindown from 1G but not during the 2 minutes of 1G exposure.  Peak heart rate decreases with each successive spin and is lowest during the control.  Highest anxiety was reported in either the first or second spin and pre-spin anxiety drops to the control level by the third spin.
publication_titles: Evaluation and Reduction of Centrifuge Rider Anxiety through Progressive Familiarization

title: Disuse Osteopenia: A Potential Vascular Coupling Mechanism
objectives:
The general hypothesis of this proposal is that skeletal unloading induces alterations in skeletal resistance artery function; these alterations serve to diminish bone perfusion and ultimately disrupt mechanisms for maintaining the normal balance between bone formation and bone resorption. The specific purpose is to elucidate vasoconstrictor and vasodilator mechanisms involved in the control of bone and marrow perfusion, including endothelial signaling pathways forming nitric oxide and prostacyclin, in bone during conditions of unloading, and to determine whether deficits in bone blood flow and endothelium-dependent vascular signaling are related to unloading-induced bone loss.

Specific Aims:
Aim 1. To determine whether hindlimb unloading alters the responsiveness of the principal nutrient artery (PNA) to myogenic and vasoconstrictor stimuli. 

Aim 2. To evaluate the contribution of endothelium-derived NO and vasodilator prostaglandins to acetylcholine (ACh), bradykinin (BK) and flow-induced vasodilation in the femoral PNA from control and HU rats.
 
Aim 3. To determine whether hindlimb unloading alters the expression of endothelial NOS (eNOS), COX-1 and COX-2 mRNA and protein, as well as M2-receptor (ACh) and B2-receptor (BK) mRNA, in the femoral PNA. 

Aim 4. To determine whether hindlimb unloading alters the PNA responsiveness to endothelium-independent vasodilator stimuli. 

Aim 5. To determine whether hindlimb unloading, radiation exposure, and the combined effects of these treatments alters endothelium dependent vasodilation through NO or PGI2 signaling pathways, and to determine whether this is related to changes in bone volume and indices of cancellous bone micro-architecture.
approach:
To accomplish these goals, the principal nutrient artery of the femur from control and 14 day HU rats will be isolated and cannulated for in vitro experimentation. Mechanisms of smooth muscle and endothelial cell function in the osseous circulation will be determined, as well as how skeletal unloading alters these vasoconstrictor and vasodilator control mechanisms (Aims 1-4). Studies will then be conducted using adenoviral gene transfer (Aim 5) to manipulate bone and marrow perfusion and endothelium-dependent vasodilation to determine whether blood flow and vascular endothelial cell signaling is coupled to disuse-associated bone remodeling in the femur (i.e., bone formation rates, mass, total, cortical and cancellous bone mineral density, and mechanical strength). 

Six-month old male Sprague-Dawley rats were randomly assigned to 7- or 14-day hindlimb unloading (HU) or weight-bearing control groups. Bone blood flow (via microspheres) was measured at the end of the HU treatment and following 10 min of standing. Histomorphometry was used to determine PNA media wall thickness and maximal diameter. Arterial pressure, bone blood flow, and PNA medial thickness and maximal diameter were used to calculate PNA shear and circumferential stresses.
results:
In total, five manuscripts have been published with support derived from this grant  that address Aims 1-5. 

Stabley, JN, RD Prisby, BJ Behnke, and MD Delp. Chronic skeletal unloading of the rat femur: Mechanisms and functional consequences of vascular remodeling. Bone 57: 355-360, 2013. 

Prisby, RD, BJ Behnke, MR Allen, and MD Delp. Effects of skeletal unloading on the vasomotor properties of the rat femur principal nutrient artery. J. Appl. Physiol. 118: 980-988, 2015. 

Ghosh, P, JN Stabley, BJ Behnke, MR Allen, and MD Delp. Effects of spaceflight on the murine mandible: Possible factors mediating skeletal changes in non-weight bearing bones of the head. Bone 83: 156-161, 2016. 

Prisby, RD, JS Alwood, BJ Behnke, JN Stabley, DJ McCullough, P Ghosh, RK Globus, and MD Delp. Effects of hindlimb unloading and ionizing radiation on skeletal muscle resistance artery vasodilation and its relation to cancellous bone in mice. J. Appl. Physiol. 120: 97-106, 2016. 

Ghosh, P, BJ Behnke, JN Stabley, CR Kilar, Y Park, A Narayanan, JS Alwood, Y Shirazi-Fard, A-S Schreurs, RK Globus, and MD Delp. Effects of high-LET radiation exposure and hindlimb unloading on skeletal muscle resistance artery vasomotor properties and cancellous bone microarchitecture in mice. Radiat. Res. 185: 257-266, 2016. 

Collectively, these data demonstrate the important relation between bone blood flow, bone vascular function and trabecular bone volume.

No data submitted.  Summarized and analyzed data are available through cited publications.

title: Effects of Microgravity on Cerebral Arterial, Venous, and Lymphatic Function: Implications for Elevated Intracranial Pressure
objectives: Approximately 29% of astronauts on short-term (~2 wk) space shuttle flights and 60% on long-duration (~6 mo) missions to the International Space Station (ISS) are reported to have experienced some impairment in distant or near visual acuity. These visual disturbances have been hypothesized to be related to increases in intracranial pressure. Increases in intracranial pressure associated with spaceflight are thought to occur through the cephalad shift of intravascular and cerebrospinal fluids due to the loss of the head-to-foot gravity vector present on Earth. In regard to the vascular component, increases in cerebral blood flow and capillary fluid filtration could elevate fluid movement into the cranium, while impaired function of cerebral veins and lymphatics could impede intracranial fluid drainage out of the cranium. Therefore, the purpose of this proposal is to examine the effects of long-term spaceflight on the vasoconstrictor, mechanical, and structural properties of cerebral arteries, veins, and lymphatic vessels.
approach:
Flight: The aim of this project is to investigate whether spaceflight on the ISS alters the blood-brain barrier in rodents, as indicated by ultrastructural examination of the cerebral capillary endothelium. To assess the changes that occur in the structure of the blood-brain barrier, 10-15 female C57BL/6 mice, 10-11 weeks old will be loaded into a transporter (Animal Enclosure Module AEM-T; 10 mice/side), and late-loaded into a SpaceX Dragon rocket. After ascent and docking to the ISS, the mice will be transferred via an Animal Access Unit (AAU) and Mouse Transfer Box (MTB) to 2 Habitats (AEM-X). The mice will be grouped into four experimental groups, each housed in one side of an AEM-X. The groups, as determined by another investigation, will consist of mice receiving intraperitoneal (i.p.) injection of saline vehicle (n=5), Tetanus Toxoid (TT) vaccine (n=5), CpG ODN adjuvant (n=5), or TT vaccine + CpG ODN adjuvant (n=5).

After mice have been exposed to microgravity for a minimum of 2 weeks, animals for this study will be inoculated with an injection of phosphate buffered saline (n=5) or CpG ODN adjuvant (n=5); a third group of animals injected with the TT vaccine (n=5) may be utilized. Two weeks later (14 +/- 2 days), the mice will be sacrificed in the Materials Science Glovebox (MSG). Sacrificing will be achieved via i.p. injection of 100/10 mg/kg ketamine/xylazine anesthesia followed by exsanguination by cardiac puncture. Following blood collection and harvest of spleen for another research investigation, the animals will have heads removed. The top of the skull will be cut along the sagittal crest and suture and retracted back to expose the brain. The brains will be will be dissected from the mice and put in a solution of 10% buffered formalin at ambient temperature. 24-48 hours later, the 10% buffered formalin will be exchanged with the Brain Fixation Kit (BFK) to 70% Ethanol and stored at ambient temperature until they can be returned to Earth and analyzed.

Ground: 20-25 (saline vehicle (n=10), Tetanus Toxoid (TT) vaccine (n=5), CpG ODN adjuvant (n=10)) ground control mice will be put in AEMs inside Orbiter Environmental Simulators (OES) on a 2-8 day delay, depending on the when telemetry data from the SpaceX Dragon and ISS environments are available. The differences in vascular structure between ground controls and flight animals with a P-value < 0.05 will be considered statistically significant.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of this study.

title: Defining Epigenetic Programming During Flight Expeditions In Differentiating Embryonic Stem Cells
objectives:
Although scientists can determine the function of every gene, limited methods exist for determining how cells interpret instructions that control gene function. The instructions, (epigenetic marks), encompass chemical modifications added "on top of" DNA that do not cause a genetic mutation. Understanding how diverse patterns of epigenetic marks coordinate the regulation of gene expression is an open question because, although many epigenetic landscapes have been mapped in cell populations, limited strategies have been developed to test and/or understand how epigenetic control occurs in single cells. Epigenetic modifications respond dynamically to environmental factors, and can change within minutes. In space, organisms are exposed to powerful environmental forces (microgravity, cosmic radiation, magnetic fields) that potentially modulate the epigenome, leading to changes in gene expression that affect mammalian development. By elucidating the mechanisms that govern epigenetic programming and gene regulation in single cells, we will provide fundamental knowledge that will broadly impact the epigenetics community, basic biology, and NASA's space biology programs.

The goal of our interdisciplinary effort is to develop an integrated approach comprised of Next Generation Sequencing and single cell microscopy platforms to understand how the space environment affects DNA methylation, hydroxymethylation, and histone modifications to regulate gene expression during differentiation using an Embryonic Stem Cell model.

Through our interdisciplinary effort, we will create a framework to explain how DNA methylation (5hmeC, 5meC) and histone modifications regulate gene expression not only in response to space flight conditions, but also to environments encountered on Earth. Answers to this question, which must be tested in single cells, have heretofore remained elusive. Upon completion, our work will transform the basic biological understanding of epigenetics, propel the current space biology research program into the realm of epigenetics and impact the planning of future long-term space flight expeditions at NASA.
approach:
HL60 and TK cells were grown in the respective conditions and subjected to Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) analysis to determine the methylated regions so that we can enhance our understanding of the gene expression under microgravity conditions. 

First comparison was done between the methylated regions of TK-6 cells (control: static) and under stimulated microgravity to identify the differentially methylated regions and the second comparison was done between the hydroxymethylated regions of the same to identify the differentially hydroxymethylated regions. The differentially methylated/hydroxymethylated regions identified by statistical analysis were then annotated for further biological interpretation using custom perl scripts of MeDUSA package, BEDTools and feature annotation files (GFF files from UCSC). The locality of the differentially methylated/hydroxymethylated regions and its feature type was determined using BEDTools, specifically intersectBed and windowBed. Metadata describing each differentially methylated/hydroxymethylated region (e.g., CpG density, nearest gene, genomic region in which the differentially methylated/hydroxymethylated region was found, and the read count within differentially methylated/hydroxymethylated region) and their summarized counts mapping to specified genomic features were also generated. Based on our analysis about 2000 differentially methylated genes and 100 differentially hydroxymethylated genes were identified. The next step of identifying the implicated genes, the regions within, and the pathways are in progress. Upon completion TK6 cells grown under static and under simulated microgravity conditions will be subjected to similar analysis as done for HL-60 differentiation to identify the pathways epigenetically and transcriptionally altered during microgravity conditions demonstrating proof of concept for the preliminary funding. 
    
Upon completion we will embark on single cell approaches as Phase 2 of the study.
results: Here, in this study we systematically examined the patterns of DNA methylation and hydroxy-methylation with its functional implications in gene regulation for the cultured TK6 lymphoblastoid cells upon exposure to micro-gravity conditions. The results reported here indicate that simulated microgravity alters methylation patterns in a limited way and subsequently the expression of genes involved in stress response. Further details of the research findings can be found by accessing the listed publication: Chowdhury B, Seetharam A, Wang Z, Liu Y et al. A Study of Alterations in DNA Epigenetic Modifications (5mC and 5hmC) and Gene Expression Influenced by Simulated Microgravity in Human Lymphoblastoid Cells. PLoS One 2016;11(1):e0147514. PMID: 26820575 (http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0147514). All processed data are available within the publication listed above and raw sequencing data is deposited at GEO, accession Number: GSE65944. (http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE65944).

title: Genes that Predict the Loss of Bone during Weightlessness
objectives: The amount of bone loss between individual astronauts is highly variable with some astronauts losing large amounts of bone while others are largely unaffected. This variability is accounted for, at least to a large extent, by genetic variations between individuals. Unfortunately, the specific genes that modulate this genetic sensitivity of bone loss in microgravity are unknown. To this end, we have recently completed a genetic study in which we identified specific regions on chromosomes (QTL or Quantitative Trait Loci) that either add to or reduce the propensity of an individual to lose bone when normal weightbearing is removed from the musculoskeleton. While providing critical information, these QTL still contain hundreds of genes, rather than a single one. The principal objective of this grant is to identify specific genes that modulate the magnitude of bone loss during unloading.
approach: Investigators propose to greatly reduce the number of candidate genes with bioinformatics tools and then experimentally test the shortlist of candidate genes using knockout mice available from the Jackson Laboratory's (JAX) Genetic Resource Repository and the JAX Knock Out Mouse Project (KOMP) production center. The identification of single genes modulating bone loss during weightlessness can be used as a critical diagnostic tool for the identification of astronauts most in need of countermeasures as well as to unravel novel drug targets that preserve bone health in space.
results:
Investigators made significant progress towards the goal of identifying specific genes and pathways that determine the amount of unloading-induced bone loss based on an individual's genetic make-up. A second approach explored how aging may influence this genetic relationship.

title: Redox Regulation of nNOS Translocation and Muscle Atrophy During Mechanical Unloading
objectives:
A critical limitation to astronaut performance during spaceflight missions including those to the International Space Station and Mars is the impaired contractile ability of skeletal muscles. Skeletal muscle is a highly specialized tissue that rapidly adapts to dynamic changes in mechanical loading by modifying its ultrastructure and mass. Detection and transmittance of loading are vital to muscle integrity and protein turnover, with a nexus associated with the subsarcolemmal cytoskeleton that includes the dystrophin-glycoprotein complex (DGC) and caveolae. This interface between myocytes and the extracellular matrix confers mechanical integrity and activates cell signaling pathways that regulate protein turnover, mitochondrial, muscle fiber cross-sectional area, and fiber phenotype. Mechanical unloading experienced in the microgravity of spaceflight elicits large decrements in force generating capacity via reduction in muscle fiber cross sectional area or atrophy. While unloading-induced atrophy is a function of reduced protein synthesis coupled with increased protein degradation, activation of the transcription factors FoxO3a and nuclear factor-kappaB have drawn significant attention as mediators. Although the upstream triggers are not fully understood, translocation of neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS) from the DGC and sarcolemma to the sarcoplasm is a novel signaling event that stimulates proteolysis, and thus atrophy. New clues can be gleaned from Duchenne and many of the limb-girdle muscular dystrophies, pathologies involving mutations to DGC and other sarcolemmal proteins. Because MDs and microgravity both increases susceptibility of muscles to damage, oxidative stress, and pro-inflammatory signaling, a shared root cause is suspected: disruption of the subsarcolemmal cytoskeleton. New studies and Preliminary Data indicate the importance of NAD(P)H oxidase (NOX), a sarcolemmal source of oxidative stress, and caveolin-3 in stimulating myopathy in muscular dystrophy models. NOX may be activated upstream by angiotensin II and c-abl, a tyrosine kinase. Further, our pilot data show that unloading-induced alterations in caveolin-3 and nNOS are redox dependent and linked to atrophy and shift in muscle fiber-type. Our central hypothesis is that oxidative stress produced by NOX pathways and mitochondria directly contribute to a sarcolemmal loss of nNOS during mechanical unloading, enhancing proteolysis and suppressing protein synthesis. The rodent hindlimb unloading model and novel fractional synthesis and degradation will be used to test our hypotheses. 

In short, our primary objective is to identify the trigger by which skeletal muscles switch from being an anabolic to a catabolic machine during spaceflight, causing muscle atrophy and weakness. We propose that oxidative stress produced by a NOX pathway and mitochondria move the master switch protein (nNOS) away from the cell membrane, which slows protein synthesis and accelerates breakdown of protein. Our study would directly address high-priority gaps in Recapturing a Future for Space Exploration including the role of reactive oxygen species in protein balance and maintaining the slow contractile phenotype. Our grant proposal would also forward our understanding of (1) alterations in the subsarcolemmal cytoskeleton and membrane domains, (2) sensing and transduction of loading and gravity, (3) protein turnover, and (4) regulatory mechanisms that govern alterations in skeletal muscle, all programmatic emphases and goals of the current Space Biology NRA.
approach:
Specific Aim 1 will identify NOX-dependent signaling on nNOS translocation and muscle fiber atrophy with hindlimb unloading. Specific Aim 2 will determine directly the role of mitochondrial and cytosolic oxidative stress in movement of nNOS away from the muscle cell membrane and disruption of DGC with mechanical unloading. 

We have made significant progress on both Specific Aims 1 & 2, including the completion of the following experiments: 
(1) "Administration of the superoxide dismutase/catalase mimetic, Eukarion-134, prevents short-duration (54hr) unloading induced atrophy." [See Results]
(2) "Administration of EUK-134 prevents 7-day unloading induced atrophy." We continued experimentation with EUK-134 to include a longer duration 7-day unloading protocol to determine the effectiveness of EUK-134 at ameliorating soleus muscle atrophy following 7-days of hindlimb unloading and compare with the 2-day unloading protocol. For this subset of 7- day EUK-134 treated animals, we also measured expression of proteins involved in protein synthesis including protein kinase b (Akt) and mTOR.
results:
We have recently published a paper in the American Journal of Physiology: Regulatory, Integrative, and Comparative Physiology entitled, "EUK-134 ameliorates nNOSµ translocation and skeletal muscle fiber atrophy during short-term mechanical unloading." In this paper, we demonstrate that administration of the cell permeable synthetic superoxide dismutase (SOD)/catalase mimetic, EUK-134, prevents a reduction in Type I fiber cross sectional area following a 2-day (~54 hour) unloading protocol in the absence of changes in soleus muscle mass. In conjunction with a reduction of Type I fiber atrophy, EUK-134 prevented a shift in fiber type from type I to type II, an effect commonly observed with mechanical unloading. In addition, EUK-134 was associated with reduced expression of NOX-2 subunits, ROS production (dihydroethidium assay), and reduced ROS-related signaling/damage (4-hydroxynonenal). The positive effects of EUK-134 may be also related to a reduction in nNOS translocation from the sarcolemma to the cytosol and inactivation of the proteolytic signaling molecule, Fox-O3a. 

Research will continue under the "Upstream Regulation of Nos2 and Skeletal Muscle Atophy During Microgravity and Countermeasure Development" investigation. Additional data and results can be found in supported publication.

title: Iron Overload and Oxidative Damage: Regulators of Bone Homeostasis in the Space Environment
objectives: Recent work in bone biology field has demonstrated that cellular damage due to molecules called reactive oxygen species (so-called oxidative damage) is a key mechanism contributing to aging-related bone loss, as well as to that occurring with the loss of estrogen after menopause. We know very little about whether the loss of gravity as experienced in the space environment modulates oxidative damage to bone. A newly recognized source of oxidative damage in bone is that due to iron overload, or an increase in body iron stores, as has now been demonstrated in both ISS crew members. Data published this month indicate that sub-pathological levels of serum ferritin (a marker for iron stores) contribute to accelerated bone loss and to incident vertebral fractures in healthy middle-aged men and women. We have preliminary data indicating that similar moderate elevations in body iron stores, as induced by a high iron diet, can result in loss of cancellous bone and diminished bone formation activity in rodents. The experiments in this proposal will utilize iron overload as an experimental intervention to exacerbate oxidative damage during a period of simulated microgravity (by hindlimb unloading in male and female mice) to define the gender-specific mechanistic role of oxidative damage as a key regulator of bone mass and, ultimately, fracture risk as modulated by the gravitational unloading of the space environment. In a final specific aim, we will test whether superimposing acute and continuous (over 42 days) radiation exposure exacerbates oxidative damage and bone decrements during hindlimb unloading. These experiments are deliberately designed to enable a rapid transition to flight experiments requiring minimal intervention during flight aboard ISS, should these ground-based simulations yield provocative findings to justify flight experiments. Iron overload can be maintained by dietary intake, and limiting the acute radiation exposure in Experiment 2 to just before the unloading period allows for testing of the same hypotheses in a flight experiment. The experiments outlined in this proposal addresses how microgravity affects key regulatory mechanisms governing alterations in bone (from OCB-2 in the Space Biology Plan) and also one of the NRC Decadal Survey's highest priority recommendations, to expand our understanding of gender differences in adaptation to the space environment, particularly in the area of bone and long-term radiation risks.
approach:
The first specific aim of this project is to comprehensively define the impact of increasing iron stores ("iron overload") on the bone response to microgravity, as simulated in a mouse model of hindlimb unloading (HU). We hypothesize that bone loss will be exacerbated and that the mechanism for this will be oxidative damage caused by iron overload.   Further, we will be able to detect sex differences by completing these experiments in both male and female mice. Comprehensive testing of iron status and oxidative damage, both systemically and tissue-specific, will enable identification of mechanisms for the changes in bone integrity observed in all specific aims.  

The second specific aim is to test our hypothesis that administration of n-acetylcysteine (NAC), a potent inhibitor of oxidative damage, will mitigate deleterious effects of iron overload on bone loss during HU.    If it does, we then have evidence that a major mechanism for iron overload effect is due to exacerbation of oxidative damage. For this experiment we will use the one sex (male or female) that exhibited the largest response to iron overload in the first specific aim.  

The third specific aim involves testing the added impact of very low-dose continuous radiation exposure during a period of HU in mice with normal and high iron stores. There is growing evidence that space-relevant levels of radiation initiate oxidative damage in the exposed tissues; hence we hypothesize that bone loss will be further exacerbated by the interactions of unweighting (HU), iron overload, and radiation exposure.  We will use the one sex (male or female) for these experiments that exhibits the largest response to combined iron overload and HU in the initial specific aim.
