title: Corcoran Ground Studies
objectives:
Experiments:
I. motion sickness in cats and squirrel monkeys to assess the role of the Area Postrema (AP) and effects of drugs, such as xylzaine and L-dopamine, on neurophysiological and visual-vestibular (VV) responses, such as vomiting and conditioned taste aversion. 

II. rodent studies
a) small study of mutant mice
b) damage and recovery of otolithic function following streptomycin treatment in rats (with Dr. Graciela Meza Ruiz of the National Autonomous University at Mexico)
c) centrifugation experiments: vestibular responses of rats subjected to varying levels (1.02 to 3G), exposure (continuous versus intermittent) and duration (3 hours to 59 days) to hypergravity conditions.  
d) EMG study comparing Sprague-Dawley rats housed in holding chambers (vivarium control) or subjected to rotation or on-center control (occ).  
e) vestibular function of gerbils (with graduate student, 1998-1999)
approach:
I. Experiment equipment included bounce or spring tables, Brizzee's "up-down-and-around" motion sickness inducing device, and the EVLAT apparatus.  

II.
c) Measurements and video observations of the righting reflex, foot placement and rail walks, bounce and swimming patterns (for example, polar plots), as well as startle responses to auditory stimulus.

title: The role of gravity on the etiology of idiopathic scoliosis
objectives:
Recent studies from our group in adolescents showed that a large proportion of the 3-D deformity of the spine is in the discs rather than in the vertebral bodies. At which point in time these non-symmetrical morphologies start to occur is currently unknown. One theory states that gravity in combination with quadrupedal locomotion automatically corrects non-symmetrical loading and subsequent non-symmetrical growth and morphology. Gravity leads to forces on the trunk that point in the ventral direction and corrects in such way that slight torsion or asymmetry is automatically counteracted and stabilized. The human upright spine is loaded essentially different from all other species, quadrupedal as well as bipedal in the sense that man's center of gravity is positioned straight above the pelvis, whereas all other species carry their center of gravity in front of the hip axis. This introduces backwardly (posteriorly) directed shear loads on the human spine that decrease rotational stiffness of the exposed spinal segments. It can be reasoned from this theory that taking away the gravity will lead to a relatively unstable situation with respect to performance of the daily activities. In other words more corrections (by the muscles) are needed to keep the trunk in position with respect to the vertebral body, or the trunk is continuously rotated with respect of the spine. It is well known that the bone morphology will adapt itself with respect to this altered (more non-symmetrical) loading and as a consequence we hypothesize that a more non-symmetrical vertebral development will result, with morphological changes in bone and discs that characterize a first subtle sign of a scoliosis.

In the current proposal we request spinal columns or serial vertebral bodies from rats that have been in space and compare the morphology of these vertebral bodies with those from ground control rats.
approach:
Rat vertebral bodies will be scanned with a micro-CT scanner (Quantum FX, Perkin Elmer) at a resolution of 20 microns. The following parameters will be determined in each vertebral body in animals that have been in space and control animals that have not been exposed to reduced gravity:
* Bone Mineral Density in the central portion of the vertebral body
* Trabecular Thickness, Trabecular Spacing, Trabecular Orientation, Structure Model Index and Connectivity Density of the inner cancellous bone in each vertebral body
* With a special software program developed for human studies at our institution, rotation axes of each endplate will be determined 
* Thickness of the vertebral end plates (average and variation per plate)
* Thickness of the cortical shells (average and variation per cortex)
* Perimeter of the cortical shell at mid-portion of the vertebral body and at the end plates.
* Height of the vertebral body (average and its variation per bone)
* Angles between cranial and the caudal endplates.
* The bilateral symmetry of the vertebral bodies. This can be performed by making a mirror representation of the lateral side and compare that with the medial side (or vice versa).

This study will potentially elucidate the etiology of idiopathic scoliosis, which is a huge problem in pediatric orthopedics. Patients suffer from disturbed self-image, pain and cardio-pulmonary compromise.  If we find aspects of scoliosis in the animals that have been exposed to microgravity it would be a breakthrough in the etiology of scoliosis. And we can start to think about more sophisticated counteractions that stabilize the vertebra.

title: High Dimensional Biology to Understand the Functional Response of Salmonella to Long-Term Multigenerational Growth in the Chronic Stress of Microgravity (EVOLVES)
objectives:
While unique responses in microorganisms are well documented for short duration culture, the impact of long duration, multigenerational growth of microorganisms in the chronic stress of microgravity has not been investigated. The implications of this gap in knowledge are tremendous, as the response to short duration growth in microgravity alters gene expression, pathogenesis-related stress responses (ex, biofilm formation and resistance to antibiotics) and virulence. Moreover, it is known that stress responses (including chronic stress) can increase mutagenesis in bacteria leading to genomic plasticity, which may provide novel mechanistic insight into genomic instability underlying resistance to antibiotics, pathogenicity, and virulence. We propose that long-term growth in spaceflight culture will induce heritable genomic and epigenetic changes in microorganisms with adverse phenotypic outcomes that could have a profound impact on the risk to crew health and vehicle system performance. 

Our previous studies aboard the Space Shuttle investigated the response of the model enteric pathogen, Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium (S. Typhimurium) to short duration spaceflight culture and demonstrated a significant increase in virulence in a murine model of infection, in addition to biofilm formation, as compared to identically grown cultures on the ground. In addition, global microarray and proteomic analyses revealed that 167 transcripts (including small noncoding RNAs/ncRNAs) and 73 proteins changed expression, with the evolutionarily conserved protein Hfq identified as a likely global regulator involved in the response to this environment. Hfq binds to small ncRNAs thereby facilitating their association with mRNAs, the outcome of which plays a diverse role in regulating gene expression, virulence, stress responses, antibiotic resistance, DNA methylation and genome stability. 

We hypothesize that long-term multigenerational culture of S. Typhimurium in the spaceflight environment will result in stress-induced mutations and genomic instability as reflected in changes in the genomic, epigenetic, transcriptomic, and select phenotypic characteristics (including virulence) that will reflect a central role for Hfq as a global regulator of these heritable responses.
approach: To address the hypothesis, this study will characterize the impact of long term spaceflight culture (~300 generations) of an S. Typhimurium wild type and hfq mutant on the genomic, epigenetic, transcriptomic, virulence, and pathogenesis-related characteristics of these strains.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Foundational In Vivo Experiments on Osteocyte Biology in Space
objectives: Bone loss due to the weightlessness of space is a major health concern for astronauts that spend significant amounts of time in space. The development of effective countermeasures to bone loss in space requires a detailed understanding of the biological alterations among cells that control skeletal homeostasis. Those alterations can take on many forms, including chemical modifications to the genome (epigenetics), changes in the expression of certain genes (RNA transcription), and certain small nucleic acid chains that regulate protein production (microRNA), and changes in the physical properties of bone matrix, among others. This study will explore the changes in osteocyte biology and bone matrix properties that are associated with long-term space flight, with the goal of elucidating new mechanisms to target for maintaining bone health during space travel. These experiments will provide insight into the limitations of ground-based models, and identify the biological processes for which they exhibit high fidelity to space flight changes. Moreover, new mechanisms fostering bone loss will emerge, which will provide new targets to counteract space travel-induced bone loss.
approach:
This experiment will involve mice that have spent 5 weeks or 4 months aboard the International Space Station (ISS) and investigate changes in tissue level properties (bone mass, turnover dynamics, and material properties). Using an "omics" approach, DNA, RNA, and protein will also be studied to capture all of the changes induced by spaceflight.  

We propose to take a different approach to solving the problems of space‐flight induced bone loss. Rather than proposing to send individual genetic mouse models up to the ISS (e.g., Wnt pathway mutants, osteoclast mutants), we propose in this application to take a step back, and dedicate some time and resources to really understand the biology that is altered in the normal "wildtype" osteocyte population in true microgravity. To this end, we propose to measure short‐ and long‐term changes in the molecular biology of the osteocyte that are induced by space‐flight. As the scientific community finds itself on the cusp of a revolution in genetic/genomic/proteomic technological advancement, we will be able to capitalize on these powerful techniques to capture the key changes—some of which will undoubtedly be novel, in light of the above discussion of models—to the osteocyte that will lead to improved strategies for bone loss in space.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Effects of Spaceflight on Gastrointestinal Microbiota in Mice: Mechanisms and Impact on Multi-System Physiology
objectives:
This proposal will examine the impact of the space environment on the population structure of the intestinal microbiota of mice, and how multiple physiological systems involving the gastrointestinal (GI), immune, metabolic, circadian, and sleep systems, known to be affected by the microbiota are impacted by the space environment. While our overall working hypothesis is that the space environment and associated stressors will have a negative impact on the microbiota (i.e., dysbiosis), and that this in turn will negatively impact multiple physiological systems known to be adversely affected by spaceflight, our integrated analysis of gene expression and multiple tissue/organ specific biomarker of tissue/organ injury will also allow us to uncover the adverse effects of spaceflight due or not due to dysbiosis. 

However, the impact of the proposed studies extends beyond understanding the mechanisms at play in the unique stresses of spaceflight. The proposed studies will elucidate mechanisms underlying interactions between GI, immune, metabolic, and sleep functions and specifically the role of the microbiota in these interactions. This knowledge has tremendous potential for guiding development of dysbiosis-targeted interventions for disorders in all of these systems.
approach:
A series of experiments in mice will be carried out to define the mechanisms by which time in space, diet, and host genotype interact to impact the composition of the gut microbiota, and how dysbiosis relates to gene expression (by RNA-seq) and physiology in serum, colon, ileum, spleen, liver, and fat as well as the sleep/wake cycle and feeding behavior. Extensive use of ground based studies will support the analysis and interpretation of spaceflight data.  Planned projects include: 1) metagenomic sequencing of microbiota from fecal samples and cecal contents (including from the tissue sharing opportunity), 2 and 3) gene expression, physiological and histologic measures of gastrointestinal and immune function respectively, 4 and 5) metabolism, sleep, and circadian behavioral organization.

In spring 2016 frozen specimens from mice were received from the Life Science Data Archive, including Cecum, Feces, Ileum, Jejunum, and Large Intestine, and work is beginning with gene expression and microbiome analyses of these samples.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
publication_titles: Reproducible changes in the gut microbiome suggest a shift in microbial and host metabolism during spaceflight

title: Spaceflight-Induced Changes in Non-Shivering Thermogenesis and Effects on Bone in Mice
objectives: The objective of this project is to test the hypothesis that spaceflight will compromise the ability of mice to employ huddling and postural strategies to slow heat loss. To maintain body heat, flight animals will become more dependent on a mechanism, non-shivering thermogenesis, which is associated with bone loss. We anticipate that fat metabolism will be increased during spaceflight in response to increased sympathetic outflow (a negative regulator of bone formation). The proposed research will address the mechanism(s) by which the spaceflight environment impacts energy balance. At the completion of this study we expect to show that spaceflight results in increased non-shivering thermogenesis and increased fat oxidation in white adipose tissue.  We have obtained and analyzed interscapular brown adipose tissue (BAT) from RR1.   The results to date demonstrate upregulation of genes related to energy metabolism, supporting our hypothesis.
approach: Intraperitoneal white adipose tissue (WAT) is requested from mice flown on the International Space Station and their respective ground controls.  We will isolate total cellular RNA from WAT and then measure mRNA levels for enzymes involved in fat metabolism.  The results will establish whether the increased thermogenesis associated with activation of BAT has a functional outcome, specifically whether fat oxidation is increased in WAT.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Spaceflight Effects on Bacterial Antibiotic Resistance and Its Genetic Basis "AntimicrobialSat"
objectives:
Effective treatment of bacterial infections has required therapy customized for the space environment, as evidence has accumulated that microbes respond differently to antimicrobials in the space environment (1). The EcAMSat experiment focuses on directly documenting alterations in antibiotic resistance in the space environment using a well-defined microbial system - the bacterium, E. coli, and a widely-used antibiotic, gentamicin, against a uropathogenic strain of this bacterium. Fully autonomous microsystems like EcAMSat reduce the cost and obviate other limitations of human-tended space biological experiments, while expanding significantly the number of launch opportunities. 

The rationale of the project is based on the fact that there is convincing evidence that microgravity (MG) increases bacterial resistance and weakens the human immune response, which necessitates careful examination of bacterial threat to astronauts in spaceflight. We previously discovered, using simulated microgravity [called low shear simulated microgravity (LSMMG)], using the high aspect to volume (HARV) bioreactors, that the resistance of uropathogenic E. coli (UPEC) to the antibiotic gentamicin (Gm) increases under these conditions, and this increase is dependent on a gene called rpoS. This bacterium causes cystitis, incidence of which has been reported in astronauts; Gm is used to treat this disease.
approach:
EcAMSat's "2U" payload will support wild-type UPEC and its isogenic rpoS mutant (lacking s, which is encoded by the rpoS gene) in four independent banks of 12 100-µL wells (6 wells each for wild-type and mutant) in a microfluidic card that delivers nutrient, confines cells using integral 0.2-µm-pore filters, and maintains temperature (37°C). Each microwell (pathlength: 8 mm) has a dedicated 3-color LED and photodetector to track growth of the organisms via light scattering (470 nm). Cellular metabolism is tracked using Alamar Blue indicator dye (615 nm) and its reduced form (525 nm), generated by bacterial metabolism.

Growth and metabolism of E. coli are measured versus the concentration of the antibiotic gentamicin (four well banks provide three concentrations and a control). Following orbital stabilization, bacteria in stasis are given nutrients and grown to stationary phase, the phase most important in disease causation. The bacteria are then rinsed with nutrient-free buffer and starved for 24 hr (to firm up the innate resistance of stationary phase cells). Finally, they are treated with gentamicin for 24 hours, followed by tracking survival dynamically via Alamar Blue. We have performed detailed baseline and feasibility experiments, showing that Alamar Blue measurements reliably indicate the increased sensitivity of the mutant to gentamicin.
results: To summarize the critical finding: bacteria protect themselves against antibiotics of different classes using their antioxidant defense machinery. The current efforts to overcome bacterial resistance are antibiotic-specific; thus for instance, a measure taken to overcome bacterial resistance to ß-lactams is to neutralize ß-lactamase that resistant bacteria produce. Our discovery of the involvement of antioxidant defense in bacterial antibiotic resistance means that an overall way to enhance the efficacy of antibiotics against bacteria is to compromise their antioxidant defense machinery. This can be done by discovering small molecule compounds that can inhibit the proteins of this defense that we have implicated in bacterial antibiotic resistance.
publication_titles: Sigma S-dependent antioxidant defense protects stationary-phase Escherichia coli against the bactericidal antibiotic gentamicin | Payload hardware and experimental protocol development to enable future testing of the effect of space microgravity on the resistance to gentamicin of uropathogenic Escherichia coli and its σs-deficient mutant | Cellular Response of Escherichia coli to Microgravity and Microgravity Analogue Culture | EcAMSat spaceflight measurements of the role of σs in antibiotic resistance of stationary phase Escherichia coli in microgravity | The EcAMSat Fluidic System to Study Antibiotic Resistance in Low Earth Orbit: Development and Lessons Learned from Space Flight

title: Fluid Shift Associated Lymphostasis of the Gut Induces Inflammation and Microbial Intolerance (Postdoctoral Fellowship)
objectives:
This proposed project would be the first in depth analysis of the inflammatory status of the gut in space-flown animals and would be the first careful comparison of potential changes in the gut microbiome of space-flown animals. In addition it would also be the first comparison of the impact on digestive system health and homeostasis between ground-based model of microgravity and space-flown animals.

Our use of calprotectin in these animal models, in particular has immediate applications to the health monitoring of astronauts. Fecal calprotectin is used regularly as a biomarker in clinical settings to access changes in the inflammatory status of the gut  If it is elevated in space-flown animals it may be possible to apply the clinical technology to astronauts as part of a regular health screening. This may be especially prudent with the advent of long duration missions and the potential for adverse health reactions. 

Another major potential impact of this study would be the determination of changes in the microbiome of space-flown animals. There are currently parallel studies of the microbiome in space-flown humans but without knowledge of whether this happens in space-flown animals it would be difficult to use them as a model for any human findings. Therefore this is critical in it's own right but also to complement current human studies that are planned.
approach:
We propose to use multiple measures of inflammation in the requested gut tissue and fecal samples as well as perform 16s RRNA sequencing of the fecal samples. To measure inflammatory status in the gut tissue of the animals we will perform myeloperoxidase (a freeze/thaw resistant enzyme produced by inflammatory immune cells) activity assays on frozen samples of tissue from the colon and ileum (the two regions most effected in our ground based work). To bolster these findings we will also measure calprotectin, which is a clinically relevant marker of intestinal inflammation, levels in both tissue and fecal frozen samples. We will also perform frozen sectioning of the gut tissue to examine lymphatic vessel density and immune cell burden of the tissue. Further more we will examine changes in both diversity and density of the gut microbiota in space flown rodents using 16s RRNA sequencing which will provide general information about genus level changes. If there are dramatic differences then we will proceed with more thorough whole genome shotgun sequencing to examine species level changes.
We will perform correlative analysis (Pearson's co-efficient of correlation) of data from the space-flown samples that we receive from the LDA/BPS and samples generated at our facilities at Texas A&M. This is to establish if ground based modeling is appropriate for examining changes in intestinal inflammation and changes in microbiota composition in space-flown animals.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
publication_titles: Acute exposure to space flight results in evidence of reduced lymph transport, tissue fluid Shifts, and immune alterations in the rat gastrointestinal system

title: Drosophila as a Model of Immune Function in Conditions of Altered Gravitational Force
objectives: Immune function is essential for survival and involves basic cellular processes such as non-self recognition, cell growth and apoptosis. The most fundamental part of immune function is innate immunity, and this is reflected by the fact that innate immunity is a phylogenetically ancient system. The main studies that have revealed the similarities in the immune systems of vertebrates and invertebrates are those that compare Drosophila and mammals. In particular, many components of the signal transduction cascades involved in the response to infection are conserved in flies and humans. In Drosophila, this results in the induction of antimicrobial peptides, the hallmark of the humoral response. Similarly, conserved pathways in the cellular system are involved in the regulation of immune cell growth, both for normal blood cell development and for blood cell cancer. In Drosophila, hemocytes mobilize throughout the body to phagocytose invading microbes, and also produce melanotic clusters, which are composed of melanized hemocytes that surround infecting organisms or self-tissue. The melanotic clusters involving self-tissue may either reflect a type of blood cell cancer or a benign melanotic lesion, both of which have the phenotype of melanotic tumors.
approach:
Drosophila was chosen as a model of innate immunity to investigate the effects of gravity on immune function. A key advantage of this approach is the ability to compare normal flies to mutant flies that are either defective in an aspect of immune function or defective in gravitational response.  We examined parameters of immune function in Drosophila under conditions of hypergravity compared to normal gravity after infection with fungus, Beauveria bassiana, or bacteria, E. coli.  Our analyses focused on the physiological response of adult flies to the fungus, as indicated through the assay of survival over time after infection. 

In addition we set up experiments to analyze melanotic tumor formation in conjunction with the host-pathogen interaction in hypergravity, using the Gal4-UAS transgenic system of Drosophila. In this system, GAL4 specifically activates the expression of any gene that has been constructed to have a UAS promoter. A UAS construct of a melanotic tumor formation gene was made to produce a corresponding transgenic strain of flies.
results:
We found that immune function in Drosophila is altered in conditions of hypergravity compared to normal gravity. Specifically, flies show a significant increase (P <0.05) in survival rates at 4g versus 1g. An increase in survival rate is consistent with either the infecting organism being weaker and/or the flies being stronger in response. By comparing these results to gravity sensing mutants, the relative roles of gravity, host and pathogen can begin to be discerned.We thus tested yuri mutants, named after the Russian astronaut Yuri Gagarin and isolated as having a defective gravitaxis response, for their survival after infection and exposure to 4g hypergravity. The UAS-yuri rescue stock, which restores normal gravitaxis to yuri mutants, showed the same increase in survival as the other stocks tested. The yuri mutants, however, showed no change in survival at 4g compared to 1g. Thus, the data indicate that gravity has an effect on the host response. 

Subsequent tests determined how gravity is sensed.  In addition, we found that the survival of infected flies is very different for male versus female flies. Generally, only male flies have been tested in immune response studies, and those that have tested females have addressed specific features, e.g. a specific immune response gene and mated/unmated conditions. Our studies have shown that females survive less well post-infection (P<0.01), with both fungus and bacteria, and for all wild type and mutant strains tested. Females are also more variable than males in survival rates, and less clear as to the effects of altered gravitational forces. We investigated the gene expression changes post-infection for females versus males.  Gene expression data sets  are available at NASA's GeneLab repository (accession number GLDS-1). 

These studies supported the FIT (Fungus, Immunity and Tumorigenesis) Flight Experiment on the space shuttle flight ULF1.1.

title: Assessment of myostatin inhibition to prevent skeletal muscle atrophy and weakness in mice exposed to long-duration spaceflight (CASIS RR3)
objectives:
NASA Mission Page:
Mice have been shown on previous spaceflight studies to undergo rapid loss of muscle and bone mass. These losses have occurred in as little as 12 days of spaceflight exposure. The muscles and bones most affected are in the hind limbs and spine, which is similar to effects reported in humans (astronauts). As such, mice appear to be a potentially valuable model for spaceflight-induced musculoskeletal disuse. Muscle atrophy and bone loss in space occurs rapidly and without any other confounding complications that might be associated with other mouse models such as hind limb suspension, sciatic nerve or spinal cord injury, casting, administration of neuro or muscle toxins, ovariectomy, or any other form of immobilization or hormonal ablation treatment. Mice exposed to spaceflight have proved to be a valuable model to understand, target and treat causes of disuse muscle atrophy and bone loss, including modeling serious muscle and bone disease such as muscular dystrophy, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, cancer cachexia, severe osteoporosis and even musculoskeletal frailty with aging.

Internal Description:
Spaceflight causes a rapid loss of bone and muscle mass especially in the legs and spine, with symptoms similar to those experienced by people with muscle wasting diseases or with limited mobility on Earth. Assessment of myostatin inhibition to prevent skeletal muscle atrophy and weakness in mice exposed to long-duration spaceflight (Rodent Research-3-Eli Lilly), sponsored by pharmaceutical company Eli Lilly and Co. and the Center for the Advancement of Science in Space, studies molecular and physical changes in the musculoskeletal system that happen in space. Results expand scientists' understanding of muscle atrophy and bone loss in space, while testing an antibody that has been known to prevent muscle wasting in mice on Earth.
approach:
Internal Description:
Twenty (20) mice (female, BALB/c mice between 10-14 weeks of age were launched in a single Transporter, transferred to Rodent Habitats onboard the ISS and maintained in microgravity for up to 6 weeks (+/- 3 days).  The mice were be scanned by a bone densitometer and performed grip strength tests as close to launch as practicable (e.g. L-2 to L-5 days) and again near the midpoint of in-flight operations. Addiitonally, the mice will be subject to two on-orbit subcutaneous injections of antibodies (control or treatment). A ground control was run on a 3-day delay.
results: Analysis is underway.
sponsoringAgency: Center for the Advancement of Science in Space (CASIS)
publication_titles: Inhibition of myostatin prevents microgravity-induced loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength

title: Muscle Atrophy of Muscle Sparing in Transgenic Mice (CASIS RR1)
objectives: This research explores the molecular basis of muscle atrophy caused by extended microgravity exposure from spaceflight by examining a transgenic mouse model deficient in the Muscle RING Finger-1 (MuRF-1) gene. Novartis scientists will assess muscle changes in mouse hind limb muscles including muscle mass, muscle fiber size and type (fast vs. slow) and compare the differential protein and gene expression in MuRF-1 Knock-out (KO) mice compared with control mice with normal expression of this gene.
approach: Ten mice (5 MuRF-1 KO and 5 wild-type controls) were flown onboard the SpaceX-4 mission to the ISS, where they were housed for approximately 3 weeks. A ground control (2 Habitats and 1 Transporter) was run on an asynchronous basis.
results: This experiment is not currently targeted for archival.  However, limited information may be available.
sponsoringAgency: Center for the Advancement of Science in Space (CASIS)

title: Microgravity May Detrimentally Influence Lipid Metabolism
objectives: The spaceflight environment impacts many physiological systems, resulting in potentially serious consequences, particularly for longer duration space exploration. As use of the International Space Station (ISS) is increased, and with the rise of commercial spaceflight and tourism, the systemic effects of microgravity must be carefully investigated to protect human health. Although most research has focused on bone, muscle, brain and cardiovascular function, several studies have shown that exposure to the space environment alters both energy and lipid metabolism in humans and rodents.  In astronauts, a diabetogenic phenotype was induced, the severity of which was linked with flight duration, suggesting the liver may be a target of spaceflight-induced deficits. Indeed, significant spaceflight-induced changes in hepatic genes linked to oxidative defense have been previously observed. The liver is a major metabolic organ and the most common chronic disease worldwide affecting the liver is nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD). NAFLD describes a spectrum of liver abnormalities, ranging from hepatic steatosis, or fat droplet accumulation, to nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), with inflammation and fibrosis causing irreversible tissue damage in many cases.  The development of NAFLD is still poorly understood; however, several insults ("hits") are thought to be jointly responsible for causing progressive liver injury. The "classical" hit typically involves excessive triglyceride accumulation in the hepatocytes and resultant dysfunction due to subsequent hits from oxidative stress or lipotoxicity inducing secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines that activate hepatic stellate cells.  Our hypothesis is that exposure to the space environment affects hepatic lipid metabolism, leading to fat accumulation and initiation of pro-fibrotic signaling in livers of spaceflight mice. The goal of this study was to use global transcriptomics and untargeted metabolomics, as well as advanced Raman microscopy techniques, to more comprehensively characterize molecular and functional changes in the liver, some of which may represent early signs of NAFLD, which result from spaceflight.  Our results may elucidate potential therapeutic targets for countermeasure development to protect astronaut health during long-duration space exploration.
approach:
To test our hypothesis, we propose to carry out the following Specific Aims using liver tissue from 6 STS-135 placebo mice and 6 matched AEM controls fed the same diet.
1. Determine whether spaceflight mice have increased hepatic fat as compared with AEM controls.
a. Used Oil Red O staining and Coherent anti-Stokes Raman spectroscopy.
2. Acquire lipid profiles from fat droplets in spaceflight and AEM livers and identify differences.  Determine whether these differences correspond with a nascent pro-fibrotic phenotype.
a. Performed untargeted mass spectrometry-based metabolomics and identified changes in lipid profiles.
b. Performed Raman spectroscopy on individual lipid droplets and identified changes in spectra between groups.
c. Performed immunohistochemistry, and analyzed fixed tissues histologically and using Second Harmonic Generation imaging to assess for the presence of collagen.
d. Mined transcriptomics data to determine whether expression of pro-fibrotic genes correlated with mass spectrometry and microscopy results.
3. Using global transcriptomics and untargeted metabolomics, perform pathway analysis to elucidate signaling pathways that are altered by spaceflight and contribute to the observed phenotype.
a. Performed data integration to generate a gene subset corresponding to upregulated metabolites and performed pathway analysis.

These studies will be repeated with RR1 tissues.
results: We observed significantly more fat accumulation in livers of spaceflight mice as compared with ground controls.  We also found that retinol was lost in the spaceflight mice lipid droplets.  Transcriptomics data suggested that the retinol loss corresponded to early signs of hepatic stellate cell activation and transition to pro-fibrotic myofibroblasts.  Integration of transcriptomics and metabolomics data showed that acyl coA-thioesterase activity was increased and peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-alpha signaling was activated, likely in response to the increased fat accretion.  A manuscript describing these observations has been published in PlosOne (PMID:27097220).

title: Responses of Cardiac Tissue to Simulated Weightlessness
objectives: Prolonged bedrest, musculoskeletal disuse and spaceflight cause complex metabolic adaptations and cardiovascular changes. In addition to exposure to microgravity, the International Space Station poses a unique and complex set of physical challenges to physiologic homeostasis; these include confinement, altered gas composition (e.g. elevated levels of ambient carbon dioxide) and space radiation. In rodents, short duration spaceflight causes pronounced changes in the expression of proteins and genes related to oxidative metabolism, implicating compensatory responses. The full extent and mechanisms of cardiovascular changes over the long term as a function of age or gender are not understood. We hypothesize that the unique environment of space alters cellular redox balance leading to oxidative damage and ultimately, degenerative changes in cardiac tissue of both male and female rats.
approach: This project will test three specific aims, taking advantage of the unique tissue sharing opportunity afforded by a University of California, Davis experiment using rats which includes hindlimb unloading and reloading of both young and older male and female rats, using biochemical and gene expression methods.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
disease_areas: Cardiovascular diseases; Women's health; Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases; Mental health

title: Bone Regeneration in Spaceflight (CASIS RR4)
objectives:
NASA Mission Page description:
Reduced gravity induces cell proliferation and propagation; a potent environment for tissue regeneration.  Contrastingly, extraterrestrial condition delays the immunological responses. Therefore, extraterrestrial condition presents a contrasting environment for the healing process. Leveraging from these reports, the present study is set to explore the biological interpretations of the microgravitational impacts on the tissue regeneration in the wound healing site.
 
The knowledge could help in evaluating the practicality of carrying out tissue regeneration programs in future spaceflights. Molecular circuits and/or markers with therapeutic significance associated with microgravity mediated wound healing/ tissue regeneration could be identified. The pan-omics interpretation may also help in comprehending the reasons of osteoporosis, a typical ailment suffered by the space travelers. It is interesting to note that vertebrates in general display a similar initial response to a wound or amputation; however at the end, only certain vertebrates like salamander and tadpole are able to generate complex tissue/ limb at the wound sites. Additional deliverables of the present project include finding the biomechanisms that prevent the healing cascade to restore the homeostatsis and the knowledge could play critical roles in building limb regeneration strategies.

Internal Description:
This project is part of a broader effort to understand the effects of space flight on tissue healing. Studies suggest that microgravity likely impairs the wound healing process, and microgravity has been shown to have negative effects on skin quality in astronauts. This project seeks to identify the molecular foundations of cutaneous (skin) wound healing that are vulnerable to space flight-induced stress, potentially revealing biologically relevant pathways for the next generation of wound healing therapies. Samples from mice will be collected over a time course of wound healing, both in space flight conditions and in ground controls. In addition, the team will attempt to identify surrogate biomarkers from the blood, which if validated in humans, could eventually provide clinically useful diagnostic markers for the state of skin wounds.

The primary objectives of this experiment are to characterize the biological and molecular events associated with tissue regeneration, in particular: (i) molecular mechanisms that prevent restoring post-injury homeostasis among the high ranked vertebrates like rodents (and humans), and (ii) the impact of microgravity on the process. The experiment results may help to evaluate the practicality of growing complex bone tissues in spaceflight and the molecular mechanisms behind the negative consequence of microgravity on bone density.
approach:
NASA Mission Page:
For both the flight and ground controls, 30 male C57BL/6 mice undergo a surgical resection to create artificial segmental femoral defects. The age-and weight matched animals are segregated in three groups during surgery. For Group A, one third of the 30 mice (10 mice) defects are filled with a novel bioabsorbable scaffold imbued with thrombopoietin (TPO).  For Group B, the defects of 10 other mice are filled by similar bioabsorbable scaffold imbued with bone morphogenic protein-2 (BMP-2).  And, for Group C, the defects of the rest of the 10 mice are filled by the bioabsorbable scaffold imbued with saline as a control. All scaffolds were held in place by a 27 gauge needle. There is another 10 mice (Group D) as control that does not undergo any surgical intervention. The animals are ready to fly 1 week after post-surgery.  Forty mice, 10 from each group (A-D) are housed in the Rodent Habitat Facility ready to be launched.  Forty mice are loaded in a separate Rodent Habitat Facility, which is kept on the ground and are used as ground controls. The mice in both spaceflight and ground are handled in similar conditions and monitored regularly. The mice are euthanized over a period of 5 days beginning at Launch +21 days.
 
Post euthanasia, the operated femur and the un-operated femur are fixed in 10% formalin and put in 4ºC cold stowage. Samples are frozen immediately till their delivery to the ground laboratory to assess the effect of microgravity on gene and protein expression in the different organs.  It is very critical that the ground based laboratory follow the same schedule of euthanasia, i.e. the time difference from euthanizing one animal to preserving the samples as per the protocol should be similar in the spaceflight and ground.  
 
Upon retrieval from the space mission, the bone characters are evaluated by histopathology study and a range of pan-omics investigations are followed. Heuristic mining algorithm sort and integrate the information to find putative markers associated with the tissue regeneration mediated by microgravity.
results: Analysis is underway.

title: Combining Space Shuttle and RR-1 Data to Comprehensively Evaluate Microgravity-Induced Skeletal Changes in Mice
objectives: We propose to evaluate mouse long bones and vertebrae from Rodent Research-1 (RR-1) for microarchitectural, bone material property, and biology alterations following 37 days in microgravity on the International Space Station (ISS). We propose to pool data outcomes from skeletal tissues from this cohort of mice (NASA cohort; n=10/group; 16 weeks old at launch) with exactly comparable data from our previous mouse flights on CBTM-01, 02, and 03 (on STS-108, 118, and 135, respectively; 9 weeks old at launch; ~14 day flight) and from the CASIS RR-1 mouse cohort (32 weeks at launch; ~21 day flight). By adding skeletal analyses from the NASA RR-1 cohort to our existing pool of data, where mice in these studies were all female C56BL/6 and only varied in age and duration of microgravity exposure (i.e.,% dose), we will build a database of bone parameters for: (1) evaluation using multivariate statistics to understand how skeletal tissues are influenced by age and microgravity dose and (2) dissemination through the NASA Life Science Data Archive (LSDA). This latter goal will maximize the impact of our work and enable those who study bone or related tissues to retrospectively use our data for testing a range of hypotheses. The direct outcome of our work will enable us to further explore the influence of microgravity on bone and to guide future musculoskeletal studies by NASA and industry in selecting the best experimental parameters for future flight experiments.
approach:
Analyze mouse bones from multiple sites from NASA RR-1. We will evaluate tissues for:
a. Structure/function properties to assess bone phenotype – microcomputed tomography, computational FEA, ex vivo mechanical testing and fracture analysis
b. Material properties and composition to assess bone quality – Nanoindentation, Raman spectroscopy, quantitative Backscattered Electron Imaging (qBSE), collagen cross-linking (HPLC)
c. Cellular profiles to assess bone metabolism – histology

2. Pool and correlate musculoskeletal outcome measures from mouse experiments on STS-108, 118, 135 and the NASA and CASIS RR-1 cohorts.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Interrogating the Unfolded Protein Response in Microgravity-Induced Osteoporosis and Sarcopenia
objectives:
Age-related sarcopenia lead to a significant degradation in quality of life in the elderly and are associated with a high economic burden. Thus, understanding of the mechanisms behind this loss of function is important with the large ageing population of the United States, which is expected to continue to increase. As sarcopenia occurs in microgravity, spaceflight is an excellent model to study these mechanisms, which may lead to therapeutic targets in the future.

Previous hindlimb-unloading models and simulated microgravity illustrate the involvement of the Unfolded Protein Response (UPR), specifically the PERK pathway, in the pathophysiology of muscle loss (1). More research needs to be done to look at the specifics of the UPR in muscle, especially for effector proteins such as CHAC1. CHAC1 degrades glutathione (2,3) and blocks Notch signaling (4,5), two functions that suggest a powerful role in cell fate regulation. Oxidative stress is a hypothesized player in the microgravity wasting disorders (6,7). However, little work has been done to explore the contribution that Notch signaling may play in microgravity-induced muscle wasting. Thus, this work looks to further refine the role of the UPR, glutathione, and Notch pathways in age-related and microgravity-induced muscle mass loss.
approach: The overall experimental approach to address the specific aims will be qPCR based. Using a protocol and primers previously validated in the lab, RNA from gastrocnemius muscle will be extracted from flight,  ground control, and vivarium control mice using TriZol reagent. RNA will be quantified using a NanoDrop, and reverse-transcribed into cDNA using a high-capacity cDNA kit. cDNA will then be quantified using the pooled standard curve method of qPCR with a Roche LightCycler 480. Primers to explore the UPR will be Ire1, Xbp1, spXbp1, Atf6, Bip, Perk, Atf4, Chop, Trib3, and Chac1. Classical Notch signaling genes, Hes1, Hey2, Herp1, and Nrarp, will also be analyzed. The glutathione pathway will be queried using primers for the glutathione anabolic enzymbes Gclc, Gclm, Gss, and the glutathione peroxidases, and glutathione transferases, which are required for the use of glutathione. Data will be analyzed using the Roche LightCycler 480 software and normalized to the housekeeping gene 36B4. Comparisons will be made using a two-tailed Student's t-test between flight and ground control samples, a p-value less than 0.05 will be considered significant. This approach allows us to query a large number of genes with accuracy to further refine mechanisms of age-related muscle loss.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Epigenetic Regulation and Skeletal Sex Hormone Receptors During Simulated Spaceflight
objectives:
Task Book Description:

This Presidential Early Career Award for Scientist and Engineers (PECASE) award consist of two mini-projects.

Project 1. Epigenetic Regulation During Simulated Spaceflight: MicroRNA Expression, Histone Deacetylase Status, and Methylation-Specific Sequencing

Description and Aim: This work complements the methylation work performed in the current Space Biology New Investigator Grant ("Bone Loss During Simulated Weightlessness: The Role of Osteoclasts," ends April 2015), by allowing 'omics approaches to cover larger swaths of the genome (e.g., whole genome sequencing and RNA sequencing). Additionally, this project will extend the type of epigenetic mechanisms to be studied beyond DNA methylation. Specifically, we will analyze microRNA expression and status of histone acetylation. Hypotheses: Removal of mechanical loading or, oppositely, elevations of mechanical loading induce pro-bone resorbing or pro-bone forming changes in bone cell gene expression, respectively, via epigenetic mechanisms.

Project 2. Skeletal Sex Hormone Receptors and Simulated Spaceflight

Description and Aim: This work complements an upcoming space mission (PI : Joe Tash) and will define the skeletal response of male or female mice to simulated long-duration spaceflight in the context of sex hormones and their receptor levels in each compartment of the femur (cancellous and cortical). The assays used will include RNA-Seq, protein quantification in bone and blood, and immunostaining of histological sections of the femur for sex hormone and receptor levels. Hypotheses: Mechanical disuse reduces the levels of circulating sex hormones and their receptor levels in bone. These changes contribute to skeletal deterioration during long-duration spaceflight.

title: Bacterial, Archaeal, & Fungal Diversity of the ISS--HEPA Filter System
objectives: The Bacterial, Archaeal, and Fungal Diversity of the ISS--HEPA Filter System is a 28-month flight study undertaken by the Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) to examine the microorganisms associated with HEPA filters. The ISS-HEPA project will develop a comprehensive census of the microbial population on the HEPA filter collected from various locations of the International Space Station (ISS), using genetic biosignatures and computational modeling. This microbial census will provide NASA with the ability to accurately and confidently assess the status of microbes associated with closed habitation and crew health maintenance. In addition to overall microbial profiles, the JPL team will determine which microbial taxa pose particular threats to crew health. Microbiomes of the dust particles collected from the ISS and Spacecraft Assembly Facilities The ISS is a uniquely built environment due to the effects of microgravity, space radiation, elevated carbon dioxide levels, and especially continuous human habitation. Understanding the composition of the ISS microbial community will facilitate further development of safety and maintenance practices. The primary goal of this study was to characterize the viable microbiome of the ISS-built environment. A second objective was to determine if the built environments of Earth-based cleanrooms associated with space exploration are an appropriate model of the ISS environment.
approach:
Microbiomes of the dust particles collected from the ISS and Spacecraft Assembly Facilities
Samples collected from the ISS (HEPA filter and vacuum debris) and dust particles from two cleanrooms at JPL were analyzed by traditional cultivation, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and propidium monoazide–quantitative polymerase chain reaction (PMA-qPCR) assays to estimate viable microbial populations.  The 16S rRNA gene Illumina iTag sequencing was used to elucidate microbial diversity and explore differences between ISS and cleanroom microbiomes.

Microbial succession in an Inflated Lunar/Mars Analog Habitat during a 30-day human occupation - Molecular technologies, along with traditional microbiological methods, were utilized to catalog microbial succession during a 30-day human occupation of a simulated inflatable Lunar/Mars habitat. Surface samples were collected at different time points to capture the complete spectrum of viable and potential opportunistic pathogenic bacterial population. Traditional cultivation, propidium monoazide (PMA)–quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR), and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) assays were employed to estimate the cultivable, viable, and metabolically active microbial population, respectively. Next-generation sequencing was used to elucidate the microbial dynamics and community profiles at different locations of the habitat during varying time points.

Additional task projects and methodology, primarily whole genome sequencing of fungal and bacterial strains, are integrated with the description of the results below.
results:
This study is still in progress, with the following preliminary results reported: 

Microbiomes of the dust particles collected from the International Space Station and Spacecraft Assembly Facilities - The results of this study provide strong evidence that specific human skin-associated microorganisms make a substantial contribution to the ISS microbiome, which is not the case in Earth-based cleanrooms.

Characterization of Aspergillus fumigatus isolated from air and surfaces of the ISS - Two strains, ISSF 21 and IF1SW-F4, were isolated from the HEPA filter and the surface of the Cupola of the ISS, respectively and identified as A. fumigatus. The whole genome sequence analysis of ISSF 21 revealed increased number of single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) when compared to the reference A. fumigatus 293. Since A. fumigatus is an opportunistic pathogen and microgravity highly influences the antibiotic susceptibility and pathogenicity of microorganisms, we examined pathogenicity of both ISS isolates using the zebrafish larval model. The ISSF 21 was more virulent than two clinical strains (Af293 and CEA10); the virulence of the second isolate IF1SW-F4 is being tested.

Draft Genome Sequence of two Aspergillus fumigatus Strains Isolated from the ISS - We determined the draft genome sequences of two A. fumigatus strains (ISSFT-021 and IF1SW-F4). Whole-genome shotgun sequencing was performed on an Illumina HiSeq2500 instrument with a paired-end module and deposited at GenBank under the accession numbers LWRT00000000 and LWRU00000000.

Metabolomic characterization of filamentous fungus Aspergillus niger isolated from ISS - To investigate the hypothesis that the ISS-isolated A. niger strain may produce novel secondary metabolites (SMs) that the citric acid-producing, genome-sequenced A. niger strain (ATCC 11414) does not, liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (LC-MS) was used to compare the SM profiles produced by these strains after growth in various conditions. The data indicated that the ISS-isolated A. niger strain produces compounds not produced by the A. niger ATCC 11414 strain. We are determining the structures of these compounds using large-scale cultivation of the ISS-isolated A. niger strain, followed by extraction, purification, and characterization of each compound by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Whole genome sequence of the ISS-isolated A. niger strain was also carried out. 

Molecular characterization of Bacillus issensis isolated from various quarters of the ISS - As part of an ongoing effort to catalogue microbial communities inhabiting the ISS, crew-associated environmental samples were collected from the Japanese Kibo, Russian, and US modules.  Initial analysis based on 16S rRNA gene sequencing identified 11 Bacillus isolates (two from Kibo Japanese Experiment Module JEM, four from US Segment Harmony Node 2, and five from Russian Segment Zvezda module sites), all belonging to the Bacillus anthracis-B. cereus-B. thuringiensis group. Isolates were further characterized by whole genome comparative analysis and were found to exhibit a very high level of similarity.  The collective results from the analysis confirm that these strains belong to a previously uncharacterized Bacillus species, hereafter referred to as Bacillus issensis. The type strain is ISSFR-003T (=NRRL B-65389T= DSMZ 101676T).

Solibacillus kalamii sp. nov., isolated from the ISS HEPA filter system -A Gram-positive, rod-shaped, endospore-forming, aerobic bacterial strain designated as ISSFR-015T was isolated from the ISS HEPA filter.  The polyphasic taxonomy data, including low DNA-DNA hybridization values and the chemotaxonomic analysis, confirmed that the strain ISSFR-015T represents a novel species, for which the name Solibacillus kalamii sp. nov. is proposed. The type strain is ISSFR-015T (=NRRL B-65388T= DSM 101595T).

Microbial succession in an Inflated Lunar/Mars Analog Habitat during a 30-day human occupation - Statistical analyses confirm that occupation time has a strong influence on bacterial community profiles, particularly of the bacteriomes existing before human occupation of the habitat (Day-0 sampling) and after occupation (Day-13, Day-20, and Day-30 sampling). Actinobacteria (mainly Corynebacteriaceae) and Firmicutes (mainly Clostridiales Incertae Sedis XI and Staphylococcaceae) were shown to increase over the occupation time period. The results of this study revealed a strong relationship between human presence and succession of microbial diversity in a closed habitat.

title: Is Gravity A Continuum? Investigations Of Mechanisms Mediating Gravity-Induced Change In Mammary Metabolism And Maternal Behavior In Pregnant And Lactating Rats
objectives:
Homeostasis, the dynamic state of physiological balance, is fundamentally changed in mammals exposed to either microgravity (spaceflight) or hypergravity (centrifugation). In pregnant and lactating mammals, failure to maintain homeostasis exerts pronounced effects on both mothers and their offspring. Our previous data, derived from pregnant rats exposed to either micro- or hypergravity, support: 1) decreased mammary metabolic activity with increased gravitational force; 2) changes in maternal and neonatal energy balance; 3) altered maternal care; and 4) neonatal mortality.   

The specific aims are:
1. To measure metabolic and hormonal changes in metabolism during hypergravity that may explain how gravitational forces alter whole body physiology during preganncy and lactation. 
2. To measure behavioral changes that are coupled with physiological changes when animals are exposed to hypergravity and have the additional stress of pregnancy and lactation. 

Further studies were designed to determine if: 1) the lactation defect was caused by changes in energy balance; 2) the lactation defect could be corrected by injections of prolactin or bromocryptine; or 3) global gene expression profiles would elucidate an alternate mechanism to explain the lactation defect that occurs in hypergravity.  Studies supported by this grant demonstrated that Perturbations in PRL concentration were accompanied by a decrease in PRL receptor (PRLR) mRNA abundance.  Further experiments were conducted to evaluate whether glucocorticoid supplementation could increase the PRLR in animals exposed to HG.  Preliminary investigations utilizing across tissue gene enrichment analysis identified derangement in a unique set of genes pivotal to lipid synthesis in HG rats, particularly, in the lactating mammary gland. Therefore, investigations were extended to map-out other alterations in transcriptional crosstalk across key metabolic tissues induced by exposure to HG during the transition from pregnancy to lactation.
approach: Gestation day 11 (G11) rats were placed on the 24 ft. centrifuge and exposed to 2-g hypergravity (HG), remained at 1-g as stationary controls (SC) or were pair-fed (PF) to match feed intake of the HG group. To determine metabolic activity, mammary tissue was collected from anesthetized animals, sliced 0.5 mm thick and placed in flasks containing Krebs-Ringer Buffer with 10mM glucose, 1 microg/ml insulin and 1 microCi/flask of U-14C labeled glucose. The flasks were gassed with O2, sealed with rubber stoppers containing filter paper and incubated at 37C for 3 hrs, in a shaking water bath. Metabolic activity was stopped by 0.5 M H2SO4. Glucose oxidation to CO2 was determined by counting 14C-CO2 trapped on the filter papers. CO2 incorporation into lipid was measured by petroleum ether extractions and measuring in a liquid scintillation counter. In order to elucidate the mechanism of this response, we have measured a variety of hormones in the blood and thyroid. Subsequent studies on the centrifuge have allowed us to examine the effects of prolactin (Prl), glucocorticoids and bromoergocryptine on mammary metabolism in animals exposed to hypergravity.
results:
Mammary metabolism as measured by glucose oxidation to carbon dioxide and incorporation into lipid was significantly decreased in response to hypergravity during pregnancy and lactation. There was also a very significant response to hypergravity with all animals exhibiting a marked decrease in mammary metabolism (p < .0001). Along with other metabolic and behavioral changes, fetal weight is decreased and survival of the neonates decreases to 50% within the first 48 hours after birth for animals exposed to 2-G. This indicates that hypergravity causes a mammary metabolic defect during both pregnancy and lactation. Changes in mammary metabolism were due to hypergravity and in general were independent of food intake. Serum Prolactin levels tends to be very variable among animals and have not shown a consistent relationship to the changes in metabolism. Neither Prolactin nor bromocryptine injection affected mammary metabolism. That is, glucose oxidation to carbon dioxide was reduced about 30% and glucose incorporation into lipid was reduced 70% compared to stationary controls, which was no different than the hypergravity controls.

Behavioral analyses indicated that the behavior was not altered to the extent previously thought, indicating that a lack of mothering ability is not likely causing the observed neonatal mortality. However, the amount of time nursing may be increased, which may be related to reduced milk production. 

Expression of number of metabolic genes such as ATP citratate lyase and acetyl co A carboxylase as well as the Prl receptor were altered due to hypergravity indicating that changes in gene expression during hypergravity acccompany the observed metabolic changes. The established novel data set encompassing gene expression signatures and in-vitro incorporation of labeled glucose into lipids across key metabolic tissues is to be utilized to develop a testable and mechanism-based model of the biological system.  Alterations in transcriptional crosstalk across key metabolic tissues that were induced by exposure to HG during the transition from pregnancy to lactation were identified.  Gene expression data sets ("Transcription profiling of rat response to changes in developmental stage - 3 types of tissue, 3 gravity conditions, 2 developmental conditions") are available at NASA's GeneLab repository (accession number GLDS-63).

title: Pilot Study of Otosclerosis
objectives: Sclerosis of the otic capsule in the inner ear eventually results in hearing loss. This is a common occurrence in the aging human population but also can occur in specific genetic conditions such as Pagets disease, osteogenesis imperfecta, and certain metabolic disorders . In addition, extreme noise, high pitched noise, or prolonged noise , can also induce damage to the hearing mechanism. Unfortunately, if otosclerosis is not treated early in its appearance (measured by hearing tests), the hearing loss and deafness may be permanent. Our immediate interest is whether animals that have been in space and in microgravity, will develop otosclerosis and subsequent hearing loss. This could be caused by constant noise, even at a normal pitch, or by the change in hydrostatic pressure within the skull that could affect the metabolism and function of the otic capsule. The presence of such sclerosis would suggest that the human crews in space may need to be monitored for hearing loss, and also strongly supports an animal flight experiment to study the cellular/molecular mechanisms responsible for development of otosclerosis and hearing loss. Such research would benefit not only the space program but also the aging population here on earth.
approach: The goal of this request for flight animals is to evaluate the presence of inner ear changes due to flight compared to ground controls and basal pre-flight animals. Because these samples are not fresh, or chemically preserved , and have been frozen, we do not expect to obtain much meaningful cellular detail or activity. However the otic capsule matrix and supporting tissues will be preserved and sclerosis of the matrix can be evaluated. This will be done using immunostaining for the presence of certain cytokines (TNFalpha, sclerostin, osteoprotegerin, and BMPs) which are stored and normally present in bone matrix. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and micro-computed tomography (microCT) analysis of the mineral and collagen content and their anatomical distribution will also be done to compare bone matrix and soft tissue analysis between flight, ground controls and basal animals.
results: No scientifically relevant results are available. Results incomplete.

title: Robust Human-System Interface Design for Spaceflight Induced Environments
objectives:
From Task Book:
The next generation of human-rated spaceflight vehicles will expose astronauts to altered gravito-inertial forces and elevated vibration levels during launch and landing that can affect their ability to perform their assigned tasks. Furthermore, current plans call for crew action in critical tasks, including the override of flight automation in off-nominal conditions, while the crew may be exposed to 4-times normal gravity or more combined with vibration levels potentially higher than those experienced during Apollo and Shuttle. The risk associated with these dynamic phases of flight becomes even more acute when critical tasks are performed by de-conditioned crew returning after a long-duration spaceflight. Altered gravitational environments experienced in spacecraft impact human manual and gaze control and high-frequency vibration is also a serious challenge to visuomotor performance and gaze control. Unfortunately, our current quantitative knowledge of these effects is too limited to develop and validate operationally relevant predictive models of crew performance of sufficient reliability to support interface design or requirement verification. In particular, the interactions between increased gravity and vibration are poorly understood. The goal of the proposed research is to measure, analyze, and model human performance in display navigation and flight control tasks during centrifugation and vibration (in isolation and combination) to simulate ascent/descent conditions of spaceflight. The proposed research will measure performance (and associated workload) in operationally relevant display navigation and manual control tasks under simulated dynamic phases of flight and will compare the effectiveness of a variety of potential display and interface configurations. These data will enable us to identify and model the human capabilities and limitations in order to provide validated quantitative design guidelines for displays and controls to support crew-cockpit design engineers and operations/training development. This research will enable the development of validated spacecraft interface design guidelines and human-system verification tools with the ultimate goal of reducing mission risks and enhancing safety.
approach: Methodology is described and integrated in the Results section below.
results:
From Task Book Description

We found that gaze stabilization (via the Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex) is not the limiting factor for sensorimotor control with Gx loads up to 3.8Gx and vibration levels of 0.5g at up to 16 Hz. We found that retinal blur is typically about 0.25 deg or less, indicating that the combined linear and translational Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex is nearly perfectly compensatory, even under challenging Gx-load and vibration conditions.

* By comparing open-loop vs. closed-loop saccadic and reach performance, we found that:
- 3.8-Gx loading significantly increases systematic errors for both saccades and manual reaches, compared to performance at 1 Gx.
- Both saccadic and reaching accuracy and precision improved under closed-loop conditions as compared to open-loop conditions, indicating that oculomotor and manual control performance is visually limited under open-loop conditions (because of visual spatial uncertainty).
- Vibration significantly decreases the precision of both saccades and reaches.
- There is a significant correlation between the errors in eye and reach end points, indicating that at least some of the error is due to shared neural noise in visual localization for both oculomotor and manual control.

* By comparing pursuit tracking performance in an upright (+1 Gz) vs. a supine (+1 Gx) posture, and under low (+1 Gx) vs. high (+3.8 Gx) G-loads, we found that visual motion processing is largely robust to altered G conditions with some exceptions:
- The cardinal-oblique anisotropy (better performance for target motion along the cardinal directions than for those along the oblique direction) is insensitive to changes in the direction and magnitude of the G vector;
- However, the horizontal-vertical asymmetry (typically higher direction gains for vertical than for horizontal target motion) is significantly flattened at 3.8 vs. 1 Gx, showing that this visual processing non-linearity is due to a different (possibly cortical) mechanism that is modulated by vestibular cues.

* Elevated Gx-loads and vibration generate changes in Autonomic Nervous System (ANS) function, consistent with increased workload:
- Both heart and breathing rates are significantly elevated during exposure to +3.8 Gx, as compared to +1.0 Gx.
- We found a correlation between heart and breathing rate and response time showing that ANS effects are linked to task performance.
- Heart and breathing are significant altered by vibration frequency, although dramatically differently for the two Gx levels.
- This observed non-linear interaction between Gx-loading and vibration confirms why one must measure them in combination, as we have done in this project.

title: Candidate Nutritional Countermeasure to Mitigate Adverse Effects of Spaceflight
objectives: In recent findings, we showed that dried plum (DP) diet conferred complete protection from the rapid bone loss induced by exposure to radiations, including gamma, protons, and High Z-High Energy (HZE) ions. Based on these very promising results on a new potential countermeasure for space radiation tissue damage, we propose to conduct additional studies and analyses, which are critical for moving the potential countermeasure to a higher countermeasure readiness level (CRL) level. We aim to test the DP diet to prevent bone loss induced by simulated spaceflight. This will be achieved by exposing mice to each factor (weightlessness and radiation) alone and combined. Furthermore, we will establish if DP protects other tissues at risk for astronauts, such as the central nervous system
approach: To achieve thses aims, we will expose 16-week-old, C57B16/J male mice to different treatments to simulate spaceflight: simulated weightlessness using the well-established Hindlimb-Unleading (HU) system and/or total body irradiation (TBI). Mice will be assigned to the following groups: 14 days of normal ambulation (NL) (Group 1,2) or HU alone  (Group 2,4), or in combination with total bosy irradiation, at a dose of 2 GY 137Cs (0.8G/min) (Group 5,6 and Group 7,8). Mice will be fed with control diet (CD, AIN93M, Teklad) or AIN93M experimental diet supplemented with DP (25%) 14 days before onset of HU treatment. TBI with 2 Gy 137Cs will be performed 3 days after onset of HU according to protocols previously shown to cause cancellous bone loss, All groups will be euthanized 14 days after initiating HU. Mice will be injected with calcein and declomycin for dynamic histomorphometry of bone. In addition, mice will be injected with BrdU to assess proliferation of osteoprogenitors cells.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
