title: Effects of Microgravity on Ocular Vascular Hydrodynamics
objectives:
Spaceflight-associated neuro-ocular syndrome (SANS) is reported to affect ~40% of astronauts completing long-duration spaceflights (as of May 2017) and has been characterized as the development of one or more findings: optic disc edema, hyperopic shifts, globe flattening, cotton-wool spots, or choroidal folds. The leading hypothesis for the development of ocular changes is that prolonged exposure to the headward fluid shift that occurs in weightlessness is the primary instigating factor, and additional factors such as genetic disposition, ambient CO2 on the International Space Station, or on-orbit exercise countermeasures may augment or diminish the development of ocular symptoms. However, the pathophysiology of SANS remains unclear. Evidence for the contribution of intracranial pressure alone in SANS is controversial. Therefore, studies of ocular vascular hydrodynamics are required to clarify if chronic mild elevations of ocular pressure variables compromise ocular structure and function. Since all blood and lymph vessels are compliant, fluid-filled structures whose pressures are strongly influenced by gravity, we propose to focus our studies on the potential changes directly to the ocular vasculature caused by microgravity. Perfusion of the optic nerve and inner retina for sufficient delivery of oxygen and nutrients is dependent on retinal blood flow. The pressure gradient for driving blood flow through the inner retina begins with the arterial pressure in the feed artery, which is the central retinal artery in humans. Changes in retinal blood flow or pressure may contribute to the formation of cotton wool spots and optic disc edema. Optic disc edema, choroidal folds, and optic nerve thickening may also result from ocular venous congestion and/or elevated venous compliance, disruption of the blood-retinal barrier, and/or reduction in ocular lymph flow. There has been no systematic analysis of the ocular vascular changes in microgravity. We have assembled a team of experts in SANS and all 3 main vascular types (arteries, veins, and lymphatics) to address this information gap. Thus, the objective of this application is to determine whether microgravity alters the structure and function of the ocular vasculature at the level of feed arteries, venous exchange and capacitance vessels, and lymph vessels. This provides a novel comprehensive evaluation of the ocular vascular elements. The central hypothesis of this proposal is that microgravity/spaceflight-induced changes in the structure/function of the ocular vasculature lead to alterations in ocular hydrodynamics and promote symptoms of SANS. We will accomplish this objective using in vivo measures of vascular function (retinal artery blood flow, retinal arteriole and venular diameter measurements, and retinal venular permeability measures) and in vitro studies of freshly isolated vascular structure and function (vessel/tissue histology, arterial vasomotor regulation, venous compliance measures, and lymphatic transport characteristics). These studies will be conducted in mice flown in space and the corresponding ground controls to address the following specific aims:

1: Evaluate the effects of microgravity on ocular artery structure/function.

2: Evaluate the effects of microgravity on ocular vein structure/function.

3: Evaluate the effects of microgravity on ocular lymphatic structure/function.
approach: To examine the Specific Aims 1-3 outlined in the research proposal, Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) will be used. The in vivo assessments of blood flow velocity (Doppler ultrasound) and permeability (intravital microscopy) between eyes (left and right) from flight and ground animals will be analyzed using two-way ANOVA without repeated measures. For in vitro assessment of vascular reactivity to pharmacological agonists at different concentrations between eyes (left and right) from flight and ground animals, two-way ANOVA with repeated measures will be used. All statistical tests will be two-sided and a test resulting in a p-value of less than or equal to 0.05 will be considered statistically significant. The post hoc unpaired least significant difference tests will be performed and interpreted with the Bonferroni adjustment for multiple comparisons. All the p values will be based on two-sided tests.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Role of DJ1 protein in dopamine dysregulation of the brain in spaceflight condition
objectives: The microgravitational condition affects human brain physiology and cognitive functioning. Astronauts returning from spaceflight have shown a number of cognitive impairment including tension, depression, anxiety, and withdrawal during and after the space flight. This phenomenon has majorly affected their health and decision-making process during and after the mission. Therefore, a great deal of mental health monitoring system has been implemented pre and post-mission for astronauts. However, presently there is no physiological marker (biomarker) that would detect their mental health condition early on. In this regard, it is worth mentioning that the Reward System (RS) deregulation in the brain can be one of the early indicators of neuronal health . RS of the brain is one of the major neuronal networks that involves many neurocognitive functions. The primary neurotransmitter that is involved in RS is dopamine (DA). DA is associated with various brain functions including emotion, cognition, pleasure, memory etc. It is also known that DJ1 (PARK7 gene) protein regulates DA through DA transporter. DJ1 is a highly conserved neuroprotective protein that was initially linked with autosomal recessive Parkinson's disease. DJ1's role in controlling reward response in the brain has recently been investigated by us and few others. For normal brain function, DJ1 must maintain the proper balance of DA among neurotransmitters. However, DJ1 functioning in the microgravity condition has not been investigated. Therefore, We hypothesize that DJ1 will serve as a potential biomarker for early detection of impaired reward system in microgravity. This project will generate a proof-of-concept to identify the role of DJ1 in DA release that will be indicative of impaired RS in the brain in microgravity condition.
approach:
In order to test our hypothesis in a mouse model, we will be requesting tissue sample from deceased mouse those were exposed to spaceflight condition in the past spaceflight mission. For negative control for the study, we will use tissue from the mice those were lived in earth condition. A comparative analysis of DJ1 function in microgravity conditions and earth condition will give a clear indication of its role in RS functioning in space environment, which affects directly affects the neurocognitive and associated behaviors. Therefore, the present project will be done in two steps. 

Task-1: To study the transcriptional and translational expressions of DJ1 in mouse brain tissues. BALB/cJ mice brain tissues and blood pellets will be requested from LSDA to perform the gene, protein and sequence analysis for DJ1 expression. 

Task-2: To determine the effect of DJ1 on DA regulation in the space flight condition. Brain tissues from BALB/cJ mice will be used for immunohistochemistry to study the DA and DJ1 expression. This will help us to establish the relation between DJ1 expression and DA release. 

Methods: Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR): Gene expression will be quantitated using real-time PCR as per previous publication from control and space flight exposed mouse brain tissues and blood cells. RNA from cell pellets will be extracted using the RNAeasy mini kit (Qiagen, Germany). Following that the cDNA synthesis will be performed using cDNA kit (Applied Biosystems) to perform qRT-PCR using TaqMan gene expression assays (Applied Biosystems) for DJ1 and GAPDH. Relative mRNA species expression will be quantitated and the mean fold change in expression of DJ1 gene will be calculated using the comparative CT method (Transcript Accumulation Index, TAI = 2−ΔΔCT). 

DJ1 Protein Levels by Western Blot  (WB) Analysis: Same amounts of brain tissues will be extracted for protein analysis from control and spaceflight exposed mouse. All the protein samples will be  separated by using Mini-Protean TGX Precast Gels (Bio-Rad) as per previous publication. ImageJ software will be used to quantify the protein expression on the blot and will be expressed as a fold change with respect to treatment. 

Immunohistochemistry and tract tracing of DA: The following antibodies will be used for immunohistochemistry studies: Tyrosine hydroxylase (TH), the rate-limiting enzyme for catecholamine synthesis, will serve as a marker for DA neurons; Fluorogold (FG), a neuronal retrograde tract tracer; and DJ1 protein. Our experiments are designed to determine the expression pattern of DJ1 in the ventral tegmental area (VTA) and the degree of co-localization of TH and DJ1. The retrograde tracer FG will be infused into the nucleus accumbens (NAcc). FG is typically absorbed by presynaptic terminals and transported to the cell body. Therefore, using immunohistochemistry, we will be able to visualize DANs that send axons to the NAcc. If a cell in the VTA stains positive for TH, FG, and DJ1, then that would indicate that this particular DJ1-expressing DAN projects to the NAcc, which will be a major breakthrough in our field.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Skeletal and systemic stress indicators using dental biomarkers
objectives:
The health of our astronauts is a priority in our efforts to explore space. At present there are no temporal biochemical tests that record an astronaut's physiology during space flight. We have developed two novel technologies to overcome this limitation—these are hair- and teeth-based biomarkers. Our technologies can provide 4-6 hourly measures of key homeostatic pathways from a single sample collected after space flight. Our method is based on the unique properties of fundamental systemic homeostatic pathways that are ubiquitous in
mammals and are highly conserved during evolution. These include key elemental pathways (such as copperzinc), heat shock proteins (HSPs), the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis products (e.g. cortisol),
metallothioneins, glucoseregulated proteins, and oxidative stress  ndicators [PMID:29596852, 31533245]. In the following section we describe the model we are proposing and provide preliminary evidence supporting our hypothesis. We provide proof of concept from analysis of elemental markers and HSP-70, a stress protein, because these are particularly responsive to physiologic and environmental stressors and are expressed in all tissues, making them the ideal targets for our study. In the study we propose here, we only ask for existing rodent teeth and hair that have been banked by NASA. Based on the data we generate here, we will submit future proposals to analyze not only human astronaut hair (and teeth if available), but also fresh samples of rodent
teeth, which contains dental biofilm (dental plaque). It's well known environmental signals regulate the expression of virulence determinants in microorganisms, which highlights that these symbionts which live inside and on the human body can behave differently in a stressed condition (e.g. microgravity). Therefore, in the future, we aim to combine the findings from teeth-based biomarkers with dental biofilm microbiome. Hence, we will obtain understanding of the microbiome integrated with specific biomarkers for stress, inflammation and
nutritional metabolism in teeth using fresh samples of rodent teeth. Our main goal is to build a knowledge base to study and prevent the effects of systemic stress to astronauts, as well as to design non-invasive biomarkers and techniques to access signs of stress due to environmental changes at early stages. We have extensively validated and published on the methods we describe in this proposal in leading journals [PMID: 31308534, 28569757, 26727334].
approach: We propose to develop a methodology that will allow us to uncover disruptions of homeostatic pathways. Our proposed method will be able to detect both the timing and intensity of homeostatic disruptions. We are proposing that for a complete understanding of the effects of spaceflight, it is not sufficient only to measure downstream effects such as bone mineral density or clinical parameters but rather it is also important to determine factors before and during flight multiple times in a 24-hour day cycle. For example, the underlying dynamic rhythmicity of a system may be altered but the overall average of a system may remain unchanged. The other main advantage of our approach is that it can directly measure the origins of health effects in a global context and also identify person-level disruptions that increase susceptibility. Our approach is different to other methodologies that measure susceptibility – for example, measuring single nucleotide polymorphisms only studies susceptibility due to the pre-selected genetic variations in isolation of other unmeasured factors. Genome-wide studies, while providing a much wider coverage of genetic predisposition to a disease, are also hampered by the need for large sample sizes when studying interactions between multiple genetic and environmental factors, and are not feasible in the context of studies addressing space travel. To accomplish the proposed research, we would like to request samples from 10 animals on the ground and 10 sent to space. We need 2 whiskers sand 2 incisor teeth (the long teeth in front of the mount) from each animal. If removing the teeth causes any problems, we can receive the entire lower jaw, remove the teeth at our lab and return any unused material.
disease_areas: Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases; Mental health

title: Countermeasures Against Adverse Effects of Space Radiation
objectives:
This is the first task book for Phase 1 study of our project entitled "Countermeasures against adverse effects of space radiation." The primary goal of our Phase 1 study is to test the efficacy of apigenin (AP) for the prevention and mitigation of cancer in male mice due to exposure to space radiation as measured by incidence, aggressiveness, burden, and latency. Our main hypothesis is that AP exhibits its beneficial effects by suppression of radiation-induced inflammation and oxidative stress during the initiation/promotion steps of carcinogenesis. Although the emphasis will be on the mitigation of lung cancer and lymphoma/leukemia, other types of cancer will be recorded.

In this project, we will give food containing AP to male mice before and after exposure to heavy silicon (28Si) ions. There are four groups of mice. These are sham controls (Group 1, no AP diet, no radiation), mice receiving 0.5 Gy of 260 MeV/n 28Si ions radiation only and without AP diet (Group 2), mice receiving AP only with no radiation exposure (Group 3), and mice receiving both AP and radiation (Group 4). Groups of mice from each treatment will be used for a serial sacrifice schedule at 1 week and 6 months post-irradiation. This will test the ability of AP to counteract heavy-ion-induced early- and late-occurring inflammation and oxidative damage in various tissues linked to cancer that are the focus of our study (i.e., bone marrow, lung, thymus, spleen). All remaining mice will be observed for morbidity and mortality until they reach about 600-700 days of age. Our data enable the evaluation of countermeasure efficacy of AP across tissues at risk for cancer induction, i.e., bone marrow and the lung. This multi-tissue of the same exposed individual approach has not been used in space research. 

Aim 1: Measure the levels of early- and late-occurring impairment of the immune system in multiple tissues (plasma, BM, spleen, thymus, lung, brain, and heart) of the same mouse (male and female) included in the serial sacrifice, with or without exposure to a single beam of 260 MeV/n 28Si ions and a mixed beam of protons 1 GeV protons and 260 MeV/n 28Si ions, with or without AP treatment.

Aim 2: Quantify the levels of early- and late-occurring oxidative damage in multiple tissues (plasma, BM, spleen, thymus, lung, brain, and heart) of the same mouse (male and female) included in the serial sacrifice, with or without exposure to a single beam of 260 MeV/n 28Si ions and a mixed beam of 1 GeV protons and 260 MeV/n 28Si ions, with or without AP treatment.

Aim 3: Determine the effectiveness of AP in prevention, mitigation or delay of lung cancer or lymphoma/leukemia in exposed male or female C57BL/6 mice after exposure to a single beam of 260 MeV/n 28Si ions or a mixed beam of protons 1 GeV protons and 260 MeV/n 28Si ions as
determined by an incidence, aggressiveness, overall burden, and latency.
approach:
We will give food containing AP to mice (males and females) before and after exposure to silicon (Si) ions. Subsequently, the mixedbeam (protons + Si) experiment will be designed. Oxidative stress and inflammation are highly relevant not only to carcinogenesis but also to cardiovascular and nervous disorders. Hence, groups of mice from each treatment will be used for a serial sacrifice schedule at 1 week and 3 months post-irradiation. This will test the ability of AP to counteract heavy-ion-induced early- and late-occurring inflammation and oxidative damage in various tissues linked to cancer that are the focus of our study (i.e. bone marrow, lung, thymus, spleen), including the heart and the brain of the same mouse. All remaining mice will be observed for morbidity and mortality until they reach about 800 days of age. Our data enable the evaluation of countermeasure efficacy of AP not only across tissues at risk for cancer but also across risk areas. This multi-tissue approach has not been used in space research.

Currently, we are conducting phase 1 of the study involving the exposure of male C57Bl6 mice. It is anticipated that phase 2 of the study involving the exposure of female mice will be initiated in 2021.
results:
This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Cardiovascular Responses to Simulated Spaceflight: Molecular Signatures and Surrogate Outputs to Measure CVD Risk
objectives:
In this ground-based rodent study, we aim to systematically define molecular signatures of cardiovascular performance across doses of acute simulated galactic cosmic radiation (Five-ion GCR) at early and late timepoints post-exposure. We also will determine the contribution of biological sex and the combined effects of GCR and microgravity on clinically relevant and emerging measures of cardiovascular health. We hypothesize that exposure to space radiation alone or in combination with microgravity results in early and late changes in the structure, transcriptome, redox signaling, and cytokine milieu of cardiovascular tissue, some of which have known links to decreased performance, aging and increased CVD risk. We further posit that other less invasive
clinically relevant measures of immune, behavior and neuromotor function will be informative towards extrapolating the effects of deep space missions on human cardiovascular health. To achieve the project goals and test the hypothesis, we will take advantage of a rare tissue sharing opportunity from a recently funded HRP study. The experiment design of this funded investigation includes a GCR dosing study on crew age-matched female and male mice (6 months old) as well as combined exposure study with simulated microgravity. A comprehensive panel of outcomes will be assessed in the funded study and includes measures of immune health, brain molecular and structural changes, behavior, anxiety, cognition and neuromotor function.

Specific Aim 1. Determine radiation dose-dependence of measures of cardiovascular performance in crew age-matched female and male mice in an acute 'five-ion GCR simulation' exposure model.

Specific Aim 2. Determine effects of acute 'five-ion GCR simulation' exposure singly and in combination with simulated microgravity on cardiovascular performance measures in crew age matched male and female mice, mimicking deep space missions.

Specific Aim 3. Determine whether cardiovascular changes induced by GCR or in combination with HU correlate with specific and measurable behavioral, neuromotor and immune outcomes.
approach:
Our proposed approach is to measure clinically relevant indicators of cardiovascular performance and perform transcriptomic profiling by RNAseq to determine dose and time-dependency of cardiovascular responses. To further facilitate extrapolation of results to humans, rodent RNAseq data will be compared to publicly available human RNAseq datasets from aging and cardiovascular disease (CVD) progression studies. The results from analysis of rodent cardiovascular tissue also will be compared to corresponding blood data to link immune and
cardiovascular changes. Further, cardiovascular findings will be interpreted in light of behavioral testing results to gain insight on any relationships between cardiovascular outcomes and changes in neuromotor, anxiety levels and cognitive performance. The significance/impact of this study is that it contributes to increased understanding on the mechanisms of degenerative changes in cardiovascular tissue and the clinical endpoints they suggest. This
study also is expected to provide insight on the latency period for radiation-induced cardiovascular changes and any sex differences in these outcomes. Lastly, our findings are expected to generate testable hypotheses for the development of countermeasures and less invasive surrogate biomarkers to monitor cardiovascular health inflight and after return.
results: This study will generate transcriptomic data and clinically relevant markers of cardiovascular health from a rodent model of spaceflight, specifically focusing on the effects of simulated microgravity and space radiation singly or in combination.
disease_areas: Cardiovascular diseases; Cancer; Neurological diseases; Women's health; Mental health

title: Particle Radiation-Induced Dysregulation of Protein Homeostasis in the Brain
objectives:
The space atmosphere is comprised of a wide spectrum of highly energetic and charged particle radiation. Exposure to this type of radiation in the galacto-cosmic environment or unplanned solar particle events (SPE) may pose a potential health hazard to humans in long-duration manned space explorations. In normal biological systems, protein stability and system homeostasis are maintained through a tightly coupled post-translational modification process that is catalyzed by the ubiquitin-proteasome system for protein turnover. This process is necessary for modifying the correct protein in the correct cellular location at the correct time to control important processes, such as cell-cycle regulation, the maintenance of genomic stability, removal of damaged or misfolded proteins, autophagy, apoptosis, and gene transcription. Depending on the radiation dose and beam quality, particle radiation may cause significant damage to proteins and oxidative stress in the cells in the central nervous system, leading to dysfunction of the cellular ubiquitin-related pathways, an increase in the levels of extracellular ubiquitin, and persistent subsequent downstream effects on regulation of stress-associated signaling pathways in many different cell types within the central nervous system (CNS), precipitating neurodegenerative disease. The investigators hypothesized that quantitative changes in cellular ubiquitin pools occur when neuronal cells are exposed to particle radiation and aim to study the impact of protein degradation after particle radiation using both in vitro and in vivo model systems.

This study had the following specific aims:
1) Quantitative assessment of particle-radiation induced cellular protein turnover to uncover alterations in pathway-specific cellular ubiquitin pools and protein modification in vitro using cultured primary human and mouse neuronal stem cells.
2) Evaluation of particle radiation induced alterations in protein ubiquitination in brain tissues of C57Bl6 mice.
3) Evaluation of particle radiation induced alterations in protein ubiquitination in brain tissues of a characterized and widely used triple transgenic 3xTg mouse model of Alzheimer's Diseases (AD).
approach:
C57Bl6 (male and female) mice were purchased from Charles River Laboratories and shipped directly to Brookhaven National Laboratory Animal Facility (BLAF) one week before scheduled beam time. Animals were shipped by World Courier shortly after exposure to SRI, the PI's institution, and housed until scheduled necropsy. Brain tissues from different regions of the brain, including the hippocampus, cerebellum, and cortex of C57Bl6 mice were harvested at one week and eight weeks after particle irradiation. Investigators also analyzed archived brain tissues from C57Bl6 mice that were harvested 11 weeks after a single dose of 1 GeV/u Iron ions.

Three primary E14 mouse neuronal stem cells in suspension (Striata, Cortex and Ventral Mesencephalon Neurospheres from StemCell Technologies, Inc Canada) and one human neuronal stem cells in monolayer (H9 hESC-Derived from ThermoFisher Scientific/Invitrogen GIBCO®) were used in these studies. Cells were thawed and cultured using vendor-recommended protocols approximately one week prior to particle irradiation. Cells were harvested 24 hours post irradiation. Cultures were pooled and cells were pelleted, rinsed with PBS and flash frozen until processing. Animals and cell cultures were irradiated with a single dose of particle irradiation. Detailed dosimetry was conducted on the day of the exposure by the NSRL Physics team. The average dose rate was approximately 30 cGy/minute.
results:
For the human neuronal stem cells, a significant 2.0-fold increase in the K63 chain linkage relative to changes in the controls was observed in cells 24 hours after 50 cGy of 1 GeV/u Iron ions. No significant changes relative to the control were observed in either the K48 or K11 chain linkages at 20 cGy or 50 cGy relative to the controls. A significant, but small magnitude changes in K48 levels after a high 100 cGy proton dose was observed. This suggested that LET may play a role in alterations in polyubiquitin (pUb) chain linkages type-dependent changes.

For the pUb chain linkage quantification, K48-linkages were noted as significantly increased after protons in the human cells and mouse cells that were derived from all three regions of the embryonic brain. This suggests that proton exposure led to an accumulation of damaged proteins that would have degraded under normal conditions. LET iron ions at 1 GeV/u showed elevated K63 linked proteins in human neuronal stem cells, mouse cortical and striatal embryonic stem cells. For the intermediate LETs, the results were not as clear. Animals that were irradiated with 1 GeV/u iron ions showed sustained K63 pUb linkages after a single 10, 50 or 100 cGy in the hippocampus of the brain. Elevated K63 linkages were seen at one week and eight weeks after a low 10 cGy dose or 50 cGy dose. On the other hand, elevated K63 pUb linkage after 100 cGy was delayed and observed at eight weeks. Since there were no intermediate time points, such as at four weeks, it is unclear whether the K63 pUb linkage increase after this high dose occurred prior to eight weeks.

Results from the hippocampal tissues from the different doses of protons and Silicon ions was not significantly different from the sham-treated controls at one or eight weeks post irradiation. With these results, investigators questioned if (1) eight weeks is an appropriate timeframe to see a low dose effect after iron ions and (2) whether other regions of the brain was affected. To address these questions, through tissue-sharing efforts, investigators were able to obtain brain tissues that were irradiated with 10 or 40 cGy of 1 GeV/u Iron ions from female CB6F1 mice and harvested at 16 months post irradiation. During harvest, the brain tissues were fractionated into the hippocampus, frontal lobe, cerebellum and the cortex. These four sections were selected from six animals that were determined to be tumor free at the time of necropsy. Using a mass spectrometry approach, the investigators demonstrated that, indeed, even though there were no changes in the K48 linkage at 16 month in these animals in the hippocampus, frontal lobe and the cortical region of the brain, significant changes in K48 pUb was observed in the cerebellum tissues.

The investigators saw no increase in Tau phosphorylation in the hippocampus' of C57BL6 mice at one or eight weeks after 10 or 50 cGy 1GeVu iron ions. They also did not see hyperphosphorylation of Tau in the hippocampus of animals that were irradiated with the same two doses of Silicon ions. The investigators questioned if eight weeks post irradiation was too early. When they examined Tau phosphorylation in the cerebellum of 16 month CB6F1 mice, significant monophosphorylated Tau was observed at the 492-site. In addition, they also saw significant increase in dephosphorylated Tau in the hippocampus tissue at this time point in the 696 site when compared to the controls.

title: Ovarian cancer and space radiation
objectives:
Women made up 50% and 45%, respectively, of the 2013 and 2017 NASA astronaut classes. Astronauts are exposed to galactic cosmic rays during travel in deep space. GCR consist of protons, helium ions, and charged particles heavier than helium, such as silicon, iron and oxygen.  Our published work demonstrates profound sensitivity of the ovary to charged particle radiation, with destruction of the irreplaceable ovarian follicle pool and 4-fold as many ovarian tumors as in control non-irradiated mice. Comparison of our data with published studies of ovarian follicle depletion and ovarian tumorigenesis by exposure to gamma radiation suggest that charged particle radiation may be a more potent inducer of both premature ovarian follicle depletion and ovarian tumors, but this has not been directly tested. 

We hypothesize that ovarian follicle depletion by iron and oxygen charged particle radiation is greater than ovarian follicle depletions by -radiation at comparable doses and that silicon and mixed heavy ion beam (Si,Fe, Ti,) charged particle radiation cause ovarian tumors at lower doses than -radiation.

Aim 1: Utilize archived ovaries to compare ovarian tumor induction by irradiation with silicon, mixed heavy ion (3-HI) beams composed of equal doses of 28Si, 56Fe, and 48Ti ions charged particles or -rays. We will compare ovarian tumorigenesis at 16 months after irradiation of female CB6F1 mice irradiated at approximately 3 months of age with 260 MeV/u 28Si (0, 4, 8, 16, or 32 cGy), 3-HI mixed beam (0, 3x10cGy=30cGy, or 3x20cGy=60 cGy),  or 137Cs gamma-rays (0, 4, 8, 12, and 16 cGy) in archived ovaries from the NASA Tissue Sharing Archive (N=30 ovaries/group). We will conduct detailed histopathology of ovaries and molecular characterization of tumors using immunostaining for tumor markers. 

Aim 2: Utilize archived ovaries harvested at various time points after irradiation with low doses of oxygen or iron charged particles to examine the persistence of ovarian oxidative lipid, protein, and DNA damage, archived serum to measure a biomarker of ovarian reserve, and evaluate these as potential early biomarkers of ovarian tumorigenesis. Irradiate mice with low doses of -radiation and harvest ovaries at 1 week after irradiation in order to compare ovarian follicle depletion by charged iron or oxygen particles with -radiation. Our published work demonstrates oxidative damage and dose-dependent apoptotic depletion of ovarian follicles after exposure to 0, 0.05, 0.3, and 0.5 Gy charged iron or oxygen particles. Serum luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) were significantly elevated in 0.5 Gy-irradiated mice 8wk after irradiation, consistent with loss of negative feedback due to follicle depletion, but serum LH and FSH are not optimal serum markers of ovarian reserve because they vary with estrous cycle stage and are secreted episodically, and we did not perform immunohistochemical analyses of ovaries at 8 wk after irradiation. We will examine oxidative lipid, protein, and DNA damage by immunostaining as potential biomarkers of ovarian tumor risk in archived ovaries from mice euthanized 8 wk after irradiation with charged iron or oxygen particles. We will measure Anti-Müllerian Hormone (AMH), a serum marker of ovarian reserve that is used clinically, in archived serum from mice euthanized 1 wk and 15 months after irradiation. 3-month old female C57BL/6J mice will be irradiated with 0, 0.05, 0.15, or 0.5 Gy γ-rays and euthanized one week post irradiation for ovarian follicle counts, which will be compared to our published data on charged iron or oxygen particle-irradiated mice.
approach: We propose to leverage these stored tissue and blood samples, together with ovaries from gamma- irradiated mice from the NASA tissue archive to 1) compare ovarian tumor prevalence and molecular characteristics after low dose mixed heavy ion (3-HI) beam or 28Si irradiation with gamma irradiation in adult female mice; 2) examine the persistence and types of ovarian oxidative damage after irradiation and evaluate serum concentrations of a clinically utilized biomarker of ovarian reserve, Anti-Müllerian Hormone (AMH), as a potential early biomarker of ovarian tumorigenesis. We will quantify the effects of charged particles on numbers of ovarian follicles and ovarian tumor number and size. We will use in situ methods to assess oxidative damage and to molecularly characterize the ovarian tumors. Our analyses will provide critical insights into whether preneoplastic changes in ovarian follicle numbers, serum AMH, as well as ovarian oxidative damage caused by exposure to charged particles demonstrate similar dose-response as ovarian tumor induction. The analyses will also examine the relative biological effectiveness of gamma versus charged particle irradiation for these endpoints. These studies will help to fill important gaps in our understanding of the effects of space radiation on ovarian function and ovarian carcinogenesis and will lead to better ways to prevent ovarian cancer and protect reproductive health in women astronauts.
results:
Aim 1: We expect the highest incidence of ovarian tumors in mice irradiated with 3-HI beam, followed by 28Si irradiated, and lowest incidence in gamma-irradiated. We expect that most of the tumors will be epithelial based on immunohistochemical characterization. We expect a greater percentage of ovarian tumors in the 3-HI beam irradiated mice will be malignant than in the other groups.

Aim 2: We expect that charged iron and oxygen particles will be more potent inducers of ovarian DNA damage, oxidative damage, and apoptosis and therefore of ovarian follicle depletion than gamma radiation. We further expect that AMH will be more strongly associated with numbers of growing follicles than resting primordial follicles at one week post-irradiation. At 16 months after irradiation, we expect low levels of AMH within all groups due to the age of the mice, but that levels will be higher in control un-irradiated mice due to some remaining follicles, while the ovaries of irradiated mice will be devoid of follicles.
disease_areas: Cancer; Women's health; Endocrine and metabolic diseases

title: Develop a novel single-cell biodosimetry for brain genomic instability and neurodegeneration to predict clinical health outcomes in human spaceflight crews
objectives:
As NASA plans future exploration missions to the Lunar and Martian surfaces, realistic ground-based analog studies and more predictive biodosimetry are needed to assess whether the space radiation poses a detrimental risk of brain genomic instability and neurodegeneration that leads to late-onset behavioral deterioration for spaceflight crews. Implementing a recently developed method of recreating the intravehicular (IVA) radiation environment expected on spaceflight vehicles and habitats and a novel genetic sensor, this proposal addresses Research Topic 3– Animal Biology Studies in support of Human Space Exploration and Sub-topic AB1-A: Behavior and underlying neural function in Appendix D: Solicitation of Proposals for Flight and Ground Space Biology Research.

We propose to determine how the space environment and sex affect brain genomic stability and consequent agerelated brain structure and function changes. Our studies will support Human Space Exploration, by contributing the first biodosimetry for quantifying brain DNA instability and neurodegenerative changes to predict clinical health outcomes in human spaceflight crews and the utility of available ground-based analogs to realize basic mechanisms that can lead to the development of biologic counter-measures.

The proposed studies offer definite answers to determine: 
1) Age-dependency of the long-term (comparable to 30-70 human years) consequences of GCR exposure elicited DNA instability in postmitotic neurons. 
2) Gender difference of the GCR exposure-elicited DNA instability and/or neurode generation. 
3) Brain region specificity: Which brain region (sensorimotor, cognition, and emotion) is most affected. 
4) Brain map of the irradiated cells across age. 
5) Progression of behavioral deficits (sensory-motor, cognition, andemotion)
6)Threshold (frequency of the cells with DNA instability in different brain regions to manifest behavioral changes.)
approach: Describe how the experiment was conducted, including methodology, total number of subjects and treatments. Determine the dose-response curve of the single-cell biodosimetry of neuronal genomic instability and consequent neurodegenerative changes after a realistic IVA radiation exposure. Specific Aim 2. Determine the sex-specific long-term consequences of GCR exposure to elicit neuronal genomic instability in sensorimotor regions and behavioral manifestations. No aim requires flight experiments of any kind.
results:
This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
