title: Thrombosis in Microgravity
objectives:
Our objective of this work is to understand the effect of microgravity and headward fluid shift on the risk of venous thrombosis. We will test the hypothesis that microgravity modulates the risk of cerebral venous thrombosis through effects on endothelial activation and local transport dynamics. Using a scale-down microfluidic endothelialized model of the vasculature subjected to microgravity in a random positioning machine (RPM).

The two aims that will specifically test our hypothesis are:
1. To evaluate the combined effects of altered flow and microgravity on venous endothelial transcriptome and miRNA-ome . We will use a microfluidic platform and a random positioning machine (RPM) to evaluate shear rate dependence and gravisenstivity of endothelial cells (ECs).

2. To evaluate the effect of microgravity on venous thrombosis . We will evaluate the kinetics of thrombus growth at near stasis and normal shear rates on endothelial surfaces conditioned to normal or microgravity conditions.

We expect that our investigative team with expertise in vascular mechanobiology, microfluidics, and system biology will provide insights into poorly understood aspects of microgravity on cardiovascular physiology, which have been mentioned as an area of Space Biology research emphases, and also as high priority in the midterm assessment of the Decadal report. Further, in our opinion, the recent incidents raise the risk of thrombosis as a health concern in astronauts. We believe that our innovative ground analog will generate testable hypothesis, and
new, high-value tools that can be translated to full scale spaceflight investigations on hemostasis and thrombosis.
approach:
Aim 1. To evaluate the effect of microgravity on venous endothelial transcriptome. 

In this aim, we will test the hypothesis that both venous shear flow and microgravity will interactively affect endothelial cell function and gene expression. The scientific premise is based on a recent report that by day 50 in ISS, 50% of crew members had flow stasis or reversal while the other 50% had normal forward flow in the left
IJV [4]. The forward flow in these cases was 20 cm/s, which is pretty typical for venous flow, and is considered to be in the 100 s -1 shear rate range [10]. As mentioned in the Background section, flow patterns greatly influence endothelial gene expression. We will use an endothelialized microfluidic model to test our hypothesis. Although
reductionist, these models recreate physiological milieu to enable visualization, identification, and understanding of biological phenomena that have previously not been possible using traditional static in vitro or in vivo models.

Aim 2. To evaluate the effect of microgravity on the development of venous thrombosis.

In normal gravity, cerebral venous thrombosis is much less common than venous thrombosis of lower extremities; and even amongst the cerebral venous thrombosis, the frequency of jugular venous thrombosis is only 12% [61]. Therefore, it is intriguing that the occlusive thrombosis in IJV after 60 days in ISS was reported in an astronaut who was not an at-risk candidate of venous thrombosis. Flow stasis and reversal seen in the IJV in at least 50% of the astronauts on flight day 50 suggest that fluid dynamics may propitiate a thrombotic event. Further, in Aim 1,
we expect to have understood the combined effects of microgravity and flow stasis on endothelial phenotype. Therefore, in this second aim, we will test the hypothesis that the altered blood flow patterns modulate thrombus initiation and growth on mechanosensitized endothelium. To test this hypothesis, we will use an endothelialized model of microvasculature conditioned to shear and gravity forces in Aim 1, and investigate the effect of blood flow patterns on thrombus formation.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Space Environment and Epigenetics of Endocrine Regulation of DNA Repair and Cell Cycle in Mammary Epithelial Cells
objectives:
Research products of this project include studies of genes that may be epigenetically susceptible to space ionizing radiation (IR) and microgravity and how these changes affect endocrine regulation of genes involved in DNA damage response (DDR) and cell cycle control. The term epigenetics refers to changes in gene expression in the absence of DNA sequence modifications and includes CpG methylation, histone modifications (e.g. histone acetylation, methylation), and expression of non-coding RNAs (e.g. microRNAs).

TYPE OF INVESTIGATION. This is a Ground-based New Space Biology (NSB) proposal that investigates one of the major recommendations of the 2011 Decadal Survey related to the influence of the space environment on animal cell and molecular biology. This work is responsive to the Space Cellular and Molecular Biology 2018 NASA Omnibus Research Announcement soliciting research answers as to how the space environment causes epigenetic changes.

Key elements of the space environment include IR and gravity (i.e. microgravity, hypergravity) which have been suggested to alter a variety of biological processes including endocrine systems (Sub-topic AB1-B). The central hypothesis of this proposal is that IR and microgravity alter epigenetically endocrine regulation of DDR and cell cycle control in mammary epithelial cells. The rationale for this hypothesis stems from our own and published evidence that 1) the estrogen receptor-alpha (ERalpha) activates epigenetically the BRCA1 gene, which participates with other DDR factors (eg. ataxia telangiectasia mutated, ATM; ataxia telangiectasia and Rad-3 related, ATR) in regulation of DNA repair during the S and G2 phases of the cell cycle; and 2) conversely,
activation of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) prevents ERalpha-dependent activation of BRCA1. Because IR and microgravity regulate AhR activity, we will test if IR, microgravity, and their combination alter epigenetically the DDR and cell cycle regulation.

SPECIFIC AIMS. Specific Aims of this NSB project are to investigate in human mammary epithelial cells under endocrine stimulation the epigenetic effects of IR (Aim 1) and microgravity alone or in combination with IR (Aim 2) on expression of genes involved in DDR and cell cycle regulation.
approach:
Aim 1. Human mammary epithelial cells (HMEC, MCF10A) will be cultured in 2D tissue culture dishes in the presence of estrogen to simulate the physiological exposure under normal gravity of mammary epithelial cells to ovarian estrogens. Cells will be exposed for various lengths of time (i.e. short-, 1, 2, and 3 days; medium-, 6, 12, 18 days; and long-term, 24, 36, and 48 days) to 3 doses (0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 mSv/day) of IR.

Aim 2. Mammary epithelial cells (HMEC, MCF10A) will be cultured in 2D, or to simulate microgravity, in rotating wall vessel (RWV) bioreactor 3D cultures in the presence of estrogen without or with IR (0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 mSv/day) for various periods of time (i.e. short-, medium, and long-term). Determinations will include changes in:
(i) mRNA and protein expression of BRCA1, ATM, ATR, ERalpha, AhR, and AhR nuclear translocator (ARNT) respectively by RT-PCR and Western blotting; (ii) CpG methylation at BRCA1, ATM, and ATR genes by methylation-specific RT-PCR ; (iii) levels of acetylated histone 3 (AcH3) and trimethylated lysine 27 at histone-3 at the BRCA1, ATM, and ATR genes by chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) assay ; (iv) recruitment of DNA methyltransferase-1 (DNMT1) and DNMT3a, lysine-specific demethylase-1 (LSD1), histone deacetylase-1 (HDAC1), and PcG enhancer of zeste homologue 2 (EZH2) at the BRCA1, ATM, and ATR genes by ChIP assay; (v) genome-wide profile of genes targeted by the ERalpha and AhR by ChIP- sequencing; (vi) DNA double strand breaks by immuno-detection of gamma-H2AX foci; and (vii) cell cycle distribution by flow cytometry.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: A Technology To Measure Gait, Egress, and Locomotor Performance in Perturbed Environmental Conditions After Simulated Spaceflight
objectives:
Long-duration spaceflight is challenging for the many body parts that help us maintain normal movements and perform well, which include our bones, joints, vision, and brain. Astronauts must perform to the best of their abilities when they are traveling to a destination like the moon or Mars, and when they reach the destination. During the travel to the destination, or on the surface of the planet/moon, astronauts could face dangerous situations that require rapid escape movements, or situations where the body could be in peril due to surroundings (like when climbing the rough terrain of a mountain or into a valley). If an astronaut is not performing well due to altered visual performance, but also has damaged bones due to low gravity or radiation, the astronaut could be at risk of catastrophic joint tears or bone breaks while exploring uneven/dangerous terrains, or during a required rapid escape into or out
of a spacecraft. Our laboratory has measured that performance is altered in rodents after ~35 days in orbit on the International Space Station. However, these measurements were taken on a treadmill moving forward at a constant speed. This does not represent the dangerous terrain of the moon or Mars, or other rapid movements astronauts would face during spaceflight. Thus our intent is to develop and fabricate a method to better reflect locomotor performance in rodent models over uneven and dangerous lunar/Martian surfaces in order to best assess how combined spaceflight hazards (e.g, microgravity and radiation) cause deficits in astronaut performance, measure time to recovery, and identify countermeasures. We will create a platform on which sits our treadmill that can measure mouse and rat performance. However, the platform can move (one movable portion under each corner support of the treadmill) in a manner that can reflect uneven terrain or a rapid escape motion. Then we can measure how the animals that have previous been exposed to spaceflight conditions (like reduced gravity or radiation) respond. This platform and performance measurement device can then be used to test ways to maximize performance, and thus improve the technologies and approaches used during successful manned space exploration.

SPECIFIC AIM 1: Engineer a platform that will permit rapid, controlled perturbation of the locomotor surface for our rodent gait-treadmill system to assess deficits in gait and egress performance after periods of reduced weight bearing. We will test the system and generate preliminary data with a short-term (14 day) hind limb unlading (HU) study of mice.
approach:
The proposed project will develop a new, portable tool that will permit quantifying functional performance responses (e.g., egress) from rodents both pre- and post-spaceflight and/or from ground-based models of individual and/or combined spaceflight hazards, importantly while
locomoting over a perturbed environment. In response to observations made during the course of our RR9 study, we have modified the plexiglass chamber that houses mice on top of the DigiGait unit with the capability to assess egress dysfunction, although egress has not yet been
tested. This new technology will serve as the basis for future proposals aimed at examining/preventing gait and performance deficits post-ISS missions, and using groundbased analogues.
results:
This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

We expect that we will be able to collect and measure gait parameters and timing metrics with and without perturbations.
publication_titles: Spaceflight and hind limb unloading induces an arthritic phenotype in knee articular cartilage and menisci of rodents
disease_areas: Neurological diseases; Eye diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases

title: Role of Mesenchymal Stem Cells in Microgravity Induced Bone Loss
objectives:
Bone loss resulting in low bone mineral density as observed in osteopenia and osteoporosis increases the risk fracture. Bone loss is a major problem at Earth gravity (1 g) and is aggravated by microgravity in space, especially during long duration space flight. Exercise slows bone loss in microgravity but even when astronauts adopt a rigorous exercise regimen, they continue to lose about 1 – 2% of their bone mass a month. While there is a vast body of knowledge on bone homeostasis under normal gravity conditions, the mechanisms of bone loss in microgravity are poorly understood and remain to be explored. It is not yet fully determined what signals allow bone tissue to adapt to a weightless environment compared to an Earth environment. Conceptually, altered biomechanical stimuli in microgravity may directly affect osteoblasts, osteoclasts and/or their precursors, while other physiological factors such as hormone levels or poor nutrition could contribute to bone loss in microgravity. The central hypothesis of our proposal is that microgravity alters the activity of mesenchymal stem cells, which are the precursors of bone forming osteoblasts, and their secretion of cytokines and growth factors that promote osteogenesis and osteolytic activity. Specifically, we postulate that microgravity may
decrease MSC number and disturb the secretion of trophic factors that impede bone homeostasis by reducing new bone formation
and/or increasing bone resorption in microgravity.

Bone homeostasis requires a complex interplay between mesenchymal osteoblasts and hematopoietic osteoclasts and is controlled by multiple cytokines, including the pro-osteolytic factors IL-1 and TNFα and the anti-osteolytic factors TGFβ, IL-4, and IL-10 that are pro-osteogenesis. Some cytokines such as IL-6 pleiotropically affect bone homeostasis. Our preliminary results show that MSCs cultured in microgravity at the International Space Station (ISS) does not significantly alter MSC proliferation, but alters their cytokine and growth factor secretion with an associated increase in the secretion of pro-osteolytic cytokines. We and others have shown that IL-6 secretion by MSC is very critical in MSC functioning. We propose that microgravity skews MSC secretomes towards osteolysis and that IL-6 plays a central role in the perturbation of the homeostatic balance.

IL-6 activity is very critical in bone homeostasis, but IL-6 effects depend on how its signal is transmitted within its target cell. IL-6 acts via the classic signaling pathway by binding to its membrane bound receptor (IL-6R) and homodimeric glycoprotein 130 (gp130) proteins. In addition, IL-6 can act via its soluble receptor (sIL-6) coupled with gp130 protein to act on non-IL-6R expressing target cells as part of a non-canonical trans-signaling pathway. IL-6 induces osteogenesis and anti-osteolytic activities through its classical signaling pathway and induces osteolysis via its trans-signaling pathway. We postulate that microgravity provokes MSC induced bone loss and suppresses new bone formation via activation of the IL-6 trans-signaling pathway and blockade of the classic pathway. The rationale for our proposed studies is that a biological understanding of the IL-6 signaling pathways in MSC-induced bone loss, may permit pharmacological activation or blockage of the classical and trans-signaling pathways as a new and innovative approach to management of bone loss both in space and on Earth. Our long-term goal is to understand the mechanism of MSC induced bone loss under microgravity conditions and to develop safe and effective therapy for bone loss. The principal experimental objectives is to determine how IL-6 signaling pathways contribute to MSC-induced bone loss under microgravity conditions.
approach:
Study design is divided into three specific aims and each aim is further divided into sub-aims as outlined in Table 1.

Specific aim 1: Assess the effect of microgravity on MSC capacity to secrete osteolytic and osteogenic cytokines.

Main hypothesis:
1. Microgravity induces MSC to enhance secretion of osteolytic cytokines and suppress the secretion of osteogenic cytokines.
2. Microgravity induce MSC to secrete cytokines associated with IL-6 transsignaling pathway
3. Impact of microgravity on MSC depends on age and gender.

Specific aim 2: Evaluate the impact of microgravity on MSC osteogenic lineage commitment and bone remodeling.

Main hypothesis:
1. MSC expanded at ISS and using RWV bioreactor have diminished osteogenic lineage differentiation and have expression of bone forming genes and transcription factors
2. Conditioned media derived from MSC under microgravity condition (ISS and RWV) induce bone resorption and diminish bone formation
3. Impact of microgravity on MSC's role in bone remodeling depends on age and gender.

Specific aim 3: Determine the effect of IL-6 signaling pathway inhibitor on bone loss under microgravity condition.

Main hypothesis:
3.1 IL-6 trans-signaling pathway inhibitor (sgp130FC) diminishes microgravity induced bone loss.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
disease_areas: Cancer; Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases

title: Effect of space radiation on innate immune system homeostasis drives immune and endothelial cell dysfunction and neurodegeneration
objectives:
The key objective of this proposal is to evaluate dose- and time dependent effects of simulated space radiation on multiple tissue endpoints associated with normal aging. These endpoints include measures of the immune system, in particular those cells in the blood and tissues that represent a first line of defense for reacting to injury, the vascular compartment, another critical component of tissue homeostasis, and the brain, a critical system that shows age-associated degeneration which also occurs after radiation exposure. These studies will be carried out with male and female mice exposed to simulated space radiation at the NASA Space Research Laboratory using modern molecular techniques to measure tissue and cellular changes in gene and protein expression, alterations in cell phenotypes by flow cytometry, and behavioral tests and computer-assisted imaging in brain to quantify degenerative changes. This proposed work responds to Topic 6, Space Radiation and Tissue Homeostasis, in NASA's solicitation, and will provide key information about space radiation effects on the promotion of age-associated changes in multiple systems that are likely tied together in the etiology of late degenerative changes that may impact future astronaut health.

Together, these findings support the hypothesis that radiation promotes age-associated, sustained proinflammatory skewing of circulating and resident innate immune cells and vascular dysfunction that, together with loss of stem cell populations, contributes to neurodegenerative
changes and risk for hematologic malignancies that will impact astronaut health. To address this hypothesis, we propose the following four Specific Aims:
1) To establish dose and time-dependent effects of simulated galactic cosmic ray (GCR) exposure on innate myeloid cell populations in the bone marrow, circulation, and brain; and stem cells in the bone marrow and brain.
2) To determine dose and time-dependent effects of simulated GCR on the endovascular compartment in bone marrow and the neurovascular unit in brain.
3) To correlate observations in Aims 1 and 2 with synaptic loss and cognitive dysfunction, and test whether intervention in proinflammatory skewing reduces these degenerative changes.
4) To correlate observations in Aims 1 and 2 with the potential emergence of clonal hematopoiesis and test whether intervention in proinflammatory skewing prevents this.
approach: Consideration of Radiation Type. In contrast to our earlier studies using multiple ion beams and different doses, we have elected to use a single beam set that mimics the spectrum of deep space GCR in a spacecraft. NASA has been actively developing such a standardized beam set and has incorporated capabilities for rapid and relatively smooth ion beam transitions to facilitate through-put of samples at the NSRL. The use of a simulated GCR spectrum, combined with reference beams and doses (50 cGy 56Fe, 600 MeV/ì and 200 cGy gamma), will allow us to effectively investigate space radiation effects with numbers of mice that provide sufficient power for multiple functional and tissue endpoints at several time points.
disease_areas: Cardiovascular diseases; Neurological diseases; Women's health; Musculoskeletal diseases; Mental health

title: Exercise training as countermeasure for anti-tumor immune dysregulation due to isolation and confinement stress
objectives:
The overarching objective of this ground-based project is to determine the effects of exercise training as a countermeasure for anti-tumor immune dysregulation due to isolation and confinement stress. We will achieve this objective with the following specific aims: (1) Determine the effects of an isolated and confined spaceflight analog environment (HERA) on the antileukemic function of human NK-cells in vivo using a leukemia-bearing humanized NOD-scid-IL2Rγnull-huIL-15 (NSG-15) mouse model; and (2) Investigate the effects of combined aerobic and resistance exercise training as a potential countermeasure to mitigate
the anticipated negative effects of a HERA mission on NK-cell function in vivo. We hypothesize that NK-cells obtained from HERA participants during the mission will have a reduced ability to control leukemic growth in the NSG-15 mice compared to NK-cells collected from the same participants before the mission. We further posit that HERA crewmembers exposed to exercise training will demonstrate smaller declines in NK-cell function compared to crewmembers who are not prescribed exercise.

The specific aims of this ground-based study are
as follows:
SA1: Determine the effects of an isolated and confined spaceflight analog environment (HERA) on the anti-leukemic function of human NK-cells in vivo using a leukemia-bearing humanized NOD-scid-IL2Rγnull-huIL-15 (NSG-15) mouse model. 

SA2: Investigate the effects of combined aerobic and resistance exercise training as a potential countermeasure to mitigate the anticipated negative effects of a HERA mission on NK-cell function in vivo.
approach: We will use our established humanized NOD-scid-IL2Rγnull-huIL-15 (NSG-15) mouse model that allows us to track the growth of human leukemias by bioluminescence imaging with and without human PBMC transfer. We will also investigate if exercise training can protect HERA crewmembers from impaired NK-cell function by comparing exercised to control HERA participants using the same humanized mouse model as an in vivo endpoint. This project will serve as a daughter-study of our currently-funded NASA project (STUDY#0194) scheduled for HERA campaigns 6 and 7 (C6+C7). Crewmembers in HERA C6 will be prescribed combined aerobic and resistance exercise training in accordance with the parent project, while C7 crewmembers will serve as the control group. To achieve the aims of the present daughter-study, we will request an additional blood aliquot from our current sampling schedule (pre-mission, day 10, day 25, day 40, and post-mission time points) for PBMC cryopreservation and subsequent transfer to luciferasetagged K562 leukemia bearing NSG-15 mice.

title: Identifying spaceflight-induced mutations and DNA damage using single-cell DNA sequencing
objectives:
The long-term effects of spaceflight on human health remain largely unknown. As an exposure that may cause or increase the risk of disease, spaceflight, and its potential liabilities, are difficult to study. From an epidemiological perspective, the number of human beings who have travelled to space is low, as is the total amount of time spent there. Additionally, it is difficult to isolate the risk specific to spaceflight owing to the complex etiology of disease. For example, a recent study observed an increase in incidence of melanoma and prostate cancer in astronauts, but was unable to come to a definitive conclusion regarding astronaut-specific risks due to confounders. Astronauts often have career experience as pilots, which increases exposure to UV radiation, a
known risk factor for skin cancer. Further, astronauts undergo rigorous health screening, and this may have reduced power to detect astronaut-specific increases in cancer incidence and mortality. 

With NASA and other organizations planning to expand activities with longer manned missions venturing beyond low-Earth orbit, elucidating the long term impacts of spaceflight on human health is becoming increasingly urgent. Here, we propose an alternative approach for studying long-term disease risk: single-cell whole-genome DNA sequencing of tissues from mice that have experienced spaceflight aboard the International Space Station (ISS) in low-Earth orbit (LEO), with the goal of describing and quantifying (with respect to time) mutations not observed in similarly analyzed ground controls. While we initially aim to complete a small pilot experiment involving approximately twenty skin cells from four animals (two from space, two from ground control) along with matched bulk DNA, with promising results we hope to expand analysis to more tissues and animals.
approach:
Among possible approaches for detecting mutations associated with spaceflight genome wide and in vivo, we reason that single-cell DNA sequencing is the best option available. Possible alternative approaches include deep sequencing of bulk DNA and RNA sequencing. However, both of these methods will only find mutations present in cells that have expanded, which given the animal exposure time among available
samples (often around thirty days), is likely to severely limit sensitivity. While these approaches will likely find some clonal mutations that have been extensively expanded, they lack sensitivity to mutations present in small cell populations. Thus, if spaceflight induces a relatively large number of distinct mutations privately in each constituent cell of a tissue, neither bulk DNA nor RNA sequencing are likely to find many of them. Single-cell whole-genome sequencing on the other hand allows us to even detect somatic mutations private to single cells that have not been expanded yet. By comparing distinct mutational signatures discovered in space mice with the existing Catalogue of Somatic Mutations in Cancer (COSMIC) database, we can reveal the underlying mechanism causing the spaceflight-specific somatic mutations and predict their effects in causing cancer.

title: Effect of spaceflight on mouse mandibular bone and teeth by MicroCT analysis
objectives: Numerous studies investigated the effects of microgravity on bones of the weight-bearing skeletons. Conversely, limited information is available on non-weight-bearing bones such as mandibles and teeth. However, it has been reported a higher prevalence of dental caries, periodontitis, and bone loss and fractures in the mandible after a sojourn in the space compared to earth environment (Rai et al., 2011). Hence, it is increasingly important to understand biological mechanisms changes associated with microgravity and spaceflight to help determine the limits of normal and abnormal physiology and develop countermeasures accordingly. Finally, it will be useful to establish a protocol for tridimensional analysis of bone and tooth.
approach:
Mice at thirty-five days old, were divided equally into 5 groups: 1) Baseline - B (n=10); 2) Cohort Group - CG (n=10); 3) Flight group (n=10); 4) Ground group (n=10) and 5) Vivarium group (n=10). All stored specimens will be scanned with SkyScan 1272 (Bruker microCT N.V., Kontich, Belgium). The hemi-mandible will be scanned into a cylindrical plastic tube filled with water to avoid dehydration. Two samples of each group will be used for calibration of the analysis. 

Scanned images will then be reconstructed with NRecon version 2.0.4 (Bruker microCT N.V.) for attenuation correction, ring artifact reduction, and beam hardening. Subsequently, samples will be aligned in a three-dimensional view according to the long axis of the maxilla with Data Viewer software version 1.5.4.0 (Bruker microCT N.V.).

MicroCT images will be analyzed with CTAn software version 1.13 (Bruker microCT N.V.). Bone microarchitecture and incisor will be analyzed using the bone morphometric parameters, enamel volume, and mineral changes.

CTVox software version 3.3 (Bruker microCT N.V.) will be used to generate three-dimensional representative images and videos. A single operator blinded to the group assignment of the samples will perform all the analyses.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Radiation resistance conferred by small proline rich repeat proteins (SPRRs)
objectives:
From an ongoing NASA funded study entitled "Proteomic signatures of space radiation induced cardiovascular degeneration" we have identified that cornification and keratinization pathways are significantly perturbed in hearts from GCR irradiated mice. A family of small proline rich repeat proteins (SPRR) are central players in these pathways and are also essential players in reactive oxygen species (ROS) detoxification. These SPRRs are upregulated in various forms of tissue injury, including myocardial infarction and burn injury. As ROS are critical mediators of radiation induced molecular and cellular injury, we hypothesize that the upregulation of SPRR could function as a defense mechanism to mitigate the detrimental effects of radiation on the cardiovascular system. To date, this class of proteins has not been implicated in radiation injury. These proteins may be novel cell protective proteins that may be exploited as countermeasures for GCR and other types of radiation. Using strategies in our ongoing TRISH funded grant entitled: "Gene therapy as a Therapeutic Countermeasure Against Space Radiation Induced Cardiovascular Degeneration" we will molecularly explore the overexpression of several SPRR proteins using viral vector mediated approaches in the following two aims.

Aim 1: Determine the effects of altered SPRR levels on cellular survival and biological functions in a variety of radiation exposure settings. Viral vectors over-expressing or down-regulating SPRRs will be developed and utilized in a variety of cell models to assess the impact of individual modulation on a variety of biological outcomes post-radiation treatment. This aim will test our hypothesis that modulation such as over-expression of any of these proteins will provide resistance to radiation exposure and will provide a quick means of down-selection of the targets. Radiation exposure experiments will occur at our institution.

Aim 2: Determine the effects of modulation of SPRR levels on animal survival, or physiology following exposure to GCR at BNL. Using viral vectors based on the SPRR proteins down-selected in Aim 1 we will test the hypothesis that chosen viral vectors when administered systemically, or to targeted organs such as the heart or brain, will confer overall or organ specific resilience to radiation exposure.
approach: Summary statistics means/standard deviations/median/range for continuous variables and counts/precents for categorical variables respectively will be used to summarize data. Normality of continuous variables will be examined using any of the normality tests (e.g. Shapiro-Wilk, Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (Lillefors test) Anderson-Darling/Cramer-von Mises tests etc.). Correlations between normally distributed continuous variables will be conducted using Pearson correlations, and correlations between non-normally distributed variables will be conducted using Spearman correlations; these correlations will be examined within each viral vector intervention. The outcomes will be examined with and without adjustments for multiple comparisons in an ANOVA) for normally distributed data or using Kruskal-Wallis test (Mann-Whitney U-test and appropriate adjustment for multiple testing) for non-normal continuous outcomes. Association between categorical variables will be examined using chi-squared tests or Fishers exact test. Statistical analyses will be performed with the SAS 9.4 statistical software (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC).

title: Partial Weight-Bearing in Female Rats: Proof of Concept in a Martian-Gravity Analog
objectives: Many studies have investigated the physiological response to microgravity in both astronauts and animals. However, while space agencies have sought to deploy more women on their missions; animal models rarely include females studies or comparisons between males and females.
approach:
Adult female rats were exposed to 2 weeks of partial weight-bearing at either 100% of their normal loading (PWB100) or 40% of their normal loading (PWB40), corresponding to Martian gravity-analog, and assess muscle function, force and histomorphometry.

Rats were singly housed in custom cages (described in Mortreux et al., J Appl Physiol, 2018) and placed in their cages 48-24h prior to baseline for acclimation. They had a 12:12 light phase cycle with lights on at 7 am and lights off at 7 pm.
results: Females exposed to PWB showed an 11.62% decline in hindlimb grip force associated with an 11.84% decrease in soleus myofiber size after 14 days of exposure, while maintaining normal blood oxygenation and stress levels. This pilot study represents the first experiment designed to understand the muscular disuse associated with a partial reduction in mechanical loading in female rats, and the first step needed to develop successful mitigating strategies.
publication_titles: Partial weight-bearing in female rats: Proof of concept in a Martian-gravity analog

title: Effects of Acute and Protracted Galactic Cosmic Radiation on Bone Strength
objectives:
The exposure to ionizing space radiation can lead to damage of multiple biological tissue systems. The proposed work investigates degeneration of the central nervous system(CNS) and bone tissues from exposure to different does of simulated space radiation. Radiation is known to affect biological pathways that regulate both the CNS and bone. The objective of the proposed work to is to investigate the relationship between declines in cognitive function and declines from exposure to simulated space radiation, as well as to quantify these changes. The objectives will be addressed through animal studies exposing rats to acute and protracted (or fractionated) simulated galactic cosmic radiation and investigating the relationship between neurobehavioral deficits and bone degradation 7, 30, 90, and 180 days after radiation exposure. 

This tissue sharing proposal is part of an on-going collaboration between Dr. Catherine Davis at Uniformed Service University, who is currently funded by NASA to investigate the cognitive degradation in rats exposed to space radiation. Our lab has been collecting the hind limbs from her studies to investigate the corresponding bone strength changes in these rats. Neurobehavioral assessments include odor recognition memory tests and sustained attention tests. A multi-length scale approach will be performed to assess the corresponding bone health changes. Bone health assessments include microstructural (microCT scans), material property (micro- 
indentation), and whole bone (3-point bending) evaluations of bone strength. Analysis will be performed on CNS and bone endpoint measurements determine whether the neurobehavioral deficits are predictive of declines in bone strength. 

The work is significant to NASA's goal for astronaut health during long duration spaceflight. Establishing a relationship between the CNS and bone response to radiation can provide valuable information for potential mechanisms and countermeasure targets for both systems. This work addresses the following risks and gaps outlined in the NASA HRP HRR Roadmap: Risk of Acute (In-flight) and Late Central Nervous System Effects from Radiation Exposure (CBS-CNS-3, CBS-CNS-5) Risk of Early Onset Osteoporosis Due to Spaceflight (Osteo-4).
approach: The present proposal will directly address these gap areas by leveraging bone tissues from rats exposed to space-relevant acute and protracted galactic cosmic radiation exposures from which multiple neurobehavioral assessments have been acquired in the same animal at different time points following radiation exposure. If radiation-induced changes in neurobehavior and bone are related, future work will focus on understanding the underlying mechanisms of this relationship. Since bone has been found to have endocrine, as well as extra- skeletal effects, in future work, we anticipate identifying bone-related targets and/or pathways that could be used to develop novel countermeasures for acute and/or late CNS effects of space radiation exposure. Finally, a secondary effect of these novel countermeasures could be the protection of bone from radiation-induced damage, and this protection could also have an indirect positive impact on CNS health and function following radiation exposure.
disease_areas: Cancer; Neurological diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases; Mental health

title: Exposure to 33-Particle Galactic Cosmic Radiation (GCR): Negative Impact on Translationally-relevant Hippocampal-based Cognition and Prevention of GCR-induced Deficits by Acute Treatment with the Anti-inflammatory CDDO-EA
objectives:
Galactic Cosmic Radiation (GCR) is an unavoidable factor in deep space that may affect astronaut health and mission success. To date, ground-based studies on the cognitive impact of GCR have exposed rodents to a single high-energy particle or a combination of a few particles. It is unknown how a complex, multiple-ion GCR spectrum - as will be encountered during a Mars mission - influences rodent cognition. It is also important to identify countermeasures that can reduce risk to crew health or performance. Preclinical and clinical studies suggest the anti-inflammatory compound CDDO-EA mediates its therapeutic effects in combating lung disease by upregulating the key antioxidant transcription factor Nrf2. Notably, in mouse models of neurological disorders, CDDO-EA ameliorates striatal- and hippocampal-dependent cognitive and cellular deficits. Thus, it is timely and urgent to assess if a complex GCR spectrum decreases rodent cognition and if CDDO-EA mitigates such changes.

Hypothesis: Exposure to a complex GCR mixture will decrease translationally-relevant cognitive function and negatively-impact associated cellular and molecular processes in female and male mice, and CDDO-EA will mitigate these changes.

Aim 1. We will determine the effects of (1a) 33-particle GCR (33-GCR) on hippocampal dentate gyrus (DG) function (pattern separation) and prefrontal cortex (PFC) functions (cognitive flexibility, extinction of stimulus-response) in 6-month-old mature male C57BL/6J mice and (1b) CDDO-EA on 33-GCR-induced cognitive changes.

Aim 2. We will also define DG neurogenesis and DG and PFC inflammation in female and male mice exposed to 33-GCR or Sham with and without CDDO-EA.
approach:
Mature C57BL/6J male and female mice will receive CDDO-EA (400 mg/kg, n=80) or Control Chow (Control; n=80) for 5 days (3 days before, during, and 1 day after 33-GCR). Mice will receive either Sham or whole-body 33-GCR (~73% 1H, 18% 4He, 2.4% 12C, 3.1% 16O, 1.6% 28Si, 0.9% 48Ti, 0.8% 56Fe; 750mGy) with the 4 groups being Control/Sham, Control/33-GCR, CDDOEA/Sham, CDDO-EA/33-GCR (n=16/group). To complement pilot data with female mice, in Aim 1, 2-mon post-irradiation male mice will undergo general touchscreen training, location discrimination (LD), and acquisition and extinction of stimulus-response. In Aim
2, brains will be collected from male and female mice at three timepoints post-33-GCR: 3 days, 2-mon, and 11-mon. 2- and 11-mon groups will be given bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU, i.p. 180 mg/kg) 2 hrs prior to perfusion and indices of neurogenesis and inflammation will be quantified via unbiased stereology. In the 3-day group, protein expression of Nrf2/ARE signaling targets will be quantified in the DG and PFC.

Aim 1 deliverables include measures of LD (session length, # trials, % correct, etc.) and acquisition and extinction (session length, # responses, # omissions, days to completion) in male mice, and the impact of 33-GCR vs. Sham and CDDO-EA vs. Control Chow on these measures.

Aim 2 deliverables include indices of DG neurogenesis (# of Ki67-, DCX-, BrdU-immunoreactive [+] cells) and DG and PFC inflammation (# of Iba-1+, GFAP+ cells), and protein expression of signaling mediators downstream from Nrf2/ARE.
disease_areas: Neurological diseases; Women's health; Mental health

title: The Effects of Microgravity on Tendon Mechanical Properties
objectives: Little data exists on the mechanical properties of tendons exposed to microgravity in vivo. Therefore, the purpose this study is to investigate potential changes in mouse gastrocnemius and quadricep tendon mechanical properties after 34 days of spaceflight measured by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM). We hypothesize that Young's Modulus (YM) and maximal stress (Smax) will significantly decrease after 34 days of spaceflight in gastrocnemius and quadriceps tendon. The findings from our proposed study will contribute to the body of knowledge investigating the effects of microgravity on the musculoskeletal system, and ultimately help identify appropriate countermeasures for Astronauts. In addition to investigating the changes in tendon mechanical properties, we will store the remaining muscle tissue at -80ºC for a later study. The remaining myotendinous junction (MTJ) will be utilized to investigate changes in transcriptomics between ground base controls and spaceflight tissue. The changes in the MTJ transcriptomics will be compared to whole muscle samples from the GeneLab Data System (GLDS-101) (Galazka and Globus, 2017).
approach: The protocol to be used for AFM tendon tissue analysis is described by Kammoun et al. (2019). The AFM measurements from Kammoun et al. (2019) were validated with MRI Texture Analysis (MRI-TA) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), which ensures that this protocol will yield the desired results (accurate and reliable measures of YM and Smax). Prior to performing the analysis on the flight tissue, lab procedures will be established with pilot tissue to ensure the methodology is solidified. Protocols from GLDS-101 will be adopted to investigate changes in the MTJ transcriptomics.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Microgravity Analogs as Proxies for Spaceflight to Validate Biofilm Formation and the Exchange of Genes Between Organisms
objectives:
The objective of the study is to compare how well popular simulated microgravity devices used on Earth mimic the bacterial response during spaceflight, and whether these SMG devices would be an acceptable proxy for flight experiments.

Microbial adaptation to space is an important area to study as it provides information on how microbes on spacecraft and the microbiome of astronauts will change when exposed to the space environment and how that may impact spacecraft integrity and function, and the health of astronauts. However, due to time and cost constraints of conducting studies on the International Space Station (ISS), scientists are not able to perform as many insightful space biology studies as they would like. For this reason, scientists use microgravity simulators to conduct space biology studies, but on Earth. However, there are very limited studies testing how these simulators compare to the "real" thing and the jury is still out on whether microgravity simulators are actually comparable to experiments performed on the ISS. For this reason, we wish to carry out a study that looks at how three simulated microgravity chambers, commonly used on the ground, compare to what would be observed on the ISS. This study would have a ground and a flight component and would look at two bacterial properties that are significant for short and long duration space missions--biofilm formation and the exchange of genetic material between bacteria.
approach: NASA's Space Biology Program has initiated a project called "Gravity as a Continuum" to promote research across the gravity spectrum to help NASA prepare for long duration spaceflights beyond low Earth orbit. The use of these microgravity analogs on Earth will expand research and our knowledge into how microbes adapt to the space environment, a high priority for NASA as outlined in the Decadal survey. While the commonly used microgravity simulators are considered reliable tools in microgravity research, there are discrepancies between the effects observed in spaceflight and with these Earth based analogs (reviewed in (12)). Since there have not yet been any well controlled studies comparing the biological effects in space to those with simulated microgravity devices on Earth, we really do not know how well these analogs mimic what occurs during spaceflight and if they truly can be used as proxies for spaceflight. The MAPS study aims to address this void in the field with a microgravity validation experiment. If this study shows results to be comparable, more microgravity research can be conducted on Earth and not have to be prioritized for a spaceflight experiment. If the results are not comparable the large amount of data collected from the study will help elucidate where differences lie.

title: Spatiotemporal Mapping of the Impact of Spaceflight on the Heart and Brain
objectives:
Taskbook description:

To prepare for future human exploration missions far from Earth, NASA's Space Biology Program is seeking to build a better understanding of the effects of spaceflight and zero gravity on the biology of microorganisms, plants, and animals in spacecraft, the International Space Station (ISS), and also in ground-based analog experiments. The National Research Council recommended that NASA undergo studies to elucidate the effects of short and long duration spaceflight on the biology of all three categories of organisms. Technological advances in next-generation sequencing (NGS), spatial transcriptomics, and proteomics (spatial omics), several of which we describe below, create an unprecedented opportunity for in-depth molecular studies applicable to the purposes of NASA's Space Biology Program. This provides scientists, engineers, and clinicians a more comprehensive view of the functional dynamics of organisms as they evolve and respond to unique or highly selective environments including the ISS.

Spaceflight causes changes in cell signaling pathways that are better understood only by increasing the analysis resolution level. In this project, we will deploy new technologies, i.e. spatial transcriptomics, single-nucleus RNA-sequencing, multi-omic spatial mapping (human and microbial), and systems biology algorithms to discover new insights relevant to the impact of spaceflight on human health. These data and methods will shed light on the complex biosystem dynamics that spaceflight causes in humans. We will be able to clearly dissect the gene expression changes occurring at the single-cell level, analyze how these changes affect the cell-cell genetic and physical interactions, and begin the first-ever in vivo human-microbial interaction maps from spaceflight. To do so we will conduct rigorous and cutting-edge omics analysis using two complementary platforms (10x Genomics Visium and Nanostring's GeoMx) with six main rodent organs collected throughout several past spaceflight missions and their corresponding ground controls. Our integrated biology approach will allow us to understand physiological, anatomic, and molecular mechanisms of adaptation and response in animals to spaceflight.
approach: For our organism-wide study we will leverage the extensive amount of samples collected throughout several Rodent Research (RR) missions which are accessible through the Life Sciences Data Repository (LSDA). Several of these specimens have already been allocated for Dr. Mason through LSDA. Our study will represent the first-of-its-kind in space biology and will provide foundational discoveries that will allow us to understand not only how astronaut conditions can be improved during spaceflight, but also how the changes induced by spaceflight can be translated into modern medicine to improve human health on Earth. Moreover, we will apply several statistical and machine learning techniques in order to predict changes induced by spaceflight at the organism level for future long-term missions.

title: High-Resolution, Murine Spatial Transcriptome Mapping of the Impact of Spaceflight (Postdoctoral Fellowship)
objectives:
Some of the greatest gaps in knowledge regarding the risks of long-duration spaceflight are the effects of spaceflight on the human body and the resulting health consequences. One way to predict the physiological consequences of long-duration spaceflight is to observe the cellular and molecular changes that occur in mammalian tissues (e.g., mouse models) and extrapolate the extended consequences using our knowledge of diseases that cause similar genomic changes. Previous NASA experiments have investigated this work by performing bulk RNA-sequencing on whole mouse tissues. However, these experiments do not have high enough resolution to adequately understand the impact of spaceflight on gene expression, especially on complex tissues. Our proposed experiments will enhance our understanding of spaceflight-induced gene expression changes by performing single-cell RNA-sequencing and spatial RNA-sequencing on mouse tissues from spaceflight experiments. These methods will allow us to determine gene expression changes within each cell sub-type, as opposed to the tissue level, and will provide us with spatial information to determine the uniformity of gene expression changes across tissues. Tissues will be obtained from the Life Sciences Data Archive (LSDA) at NASA and from previous rodent reference missions at the International Space Station (ISS) National Labs. This project will focus on tissues composed of multiple cell types and tissues with complex spatial organization, such as the brain and retina. 

The project is divided into the following aims:

Aim 1) Data Generation: Single cell sequencing with the Chromium system (10X Genomics);

Aim 2) Data Generation: Spatial sequencing with the Visium system (10X genomics); and

Aim 3) Data analysis: Analyze and interpret the data generated in aims 1 and 2 to determine differentially expressed genes at the cell type level and uniformity of differential gene expression spatially across tissues using a combination of existing software packages and comparison to the GeneLab repository.
approach:
Aim 1: Single-cell sequencing of retinal tissue from the RR-6 mission with the 10X Chromium System.
Retinal tissue will be dissociated into single cells and polyadenylated mRNA from each cell will receive a unique genomic barcode. This will be performed using the Gel Beads-in-Emulsion (GEMs) technology in the Chromium System from 10X genomics. Post-sequencing analysis will use this barcode to assign mRNA sequences to its cell of origin.

Aim 2: Spatial Transcriptomics of RR-6 retinal tissue with the 10X Visium Technology.
Tissues will be cryosectioned into thin slices and placed on a Visium slide (Figure 4), which is commercially available from 10xGenomics and is fully supported by the Mason lab's resources (we have our own 10x instrument and so does our Core facility). Once on the slide, the cells of the tissue will be permeabilized to release and bind mRNA to nearby barcoded oligos. Post sequencing, he barcodes will be used to identify the spatial location of each mRNA sequence. Tissue optimization will be performed before we use the samples from spaceflight.

Aim 3: Data analysis and interpretation to determine gene expression at high resolution.
Differentially expressed genes will be quantified between cell-types using the results from single cell sequencing in aim 1 and between tissue locations using the results from the spatial transcriptomics in aim 2. In order to reconstruct gene expression in the original 3-dimensional tissue, single-cell data will be projected onto the spatial data in order to further quantify differences in cell-type subpopulations.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Novel Explorations Into the Interactions Between Light and Gravity Sensing in Plants - Seedling Growth-3
objectives:
The major goals of this project are to determine how gravity and light responses influence each other in plants and to better understand the cellular signaling mechanisms involved in plant tropisms. This proposed project builds on our previous spaceflight experiments on the ISS (TROPI-1, 2006; TROPI-2, 2010) with the EMCS using the model plant Arabidopsis. In this new proposed project, we plan to confirm and extend our discovery during the recent TROPI-2 ISS experiment of a novel red-light-based positive phototropic response in plant shoots. 

Thus, the specific objectives of this proposed research are: (1) To confirm and characterize the novel red light-dependent phototropic response in flowering plants. (2) To investigate the relationship between light and gravity by measuring thresholds in fractional gravity. (3) To determine whether the red light effect on blue-light-based phototropism is a direct or indirect effect.

Seedling Growth 1: Phototropism
Seedling Growth 2: Tropisms, Auxin, and Cell Culture
Seedling Growth 3: Proliferating Cell Structure
Seedling Growth 4: Synthesis/Experiment Contingency
approach:
The Seedling Growth series of experiments (1, 2, 3 and possibly 4) is supported collaboratively by NASA and the European Space Agency (ESA). The experiment will be conducted in the European Modular Cultivation System (EMCS) on the International Space Station (ISS).  The EMCS is operated by ESA and is located in the Columbus Module of the ISS.

The combined experiments will be divided into four flight payloads in order to maximize the science return from the original proposals. The experiments are to be carried out with 3+1 flights in series. The first three flight experiments are designed to accomplish specific aims enumerated in the two NASA and ESA approved proposals. The last flight experiment is intended to allow a final flight to confirm and/or extend the findings from the first three.

These experiments can be accomplished with telemetric science and therefore fit with the extremely limited down mass capabilities of current spaceflight opportunities. The EMCS is an automated facility and can be operated largely with telemetry. Experimental containers will be launched via Space-X, and during an experimental run, images of seedling growth responses can be downlinked to Earth. Seedlings will be returned to Earth in the GLACIER freezer.
results:
Summary of Major Results:

 Project 1: Phototropic curvature

Utilizing the European Modular Cultivation System (EMCS) on board the International Space Station (ISS), we investigated the interaction between phototropic and gravitropic responses in three Arabidopsis thaliana genotypes, Landsberg wild type, as well as mutants of phytochrome A and phytochrome B. Onboard centrifuges were used to create a fractional gravity gradient ranging from reduced gravity up to 1 g. A novel positive blue-light phototropic response of roots was observed during conditions of microgravity, and this response was attenuated at 0.1 g. In addition, a red-light pretreatment of plants enhanced the magnitude of positive phototropic curvature of roots in response to blue illumination. Additionally, a positive phototropic response of roots was observed when exposed to red light, and a decrease in response was gradual and correlated with the increase in gravity. The positive red-light phototropic curvature of hypocotyls when exposed to red light was also confirmed. Both red-light and blue-light phototropic responses were also shown to be a product of directional light intensity. To our knowledge, this is the first characterization of a positive blue-light phototropic response in Arabidopsis roots, as well as the description of the relationship between these phototropic responses in fractional or reduced gravity.

Project 2: Gene Profiling

The transcriptome of seedlings were analyzed from experiments performed on the International Space Station (ISS) to study the interacting effects of light and gravity on plant tropisms. Seeds of Arabidopsis thaliana were germinated and grown in cassettes in the European Modular Cultivation System (EMCS) for 4 days at 1g then exposed to a range of gravitational accelerations (microgravity, 0.07g, 0.13g, 0.21g, 0.39g, 0.53g, and 1g) and light treatments (blue light with or without a 1 hour pretreatment with red light). At the end of the space experiments, the cassettes containing the seedlings were frozen in the Minus Eighty Laboratory Freezer (MELFI) and returned to Earth on space shuttle STS-131. The RNA was extracted from whole seedlings and used for the transcriptome analyses. A comparison of 1g-treated-spaceflight samples with ground controls identified 230 genes that were differentially regulated at least two-fold (p < 0.01). A further comparison of all spaceflight samples with ground controls identified approximately 280 genes that were differentially regulated at least two fold (p < 0.01). Of these genes, several were involved in regulating cell polarity (i.e., auxin, calcium, lipid metabolism), cell wall development, oxygen status, and cell defense or stress. However, when the transcriptome of the all g-treated spaceflight samples were compared with microgravity samples, only approx.130 genes were identified as being differently regulated (p < 0.01). Of these, only 27 were at least 2 fold differently regulated between microgravity and 1g samples and included putatitive/pseudo/undefined genes (n=14), transposable elements (n=5), an expansin (ATEXP24; At1g21240), a cell-wall kinase (WAK3; At1g21240), a laccase-like flavonoid oxidase (TT10;At5g48100), among others. Not surprisingly, there was a difference in the % of genes represented in individual gene categories when comparing spaceflight samples with ground controls and spaceflight microgravity versus spaceflight g-treated space samples. These results emphasize the need for tissue fixation on the centrifuge for an accurate spaceflight control. These results are one of the few reports of transcriptome analyses from a space experiment with plants that had gravity treatments using an on-board centrifuge.

Thaliana Seedlings in a Reduced Gravity Environment Is Enhanced by Red-Light Photostimulation

title: Rodent spine deconditioning after 30 days of microgravity
objectives:
The vertebral bodies and flexible intervertebral discs of the spine are important, weight-bearing tissues that are adapted to gravitational stress. The absence of gravitational axial loads on the body during exposure to microgravity may disrupt normal spine physiology and function. During microgravity exposure, deconditioning of the intervertebral discs and spinal tissues poses a serious risk upon re-exposure to 1-G upright posture and exercise. Our recent data in identical twins exposed to 30-days bed rest indicate that compressibility increases in the lumbar spine and its intervertebral discs during 30-days simulated microgravity. Because intervertebral discs become more collapsible during simulated microgravity, the probability of disc prolapse or herniation upon re-exposure to gravity increases.  

We hypothesize that nucleus pulposus swelling pressures in mouse spines will significantly decrease following 30-days of microgravity. In addition, we hypothesize that lack of gravitational loading on lumbar discs during microgravity will lead to extracellular matrix damage and significantly reduced disc compression performance following 30-days of microgravity. Our research will aid understanding of spinal deconditioning during 30-days microgravity and of the higher incidence of disc prolapse or herniation following re-exposure to 1-G with a long-term view to prevent such spinal deconditioning with exercise or other physiologic countermeasures.

Separately, evidence suggests headward fluid shifts alone influence bone alteration. However, most conclusions are drawn from human bed rest studies and hindlimb unloading of rats aimed to simulate actual weightlessness. As a member of NASA's Biospecimen Sharing Program, our lab acquired murine calvariae from a 15-day mission aboard Space Shuttle STS-131. In concordance with current theory, we hypothesized that compared to ground control mice, headward fluid shifts in mice exposed to microgravity will induce adaptive growth in bones that normally do not bear weight.
approach:
To test our hypotheses, we will harvest and test spines from ground-based control mice and flight mice exposed to 15 and 30-days of actual microgravity on STS-131 and Bion-M1, respectively, and participate in a tissue sharing opportunity to initiate a new NASA project to study spines and calvaria from rats exposed to 90-days hind-limb suspension. Spines will be frozen and transported to UCSD and UCSF for accepted methods of testing nucleus pulposus swelling pressure, lumbar disc dimensions, proteoglycan content, and spine biomechanics. We will compare the results of the present study to our peer-reviewed and published results for identical twins exposed to 30-days simulated microgravity and control mice exposed to 1-G. 

1. We examined the effect that microgravity poses on biomechanical properties of lumbar and caudal discs from mice on 15 day shuttle mission STS-131. Sixteen mice (space flight group, n=8; control group, n=8) were sacrificed immediately after space flight. 

2. We also hypothesized that the biomechanical properties of the intervertebral disc deteriorate during space flight but then recover after acclimation to normal gravity. To test this hypothesis, we compared the compressive creep properties of caudal discs after return from a 13-day Shuttle mission (STS-133) to those of ground-based control mice. Six flight and ten control mice were sacrificed +1 day, +5 days, or +7 days after the landing of STS-133. 

3. Space flight Decreases Bending Strength and Alters Failure Mode in Murine Spinal Segments. The 14 animals used for this study were obtained through NASA's Biospecimen Sharing Program (BSP). Six mice were sent on a 30-day microgravity Bion M1 mission and acclimated to 1-G for 12 hours before they were sacrificed, while eight mice remained on the ground for the 30 days in standard vivarium cages. BSP personnel sacrificed mice and dissected and flash froze tissues. We received isolated frozen tails from the six post-space flight mice (flight, n=6) and eight ground vivarium controls (control, n=8).
results:
We completed a well-documented and validated battery of histological, biochemical, and biomechanical measures, developed and validated to identify and quantify spinal features for mice during space flight, specifically: morphology and kinematics of spines (including the vertebrae, intervertebral discs, spinal cord, and spinal muscles) after 15 and 30 days of actual microgravity.  Prolonged exposure to microgravity causes degeneration of the human spine, indicated by low back pain during space flight and increased incidence of post-space flight herniated nucleus pulposus. 

1. For caudal discs, the space flight group exhibited a 32% (p=0.012) lower physiologic disc height and 70% (p=0.02) lower strain dependence compared to the control mice. Decreases in both disc height and strain dependence indicate disc degeneration and injurious effect of microgravity. By contrast, differences were not noted for lumbar discs. 

2. Compared to controls, space flight mice had 12% lower disc height and 21% lower strain-dependence on swelling pressure. In addition, biomechanical properties did not recover significantly over the 7-day post-flight period. Overall, biomechanical properties of the caudal discs were diminished by space flight, consistent with observations that prolonged exposure to microgravity increases disc herniation risk. These properties did not recover after short-term (7-days) re-acclimation to 1-G loading. 

3. Space flight reduced bending strength by 17% (6.27 ± 0.94 vs. 7.53 ± 1.13 N, p < 0.05) and reduced toe region by 32% (0.53 ± 0.13 vs. 0.77 ± 0.21 mm, p < 0.05) with no significant effect on bending stiffness (p > 0.25). Disc height was reduced by 16% in flight mice (0.25 ± 0.03 vs. 0.29 ± 0.04 mm, p = 0.054).

The two modes of failure identified by histology were: separation within the growth plate; and annulus avulsion at the disc-vertebra junction. Post-flight mouse spinal segments tended to fail within the growth plate while control spinal segments tended to fail at the disc-vertebra junction. Polarized light microscopy showed annulus collagen fibers inserting into the cartilage endplate but not continuing through the endplate to the subchondral bone. 

The reduction in bending strength following space flight supports our hypothesis that reduced bending properties may be an important factor for explaining microgravity-associated increased herniation risk. The reduction in toe region is consistent with reports of decreased range of motion in astronauts, and the shortened disc height is consistent with prior studies investigating post-flight mice [2]. All specimens failed at soft-hard tissue interfaces between cartilage and bone, presumably due to stress concentrations caused by abrupt changes in material properties at these junctions. The increased likelihood of post-flight mice to fail at the growth plate before the disc-vertebra junction suggests a decrease in growth plate integrity, and may explain the reduction in bending strength without a corresponding reduction in stiffness.

A limitation of animal models used in shuttle missions is the lack of clarity on whether post-flight changes are caused by the same mechanisms as in human astronauts. Microgravity likely has different effects on murine spines due to anatomical and physiological differences. The growth plate separations observed in this study are not relevant to adult humans, but may provide insight for mechanisms of vertebral growth plate fractures observed in children before growth plate closure. Interestingly, mice have been observed using their tails to move about inflight enclosures [2], which may be a confounding variable in accelerating disc degeneration. It should also be noted that the flight mice in this study acclimated to 1-G for 12 hours before they were sacrificed, and the effects on tissue properties during this period are unknown. Despite these limitations, this study provides the first analysis of bending properties of intervertebral discs following space flight and provides insight into possible mechanisms for increased herniation risk following space flight. 

No data submitted. Summarized and analyzed data may be available through publications.

title: Integration of in-silico and in-vivo models for determining preclinical indicators and/or integrated biomarkers of radiation-induced vascular dysfunction
objectives:
The overall goal of this proposal is to develop and validate a novel ground-based model that will more accurately predict short- and long-term degenerative tissue effects, with an initial focus on the cardiovascular system. For this purpose, we first propose the use of a novel radiation moderator block that we believe generates an organ radiation dose distribution that more closely replicates that encountered by humans in deep space vehicles. Following in silico verification of
organ dose distributions, we will use the block to expose female and male mice of astronaut equivalent age to the novel space radiation analog, administering various ions simultaneously, at relevant organ dose distributions and low dose rates. To account for other space-relevant environmental factors, the radiation exposures will be performed in combination with simulated microgravity. To validate this model, both mice and human organs-on-a-chip will be exposed to
radiation doses relevant to 6-8 or 10-12 months stays at the ISS or the Mars mission; these doses were selected in anticipation of future projects where we plan to compare results from the animal model and organ-on-a-chip to equivalent data from astronauts on various space missions. At various time points post-exposure, analysis of mouse plasma markers, epigenetic changes in blood and tissue, and indicators of endothelial function will be performed and results compared to those
from organs-on-a-chip that model the human vasculature. Animals and organs-on-a-chip also will be exposed to γ-rays to assess relative biological effectiveness (RBE) values.

This work will be completed in the following Specific Aims:
1. Replicate the relative organ dose distribution seen in humans exposed to radiation in deep space vehicles in an appropriate mammalian model(s).

2. Identify functional and cellular indicators of cardiovascular function in a mouse model of space-relevant radiation exposures and determine their translatable value in human organs-on-chip.

3. Characterize short- and long-term radiation-induced epigenetic changes in blood and tissue.

title: RR-20: Static Bone Properties and Estrogen Receptor Expression
objectives: Determine skeletal response to spaceflight in the axial and appendicular skeleton.
approach: The tissues requested relate to and complement, the original request and science plan to analyze static bone properties and estrogen receptor expression in the right and left femur. Tibia and vertebrae (lumbar and sacral) are being requested to assess new and related hypotheses relating to bone loss during spaceflight.  Specifically, the right and left tibia will be used to assess changes in vasculature phenotypes within the skeletal system to assess the marrow-blood barrier status and osteocalcin expression within the bone cells, which is a potent endocrine signal originating within the bone. Thus, knowledge on tissue-level effects and signaling will be advanced.
results: Results pending.

title: HZE Particle Exposure-Induced Improvement of Pattern Separation in Mature Mice: Alterations in Mission-Relevant Behaviors and Neural Circuitry
objectives: An unavoidable consequence of deep space missions is exposure to galactic cosmic radiation (GCR), which includes high (H) atomic number (Z) and energy (E) particles particles like Fe, Si, and O. Estimating radiation risks to the central nervous system (CNS) by HZE particles encountered during space missions is a high research priority. Past research has shown that rodents exposed to HZE particles have cognitive and performance deficits in numerous behavioral tasks, including those that rely on the hippocampus, a brain region involved in learning and memory. Notably, we have found that mature mice (of equivalent age to astronauts) exposed to either Si or Fe HZE particles actually show improved performance on a very difficult hippocampal task to assess the ability to discrimination two contexts that differ in discrete ways. Here we propose three aims to understand this improved context “pattern separation” after HZE particle exposure.
approach:
In Aim 1, we hypothesize that HZE particle exposure-induced improved pattern separation is linked to improved performance on related learning and memory tasks, as well as executive function tasks, in the short-term, but to decreased performance in the long-term. 

In Aim 2, we hypothesize that HZE particle exposure-induced improved pattern separation is linked in the short-term to diminished stress-induced emergence of anxiety and depression-like behaviors, but to greater emergence in the long-term. 

In Aim 3, we hypothesize that HZE particle exposure-induced improved pattern separation is associated with disrupted hippocampal-cortical neural networks. All aims will rely on both classic and cutting-edge techniques. In sum, these aims will address whether the HZE particle exposure-induced improvement in pattern separation is beneficial or detrimental to mission success (Aims 1, 2), will indicate the integrity of neural circuitry contributing to mission-relevant behaviors (Aim 3), and will define both the short- and long-term health of neural networks needed to complete deep space missions.
results:
As of January 2022:

We have published that mice irradiated at “astronaut-age” show improved hippocampal-dependent performance in an aversive task (context discrimination fear conditioning, CDFC). Notably, we have found that mature mice (of equivalent age to astronauts) exposed to either 28Si or 56Fe HZE particle irradiation (IRR) actually show improved performance on a difficult hippocampal task (discrimination of 2 similar contexts) without influencing performance on an easier hippocampal task or other behaviors. For NASA Space Research Laboratory (NSRL)16B, we found that males have improved pattern separation (location discrimination, LD). This extended our findings on IRR-induced improvement in pattern separation from an aversive task (CDFC) to an appetitive task (LD).

These ground-based studies on the cognitive impact of space radiation show male rodents exposed to a single high-energy particle unexpectedly have enhanced discrimination learning with similar stimuli. However, far less work has been done in female rodents. It was specifically unknown how space radiation influences cognition of female rodents when assessed via touchscreen testing, a translationally-relevant approach that reveals integrity of many cognitive domains. In mice run in NSRL17B and 18A, we showed mature female mice exposed to a mission-relevant dose of a space radiation particle have a) improved performance on a hippocampal-dependent task, b) no change in performance on a prefrontal cortex-dependent task, and c) impaired performance on a striatum-dependent behavior task. These data support the idea of competition between hippocampal and striatal memory systems, and fill a key knowledge gap on the influence of space radiation on female cognition.

title: S. mutans HARV competence assays
objectives:
This record captures data related to Dr. Rice's "Investigation of Microgravity Effects on Physiology, Global Gene Expression, and Biofilm Phenotypes of the Caries-Causing Bacterium Streptococcus mutans" experiment. 

Related records include:
OS-111 S. mutans HARV growth assays (planktonic)
OS-112 S. mutans HARV competence assays
OS-113 S. mutans HARV biofilm and microscopy
OS-114 S. mutans HARV oxidative stress assays
OS-115 S. mutans HARV metabolic assays
OS-116 S. mutans RNAseq experiment
results:
Dataset represents the HARV competence assays in the form of two graphs. Detailed data descriptions can be found below and included with each figure legend. Treatment details follows the dataset description.

Figure 1- S. mutans competence phenotype assessed in simulated microgravity and normal gravity HARV cultures. Simulated microgravity and normal gravity HARV cultures, as well as a parallel static Falcon tube control culture, were each grown for 1.5 hours in biofilm media prior to addition of 2 μg pOri23 plasmid DNA. All three cultures were then grown or 2.5 more hours followed by harvest and serial dilution plating on BHI agar plus/minus 10 μg/ml erythromycin (selective antibiotic to monitor plasmid transformation). Transformation efficiency was calculated as the percentage of transformants (CFU/ml on BHI plus erythromycin) among total viable cells (CFU/ml on BHI). Data represent the average of n equals 3 independent experiments. Error bars equals SEM. 

Figure 2-  Quantitative competence assay. S. mutans HARV cultures were grown for 1.5 hours in BHI containing 5 percent horse serum prior to addition of 2 μg plasmid DNA. HARV cultures were then grown for 2.5 more hours followed by harvest and serial dilution plating on BHI agar plus/minus selective antibiotic. Transformation efficiency was calculated as calculated as the percentage of transformants (CFU/ml on BHI plus selective antibiotic) among total viable cells (CFU/ml on BHI). Data represents the average of n equals 3 independent experiments. Error bars equals SEM.

Treatment simulated normal gravity and simulated microgravity HARV (10 ml size) growth, 37C, 5 percent CO2, 30 RPM in Brain Heart Infusion (BHI) broth or Biofilm media containing glucose and sucrose (as indicated in graph figure legends). HARVS were removed from rotator bases after 90 minutes growth, and 2 µg of unmethylated plasmid DNA was added to each reactor. Microgravity and normal gravity HARVs were then incubated for 2.5 h prior to serial dilution and plating of each culture on BHI agar plus/minus10 µg/ml erythromycin (Erm; selectable antibiotic for plasmid). CFU/ml were determined after 48 h plate growth, and transformation efficiencies were calculated as the percentage of transformants (BHI-Erm CFU/ml) among the total viable cells (BHI CFU/ml). For each biofilm media experiment, a non-rotating control culture (15- ml Falcon tube containing 15 ml of S. mutans culture) was grown in parallel and assessed for competence as described above.

title: S. mutans HARV growth asssays (planktonic)
objectives:
This record captures data related to Dr. Rice's "Investigation of Microgravity Effects on Physiology, Global Gene Expression, and Biofilm Phenotypes of the Caries-Causing Bacterium Streptococcus mutans" experiment. 

Related records include:
OS-111 S. mutans HARV growth assays (planktonic)
