OS-114 S. mutans HARV oxidative stress assays
OS-115 S. mutans HARV metabolic assays
OS-116 S. mutans RNAseq experiment
results:
Dataset represents the HARV growth assays in the form of graphs and images. Detailed data descriptions can be found below and included with each figure legend. Treatment details follow the dataset description.

Figure 1: End-point growth curve analysis of S. mutans simulated microgravity and normal gravity HARV cultures. Simulated microgravity (dark grey bars) and normal gravity (light grey bars) HARV end-point growth was measured by serial dilution and CFU/ml plating at t equals 0 (time of inoculation; average of n equals 4 independent experiments), and t equals 4, 6, 8, 12, 24, and 48 hours’ post- inoculation (average of n equals 2 independent experiments per time point per growth condition). The vertical dotted line on the graph indicates that the t equals 24 and 48-hour data were obtained in a separate experimental run from the t equals 4, 6, 8, and 12-hour time points. Error bars equals standard error of the mean (SEM).

Figure 2: Continuous growth curve analysis of S. mutans simulated microgravity and normal gravity HARV cultures. Each simulated microgravity (triangles) and normal gravity (circles) HARV growth curve was performed in biofilm media for 24 hours at 37°C and 5% CO2. HARVs were sampled for serial dilution and CFU/ml determination at t equals 0 (time of inoculation), 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, and 24 hours’ post-inoculation, as described in Materials and Methods. Data represent n equals 3 independent experiments per growth condition, error bars equals standard error of the mean (SEM).

Figure 3: OD600 (A), CFU/ml (B), and pH (C) data corresponding to end-point S. mutans microgravity and normal gravity HARV cultures harvested for metabolomics (t equals 4, 8, and 24 hours’ growth) and for RNA-seq (t equals 8 hours only). Data represent n equals 3 independent experiments per time point per growth condition. Error bars equals standard error of the mean (SEM).

Figure 4: Representative S. mutans HARVs grown for 8 hours, 30 RPM, in BM under either simulated microgravity (Left) or normal gravity (Right) conditions. Note the structural differences of the self-aggregating bacterial cells under each condition (indicated by arrows).

Figure 5: Diagrams of Disposable Vessel Rotary Cell Culture Systems used to simulate microgravity (A; HARV rotated on an axis perpendicular to gravitational vector) and normal gravity (B; HARV rotated on an axis parallel to gravitational vector) growth conditions

For the continuous HARV Growth Curves experiment, overnight S. mutans cultures were used to inoculate 120 ml of sterile BM containing 11 mM glucose and 10 mM sucrose to an OD600 equals 0.06. This inoculum was mixed well by vigorous swirling and vortexing, and 1 ml withdrawn to measure the time-of-inoculation (t equals 0) CFU/ml. The remaining inoculum volume was then subjected again to vigorous swirling and vortexing and immediately used to fill 2 x 50 ml disposable HARVs as described above. Each HARV was rotated either vertically (“simulated microgravity”) or horizontally (“normal gravity”) at 37°C, 5% CO2 for 24 hours. HARVs were rotated at 30 RPM for the first 8 hours of growth, and the rotation speed of each reactor was increased to 32 RPM after 8 hours of growth to ensure that S. mutans cell aggregates did not settle to the bottom of the simulated microgravity HARV. At 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, and 24 hours’ post-inoculation, each HARV was removed from the incubator, vigorously agitated to evenly disperse cell clumps, and 1 ml culture was immediately removed for CFU/ml assessment. The same volume of sterile media was then added back to each HARV and air bubbles removed prior to continued growth. For CFU/ml quantification, each 1 ml sample was vortexed vigorously for at least 30 seconds to resuspend cell clumps, followed immediately by serial dilution (each tube vortexed vigorously for at least 15 seconds between each dilution) and CFU/ml determination using the track plating method.

Treatment for the HARV "End-Point" Growth Curves used overnight S. mutans cultures to inoculate 500 ml sterile BM containing 11 mM glucose and 10 mM sucrose to an approximate OD600 equals 0.06/ml as described above. 1 ml was withdrawn to measure the t equals 0 OD600, pH, and CFU/ml. The remaining inoculum volume was then immediately used to fill 8 x 50 ml disposable HARVs. For end-point growth curve analysis at t equals 4, 6, 8, and 12 hours conditions, 4 simulated microgravity and 4 normal gravity HARV cultures were grown in parallel at 37°C, 5% CO2. HARVs were rotated at 30 RPM for the first 8 hours of growth, and the rotation speed of each reactor was increased to 32 RPM after 8 hours of growth. At each time point, one simulated microgravity and one normal gravity HARV were each harvested for CFU/ml, OD600, and pH measurements

Figure 1: Distribution of gene functional categories differentially expressed during normal gravity growth relative to simulated microgravity. RNA isolated from 8-hour normal gravity and simulated microgravity S. mutans HARV cultures (n equals 3 independent experiments per growth condition) was subjected to RNA-seq transcriptome profiling as described in Materials and Methods. Differential expression and statistical analysis was carried out using a cutoff FDR-adjusted p value of 0.05. Of this dataset, those genes with a log2 (fold-change) value of at least plus/minus 1.0 were grouped by functional classification based on DAVID gene functional categorization analysis, NCBI gene annotations and KEGG pathway analysis. Figure 2: qRT-PCR validation of RNA-seq data. RNA isolated from microgravity and normal gravity S. mutans HARV cultures (n equals 3 each) was converted to cDNA and qRT-PCR was performed using the indicated primers. Amplification of gyrB served as the reference gene. In each case the normal gravity sample was used as the calibrator, and fold change was calculated using the Livak method. *Denotes significant difference relative to normal gravity (T-test, p greater than0.05). **Denotes difference approaching significance relative to normal gravity (T-test, p greater than 0.08). All data represent the average ± SEM.

Figure 3: Metacyc analysis of RNA-seq data. The top three metabolic pathways to which statistically significant differentially- expressed genes matched were mixed-acid fermentation (A), arginine biosynthesis (acetyl cycle) (B), and pyrmidine deoxynucleotide de novo biosynthesis (C). Heat-map indicates log2-fold change in gene expression (normal gravity/simulated microgravity).

For each experiment, three simulated microgravity and three normal gravity HARV cultures were inoculated and grown as described for the metabolomics and end-point growth curve analysis. For each experiment, one simulated microgravity and one normal gravity HARV were each harvested at t equals 8 h. After harvesting (as described for end-point growth curves and metabolomics), a 10-ml aliquot of each HARV culture was centrifuged at 3,000 RPM, 4°C for 20 minutes and immediately placed on ice. Culture supernatants were decanted and 1 ml RNAlater (Ambion) was added to each cell pellet prior to immediate storage at -80°C. RNA was isolated with the RNeasy Kit (Qiagen) and FASTPREP lysing matrix B tubes (MP Biomedical). Each RNA sample was then subjected to a second DNAseq treatment using the TURBO DNA-free™ Kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific) per the manufacturer’s protocols. Lack of contaminating genomic DNA in each RNA sample was then determined using quantitative real-time PCR (i.e. no cDNA synthesis step) and S. mutans-specific primers prior to sending ≥ 5 µg of each RNA sample to Seqwright Genomic Services (Houston, TX), who performed all steps of the RNA-seq workflow, data curation, differential expression and corresponding statistical analysis. In brief, each RNA sample (less or equal to 2.0 purity ration) was subjected to an mRNA enrichment step and generation of a library of cDNA template molecules of known strand origin using the TruSeq Total mRNA Sample Preparation Kit (Illumina). Sequencing was performed using the Illumina HiSeq 2500 platform, generating 2 x 100bp read lengths for a total of 2 x 10M reads per sample. Illumina CASAVA software was used to create genomic builds and count reads, whereas TopHat-2.0.8b and Cufflinks-2.0.1 were used to align reads to the S. mutans UA159 genome (Genbank accession # NC_004350.2) and perform DE analysis, respectively

title: Fos-positive Cells in the Vestibular Nuclei
objectives:
This record captures data related to Dr. Pompeiano's " Effects of Microgravity on Gene Expression in the Brain" experiment. 

The mammalian brain exposed to space flight experiences profound changes in neuronal activity. Immediate early genes (IEGs), such as c-fos and Fos-Related Antigens (FRA), are useful indicators of changes in neuronal activity and plasticity. Within minutes of exposure to neural stimulation, the mRNA levels of these genes increase. The corresponding proteins can be detected for hours (Fos) or days (FRA) afterwards. These proteins may act as messengers to regulate the transcription of target genes involved in the physiological responses and adaptation to mG or readaptation to 1 G. This experiment investigated whether changes in IEG expression, occurring during space flight, affect brain structures involved in the control of: a) somatic (postural and motor) functions; b) vegetative (cardiovascular, respiratory and gastrointestinal) functions; and c) the regulation of the sleep-waking cycle. These changes were investigated to see if they depend on alterations in neuronal activity induced by macular gravity signals and, if so, whether they could be related to or modified during different phases of the sleep-waking cycle.

OS-35 Immunohistochemical Visualization of Fos Protein in the Brain

OS-37 Vestibular Projections Schematic
approach: Flight rats used in this study were part of the integrated adult rat dissections performed on FD 2 and FD 14. Postflight, remaining animals were sacrificed at different days after landing (R+1 and R+13, respectively). Simulated Caged Control (SIM) and vivarium animals were sacrificed on a 48-hour and 96-hour delayed basis. All brain tissue, excepting the cerebellum, was used in this experiment. Adjacent sections of the same brain tissues were alternatively stained with either a Fos or a FRA antibody, to mark both short- and long-lived responses to changes in gravity stimulus. These were characterized by hypergravity followed by exposure to mG at FD 2 after launch, stabilization to mG at FD14, hypergravity followed by exposure to 1 G at R+1 after landing, stabilization to 1 G at R+13. Tissue was removed from the fixative, cryoprotected in 30% sucrose solutions in phosphate buffer (PB) at 4 °C, frozen, and sectioned into 40 micrometer sections with a cryostat. Immunocytochemistry was used to detect Fos and FRA proteins in the forebrain and brainstem. The distribution of labeled cells in each brain region was examined and quantified. The number of labeled cells was compared between flight and SIM groups.
results:
Variable numbers of Fos- and FRA-positive cells were found during different space flight conditions in Medial (MVe) and Spinal Vestibular nuclei (SpVe), which contribute to the vestibulo-ocular and the vestibulo-collic reflex, and in Group F nuclei, which projects to the vestibulocerebellum. In particular, all these structures showed a small increase in Fos and FRA expression at FD2, due to exposure to hypergravity partially compensated by exposure to mG, while a prominent increase of this expression occurred at R+1, due to hypergravity followed by exposure to 1 G. Similar changes in Fos and FRA expression occurred in the Area Postrema, which appears to be involved in the development of motion sickness, in the Nucleus of the Solitary Tract (NST) and the related ventrolateral reticular structures, which intervene in the central and reflex regulation of cardiovascular and respiratory activities, and finally in the Central nucleus of the Amygdala.

An increased level of Fos expression occurred in the noradrenergic Locus Coeruleus nucleus (LC) of flight rats with respect to ground controls, without variations at different time points of the space flight. However, the number of FRA-positive cells selectively increased in the LC of flight animals sacrificed at FD 2 and more prominently at R+1. These differences were attributed to the fact that, while the Fos protein induced by the gravity force returns to basal levels within hours after stimulation, thus being undetected 24 h after launch or the reentry, FRAs persist for days after induction. Since activity of noradrenergic LC neurons has been shown to produce gene expression in several target structures, it is likely that the long-lasting induction of FRAs in the LC at FD 2 and more prominently at R+1 contributes to the molecular changes occurring in the vestibular and the related structures indicated above.

An increase in Fos expression was also observed in the spinal areas of both the Inferior Olive (IO) and the Lateral Reticular nucleus (LRt) at FD 2 and FD 14, i.e., during and after the launch. However, only few labeled cells were observed in these precerebellar nuclei at R+1 and R+13. This last finding was partly attributed to reduced somatosensory and motor activities occurring at these specific time points of the space flight.

In addition, the increase in gravity force at R+1 selectively increased the number of FRA-positive cells in the dorsomedial cell column of the IO (which corresponds to the vestibular area of this structure), while no such changes affected the Fos expression. This increase in gene expression could then be detected by using only a long-lasting (FRA) and not a short-lasting (Fos) marker of neuronal plasticity.

The results of this experiment indicate that the gravity force acts on specific vestibular nuclei and related structures controlling somatic and vegetative functions, and that the noradrenergic LC neurons, driven by the gravity force, may contribute to long-term molecular changes occurring in the brain during adaptation to mG and readaptation to 1 G. Changes in gene expression in forebrain structures, such as the cerebral cortex, were finally used to identify the different phases of the sleep-waking cycle and relate the molecular changes occurring during different time points of the space flight to the animal state.

Data
Fig. 1. - Schematic drawings of frontal sections of the medulla, showing the localization of Fos-positive cells in the vestibular nuclei of both sides in four representative rats of the FLT group sacrificed at FD2, FD14, R+1 and R+13, respectively.

The number at the extreme right corner of each scheme corresponds to the stereotaxic plane of bregma (B) as reported in the atlas of appaxinos and Watson. At FD2 (R3) a discrete number of labeled cells occurred in the MVe and SpVe of both sides at caudal level, while scattered cells where found within the group F (FVe), the Pr and the IN as well as the ECu/X structures. At FD14 (R5) the number of labeled cells was particularly reduced with respect to that obtained at FD2 in all the structures indicated above. At R+1 (R17) there was a prominent and bilateral increase in number of labeled cells in the caudal part of MVe and SpVe (including the group F) and to some extent also in the nuclei ECu/X, but not in the Pr. However, a large number of labeled cells occurred in EF. At R+13 (R19) the number of labeled cells was greatly attenuated in all the structures indicated above, so as to reach the level obtained in rats of the simulated control group (AGC) sacrificed at the same time interval after return of the animal to 1 G. No labeled cells were found in the caudal half of the LVe at any time interval of the flight, as seen at more rostral levels (not shown).

title: Immunohistochemical Visualization of FRA Protein in the Inferior Olive and Locus Coeruleus Nucleus
objectives:
This record captures data related to Dr. Pompeiano's " Effects of Microgravity on Gene Expression in the Brain" experiment. 

The mammalian brain exposed to space flight experiences profound changes in neuronal activity. Immediate early genes (IEGs), such as c-fos and Fos-Related Antigens (FRA), are useful indicators of changes in neuronal activity and plasticity. Within minutes of exposure to neural stimulation, the mRNA levels of these genes increase. The corresponding proteins can be detected for hours (Fos) or days (FRA) afterwards. These proteins may act as messengers to regulate the transcription of target genes involved in the physiological responses and adaptation to mG or readaptation to 1 G. This experiment investigated whether changes in IEG expression, occurring during space flight, affect brain structures involved in the control of: a) somatic (postural and motor) functions; b) vegetative (cardiovascular, respiratory and gastrointestinal) functions; and c) the regulation of the sleep-waking cycle. These changes were investigated to see if they depend on alterations in neuronal activity induced by macular gravity signals and, if so, whether they could be related to or modified during different phases of the sleep-waking cycle.

approach: Flight rats used in this study were part of the integrated adult rat dissections performed on FD 2 and FD 14. Postflight, remaining animals were sacrificed at different days after landing (R+1 and R+13, respectively). Simulated Caged Control (SIM) and vivarium animals were sacrificed on a 48-hour and 96-hour delayed basis. All brain tissue, excepting the cerebellum, was used in this experiment. Adjacent sections of the same brain tissues were alternatively stained with either a Fos or a FRA antibody, to mark both short- and long-lived responses to changes in gravity stimulus. These were characterized by hypergravity followed by exposure to mG at FD 2 after launch, stabilization to mG at FD14, hypergravity followed by exposure to 1 G at R+1 after landing, stabilization to 1 G at R+13. Tissue was removed from the fixative, cryoprotected in 30% sucrose solutions in phosphate buffer (PB) at 4 °C, frozen, and sectioned into 40 micrometer sections with a cryostat. Immunocytochemistry was used to detect Fos and FRA proteins in the forebrain and brainstem. The distribution of labeled cells in each brain region was examined and quantified. The number of labeled cells was compared between flight and SIM groups.
results:
Variable numbers of Fos- and FRA-positive cells were found during different space flight conditions in Medial (MVe) and Spinal Vestibular nuclei (SpVe), which contribute to the vestibulo-ocular and the vestibulo-collic reflex, and in Group F nuclei, which projects to the vestibulocerebellum. In particular, all these structures showed a small increase in Fos and FRA expression at FD2, due to exposure to hypergravity partially compensated by exposure to mG, while a prominent increase of this expression occurred at R+1, due to hypergravity followed by exposure to 1 G. Similar changes in Fos and FRA expression occurred in the Area Postrema, which appears to be involved in the development of motion sickness, in the Nucleus of the Solitary Tract (NST) and the related ventrolateral reticular structures, which intervene in the central and reflex regulation of cardiovascular and respiratory activities, and finally in the Central nucleus of the Amygdala.

An increased level of Fos expression occurred in the noradrenergic Locus Coeruleus nucleus (LC) of flight rats with respect to ground controls, without variations at different time points of the space flight. However, the number of FRA-positive cells selectively increased in the LC of flight animals sacrificed at FD 2 and more prominently at R+1. These differences were attributed to the fact that, while the Fos protein induced by the gravity force returns to basal levels within hours after stimulation, thus being undetected 24 h after launch or the reentry, FRAs persist for days after induction. Since activity of noradrenergic LC neurons has been shown to produce gene expression in several target structures, it is likely that the long-lasting induction of FRAs in the LC at FD 2 and more prominently at R+1 contributes to the molecular changes occurring in the vestibular and the related structures indicated above.

An increase in Fos expression was also observed in the spinal areas of both the Inferior Olive (IO) and the Lateral Reticular nucleus (LRt) at FD 2 and FD 14, i.e., during and after the launch. However, only few labeled cells were observed in these precerebellar nuclei at R+1 and R+13. This last finding was partly attributed to reduced somatosensory and motor activities occurring at these specific time points of the space flight.

In addition, the increase in gravity force at R+1 selectively increased the number of FRA-positive cells in the dorsomedial cell column of the IO (which corresponds to the vestibular area of this structure), while no such changes affected the Fos expression. This increase in gene expression could then be detected by using only a long-lasting (FRA) and not a short-lasting (Fos) marker of neuronal plasticity.

The results of this experiment indicate that the gravity force acts on specific vestibular nuclei and related structures controlling somatic and vegetative functions, and that the noradrenergic LC neurons, driven by the gravity force, may contribute to long-term molecular changes occurring in the brain during adaptation to mG and readaptation to 1 G. Changes in gene expression in forebrain structures, such as the cerebral cortex, were finally used to identify the different phases of the sleep-waking cycle and relate the molecular changes occurring during different time points of the space flight to the animal state.

title: Vestibular Projections Schematic
objectives:
This record captures data related to Dr. Pompeiano's " Effects of Microgravity on Gene Expression in the Brain" experiment. 

The mammalian brain exposed to space flight experiences profound changes in neuronal activity. Immediate early genes (IEGs), such as c-fos and Fos-Related Antigens (FRA), are useful indicators of changes in neuronal activity and plasticity. Within minutes of exposure to neural stimulation, the mRNA levels of these genes increase. The corresponding proteins can be detected for hours (Fos) or days (FRA) afterwards. These proteins may act as messengers to regulate the transcription of target genes involved in the physiological responses and adaptation to mG or readaptation to 1 G. This experiment investigated whether changes in IEG expression, occurring during space flight, affect brain structures involved in the control of: a) somatic (postural and motor) functions; b) vegetative (cardiovascular, respiratory and gastrointestinal) functions; and c) the regulation of the sleep-waking cycle. These changes were investigated to see if they depend on alterations in neuronal activity induced by macular gravity signals and, if so, whether they could be related to or modified during different phases of the sleep-waking cycle.

Related records include:
OS-31 Fos-positive Cells in the Vestibular Nuclei
OS-32 Immunohistochemical Visualization of Fos Protein in the MVe and SpVe
OS-33 Immunohistochemical Visualization of FRA Protein in the Inferior Olive and Locus Coeruleus Nucleus
OS-34 Immunohistochemical Visualization of Fos Protein in the MVe and SpVe: larger image
OS-35 Immunohistochemical Visualization of Fos Protein in the Brain
OS-36 Schematic Drawing of Vestibular and Coerulear Projections
OS-37 Vestibular Projections Schematic
approach: Flight rats used in this study were part of the integrated adult rat dissections performed on FD 2 and FD 14. Postflight, remaining animals were sacrificed at different days after landing (R+1 and R+13, respectively). Simulated Caged Control (SIM) and vivarium animals were sacrificed on a 48-hour and 96-hour delayed basis. All brain tissue, excepting the cerebellum, was used in this experiment. Adjacent sections of the same brain tissues were alternatively stained with either a Fos or a FRA antibody, to mark both short- and long-lived responses to changes in gravity stimulus. These were characterized by hypergravity followed by exposure to mG at FD 2 after launch, stabilization to mG at FD14, hypergravity followed by exposure to 1 G at R+1 after landing, stabilization to 1 G at R+13. Tissue was removed from the fixative, cryoprotected in 30% sucrose solutions in phosphate buffer (PB) at 4 °C, frozen, and sectioned into 40 micrometer sections with a cryostat. Immunocytochemistry was used to detect Fos and FRA proteins in the forebrain and brainstem. The distribution of labeled cells in each brain region was examined and quantified. The number of labeled cells was compared between flight and SIM groups.
results:
Variable numbers of Fos- and FRA-positive cells were found during different space flight conditions in Medial (MVe) and Spinal Vestibular nuclei (SpVe), which contribute to the vestibulo-ocular and the vestibulo-collic reflex, and in Group F nuclei, which projects to the vestibulocerebellum. In particular, all these structures showed a small increase in Fos and FRA expression at FD2, due to exposure to hypergravity partially compensated by exposure to mG, while a prominent increase of this expression occurred at R+1, due to hypergravity followed by exposure to 1 G. Similar changes in Fos and FRA expression occurred in the Area Postrema, which appears to be involved in the development of motion sickness, in the Nucleus of the Solitary Tract (NST) and the related ventrolateral reticular structures, which intervene in the central and reflex regulation of cardiovascular and respiratory activities, and finally in the Central nucleus of the Amygdala.

An increased level of Fos expression occurred in the noradrenergic Locus Coeruleus nucleus (LC) of flight rats with respect to ground controls, without variations at different time points of the space flight. However, the number of FRA-positive cells selectively increased in the LC of flight animals sacrificed at FD 2 and more prominently at R+1. These differences were attributed to the fact that, while the Fos protein induced by the gravity force returns to basal levels within hours after stimulation, thus being undetected 24 h after launch or the reentry, FRAs persist for days after induction. Since activity of noradrenergic LC neurons has been shown to produce gene expression in several target structures, it is likely that the long-lasting induction of FRAs in the LC at FD 2 and more prominently at R+1 contributes to the molecular changes occurring in the vestibular and the related structures indicated above.

An increase in Fos expression was also observed in the spinal areas of both the Inferior Olive (IO) and the Lateral Reticular nucleus (LRt) at FD 2 and FD 14, i.e., during and after the launch. However, only few labeled cells were observed in these precerebellar nuclei at R+1 and R+13. This last finding was partly attributed to reduced somatosensory and motor activities occurring at these specific time points of the space flight.

In addition, the increase in gravity force at R+1 selectively increased the number of FRA-positive cells in the dorsomedial cell column of the IO (which corresponds to the vestibular area of this structure), while no such changes affected the Fos expression. This increase in gene expression could then be detected by using only a long-lasting (FRA) and not a short-lasting (Fos) marker of neuronal plasticity.

The results of this experiment indicate that the gravity force acts on specific vestibular nuclei and related structures controlling somatic and vegetative functions, and that the noradrenergic LC neurons, driven by the gravity force, may contribute to long-term molecular changes occurring in the brain during adaptation to mG and readaptation to 1 G. Changes in gene expression in forebrain structures, such as the cerebral cortex, were finally used to identify the different phases of the sleep-waking cycle and relate the molecular changes occurring during different time points of the space flight to the animal state.

title: Influence of sex hormones on nervous system and musculoskeletal health in micro- and Martian fractional gravity in rat analogues
objectives:
In this postdoctoral fellowship, we propose to evaluate the impact of sex hormones on neuronal and musculoskeletal health in micro and Martian gravity environments by assessing naive and castrated/ovariectomized male and female rats. We hypothesize micro- and fractional gravity will result in differential aberrations to sex hormone status in female and male rats; these sex hormone aberrations will moderate neurological and musculoskeletal declines in micro- and fractional gravity environments. We will study these rats over a 4-week period and investigations will include a series of neurophysiological and functional measures, blood analyses, and a detailed series of post-mortem histological studies. With completion of this work, we will have a far deeper understanding of the
relationship between hormonal status and neuronal and musculoskeletal function. With the completion of this work, I will achieve the following goals:
A. Establish the impact of sex hormones on neurological and musculoskeletal systems in these gravitational analogues.
B. Identify potential sex differences in response to Martian fractional gravity.
C. Determine the influence of micro- and partial- gravity environments on circulating hormonal status in males and females.
approach:
Specific Aim 1: Determine the impact of sex hormones on neurological health during micro- and Mars partial gravity in male and female rats over a 4-week period. Outcome Variables will include: Electrophysiology, Novel Object Recognition, and potentially histological Assessment of components of the neurological system. 

Specific Aim 2: Determine the impact of sex hormones on musculoskeletal health during micro- and Mars partial gravity in male and female rats over a 4-week period. Outcome variables will include: Electrical Impedance Myography, pQCT analysis of bone density, microCT analysis of bone microstructure, body and muscle weight, myofiber cross sectional area, and possibly plasma markers of bone turnover. 

To test these specific aims, I will use hindlimb unloading and partial unloading as our laboratory group has previously validated rat analogs for deep space (0g) and Martian (0.4g) gravitational loads. Male and female rats will be divided into three separate gravitational loads (1g, 0.4g, or 0g). Additionally, sex organs will be removed from half of the animals to investigate the role of sex hormones on neuronal, muscle, and skeletal health across gravitational loads. Longitudinal Outcome variables will be measured before intervention and after 28 days of intervention. Cross sectional outcome variables will be measured after 28 days of intervention.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
disease_areas: Neurological diseases; Women's health; Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases

title: Musculoskeletal Response to a Partial-gravity Analog in Rats: Structural, Functional, and Molecular Alterations
objectives:
Health of the musculoskeletal system depends on exposure to mechanical loading. Removal or reduction of mechanical loading precipitates muscle atrophy and bone loss. Thus, despite rigorous exercise protocols and other countermeasures, muscle atrophy and bone loss are still virtually inevitable consequences of spaceflight. One approach for ameliorating the development of these untoward consequences of space travel is by the induction of artificial gravity during spaceflight via centrifugation. The strength of the artificial gravity depends both on the speed of rotation and diameter of the ship. While it is possible to achieve centrifugal force equivalent 1.0 G, it is also possible that lower levels of force may also be effective for preventing muscle atrophy and irreversible bone loss. In this study, we propose to evaluate the potential effectiveness of artificial gravity for the prevention of muscle atrophy and weakness and bone loss by studying rats using an innovative system for partial unloading that has been developed previously for mice by members of our team. 

This work will also allow us to more effectively understand the relationship between the development of muscle atrophy and weakness and bone deterioration at varying levels of artificial gravity. At the conclusion of this study, we will have defined the potential impact of partial artificial gravity for prevention of musculoskeletal dysfunction and deterioration. This work will inform the future application of artificial gravity in human research and space exploration.
approach: We will study the musculoskeletal response of adult male rats (n=14 / group) following exposure to different degrees of unloading representing 0.2 G, 0.4 G, and 0.7 G as well as fully loaded animals for 1, 2, and 4 weeks. We will study the impact of this partial gravity analog environment on muscle and bone structure and function, including performing histological analyses, in situ force measurement of muscle contraction, as well as measurement of bone mass, microarchitecture, and strength. In addition, we will perform both serological and tissue molecular analyses to better understand the mechanisms and time course of development of these changes at different degrees of unloading.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of this study.

title: STS-106 Plate Fate Logsheets
objectives:
This paragraph represents the "Synapteogenesis in Microgravity" experiment objective. 

The effects of spaceflight on nervous system development and neuromuscular synapse formation were tested in a model genetic organism, Drosophila melanogaster. Specific objectives were: 
1) To utilize the organism's genetic expression of a highly fluorescent mutant form of Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP) to visualize singly identified motoneurons and muscle targets as well as all aspects of the nervous system development
2) To examine the critical developmental times of Drosophila embryos and larvae for the effects of microgravity during spaceflight.
approach: Both embryos and larvae at different stages in development were prepared, examined with fluorescent microscopy, and stored at 11 degrees C in order to arrest development. Specimens were housed on petri plates containing a culture medium in the incubator containment module (ICM) and launched on the STS-93 mission. The petri dishes were housed in seven Group Activation Packages (GAPs) within the ICM. At specific time points inflight, each GAP was heated to 25 degrees C to restart development for a set duration. Prior to landing and recovery, the GAPs were be cooled to 11 degrees C to halt development. Cell-specific Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP) expression methodologies were used to analyze the data. This allows targeting errors and subtle defects to be detected in a large sample set.
results:
Results include data on egg and embryo counts for sample preparation during launch cycles 1-4.

title: Gene Expression and Activity of DMEs and Transporters in Mouse Livers Exposed to Space Travel
objectives:
Experiment stemmed from a RR-1 liver tissues request.

Astronauts take a variety of medications for common ailments such as pain, congestion and motion sickness and insomnia that might precipitate undesirable effects such as adverse drug reactions (ADRs) and drug-drug interactions (DDIs). Space flight causes a number of physiological changes, which have the potential to alter the effect of medications on the body. Thus, astronauts may be at a higher risk of undesirable effects of medications, causing these medications to be toxic or ineffective. Safety/efficacy of medications are regulated by the enzymes and transporters which govern their pharmacokinetic (PK)/pharmacodynamic (PD) properties. To date, there is a lack of understanding of the impact of space travel on drug metabolizing enzymes (DMEs) and transporters. 

The central hypothesis for this research proposal is that expression and activity of DMEs/transporters are reduced in mouse livers which underwent space travel. Specifically, the aims are: 
Aim 1. To determine gene expression of DMEs and transporters in liver tissues from mice flown on a space mission. 
Aim 2. To determine protein expression of DMEs and transporters in liver tissues from mice flown on a space mission.
approach:
The major focus of the proposed research is to utilize bioinformatics approaches to determine the alterations in DMEs and transporters in liver tissues isolated from mice undergoing space travel.  

RNA will be isolated from the liver tissues of Rodent Research-1 mice obtained from NASA’s Biospecimen Sharing Program, and gene expression of the DMEs and transporters will be determined by DNA microarray analysis. Functional analysis of significant genes will be performed and confirmed by real-time PCR.   

Proteins will also be extracted from the liver tissues.  Reverse phase protein array (RPPA) will be used to analyze protein expression, and the results will be confirmed by immunoblotting with specific antibodies against DMEs and transporters.
results: No results generated from this research. PI was unable to extract a sufficient amount of good quality RNA.
all_people: Romi Ghose

title: Evaluating spaceflight-induced mitochondrial and zinc homeostasis changes in the murine brain
objectives:
Long-term spaceflight has been shown to cause neurodegeneration, neuroinflammation, and neurovascular damage. The potential promise of future deep-space missions reinforces our need to understand cellular changes in the brain and develop interventions to prevent immediate and late-emerging cognitive and behavioral changes. Mitochondrial dysfunction is believed to contribute to the onset and progression of neurodegeneration, while driving oxidative stress production also observed in spaceflight. Recent -omics studies show that, across multiple tissues and organisms, mitochondria are altered during spaceflight but with limited information about changes in the brain. Our proposed research will 1) characterize the effects of spaceflight on mitochondria protein markers in the brain and 2) define the role of zinc in mediating spaceflight-induced changes in neuronal mitochondria. Examining the cellular mechanisms that contribute to cognitive decline will help develop mitigation strategies not only for spaceflight but also neurodegenerative disease. 

Our proposed research seeks to examine the effects of spaceflight on neuronal mitochondrial and zinc-regulating proteins. With the female BALB/c mice flown aboard the International Space Station (ISS) in the RR3 mission for 33 days, we would like to monitor regulators of mitochondrial fragmentation, clearance, and ROS production then correlate these changes with expression of zinc-regulating proteins. We hypothesize that, as observed in other tissue, mitochondrial proteins will be differentially expressed in the brain after spaceflight. Given the role of zinc in mediating mitochondrial changes in neurodegeneration, we also anticipate increased expression of zinc-regulating proteins after spaceflight.
approach:
Samples: We are making the general request for all ISS related RR3 brains from mice, including the flight group (FLT) and controls for basal (B) and ground control (GC), which were housed on earth at the same time. (Mice treated with countermeasures are fine for our work.) The requested tissue will be used to quantify the expression of mitochondrial and zinc proteins through Western blotting. Due to limited preservation methods used on the ISS at the time of RR3, the tissue requested is best suited for Western blot analysis. All antibodies will be pretested with similar wildtype murine tissue then validated in excess basal samples before use in FLT and GC. Preliminary studies will be performed on Baseline to validate our approach and provide experimental evidence, and then we will proceed with Flight and Ground samples. 

Western blotting: Western blotting will be used to quantify protein levels of mitochondrial markers in the brain, listed below (Table 1). As measures of oxidative stress, we will also assess total protein nitrosylation, nitration, and 4-hydroxynonenal adduction, previously done in soleus muscle post-spaceflight by collaborator Dr. Val Fajardo. We anticipate that these markers of oxidative stress will be elevated in the flight group. Western blotting will also be done to study changes in key zinc binding proteins responsible for zinc homeostasis (Table 1).

Statistical analysis: Our analyses will compare the following groups: FLT, B, GC. We will conduct a one-way ANOVA to determine the statistical significance of differences in protein expression between these groups. We anticipated there will be difference in the flight group from B and GC groups.
results: Experiment in progress, results pending.

title: Partial Gravity and Sex-Difference Effects on the Venous Circulation
objectives:
The main objective of this and Space Biology (as part of the Biological and Physical Sciences division) research is to build a better understanding of how spaceflight affects living systems in spacecraft such as the International Space Station (ISS), or in ground-based experiments, and to prepare for future human exploration missions far from Earth. The experiments we conduct examine how astronauts, plants, and animals regulate and sustain their growth in space. I will be studying how physiological processes of organisms adapting to different states of gravity (e.g. micro-, Lunar-, and Martian-gravity) occur. This will be done across the spectrum of biological organization, from molecules to cells, from tissues and organs, and from systems to whole organisms. In addition to providing useful information on how living organisms adapt to spaceflight, the discoveries I will be making from space biology research have enormous implications for life on Earth. For example, these research findings provide a new perspective of biological and medical adaptations through discovery of new pathways, interactions, and processes that factors such as gravity influence, and in turn allow us to explore terrestrial medical conditions from new perspectives.

Specific Aims
- Proposed objective: there are currently no data of spaceflight venous adaptations. My study will investigate venous adaptations in simulated micro-, Lunar-, and Martian-gravity, as well as explore sex differences, to address this knowledge gap.
- Specific Aim 1: To investigate the effects of simulated micro-, Lunar, and Martian gravity on the venous circulation, including function, structure, and biomolecular pathways that regulate function in male and female rats.
approach:
Methods include:
- Myography, to measure physiological function
- Histology, to measure structural adaptations
- Proteomic analyses, to measure global venous tissue protein pathways
- Immunofluorescence analyses, to measure and see cell-type specific protein adaptations of key vascular pathways, as well specific pathways identified by proteomic analyses modulated from experimental conditions
results: Experiment in progress, results pending.

title: Characterization of Fungal Biofilm (Molds) Formation, Growth, and Gene Expression on Different Materials in Microgravity
objectives: Biofilm formation and its concomitant risk of causing equipment malfunction and human illnesses is a problem that needs to be addressed to enable safe long-term human space exploration. With that objective, this proposal aims to characterize biofilm growth during one International Space Station (ISS) increment in terms of mass, thickness, morphology, and associated gene expression using different spaceflight relevant material substrata.
approach: The experiment is designed to assess the expression of genes associated with conferring microorganisms with resistance to oxidative stress, acidity, and antimicrobials in biofilms formed in space, and to determine physical mechanisms of material/microorganisms interaction in biofilms. The experiment will be conducted using spaceflight-compatible well plates housing substrata coupons exposed to different microorganisms and media. Post-flight analyses of fixed samples include confocal laser scanning microscopy and RNA sequencing.
results: The significance of this project stems from gaining valuable new knowledge that may lead to improved methods and/or material properties for controlling biofilm formation during long duration space missions. The selection of fungal species and substrata material make the results applicable toa) the safety and maintenance of crewed spacecraft, b) planetary protection, c) mitigation of biofilm- associated illnesses on the crew, and d) biofilm-associated illnesses on Earth, thus making it relevant not only to this ResearchAnnouncement, but also to NASA’s Space Biology Program, and Human Research Roadmap.

title: Probing the synergistic effects of radiation, altered gravity and stress on behavioral cognitive and sensorimotor functions to predict performance decrement in astronauts
objectives:
The central hypothesis of this proposal is that (a) exposure to CSS induces inflammatory responses and loss of synaptic integrity in behavior-controlling brain subregions that differ between males and females, (b) correlates of CNS inflammation are detectable in peripheral blood, and (c) quantitative measures of CNS tissue and plasma responses after CSS exposures can be used to build predictive models of differential susceptibility of males and females for long-term loss of sensorimotor, behavioral and cognitive functions.

Aim 1. Characterize the relative contributions of radiation, social and gravity stressors on sensorimotor, behavioral and cognitive functions and their possible synergistic interactions.

Aim 2. Investigate the quantitative relationships between neuroinflammation and synaptic changes in mice with sensorimotor, behavioral and cognitive deficits following combined spaceflight stress.

Aim 3. Characterize the molecular profiles in peripheral blood of mice exposed to CSS to develop surrogate blood biomarkers of susceptibility and resistance for specific behavioral impairments.

Aim 4. Integrate the relationships between tissue damage markers and behavior phenotypes in male and female mice exposed to space stressors, and develop multivariate models that predict impaired performance for behaviors that are relevant to astronauts on deep space
missions.
approach:
The proposed research plan will determine whether each of the behavioral phenotypes evaluated in this project show sex differences and whether those differences are associated with different effects on synaptic function and inflammation. 

Taken together, our previous studies demonstrate that microgravity, radiation exposure and IS investigated separately can induce robust inflammatory and neuronal changes that are associate in a sex-dependent manner with behavioral deficits. The proposed research plan will expose animals to combination of stressors and evaluated a panel of behavioral phenotypes measured in each animal, and quantitative relationships of cellular and molecular damage markers evaluated in each animal for the purpose of building a multivariate model that uses tissue damage markers to predict the susceptibility for specific behavior decrement in males and females.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Determining Muscle Strength in Space-Flown Caenorhabditis elegans
objectives:
A major impediment to long-duration space travel is loss of muscle mass and strength during spaceflight. Surprisingly, the small worm Caenorhabditis elegans is a good, cost-effective model for studying the effects of spaceflight upon muscle. Our past experiments on Earth and on the International Space Station (ISS) have shown that the expression of key muscle genes that encode members of a muscle attachment complex is reduced in C. elegans during spaceflight. A key open question is if and how these changes in gene expression result in reduced muscle strength i n response to spaceflight. While reduced force production is documented for astronauts, it is yet to be demonstrated for C. elegans. Our proposed studies have important spin out implications for sarcopenia (an urgent public health problem on Earth) and may produce a terrestrial model for testing potential therapeutic interventions for muscle strength decline in flight.

Aims:
1. Demonstrate the flight readiness of a miniaturized device (NemaFlex) for quantitative characterization of muscle strength of C. elegans

2. Test the hypothesis that muscle strength decreases in flight as a result of altered muscle gene expression in the nematode

3. Quantify muscle strength loss in spaceflight across multiple nematode generations
approach: To address this critical knowledge gap, we developed a tin y new device that enables continuous measurement of worm muscle forces. Using this device, on Earth, we have shown that defects in muscle attachment complexes lead to decline in worm muscle strength. Motivated by these past flight data and ground results, we propose to (a) demonstrate the flight-readiness of our miniaturized device for quantitative characterization of muscle strength of C. elegans, (b) test our hypothesis, based on past flight data, that muscle strength decreases in flight as a result of altered muscle gene expression in the worm, and (c) quantify muscle strength loss in spaceflight across multiple worm generations. Successful measurements of muscle strength in flight will make C. elegans a much more complete and comprehensive genomic model for muscle physiology, enabling meaningful translation of discoveries made in C. elegans to astronauts.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Feasibility study: Use of Neural Networks to Predict Adaptability and Multiday Performance Saving in Dual Motor-Cognitive Tasks After Exposure to Space Flight Stressors
objectives:
This proposal will use a rodent model of space flight stressors (SFS), including space radiation (SR) and sleep disruptions (SD), that are known to impair mission-relevant performance that is dependent on cognitive and sensorimotor systems. Astronauts must engage in complex tasks that rely on the integration of information from these multiple systems simultaneously, both independently and cooperatively in team cohesion while on deep space missions. Engagement in multiple tasks at once, or dual tasks, is very common, such as navigating (i.e., walking or floating in space) through the environment while operating a device (i.e., tablet or radio). Independent, or individual, dual task performance has been shown to be disrupted in astronauts on both short and long duration space missions that was attributed to microgravity. Yet it is not known how SR and SD will impact performance during complex tasks, including adaptations to new stimuli and readaptations to old stimuli, nor whether these deficits will extend to team cohesion beyond the individual, which is critical for performance and mission success. Surprisingly, no work to-date has examined dual task performance in a rodent model of SFS.

Dual tasks involve performance in multiple systems simultaneously and provide the opportunity to evaluate adaptations, readaptations, and multiday performance savings in rodents and humans. Savings refers to faster relearning, or gains in performance that come from repetition on a task. Both savings and adaptations are imperative to mission success with exposure to SFS and varying task demands. Therefore, the main objectives of this proposal are two-fold: 1. To evaluate the impact of SFS, alone and combined, on neural activity during dual tasks, periods of inactivity, and sleep, and 2. to assess the feasibility of using neural activity to predict subsequent adaptations and multiday performance savings.
approach: To accomplish these research objectives, neural recording techniques will be used to establish system wide SFS effects on independent and cooperative (team cohesion) dual task performance and to characterize the neural mechanisms underlying adaptations and savings as well as the feasibility of predicting future performance. Our state-of-the-art established wireless neural recording techniques will be conducted while rats perform versions of the behavioral assessments that vary in complexity and during offline periods of inactivity (i.e., neural replay) to examine cognitive, sensorimotor, and vestibular function. Sleep disruptions are commonly reported among astronauts which have deleterious effects on performance, including reaction time. Therefore, we will also investigate the initial effects of SR exposure on neural activity during sleep, as well as the effect of SD on sleep characteristics (spindles, stage duration). In addition, we will assess the feasibility of predicting neural network function, adaptation and savings, from sleep characteristics, including sleep stage durations and sleep spindles that are critical to memory.
results: Results Pending.

title: Behavior of Mice Aboard the International Space Station
objectives: This study presents the first detailed behavioral analysis of mice flown in the NASA Rodent Habitat on the International Space Station (ISS) as part of the NASA Rodent Research-1 (RR1) mission. Behavior comprises a remarkably well-integrated representation of the biology of the whole animal that informs overall neural and physiological status. Behavior not only ensures survival and reproductive success, it also provides a means by which the animal can control its environment to promote homeostasis3. Behavioral analysis can reveal how animals acclimate to the space environment, and how altered physical activity, feeding, drinking, circadian shifts, social interactions may alter other experimental measures. Without knowing what an animal is doing in space, it can be difficult to interpret findings acquired from a single organ, tissue, cell type or signaling pathway.
approach:
Following 4-day transit from Earth to ISS, video images were acquired on orbit from 16-and 32-week-old female mice. Spaceflown mice engaged in a full range of species-typical behaviors. Physical activity was greater in younger flight mice as compared to identically-housed ground controls, and followed the circadian cycle. Within 7–10 days after launch, younger (but not older), mice began to exhibit distinctive circling or 'race-tracking' behavior that evolved into coordinated group activity. Organized group circling behavior unique to spaceflight may represent stereotyped motor behavior, rewarding effects of physical exercise, or vestibular sensation produced via self-motion. Affording mice the opportunity to grab and run in the RH resembles physical activities that the crew participate in routinely. Our approach yields a useful analog for better understanding human responses to spaceflight,
providing the opportunity to assess how physical movement influences responses to microgravity.
results:
Summarized and analyzed data of the Mission outcome, Video image quality, Behavior of validation mice, and Behavior of experimental versus validation mice can be found in the Behavior of mice aboard the International Space Station publication cited below.

Ronca, A.E., Moyer, E.L., Talyansky, Y. et al. Behavior of mice aboard the International Space Station. Sci Rep 9, 4717 (2019) doi:10.1038/s41598-019-40789-y
publication_titles: Behavior of Mice Aboard the International Space Station

title: Does Spaceflight Alter the Virulence of a Natural Parasite of Drosophila?
objectives: The seminal discovery of Toll and Toll-NF-kB signaling as a central mediator of innate immune responses in the model insect Drosophila catalyzed similar discoveries in other organisms including innate immunity in humans. These findings led to an appreciation of the fundamental roles of innate immunity in human immune deficiency on the one hand, and immune health of insects on the other. In Drosophila larvae, primed, but quiescent hematopoietic progenitors are called into action from an organ with functional similarities to mammalian bone marrow. Blood cells signal the fat body (equivalent to the mammalian liver) to activate the expression of antimicrobial peptide genes (humoral immunity). Antimicrobial peptides efficiently combat deadly bacteria and fungi. The Leptopilina wasps are natural metazoan enemies of Drosophila and their attack kills fruit flies. Wasp attack (e.g., L. boulardi) activates both the humoral and cellular immune arms of larval hosts, each being dependent on Toll-NF-kB signaling. Previous NASA-funded studies of Drosophila have shown that stresses in the space environment compromise hematopoietic development and modulate larval and adult innate immunity. We hypothesize that stimuli from the space environment will also alter the virulence of the parasitic wasps, and therefore their success on the fly hosts cultured in space.
approach: In Drosophila-wasp co-culture experiments using the NASA Fruit Fly Lab hardware over 30-days, we propose to (1) examine whether the fly or the wasp partner wins the arms race in space; (2) examine the effects of spaceflight on the anti-parasite response in fruit fly hosts; and (3) examine if virulence of "space-cultured" wasps is different from counterparts grown on Earth. While both L. boulardi and L. heterotoma parasites always succeed on the wild type, only the latter wasp species produces unique cell-killing activities residing in its virus-like particles. It also suppresses humoral immunity completely. We will examine if changes in the expression of VLP genes underlies changes in parasite virulence affected by spaceflight. Host defense and virulence will be inferred from a suite of other well-established assays of fly/host survival and measures of immune response.
results: Experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Multi-use Variable-gravity Platform Validation Fly (MVP-Fly-01)
objectives: Validation testing of the facility uses Drosophila melanogaster (fruit flies) to verify environmental controls and check MVP's overall performance. For MVP's hardware validation run, one carousel will stay stationary to allow scientists to collect a baseline of behavior in microgravity. The other carousel will spin at the normal force of gravity on Earth (1G) as control group. In addition to validating the controls and operability of MVP, this initial investigation – known as MVP-Fly-01 - will provide science data for researchers at NASA's Ames Research Center. MVP is especially exciting for fruit fly research because it can host larger samples for multiple generations. This initial study will look at biological issues common between humans and fruit flies. The investigation concluded in late April, and the modules were removed from MVP and returned to Earth aboard the same Dragon that delivered them to space.
approach: The Multi-use Variable-gravity Platform (MVP) Validation mission will install and test the MVP, a new hardware platform developed and owned by Techshot Inc., on the International Space Station (ISS).  This new research platform is equipped with temperature, light cycle, and humidity control, video feed from inside the hardware, and two 390mm diameter, identical and independently-controlled centrifuges that can generate artificial gravity from .1 to 2g. Its versatility will greatly expand the range of life sciences hardware that is available to space biology researchers and will allow researchers to plan longer and more advanced microgravity experiments on the ISS.
results: The MVP-FLY-01 payload was launched and safely returned to ARC after the SpaceX CRS-14 mission. We estimate that greater than 14,000 eggs, larvae and adults were returned live from spaceflight. Frozen/fixed samples and video data were also obtained from this experiment. The team is hard at work to analyze the samples that were returned post-flight to Bhattacharya's lab at ARC on 5/7/18. In addition to the value of the samples and science data that were returned, this experiment also served to validate an important piece of spaceflight hardware that was built by NASA's commercial partner, Techshot Inc (now Redwire). This hardware has a 1G centrifuge built into the habitat along with environmental sensing and control, automated food change-out for multigenerational growth of Drosophila cultures in space, videography and circadian lighting.
publication_titles: Artificial gravity partially protects space-induced neurological deficits in Drosophila melanogaster

title: Examining the Effects of Spaceflight on GSK3 Activation in Murine Muscle and Bone
objectives:
It is well established that spaceflight leads to muscle and bone loss, and the promising future of long-term spaceflight missions heightens the need to develop interventions that maintain musculoskeletal health. On earth, the deleterious effects observed on muscle and bone with spaceflight mimics the effects observed with human aging (ie. osteoporosis and sarcopenia). Therefore, examining the cellular mechanisms that contribute to muscle and bone loss will help in directing the development of mitigation strategies not only for spaceflight but also for aging on earth. Glycogen synthase kinase 3 (GSK3) is an enzyme that has been implicated in several other musculoskeletal conditions. Thus, our proposed research seeks to
examine the effects of spaceflight on GSK3 activity in murine soleus muscle and bone while correlating it with markers of muscle proteolysis, oxidative metabolism, and bone mineral density, structure and strength. The specific aims and corresponding hypotheses of our proposal are listed below. For our muscle analyses, we propose to examine the effects of spaceflight on the soleus muscle, given that this muscle is primarily affected by microgravity.

Aim 1: Examining GSK3 activation in muscle and bone samples collected from male C57BL/6 mice from the RR9 mission

Aim 2: Examining GSK3 activation in muscle samples collected from female C57BL/6 from the RR1 mission
approach:
Aim 1 Experimental groups: RR9 male C57BL/6 mice (3-4 month old) were flown onboard the SpaceX Dragon capsule to the International Space Station. The flight group (FLT) mice were in orbit for 33 days. A second set of Vivarium (Viv) and Ground control (GCrerun) experiments were conducted 9 months after the FLT experiment. Our analyses will compare the following groups: FLT, Viv and GCrerun. To control for any variation between cohorts, we will first make comparisons between cohort control 1 (CC1), which was run alongside the FLT group, and cohort control 2 (CC2), which was run alongside Viv and GCrerun. In the event of a significant difference (p < 0.05) between CC2 and CC1, then GCrerun and Viv will be normalized using a calculated factor (CC2/CC1) prior to running a one-way ANOVA with FLT. Based on our previous studies (1-3), we estimate a minimum of 4-10 samples per group across all measures in muscle and bone. According to the Life Sciences Data Archive the right soleus (RNA later), right and left tibias are available for request.

Aim 2 Experimental groups: RR1 female C57BL/6 (3-4 month old) mice were flown onboard the SpaceX4 Dragon capsule to the International Space Station. The flight group (FLT) mice were in orbit for 35 days. A Vivarium control and Ground control were housed on earth at the same time. Similar to Aim 1, we estimate a minimum of number of 4-10 samples per group across all measures in muscle. According to the Life Sciences Data Archive the left and right soleus are both available and stored in RNA later. For the purposes of our experiments we are only
requesting right soleus muscles from each experimental group (n = 9-10 per group).

GSK3 activation will be assessed via an enzyme-linked GSK3 activity assay developed in our lab, as well as Western blotting for serine phosphorylation. Increased serine phosphorylation of GSK3 inactivates the kinase and thus an reduction in serine phosphorylation may be indicative of enhanced GSK3 activity. These assays will be completed on muscle (soleus and tibialis anterior) and bone (femur) samples.

Measures of muscle oxidative phenotype will be assessed via Western blotting for proteins that are abundant in oxidative fiber types including: myosin heavy chain I, PGC-1alpha, and other mitochondrial markers. Western blotting will also be conducted to examine muscle proteuloysis, specifically the ubiquitin proteasomal degradation pathway with total ubiquitinylation and the expression of E3 ligases MuRF1 and Atrogin-1. These assays will be completed on muscle (soleus and tibialis anterior) samples.

We will examine bone structure of right tibias of male mice from the RR9 mission using µCT. Trabecular structural measurements assessed using 3D analyses will include: percent bone volume (BV/TV), trabecular thickness (Tb.Th), trabecular number (Tb.N), trabecular separation (Tb.Sp). Cortical structural measurements using 2D analyses will include: cortical area fraction (Ct.Ar/Tt.Ar), cortical thickness (Ct.Th), periosteal perimeter (Ps.Pm), endocortical perimeter (Ec.Pm), and medullary area (Ma.Ar).
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.

title: Adaptation of Mouse Systems Physiology to Artificial Gravity via Centripetal Acceleration: Timing, Metabolism, and Aging
objectives:
Exposure to the spaceflight environment adversely affects many aspects of physiology and behavior. Included among these deleterious changes are alterations in circadian and sleep/wake function that not only exacerbate physiological and performance decrements, but also correlate with disrupted metabolism and elevated aging biomarkers. Artificial Gravity (AG; produced via the centripetal acceleration of centrifugation) has long been proposed as a countermeasure to mitigate or even prevent these deleterious changes during spaceflight and has proved successful in a limited study on mice in the areas of muscle and bone. Planning for a mission to Mars raises the additional question of how effective the Martian gravitational environment will be in combatting microgravity induced deficits. Finally, in order for an AG countermeasure to be successfully implemented, the parameters of AG exposure must be experimentally defined. This program seeks to elucidate the dose-response curve for an AG exposure countermeasure using an established mouse model and centrifuge aboard the ISS. We propose to record circadian rhythms of body temperature, food intake, rest/activity and other video-recorded behaviors during 4 weeks of AG exposure produced via centrifugation in up to four sequential ISS studies as well as to examine the effects of AG on clock genes underlying circadian function. Additional outcome measures using 'omics analyses (e.g., proteomics and transcriptomics) on select tissues postflight will be used to examine regulatory factors and biomarkers related to the physiological and behavioral measures. Data from the subjects exposed to AG will be compared with cohorts studied in microgravity on the same missions, as well as to terrestrial ground controls. Four AG levels are proposed, allowing the establishment of an AG countermeasure dose-response curve. The AG levels we propose to test include lunar (0.17G), Martian (0.38G) and two additional levels intermediate between Martian and terrestrial. This series of studies will be the first to establish a dose response relationship for an AG countermeasure as well as to begin to answer the question of whether such countermeasures would also be necessary during lunar or Martian habitation.

Specific Aim 1. Determine changes in mouse circadian rhythms resulting from spaceflight and AG.

Specific Aim 2. Determine if central and peripheral circadian clock genes are altered as a result of spaceflight and AG.

Specific Aim 3. Determine if hypothalamic neuropeptides known to play a central role in the regulation and neural control of metabolism are altered as a result of spaceflight and AG.

Specific Aim 4. Determine if biomarkers correlated with CTS function, metabolism and aging are altered as a result of spaceflight and AG.
approach: The proposed approach will utilize a combination of organismal physiology outcome measures and cellular-molecular (‘omics) analysis of selected tissue samples. Programmatic alignment of this work includes the Space Biology interest in elucidating the animal organ, tissue and system responses to gravity changes and the genomic and regulatory mechanisms driving them and the decadal objectives to examine how the space environment affects basic physiological function and relevant regulatory mechanisms and pathways. This project requests measurements and tissues that will allow us to compare circadian responses among rodents exposed to 1) microgravity, 2) microgravity + AG, and 3) 1G control subjects (SBP AN-1; SBP AN-3; SBP AN-4; DS CC2).
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
