title: Tailoring Lunar Regolith to Plant Nutrition
objectives: Plants have long been recognized as candidates for elements of bioregenerative approaches to life support. In the case of the return to the Moon, regolith may reduce resupply requirements related to growing plants by potentially replacing the need to ship growth media from the Earth. Lunar regolith and regolith simulants that chemically and physically resemble true lunar regolith have been used to show that this medium can support plant growth. However, these plants show developmental and molecular symptoms of stress likely related to the substrate’s chemical and physical composition. The soil microbiome and microbial symbioses with roots are central to plant adaptations to similar nutrient-limiting and stressful conditions on Earth. This proposed research program therefore seeks to define the effects of lunar regolith simulants on the growth of both plant and nitrogen fixing bacteria to ask how well establishing their symbiotic interactions can alleviate some of the negative effects of lunar regolith on plant growth and development. These questions will be addressed using molecular/genetic dissection of the effects of regolith simulant on the well-defined plant-bacterium model of Sinorhizobium meliloti (hereafter ‘rhizobium’) -triggered nitrogen-fixing nodulation in Medicago truncatula in comparison to soil bacteria engineered to fix nitrogen. Aim 1 will be to characterize plant and microbial growth in regolith simulant by monitoring parameters such as plant biomass, morphology, chlorophyl content, and characteristics of root system architecture and nodule formation. In addition, bacterial population dynamics will be assessed alongside the monitoring of plant and bacterial responses through transcriptomic profiling via RNAseq. Aim 2 will address similar analyses but using a set of novel, genetically engineered associative diazotrophs (Azotobacter vinelandii, Azospirillum brasilense and Klebsiella variicola) that fix nitrogen on the surface of plant roots without needing root nodules. Aim 3 will follow plant growth and microbial population dynamics in subsequent generations of plants grown in the initial microbe and plant conditioned regolith to assess how well the previous generations of plants and microbes can condition regolith for improved subsequent plant performance. This research will generate new insight into how plants respond to the environment of the lunar regolith, define effects on microbial populations in this substrate, and assess how regolith impacts plant- microbe interactions. At a practical level, it will define how bacterial symbiosis may help alleviate some of the issues that arise for plants growing in the regolith in the context of bioregenerative life support. Results from this research, therefore, fit well with the goals of the TIDES initiative. HRP has defined inadequate food and nutrition as Human Health Countermeasures Risk 11. Thus, results from this research will also help inform strategies to adapt plants as a nutritional source to assist with mitigating this risk.
approach: Aim 1: The regolith environment will impose stresses that alter plant growth and affect the population dynamics of symbiotic root-associated bacteria. Approach: Characterize plant and bacterial growth in regolith simulant by monitoring growth, photosynthesis, biochemical markers of stress, ionome and the transcriptome. Aim 2: Disruption of growth will alter the symbioses between plants and N-fixing bacteria. Approach: follow nodulation, N fixation, and plant nutrient content with and without the presence of the nodulating N-forming bacterium rhizobium or with engineered associative N-fixing diazotroph bacteria. Aim 3: Once established, interactions between N-fixing bacteria and the plant will alleviate some of the adverse effects of regolith on plant growth over successive generations. Approach: follow plant growth and development over multiple generations in regolith simulant with and without the presence rhizobium or engineered diazotrophs.

title: Growth, physiology and nutrition dynamics of potato plants grown on lunar regolith simulant medium
objectives: This project will characterize the responses of potato to exposure to lunar regolith simulants at the growth, developmental, physiological and molecular levels. It is directly relevant to the scope of the E.9 Research Studies program element of Space Biology “for ground-based plant studies (and/or their associated microbes) that will characterize the responses of these organisms to conditions that recapitulate the stressors encountered in space exploration, specifically, exposure to lunar regolith (simulant)”. Potato (Solanum tuberosum L.) is an ideal clonally propagated food crop for space exploration because it provides more calories per land area than many other crops, grows on a wide variety of soil media, and it is nutritious and has a high satiety index. Potato is also a good model organism for plants that produce underground tubers because its genome has been sequenced and many large-scale omic datasets are publicly available. It also has a large untapped genetic diversity that makes it amenable to genetic studies and trait improvement. However, we currently do not know how potato might respond to lunar regolith simulant as a growth substrate, and whether there are potato genotypes that perform better than others on such substrate. It follows that we have no knowledge about the adaptability of potato to grow on lunar regolith simulant and the underlying mechanisms of acclimation. We have no knowledge either of the chemistry of potato tubers produced on lunar regolith simulant and whether these are safe for consumption by space exploration crews. By combining phenotypic, physiological and molecular characterization of a genetically diverse set of potato genotypes, this project will advance our knowledge of how potato responds to lunar regolith simulant and how it regulates and sustains growth and metabolism. The objective of this proposal is to characterize and compare the responses of potato to exposure to Lunar Highlands and Lunar Mare regolith simulants. Specifically, this project will: Aim 1. Characterize and compare the growth, physiology, leaf gene expression and leaf and tuber metabolism of the reference potato genotype ‘Modoc’ grown on Lunar Highlands regolith simulants OPRH4W30 and LHS-1E, Lunar Mare regolith simulants JSC-1A and EJSC-1A, and Earth Quincy Series Soil; Aim 2. Assess the genetic potential of potato to grow on Lunar Highlands and Lunar Mare regolith simulants LHS-1E and EJSC-1A by characterizing the performance of ten potato genotypes. This project will pinpoint potentially impaired physiological/biological processes that hinder the full growth potential of potato and inform on physiological and molecular mechanisms of adaption to growth on lunar regolith simulants.
approach:
•	Grow the potato reference genotype ‘Modoc’ on Lunar Highlands (OPRH4W30, LHS-1E) and Lunar Mare (JSC-1A, EJSC-1A) regolith simulants mixed with compost and compare the growth, physiology, leaf transcriptome and leaf and mini-tuber metabolites 
•	Grow ‘Modoc’ + 9 genetically diverse genotypes on the Lunar Highlands (LHS-1E) and Lunar Mare (EJSC-1A) regolith simulants mixed with compost and compare the growth, physiology, tubers’ metabolites and yield between simulants and between genotypes.

title: The metabolomic impact of lunar regolith-based substrate on tomatoes
objectives: Use of local materials in growth substrates for plants is an inherent component of in situ resource utilization (ISRU), a concept intrinsic to plans for extraterrestrial exploration in general and thus to lunar habitation specifically. However, little is known about how continuous exposure of crop plants to lunar regolith will impact fundamental aspects of plant biology. Plants have evolved to respond to environmental stimuli and stresses via metabolic alterations, thereby impacting not only plant growth itself, but also the phytochemical composition of any harvested tissues. This Plant Early Career Research Investigation will create a baseline knowledge of the metabolic perturbations in plants induced by exposure to a lunar regolith simulant. Using tomato, a valuable crop plant with potential to be among the first utilized in support of lunar habitation, as our model system, we will address two objectives. Under the first objective, we will establish a ground-based system for studying plant responses to regolith exposure, thereby facilitating short and long-term goals of our lab. To accomplish this, growth of tomatoes in amended regolith simulant will be monitored to determine the minimal supplementation needed to sustain full life-cycle growth. Under the second objective, we will create a tissue-level atlas of metabolite alterations induced by exposure to a simulated lunar regolith in tomatoes. Specifically, tissues from plants grown in the conditions determined under Objective 1 will be subjected to a variety of analytical strategies, the results of which will be combined into a comprehensive dataset. The results obtained from this study will not only provide immediate information about fundamental plant responses to one critical aspect of lunar plant growth, but will also lay the groundwork for continuing efforts of this early career researcher to optimize production of plant-derived resources in support of humanity’s exploration goals.
approach:
Red Robin tomatoes will be grown to fruiting using three different substrate formulations: 1) OPRH4W30 regolith simulant substrate, amended as determined appropriate; 2) JSC-1A regolith substrate, amended identically to condition 1, and 3) a regolith-free control, composed only of the materials used as amendments under the conditions 1 and 2 (tentatively planned as Turface alone, pending validation of conditions). Substrate volume will be held constant between the substrates to eliminate impact of root growth volume on overall plant growth characteristics. Each substrate formulation will be isolated to its own grow system. Feed water will be supplemented with a MS nutrient formulation as found to be sufficient.

Upon fruit ripening, whole plants will be harvested and separated into roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits for each plant. To understand the transcriptional context in which changes in metabolite profiles (to be assessed in C.2.3) occur, we will perform RNAseq analysis. Untargeted metabolomic analysis will be performed by parallel LC-MS analyses that rely on differing analytical strategies for global unbiased profiling of nonvolatile metabolites, with use of a chemical library to provide unparalleled high-confidence identification and quantification of diverse metabolites. Volatile and semi-volatile compounds will be analyzed by established methods GC-MS based methods. Tentative identities will be assigned by comparison of mass spectra to those in the NIST mass spectral library and confirmed by comparison to authentic standards when available. For both volatile and non-volatile metabolites, abundances will be compared across growth substrate conditions on a tissue-by-tissue basis

title: Investigations of the Plant Cytoskeleton in Microgravity With Gene Profiling and Cytochemistry
objectives: The major goal of this research is to study the effects of microgravity on the structure and organization of actin cytoskeleton in plants. This proposed project builds on our previous ground-based and spaceflight research using the model plant Arabidopsis. Thus, the specific aims of this proposed flight research using the BRIC-LED system are: (1) to investigate plastid position in statocytes (=gravity-perceiving cells) in microgravity; (2) to determine the effect of microgravity on the actin cytoskeletal organization in gravity-perceiving cells; (3) to study microgravity effects on actin cytoskeleton-related gene expression in plant cells. This project also will allow us to directly correlate results from cytological investigations and gene profiling in order to understand the nature of the actin cytoskeleton in mechanisms of gravity perception.
approach: The NASA Biological Research in Canisters (BRIC) -16 project offered a unique opportunity to conduct a spaceflight experiment with minimal preparation time. Procedures were adapted from previous spaceflight studies conducted by our lab on the Space Shuttle and the ISS. Seedlings of Arabidopsis thaliana were grown from seed on the shuttle Discovery mission STS-131 in the BRIC- Petri Dish Fixation Unit (PDFU) flight hardware. After fourteen days of growth, samples were then stabilized by either an aldehyde-based fixative or RNAlater. We compared flight samples with a ground sample control, which was conducted in the flight hardware at 1g. Through additional studies, samples will be analyzed using molecular genetic techniques and three methods of microscopy including light (LM), transmission electron (TEM), and confocal. LM will be used to understand any overall anatomical differences and the placement of the statoliths. TEM will elucidate the interaction between statoliths and the sub-cellular structures such as cytoskeleton and organelles. Confocal microscopy will show us the structural differences in the development of the actin cytoskeleton between ground and flight.
results: No results available. Data available through GeneLab.

title: Echocardiography and Oxygraph analysis of the mouse and rat heart after exposure to chronic neutrons, silicon ions, with and without the administration of aspirin as a potential countermeasure
objectives: Recent evidence suggests that exposure to high-linear energy transfer (LET) radiation encountered during deep space travel may be associated with short- or long-term risks of cardiovascular disease. However, these risks are not well defined, and potential gender differences are unknown. We have shown that exposure of male mice to low-dose oxygen ions causes small but significant changes in cardiac function and that both protons and oxygen ions cause dose-dependent increases in the markers of inflammatory cells in the heart. While these studies have started to characterize the effects of low-dose charged particles on cardiac function and structure in the male mouse, the dose rates in these studies were relatively high compared to chronic low-dose rate exposures in space. Therefore, additional work needs to be done in animals chronically exposed to low-dose rate charged particle irradiation. Moreover, the inclusion of additional animal species will greatly enhance the potential for translation of the results into humans compared to research in mouse models alone. Here, we aim to provide insight into the effects of chronic low-dose rate high-LET radiation exposures on the heart and identify how animal species differ in their response to high-LET radiation, addressing the Risk of Cardiovascular Disease and Other Degenerative Tissue Effects from Radiation Exposure and assisting in closure of the Gaps Degen – 1, Degen – 2, and Degen – 3. We pursue three experiments: 1) In collaboration with the Colorado State University Specialized Center of Research on Carcinogenesis, we make use of its long-term studies in male and female mouse models exposed to chronic low-dose rate neutrons to assess cardiac function with echocardiography, and we collect and analyze cardiac tissues obtained from these mice; 2) We perform echocardiography and tissue analysis in rats that are exposed to low-dose rate neutrons in the neutron facility that is part of the Specialized Center of Research on Carcinogenesis; and 3) We assess markers of inflammatory infiltration and mitochondrial morphology and function in cardiac tissue samples of rats and rabbits that had received acute exposures to protons and oxygen ions as part of the Center for Space Radiation Research. These experiments make use of existing animals and tissue samples to aid in defining the risk of cardiovascular disease from space radiation exposure.
approach:
These studies aim to characterize the risk of degenerative cardiovascular disease of space radiation, by comparing the effects of exposure to low-dose protons, oxygen ions, and low-dose rate neutrons in mouse, rat, and rabbit models. Experiment 1 makes of female BALB/c mice and male C3H mice. As part of the Colorado State University (CSU) Specialized Center of Research on Carcinogenesis large cohorts of mice are exposed to low-dose rate or high-dose neutrons, starting at the age of 100-120 days, to a total dose of 0.1 Gy, 0.2 Gy, and 0.4 Gy. Subgroups of mice receive aspirin in the drinking water as a potential countermeasure against adverse radiation effects. At the age of 500 days, 10 mice of each experimental group are anesthetized with isoflurane inhalation, and an ultrasound scan of the heart is obtained. At the age of 800 days, this procedure is performed once more. All animals are euthanized at the age of 800 days, and heart tissue collected for histology and western-blot analysis of markers of inflammatory infiltration and mitochondrial function.
For experiment 2, a total of 10 male Long Evans rats are exposed to low-dose rate neutrons in the same chronic neutron facility, starting at the age of 100-120 days, to a total dose of 0.4 Gy. A total of 10 rats receive sham-irradiation. At the age of 500 days, the rats are anesthetized with isoflurane inhalation, and an ultrasound scan of the heart is obtained. At the age of 800 days, this procedure is performed once more. All animals are euthanized at the age of 800 days, and heart tissue collected for histology and western-blot analysis of markers of inflammatory infiltration and mitochondrial function.

For experiment 3, tissues have been obtained from male Long Evans rats and male New Zealand White rabbits that were exposed to oxygen ions (600 MeV/n, 0.08-0.11 Gy/min tot a total dose of 0.5 Gy), protons (250 MeV, 0.10-0.12 Gy/min to a total dose of 0.5 Gy), or sham treatment at the NASA Space Radiation Laboratory (9-11 animals per group) and were followed to 12 months after irradiation. Specimens of left ventricle are used for western-blots to quantify protein markers of leukocytes, T-cells, monocytes/macrophages, extracellular matrix, mitochondrial fission/fusion, and mitochondrial enzyme levels.
results:
Echocardiography data obtained at 400 and 800 days after the start of radiation exposure in the following groups (each in a separate tab in the datasheet):
- Male C3H mice exposed to a single dose of silicon ions and treated chronically with low-dose aspirin for 400 days after radiation
- Female Balb/C mice exposed to low-dose rate neutrons for 400 days
- Male C3H mice exposed to low-dose rate neutrons for 400 days
- Male C3H mice exposed to low-dose rate neutrons for 400 days and treated chronically with low-dose aspirin during radiation
- Male Long Evans rats exposed to low-dose rate neutrons

Transthoracic echocardiography was performed in rats and mice under light (2%) isoflurane anesthesia using a 12 MHz sector or 15 MHz linear array transducer connected to a Phillips XD11 Ultrasound system. Short axis 2D images were used to determine left ventricular end-diastolic and end-systolic areas, fractional shortening, and wall thicknesses. No outliers identified. Cardiac mitochondrial function was measured in permeabilized myocytes (~1 mg) and mitochondria isolated from ~30 mg of myocardial tissue for assessment of respiratory function and reactive oxygen species release. Experiments were performed with a high resolution respirometry system (Oroboros Oxygraph) using various mitochondrial substrates. Mitochondrial release of reactive oxygen species from freshly isolated mitochondria was measured with Amplex Red using a fluorometer module coupled to the oxygraph chamber. No outliers identified.

title: Engineering plant-microbial interactions for improved plant growth on simulated lunar regolith
objectives: Plants are remarkable for their ability to acquire nutrients and withstand abiotic stress across diverse soil substrates. This success is in part mediated by the interaction of roots with diverse microbial communities that facilitate the acquisition of nutrients, ameliorate various abiotic stresses, and protect plants from disease. However, the plant associated microbiome is remarkably diverse and little is known about the assembly, community dynamics, or drivers of the beneficial attributes of the plant microbiome. This complexity has hampered the engineering of beneficial microbiomes. Our aim is to engineer specific plant-microbiomes through successive bouts of selection that increase plant survival and growth on substrates of simulated lunar regolith. We will use an artificial selection strategy called host-mediated microbiome selection to engineer the microbiome and synthetic microbial community function. We will use sequencing to identify microbes associated with improved or decrease performance in the regolith selected lines. Bioinformatics analyses of the composition of taxa and gene content from the selected communities will identify enrichment of particular gene functions that improve tolerance to regolith. Ultimately, these studies will help to identify critical plant–microbe–soil traits that we can manipulate and engineer for improved plant growth on regolith.
approach:
Enter Approach Here

Our project will focus on three main objects.  First, we will create a new microbial isolate collection derived extreme soil substrates and habitats.  These new isolates will be characterized by several sequencing efforts.  Second, we will use microbiome selection experiments to engineer microbial communities to improve plant growth and established on simulated regolith.  Finally, we will test plant performance on JSC-1A and OPRH4W30 simulants, and if possible, genuine regolith using engineered microbiomes.

title: Plant Habitat-01 (PH-01): An Integrated Omics Guided Approach to Lignification and Gravitational Responses in Plants
objectives:
1) Establish multi 'omics' effects of modulating lignin and CCM levels at 1g and in spaceflight.
2) Compare/contrast data, using an ICB approach, to better define and understand gravity sensing and responses.
3) When lignin and CCM levels are varied, determine if threshold/induction parameters are modified or changed.
4) Address distinct hypotheses for the various teams, and integrate, dissect and incisively analyze them holistically in a manner previously not possible.
approach: A multi-omics study using the model plant Arabidopsis under both 1g and in microgravity conditions (spaceflight). The approach spans comprehensive phenomics, metabolomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics strategies. It incisively and uniquely melds this pioneering approach via deployment of an integrated computational biology (ICB) approach. Studies identify gene/metabolic networks, mechanisms and/or pathways, and adaptations that are differentially modulated, i.e. through using various omics (phenomics, transcriptomics, genomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and ICB) approaches.
results: Plants grew in spaceflight and in ground controls. Experimental results are pending and will be presented in future publications.

title: Automated deep learning for spaceflight rodent behavior quantification and health phenotyping
objectives: The proposed work will demonstrate the feasibility of using this technology to automate spaceflight behavioral health monitoring by applying it to previously collected videos from the NASA Rodent Research-1 mission. These have been painstakingly manually annotated by human expert observers with frame-by-frame labels of behaviors (e.g., feeding, grooming) to enable quantitative analysis of rodent behavior during spaceflight. This laborious effort was necessary due to the challenging imaging conditions inherent in spaceflight videography – but which are overcome through the use of AI in the proposed work. By developing a platform capable of automating this process, we will establish the foundation for future systems that will be able to monitor behavioral health in research missions – automatically and in real-time, opening the door to interventional studies aimed at maintaining positive behavioral health conditions. Future work may adapt this technology for behavioral monitoring in humans to detect and mitigate the risks of crewed deep space missions.
approach:
Aim 1: Develop and validate system for automated motion capture of rodents in spaceflight.
Aim 2: Establish feasibility of automated analysis of rodent spaceflight behavioral motifs.

title: Effect of space radiation on cytomegalovirus reactivation and lytic replication
objectives: Opportunistic pathogens such as latent viruses pose a unique hazard to crewmembers because of the ineffectiveness of preflight quarantine periods and decreased immune function. Latent herpesviruses reactivate and shed in higher quantities during and after long and short spaceflights. Human cytomegalovirus (CMV) is an important herpesvirus from public health viewpoint that causes severe systemic infections upon primary infection or on reactivation from latency. The few antiviral drugs available to treat CMV infection suffer from safety, efficacy, and resistance issues, and no vaccine against CMV is currently available. As NASA and collaborating space agencies plan for future exploration class missions (i.e. to Mars or an asteroid), eliminating the risk of an adverse health event due to alterations in virus latency, reactivation and lytic replication is paramount. Evidence of impaired control of latent viral infections has been documented during short (i.e. 14-day Space Shuttle missions) as well as long (i.e. 6-month ISS missions) duration spaceflight missions, with the magnitude of this response being positively associated with time in flight. Shedding of herpesviruses including Epstein–Barr virus (EBV), varicella-zoster virus (VZV), and CMV was detected in >30% astronauts in a recent study looking at long-duration spaceflight (60–180 days). These viruses reactivated independently of each other and reactivation frequency increased in duration, and amplitude (viral copy numbers) when compared to short duration (10 to 16 days) space shuttle missions. Thus, it is easy to speculate that the substantially longer durations being proposed for exploration class missions (i.e. up to 3 years) will further increase the risk of an adverse health event due to increased virus reactivation and/or replication. Add increased radiation exposure (both duration and magnitude), limited clinical care and the impossibility of rapidly returning a casualty back to Earth to the list of potential hazards and it is clear to see why obtaining a better understanding of virus replication under space like conditions is critical to successfully and safely send humans to, and return them from, these remote extraterrestrial destinations. In this proposal, we aim to determine the impact of space radiation on CMV latency, reactivation and lytic replication. While most studies on this topic in the past have focused on changes in human immune system that could induce virus reactivation, we will approach this topic from both virus and host standpoints. We will study differences in CMV latency and reactivation before and after radiation exposure and determine if these changes can be correlated with specific changes in virus genome and/or transcriptome. Also, because virus control, dissemination and resolution is ultimately governed by the competency, or lack thereof, of the host immune system, we will determine the impact of pre-exposing NK-cells and T-cells to space radiation on their ability to control active CMV infections. In this project, we will focus on a single variable: the space radiation, rather than looking at multiple variables such as microgravity, physical and psychological stresses of space travel, diet, medications, or other factors associated with space flight. The overarching hypothesis is that modeled space radiation will induce specific changes in CMV genome/transcriptome or immune control of infection that will induce CMV reactivation and/or increase in virus replication and pathogenicity.
approach:
In this proposal, we aim to determine the impact of space radiation on CMV latency, reactivation and lytic replication. While most studies on this topic in the past have focused on changes in human immune system that could induce virus reactivation, we will approach this topic from both virus and host standpoints. We will study differences in CMV latency and reactivation before and after radiation exposure and determine if these changes can be correlated with specific changes in virus genome and/or transcriptome. Also, because virus control, dissemination and resolution is ultimately governed by the competency, or lack thereof, of the host immune system, we will determine the impact of pre-exposing NK-cells and T-cells to space radiation on their ability to control active CMV infections. In this project, we will focus on a single variable: the space radiation, rather than looking at multiple variables such as microgravity, physical and psychological stresses of space travel, diet, medications, or other factors associated with space flight. The overarching hypothesis is that modeled space radiation will induce specific changes in CMV genome/transcriptome or immune control of infection that will induce CMV reactivation and/or increase in virus replication and pathogenicity. The specific aims to test this hypothesis are:

Specific Aim 1: Determine the impact of space radiation on CMV genome and transcriptome. The CMV genomes and transcriptomes harvested from mock-exposed vs. radiation-exposed infected cells will be harvested and sequenced using next generation sequencing. The sequences will be compared with each other as well as with the reference genome/transcriptome to find changes in the coding regions that could be attributed to radiation.

Specific Aim 2: Correlate radiation induced changes in CMV genome and/or transcriptome with CMV latency, reactivation and lytic replication. Any identified and confirmed changes in CMV genome and/or transcriptome after radiation exposure will be individually engineered into wild-type CMV genome and their impact on CMV latency, reactivation and lytic replication will be determined using established in vitro and in vivo models.

Specific Aim 3: Determine the impact of space radiation on immune cell control of CMV infection. CMV reactivation and latency is largely dependent on the actions of NK-cells and viral specific T-cells. However, it is not known if space radiation will impair the ability of these cells
to control active CMV infections. We will determine the effects of in vivo space radiation exposure on the ability of mouse splenocytes to control mouse cytomegalovirus (MCMV) infection. We will use inbred mouse strains and adoptive transfer models to address this knowledge gap.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
publication_titles: Effect of Simulated Cosmic Radiation on Cytomegalovirus Reactivation and Lytic Replication.

title: Osteocyte Plasma Membrane Disruptions in Skeletal Adaptation to Loading and Unloading
objectives:
Plasma membrane disruptions (PMD) develop with in vitro and in vivo mechanical loading in bone osteocytes and initiate skeletal
mechanotransduction. We consistently observe that ~20% of long bone osteocytes develop PMD with cage activity in mice, suggesting that
formation of osteocyte PMD may be essential to bone's sensation and response to normal gravitational loads. Accordingly, our central
hypothesis is that osteocyte PMD formation is impaired during skeletal disuse, leading to bone loss. Our goals are to test the effects of
disuse on osteocyte PMD formation, to determine whether osteocytes become sensitized to PMD formation with impaired PMD repair
or survival during reloading, and to determine whether modulating osteocyte PMD formation and/or repair affect these processes. Our
strategy is to test these concepts in an in vivo mouse model of hindlimb unloading, as well as with in vitro osteocyte models of unloading
(rotating wall vessel bioreactor) and reloading (fluid shear stress)

AIM 1: Test the hypothesis that osteocyte PMD formation is decreased by disuse and enhanced, but with impaired repair, during reloading. For in vivo studies, mice will be subjected to unilateral hindlimb casting, which we established in pilot studies to cause loss of cortical bone density and strength in 2 weeks. Osteocyte PMD will be histologically quantified in the immobilized and contralateral limbs. PMD magnitude and density will be regressed against metrics of bone architecture, mineralization, and strength. For in vitro studies, osteocytes (immortalized and primary cells) will be cultured in a rotating wall vessel bioreactor to simulate microgravity. Regarding reloading, in a pilot experiment, we discovered that mice subjected to disuse followed by ~5 minutes of reloading surprisingly had more osteocyte PMD than controls with normal cage activity. Accordingly, we hypothesize that osteocytes become sensitized to PMD formation during reloading, and may be less likely to repair and survive this damage. Mice will be subjected to unilateral hindlimb casting, after which they will be allowed to remobilize the casted limb (30 min to 7 days) prior to sacrifice. PMD formation, post-wounding survival, and relationships to cortical bone properties will be quantified as above. Mechanistic in vitro studies will utilize rotating wall vessel bioreactors and fluid shear stress to simulate disuse and reloading.

AIM 2: Test the hypothesis that altering membrane repair dynamics will alter the osteocyte PMD response to disuse. We have shown that
susceptibility to PMD, repair rate of PMD, and cell survival after PMD can be genetically and pharmacologically altered to control bone's
adaptation to its loading environment. These strategies will be used in concert with the disuse and reloading models described above to
determine whether
approach:
HYPOTHESIS 1: Osteocyte PMD formation is decreased by disuse and enhanced, but with impaired repair, during reloading.

AIM 1 Approach: For in vivo studies, mice will be subjected to unilateral hindlimb casting, which we established in pilot studies to cause loss of cortical bone architecture and strength within two weeks. Osteocyte PMD will be histologically quantified in the immobilized and contralateral limbs. PMD magnitude and density will be regressed against metrics of bone architecture, mineralization, and strength. For in vitro studies, osteocytes (immortalized and primary cells) will
be cultured in a rotating wall vessel bioreactor (Synthecon Rotary Cell Culture System) to simulate microgravity. Regarding reloading, in a pilot experiment, we discovered that mice subjected to disuse followed by ~5 minutes of reloading surprisingly had more osteocyte PMD than controls with normal cage activity. Accordingly, we hypothesize that osteocytes become sensitized to PMD formation during reloading, and may be less likely to repair and survive this damage. Mice will be
subjected to unilateral hindlimb casting, after which they will be allowed to remobilize the casted limb (30 min to 7 days) prior to sacrifice. PMD formation, post-wounding survival, and relationships to cortical bone properties will be quantified as above. Mechanistic in vitro studies
will utilize rotating wall vessel bioreactors and fluid shear stress to simulate disuse and reloading.

HYPOTHESIS 2: Altering membrane fragility or repair dynamics will alter the osteocyte PMD response to disuse.

AIM 2 Approach: Susceptibility to PMD, repair rate of PMD, and cell survival after PMD can be genetically and pharmacologically altered to control bone's adaptation to its loading environment. These strategies will be used in concert with the disuse and reloading models described above to definitively test and establish whether osteocyte PMD mechanisms are a key component of disuseinduced bone loss, and to determine whether disuse-induced alterations in osteocyte PMD
mechanisms can be targeted to improve cortical bone responses to disuse and remobilization.

title: Potential Role of the Endothelium in Internal Jugular Venous Thrombosis due to Abnormal Venous Flow Patterns During Spaceflight
objectives:
The recent report of complete and partial thrombosis of the internal jugular vein in crew members on the Internal Space Station raises concerns for the potential of life threatening pulmonary emboli. There appear to be very abnormal blood flow patterns in the Internal jugular vein in zero gravity that may predispose to local thrombus formation. Given that the endothelium is uniquely sensitive to changes in the local flow/shear stress environment, the focus of this proposal is on the potential role of the endothelium in mediating localized thrombus formation. The purpose of this proposal is to identify changes in gene expression in venous endothelium exposed to the same flow patterns as those observed in the ISS crew members using isolated vein organ culture system. These studies will help us to develop a better understanding of the basic mechanisms responsible for thrombosis formation with the ultimate goal of potentially identifying biomarkers that would enable screening and risk stratification of crew members.

Aim I: Recapitulate the Abnormal IJV flow patterns observed in crew members aboard the ISS in an isolated, perfused vein organ culture system and determine if there are changes in endothelial gene expression using RNA sequencing.

Aim II: Confirm that any changes in candidate genes identified in Aim I are expressed at the protein level.
approach:
The overall strategy will be to mimic both normal and abnormal flow patterns that were observed in crew members on the ISS in an isolated vein culture systems and determine the effects of the resultant shear forces on endothelial inflammatory and prothrombotic gene expression.

AIM I Approach:
For these studies, we will employ an isolated vein perfusion system with a servo-controlled pump programmed to deliver the flow patterns and shear forces that mimic those reported to occur in crew members aboard the ISS. The inferior vena cava from mice will be isolated from the heart to the level of the kidneys and branch vessels ligated. The vessel will be stabilized at steady flows and then have one of the 4 flow patterns described above applied. The magnitude of the flow velocity will be adjusted to result in the same wall shear stress as estimated to exist in humans. After 24 hours, the vessels will be removed from the perfusion apparatus and treated with Trizol reagent intraluminally as previously described to yield an endothelial enriched fraction of RNA. Two vessels will be pooled for each condition in order to ensure adequate RNA harvest. A total of 10 animals for a N of 5 will be studied for each flow pattern based on a power analysis (α=0.05 and a power = 0.95) derived from previous data in our lab.

AIM II Approach:
We will confirm a panel of candidate genes identified in Aim I using immunostaining with en face quantum dot enhanced imaging as previously described and shown in figure 2.  Samples from each of the flow conditions will be analyzed initially by en face analysis. The use of quantum dots and confocal microscopy enhances the quantitative rigor of the analysis. Or these studies, 3 animals per condition for each candidate gene will be studied.
results: This experiment is currently in progress.  Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
