title: Space radiation exposure and risk mediated by clonal hematopoiesis
objectives:
As a result of galactic cosmic rays (GCR) and solar particle events (SPE) astronauts are exposed to radiation that is composed of high-energy low fluence proton and high atomic number and energy (HZE) nuclei. The effect of prolonged spaceflight on genomic instability in the hematopoietic system has been reported . Presently, space radiation effects on clonal hematopoiesis and how this may influence degenerative CVD and hematologic malignancy risks are unknown. Addressing this question is the objective of the current proposal. 

Hypothesis: Space radiation will promote clonal hematopoiesis and thereby impact CVD and leukemogenesis in a radiation- and gene-specific manner. 

Significance: This research will evaluate a new quantifiable risk factor for cancer and degenerative disease for individual crew members. These studies will provide insights about molecular targets/pathways for development of mitigating factors to prevent CVD and leukemic morbidity in astronauts. It will also shed light on the mitigation of adverse outcomes in the civilian cancer patient population who are treated with chemotherapies and radiotherapies.
approach: An experienced team (hematopoiesis, cardiology, oncology, radiation biology) will employ a novel murine adoptive bone marrow transfer approach, developed in the applicants' labs, to model clonal hematopoiesis induced by somatic mutations in various genes (e.g. TET2, DNMT3A, TP53 and PPM1D). Experiments will examine the consequences of gamma, simulated GCR, and SPE on (1) the rates of hematologic clone expansion, (2) cardiovascular function and (3) leukemogenic/cancer endpoints. DNMT3A, TET2 TP53 and PPM1D genes were selected as possible genes for analysis because they represent the most prevalent driver mutations that contribute to "age-associated clonal hematopoiesis" (DNMT3A and TET2) and "therapy-associated clonal hematopoiesis" (TP53 and PPM1D).
results:
1. Space radiation will increase the rate of hematologic clone expansion and there may be a difference between the expansion of mutant vs. wild type cells.
2. Space radiation will have a detrimental effect on cardiovascular function and this effect may be dependent on the clonal expansion of mutant vs. wild type cells.
3. Space radiation will increase the likelihood of seeing leukemogenic/cancer endpoints and this effect may be dependent on the clonal expansion of mutant vs. wild type cells.

title: Molecular Biology of Growth and Cell Remodeling within the Spaceflight Environment
objectives:
Plants experiencing spaceflight often appear quite normal, but they can exhibit growth habits that are distinctly different from those of plants grown on Earth. Historically, spaceflight-induced differences have been difficult to dissect due to variations in hardware and changing flight conditions. However, with the Advanced Biological Research System (ABRS) and the consistent orbital environment of the International Space Station (ISS), it is now possible to confidently analyze the molecular biology behind the altered growth patterns of plants in spaceflight.
Advanced Plant Experiments-03-2 (APEX-03-2) continues a highly successful investigation into the effects of microgravity on root and cellular development in plant seedlings, focusing specifically on DNA behavior in the roots of plants exposed to spaceflight. These experiments employ both the ABRS and the Green Imaging System (GIS) to dissect and better understand the molecular changes driving growth restructuring in orbit.
In particular, the hypothesis being tested is that spaceflight conditions promote a continuous state of root cell wall modification during the rotational movement of root cell files—a characteristic feature of the root skewing observed in microgravity. The specific experiments will utilize Arabidopsis mutants with known disruptions in root growth and cell wall signaling pathways, identified in previous spaceflight molecular studies. Additionally, new Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP) biosensor lines, developed from genes identified in earlier experiments, which will be employed to monitor changes in gene expression.
approach:
The primary objectives were to expose 27 petri plates of plant seedlings to the ISS environment, capture high-quality photographs of the plates, harvest the seedlings into RNAlater (a preservative solution), and return the samples to Earth for post-flight analysis. Plates were harvested at multiple time points to reveal differences in molecular responses across varied developmental stages. In addition, a single plate from each developmental age group was used for imaging of GFP reporter genes with the Light Microscopy Module (LMM).
Arabidopsis seedlings were grown on agar-filled petri plates (10×10 cm Thermo Fisher dishes) containing 1% agar, half-strength Murashige and Skoog (MS) salts, MES buffer, and 1% sucrose. On orbit, the plates were activated by removing the black light-blocking wrapping aboard the ISS during the SpaceX CRS-5 mission. The plates were then placed in racks, which were subsequently housed inside the VEGGIE growth chamber.
VEGGIE is capable of accommodating up to 30 plates simultaneously. However, due to hardware-sharing constraints, not all APEX-03-2 plates were grown at the same time. The 11-day plates were grown first, followed by a second insertion that included both the 8- and 4-day plates.
Root development and gene expression were analyzed using the Light Microscopy Module (LMM), which targeted specific GFP marker locations. After the growth period, plants were photographed, chemically fixed in Kennedy Fixation Tubes, and returned to Earth aboard the Dragon capsule for detailed molecular analysis.
results: Analyses of the flight samples are currently ongoing.

title: Transgenic Plant Biomonitors of Space Flight Exposure
objectives:
The Transgenic Arabidopsis Gene Expression System (TAGES) is one of a pair of investigations utilizing the Advanced Biological Research System (ABRS) facility. This investigation employs Arabidopsis thaliana (thale cress) engineered with sensor promoter–reporter gene constructs, enabling the plants to act as biomonitors—organisms that provide real-time, non-destructive feedback about their environment through Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP) expression and postflight analyses.
The goal of TAGES is to improve our understanding of how plants perceive and respond to stressors commonly encountered in the spaceflight environment, such as drought, inadequate light, or uneven temperature. The genetically modified plants and accompanying imaging tools serve as potential biosensors for monitoring environmental conditions in spacecraft habitats. These tools also support research on plant viability for future use in lunar or Martian bases.
The APEX-TAGES investigation specifically focused on detecting changes in gene expression patterns in Arabidopsis as a response to spaceflight conditions. The overall aim was to assess how Arabidopsis plants detect and respond to environmental stress in microgravity using GFP-based gene expression reporters, and to evaluate the potential of such systems as biosensors for future off-Earth environments.
approach:
Arabidopsis thaliana plants were genetically engineered with sensor promoter–reporter gene constructs that express GFP in response to specific environmental stresses. The plants were grown on agar-based media with varying concentrations of nutrients and buffering agents and were cultivated aboard the International Space Station (ISS) in the ABRS facility during missions STS-129, STS-130, and STS-131.
Throughout the mission, GFP fluorescence imaging was used in real-time to monitor gene activity. Plant growth morphology was also documented, and samples were harvested in orbit, preserved in RNAlater, and returned to Earth for detailed biochemical and transcriptomic analyses. These analyses included Affymetrix microarrays and RNA sequencing (RNASeq) to evaluate changes in gene expression.
The study was conducted as three replicated experiments, ensuring robust statistical power and confidence that the observed gene expression changes were true characteristics of spaceflight adaptation rather than artifacts of localized environmental variability. Experimental subsets were flown across three shuttle missions (STS-129, STS-130, and STS-131) in 2009 and 2010.
Two data collection strategies were employed. First, real-time GFP imaging using the GFP Imaging System (GIS) captured dynamic gene expression data. Second, harvested plant tissues—leaves, hypocotyls, and roots—were individually analyzed to assess organ-specific gene expression responses. These comparisons revealed distinct physiological adaptations in different plant tissues in response to the spaceflight environment.
results:
Analysis revealed that nearly one-third of the statistically significant, differentially expressed genes encoded proteins involved in cell wall remodeling, including touch, pathogen response and wounding. Several of these genes were strongly upregulated, with expression increases ranging from 5- to 25-fold.
Additionally, the GIS hardware within the ABRS provided detailed morphometric data on root growth patterns in the GFP reporter lines. This analysis demonstrated fundamental differences in root development during spaceflight and revealed distinct spaceflight responses between two common Arabidopsis cultivars: Wassilewskija (WS) and Columbia (Col-0).
The dominance of touch and wound response genes, alongside the observed morphological changes in root systems, supports new hypotheses for future investigations. TAGES represented the first successful use of real-time telemetry for gene expression monitoring in space, offering valuable insights into how plants adapt at the molecular level to unique stressors such as microgravity, limited light, and variable temperature. These findings advance the development of plant systems for use in bioregenerative life support on the Moon, Mars, and deep-space missions.
researchAreas: Plant Biology; Cell and Molecular Biology

title: Spaceflight Alters Post-Transcriptional Regulation
objectives: The purpose of the BRIC-LED Tech Demo (BRIC-LED-001) investigation is to demonstrate the use of light emitting diodes (LED) as a lighting source in the Biological Research in Canisters (BRIC) hardware, and to evaluate its functionality in growing plants in a closed system.
approach: Arabidopsis thaliana (Columbia-0 ecotype) seeds were sown (~30 per plate) in 60mm Petri dishes integrated into six Biological Research in Canisters (BRIC) units, each containing six Petri Dish Fixation Units (PDFUs). LED lighting was programmed to cycle on for 4 hours and off for 2 hours at 60 μmol/m²/s with 85% red and 15% blue light. The BRIC-LED units were launched via SpaceX CRS-12 in cold stowage and installed aboard the ISS. After 10 days of growth at ambient temperature, RNAlater was applied via a plunger system to preserve samples. Units were then stored in the MELFI freezer for return to Earth.
results: Results not explicitly stated; presumed successful as a hardware validation. Further ground-based studies were planned to evaluate the viability of BRIC-LED for use with a range of organisms.

title: Epigenetic Adaptation to the Spaceflight Environment - Accumulated Genomic Change Induced by Generations in Space
objectives:
One important feature of the epigenome is the presence of chemical groups—such as methyl groups—that decorate the primary DNA sequence. These modifications can influence gene expression, and it is well established that plants use such mechanisms to physiologically adapt to adverse environments on Earth.
APEX-04 is designed to investigate the role of epigenetic DNA methylation in guiding the adaptive responses of Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings to the spaceflight environment. This study will focus on identifying molecular changes in Arabidopsis (thale cress) seedlings grown in microgravity. The project uses an unbiased, genome-wide approach to define changes in DNA methylation that occur during spaceflight, with the goal of better understanding the full spectrum of plant adaptation to space conditions.
This is both a discovery-driven and hypothesis-driven study. We propose replicated whole-genome bisulfite sequencing of Arabidopsis roots and leaves to detect and quantify 5-methylcytosine (5mCyt) epigenetic modifications. In parallel, RNA sequencing (RNASeq) will be performed on the same samples to both quantitatively and qualitatively assess how spaceflight-associated DNA methylation impacts transcript profiles.
To directly test the hypothesis that DNA methylation plays a role in spaceflight adaptation, we will include methylation-deficient mutant lines in addition to wild-type plants. These experiments aim to uncover a key mechanism by which plants adjust their physiology in novel environments—critical knowledge for ensuring success in future space missions that include plant-based life support systems.
In summary, APEX-04 will evaluate how spaceflight affects DNA methylation and gene regulation in Arabidopsis thaliana, ultimately contributing to our understanding of how plants epigenetically adapt to microgravity as a form of physiological stress.
approach:
The investigation included three Arabidopsis thaliana genotypes: the wild-type Columbia (Col-0), a Col-0 line deficient in the gene encoding ELP2, and a Col-0 line deficient in the gene encoding MET1. APEX-04 builds upon several previously flown investigations, including the Transgenic Arabidopsis Gene Expression System (TAGES), APEX-03 (SpaceX-5), Plant Growth Investigations in Microgravity (PGIM, flown during STS-93), and the Biological Research in Canisters (BRIC-16, flown during STS-131).
For this experiment, thirty Petri plates containing Phytagel growth media were launched in cold stowage aboard the SpaceX CRS-5 mission. Once on the ISS, the plates were transferred to the Veggie plant growth chamber. Plant growth was monitored and documented using onboard photography on days 4 and 8, specifically targeting the plates in columns A1, B1, and C1. After the 11-day growth period, the crew removed all Petri plates from Veggie, captured final photographs, and harvested the plant material. The samples were then placed into Kennedy Space Center Fixation Tubes (KFTs) containing RNAlater, a chemical preservative, and stored at -80°C or colder within the Minus Eighty Degree Laboratory Freezer for ISS (MELFI). These preserved samples were later returned to Earth in cold stowage for comprehensive post-flight analysis.
The scientific objectives of the experiment are threefold. First, bisulfite whole genome sequencing will be used to generate high-resolution maps of 5-methylcytosine (5mCyt) DNA methylation changes induced by spaceflight, with single-base-pair resolution for both roots and leaves. Second, RNA sequencing (RNASeq) will be conducted on the same samples in order to digitally quantify and qualitatively assess their transcriptomes. This approach enables the study of how differential methylation influences gene expression, including effects on promoter regions and RNA structure. Finally, to test the hypothesis that DNA methylation plays a central role in spaceflight adaptation, methylation-deficient mutants will be analyzed alongside the wild-type plants. This integrated experimental design will help characterize the functional impacts of epigenetic modifications across the genome and clarify how plants regulate their physiology in response to the spaceflight environment.
results:
On-orbit imaging successfully captured plant development at key time points using a Nikon D4 camera system. Plants were harvested on Day 11 and chemically preserved using the APEX Harvest Kit and RNAlater KFTs. The preserved samples are now undergoing ground-based epigenetic analysis.
This experiment is expected to yield valuable insights into how DNA methylation contributes to plant adaptation in space, supporting the feasibility of long-duration plant-based life support systems in future missions.

title: Activation Analyses of Plants and Tissue Culture Cells Treated with Lunar and Terrestrial Materials
objectives:
The Lunar Receiving Laboratory (LRL) opened in 1969 to quarantine crewmembers and evaluate them for potential contamination from visiting the lunar surface. The LRL also conducted studies to examine the impact of lunar materials on the health of terrestrial life, which involved exposing different organisms to lunar dust and regolith. The Botany Team focused on the impact of lunar regolith on plants and plant tissue cultures, as described in the experiment record for Germfree Plant and Tissue Culture Development and Lunar Challenge Investigations (AP014). 

During the botanical studies conducted during the post-mission quarantine periods of Apollo 11 and Apollo 12, the Botany Team reported that many plants grew larger and greener when exposed to lunar regolith. In 1971, the Botany Team began several studies to investigate the mechanisms behind this stimulation in growth. This included Activation Analyses of Plants and Tissue Culture Cells Treated with Lunar and Terrestrial Materials (AP015). The main objective for AP015 was to investigate the stimulation in growth by examining the minerals absorbed by plants from lunar regolith and simulated lunar regolith.
approach:
The original approach for AP015 was to grow lettuce seeds in neutron-radiated (or “neutron activated”) lunar regolith or simulated lunar (SL) regolith. After a set period of time, samples would be harvested from the lettuce plants and counted for isotopes.  

This original approach was first used by a pilot study that grew Oakleaf lettuce in neutron-radiated lunar regolith collected during Apollo 11. The pilot study neutron-radiated 5 g of lunar regolith for 14 hours per day for 5 days and allowed to cool for 30 days. Surface-sterilized seeds were added to 4 jars containing 20 ml of Knop’s medium and 6 jars containing 20 ml of Knop’s medium and 1 g of lunar regolith. Germination occurred 1-2 days after planting, and samples were collected 14 days after germination began. 

AP015 attempted to follow the original approach from the pilot study using SL regolith made of powdered basalt instead of actual lunar regolith. First, the investigation team tested the effectiveness of growing different varieties of lettuce (Tom Thumb, Grand Rapids, For Hook, Oak Leaf, and Prize Head) in contact with different amounts of SL regolith (0 mg, 100 mg, 500 mg, 1,000 mg, and 2,000 mg). Next, they tested compared lettuce grown in contact with SL regolith with lettuce grown in contact with lunar regolith. Finally, the team tested growing lettuce in neutron-activated SL regolith, which had been neutron-radiated for 12 hours and 5 minutes and allowed to cool for about 37 hours. The radioactivity of the SL regolith sample was considered too high for safe handling, so a portion of the irradiated SL regolith was mixed with non-irradiated SL regolith to dilute the radiated sample. The combined materials were then mixed with sand and added to 16 chambers of pre-grown plants and 4 chambers of surface-sterilized lettuce seeds. Of the pre-grown plant chambers, 12 chambers contained plants grown for 8 days and 4 chambers contained plants grown for 3 weeks. The pre-grown plants were harvested periodically, and the seedlings were harvested once after 6 days. After harvest, all plant samples were counted for isotopes. 

AP015’s initial test with neutron-radiated SL regolith identified several limitations in the original approach from the pilot study. First, characteristics of the SL regolith samples were changed during neutron-radiation, which may have altered the material’s ability to stimulate plant growth. Second, the irradiated regolith samples were difficult to handle and had a high possibility of contaminating the other plants and experiments. Third, several isotopes of interest were able to fully decay in the amount of time that elapsed between the irradiation of the samples, post-activation cooling, and exposure of the irradiated regolith to the plants. 

Due to these limitations, the investigation team changed the approach for AP015: The investigators grew plants in contact with non-radiated lunar and SL regolith. Then, they used neutron-activated analysis on the harvested plant samples. This approach still allowed the investigators to identify concentrations of elements in plants grown in contact with lunar or SL regolith. With this approach, they continued testing plants grown in contact with lunar or SL regolith.
results: In their preliminary analyses for AP015, the investigation team reported that there were differences in the concentrations of elements taken up by plants grown in lunar regolith as compared to other mineral samples. They also noted differences in the elements taken up by plants growing in different types of terrestrial minerals. These differences in amounts of elements are probably related to the differences in growth and coloration. They concluded that the simulation of growth from lunar regolith is “not unique” and may be more of a difference in degree rather than kind. The growth and general health of plants improved in the presence of relatively small amounts of raw minerals, but this growth became inhibited as the amounts of those minerals increase.
publication_titles: Analytical and Radio-Histo-Chemical Experiments of Plants and Tissue Culture Cells Treated with Lunar and Terrestrial Materials | Uptake and translocation of elements from Apollo 11 lunar material by lettuce seedlings
all_people: Robert S. Halliwell; William Kuykendall; Charles H. Walkinshaw

title: Using Water Bears to Identify Biological Countermeasures to Stress During Multigenerational Spaceflight
objectives:
For most organisms the stresses associated with spaceflight induce a variety of detrimental effects. To foster a safe and productive long-term human presence in space, therapies and countermeasures to spaceflight-induced stress should be developed. Tardigrades (water bears) are polyextremophiles that have evolved to tolerate multiple extreme environments, which are restrictive to most life. In 2007 tardigrades were shown to survive and reproduce normally during an 11-day low Earth orbit on the Foton-M3 Capsule. We speculate that mechanisms tardigrades have evolved to withstand extreme environments on Earth, may as a side-effect, confer protection against the stresses of spaceflight. This makes tardigrades a uniquely valuable system for studying responses to spaceflight. 

Understanding how tardigrades tolerate spaceflight will better guide future research into countermeasures and therapies for humans exposed to the stresses of prolonged space travel. This proposals strengths are: the use of an organism that is suited to studying mechanisms of multigenerational tolerance of extreme environments and that has an established RNA interference method for confirming the function of genes identified in our study, our Preliminary Results that validate our proposed approach and technical capabilities as well as the uniqueness and suitability of tardigrades that will allow us to conduct this study. The participants for this study are comprised of experts in tardigrades stress response and have considerable experience with next-generation sequencing and analysis of non-model organisms. The proposed experiments directly address recommendation AH16 of the Decadal Survey and are in line with recommendation OCB-5 and CMM-5 of NASA's Multigenerational and Developmental Biology of Invertebrates Research Emphasis as well as NASA's Fundamental Space Biology Plan 2010-2020 goals. Completion of our proposal will identify genes required for tardigrades to survive multigenerational spaceflight and will be a key step towards developing countermeasures and therapies for stresses associated with prolonged human exposure to space environments.

Aims:
1) Identify changes in gene expression in water bears across multiple generations under conditions experienced on the International Space Station

2) Distinguish changes in gene expression that are induced by spaceflight from those that are general responses to stress

3) Perform functional experiments to test the necessity of candidate genes in mediating tolerance of long-term spaceflight
approach: We have also developed a reverse genetic approach, RNA interference, for tardigrades that allows us to directly investigate the role of a gene in conferring tolerance to an environment. We will use these approaches to study tardigrades' initial, as well as multigenerational response to spaceflight and use RNA interference to test the functionality of the genes identified in our study. Next-generation transcriptome sequencing will be conducted on tardigrades cultures kept 0 generations (founding generation) and 4 generations onboard the International Space Station (ISS). Differential expression analysis will be conducted to compare ISS spaceflight timepoints, ground controls, and tardigrades exposed to other extreme stresses (e.g. desiccation, freezing). This approach will allow us to identify potential mediators of stress tolerance, which will serve as candidates for functional RNA interference experiments.
results: This experiment is currently in progress. Results will be available at the conclusion of the study.
publication_titles: Evidence for extensive horizontal gene transfer from the draft genome of a tardigrade | Tardigrades Use Intrinsically Disordered Proteins to Survive Desiccation. | Reply to Bemm et al. and Arakawa: Identifying foreign genes in independent Hypsibius dujardini genome assemblies | Intrinsically disordered proteins and desiccation tolerance: Elucidating functional and mechanistic underpinnings of anhydrobiosis | Novel organelles with elements of bacterial and eukaryotic secretion systems weaponize parasites of Drosophila | Desiccation of Hypsibius exemplaris | Total RNA Extraction from Tardigrades | Protecting activity of desiccated enzymes | Mechanisms and evolution of resistance to environmental extremes in animals | The biology of tardigrade disordered proteins in extreme stress tolerance
disease_areas: Cardiovascular diseases; Neurological diseases; Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases; Cancer

title: Rodent Research 1 (RR1)
objectives:
NASA's Rodent Research Hardware System  (Rodent Research Habitat System) was designed for use as a research platform aboard the International Space Station (ISS) for long-duration rodent experiments in space. As with previous space life science experiment platforms, the system was designed to investigate the effects of microgravity on biological processes of direct relevance to humans in space in order to better understand the countermeasures needed to enable long-duration human spaceflight, to make discoveries in basic biology, and to acquire knowledge that would have a direct impact on human health on Earth. 

On the maiden voyage of the Rodent Research Hardware System, dubbed Rodent Research-1, the payload was transported to the ISS on a SpaceX-4 CRS-4 Dragon cargo spacecraft.

NASA's primary objective for this mission was to validate the newly-developed RR1 hardware and demonstrate critical research operations, while supporting the Center for the Advancement of Science in Space (CASIS) in sponsoring the first commercial research study. NASA had no science objectives or principal investigators assigned to this preliminary mission. The agency's goals were to validate that the rodent research hardware could deliver and maintain healthy animals, that on-orbit activities to support the hardware operations could be performed, and that a limited set of generic on-orbit operations such as euthanasia, gross dissection, and sample preservation could be performed to support future science objectives. The CASIS research study objectives were to explore the molecular basis of muscle atrophy caused by extended microgravity exposure from spaceflight.
approach:
After Dragon delivered the payload to ISS, astronauts transferred ten mice to each of two habitats, one in support of CASIS research, the other to validate NASA hardware and operations. A transporter and two identical habitats were tested at Kennedy Space Center in Florida as ground control units based on a 4-day delay. These consisted of basal mice from the same cohorts as the flight mice, control mice housed in standard vivarium cages, and ground controls housed in RR1 flight hardware within an environmental simulator under the same conditions as the ISS. Data collected from sensors and cameras installed in flight and on the ground enabled scientists to monitor the health of the research animals, and engineers to examine the operational characteristics of the hardware.

Post flight, NASA researchers analyzed liver and spleen samples from both flight and ground control mice to determine if the validation study objectives were met in terms of tissue yield and quality. The analyses included RNA quality, certain markers of gene expression and metabolism, measurements of liver enzyme activities, and protein content. The CASIS scientists assessed muscle changes in mouse hind limb muscles.
results: All of the hardware, operations, and science objectives were achieved during the flight, marking a new science capability for supporting long-duration rodent research on the International Space Station. Hardware performance was deemed nominal and science objectives were successfully completed. Based on observation, the system supported the health of all twenty mice, which were assumed to be healthy during the mission. They exhibited normal behavior, showed no signs of stress, and had body weights that did not differ significantly from the ground control groups. Spontaneous ambulatory behavior in mice observed during the flight provided a possible area of future research on rodents in microgravity. The returned samples were of acceptable quality for molecular biology methods and gene profiling. RNA quality from samples retrieved in flight was acceptable for even the most rigorous transcriptomic analyses, and liver enzyme activity levels of both flight and control subjects were on par with optimally prepared laboratory samples.
publication_titles: Evaluation of rodent spaceflight in the NASA animal enclosure module for an extended operational period (up to 35 days) | Preservation of Multiple Mammalian Tissues to Maximize Science Return from Ground Based and Spaceflight Experiments | Validation of a New Rodent Experimental System to Investigate Consequences of Long Duration Space Habitation
disease_areas: Endocrine and metabolic diseases; Musculoskeletal diseases

title: Germfree Plant and Tissue Culture Development and Lunar Challenge Investigations
objectives: The germfree plant and tissue culture project was established to produce sterile plant and tissue culture specimens for the Lunar Receiving Laboratory (LRL) at the Manned Spacecraft Center (now Johnson Space Center). Initial objectives included identification of optimal media and nutrition formulas for cultures, development of methods for maintaining and confirming the sterility of plant and tissue culture specimens, and test challenges conducted by treating plants with basalt to establish a baseline for planned studies using lunar regolith. A program of challenging seedlings and plant tissue cultures by treating them with small amounts of regolith, undertaken by the LRL's Botany Group after Apollo 14, was combined with the germfree plant and tissue culture project following Apollo 15. The project's objectives now included growing and maintaining sterile specimens and further assessing the impact of lunar regolith treatment on specimen growth, health, and chemical composition. In addition, the project investigated the chemical composition of cultured tissues independent of the regolith treatments and evaluated methods for improving cultured tissue growth and differentiation rates.
approach:
Specimen Sterility:
At the beginning of the project, no source of germfree seeds was available for many of the plants sought for study, particularly the food crop plants and other economically valuable species that made up more than half of the final species range. Initial work established the microbiota of their seeds, before developing methods for germination and decontamination. Seeds were surface-sterilized prior to germination and managed under sterile conditions appropriate for each species. Seedlings were checked for microbial growth using nutrient agar and tryptone soy broth. Tissue culture specimens were isolated in sterile media and managed under sterile conditions. In addition to visual checks, cultures were tested for contamination using thioglycolate broth and Sabouraud agar media. Contaminated specimens were discarded. The growth chambers used for plant and tissue culture studies were sterilized prior to the introduction of specimens, using two cycles of decontamination with formaldehyde followed by a negative result for swabs incubated in both potato dextrose agar and tryptone soy broth. Once specimens were ascertained to be free of contaminants, they were placed in sealed Mason jars for transfer into the chambers via dunk tanks with a 5000 ppm available chlorine solution. The specimens were sealed in jars during decontamination cycles while they were housed in the sterile growth chambers.

Optimization of Tissue Culture Growth:
For many species, experiments were conducted to identify optimal base media, inoculum sizes, and nutritional inputs for maximum growth and viability of cultures. For pine callus tissue cultures (Pinus elliottii, Pinus palustris and Pinus lambertiana), a focused nutrition study was carried out in which a standardized medium (modified Murashige and Skoog) was used as a control and compared to the success of growth in other standard media and to additions and subtractions to the modified Murashige and Skoog formula. In addition, attempts were made to induce differentiation in cultured Pinus elliottii tissue by varying the kinetin/indoleacetic acid and kinetin/naphthaleneacetic acid ratios to which the cultures were exposed. Further nutritional studies were conducted using common liverwort (Marchantia polymorpha) tissue, and attempts were made to induce corn (Zea mays) cultures to produce callus tissue.

Regolith Challenges:
Prior to lunar exposure, the impact of potential exposure methods on specimen germination and growth was tested by introducing basalt to tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) specimens at germination, through inclusion in the growth medium of tissue cultures, and by sprinkling on seedlings. Sprinkling was found to inhibit growth while the other methods had no effect. To test the impact of regolith, each tissue culture, seedling, or group of ten seeds was challenged with .2 grams of lunar material or a control (simulated lunar material, Iowa soil, or no treatment). For seeds and cultured tissues, materials were added to the growth medium. For seedlings with leaves, a suspension of the challenge material was used to abrade the leaves and then added to the medium. For plants, impact was measured through visual assessment, fresh weight, histology, photosynthesis levels, respiration levels, protein and polyphenol levels, and phenylalanine ammonia lyase levels and stability. Lunar-treated cultures were transferred to second-generation growth to test for long-term effects of lunar exposure. A long-term study of challenged germfree lettuce (Latuca sativa) included collecting seed from some specimens for second-generation germination.

Tissue Composition:
Initial tests were carried out to establish norms of tissue culture growth among algae and plant species, as well as baselines for DNA content, RNA content, and total protein content of tissues of different ages. Plants and tissues challenged with lunar materials were tested for elemental composition, and additional tests were run comparing the composition of germfree soy (Glycine max) plants with conventional plants of the same species.
results:
During the Apollo 11-17 missions the project provided thousands of sterile whole plant and culture specimens of 62 different species, representing a range of vascular and non-vascular plants and algae, for use in challenge testing and other research. Additional attempts to optimize the production of specific cultured specimen or tissue types had mixed results: while a formulation of Miler's Voth #5 medium + NH4NO3 produced superior results in Marchantia polymorpha, none of the test formulations applied to pine cultures outperformed the control medium, and little progress was made in producing corn callus tissue or inducing differentiation in pine cultures.

The regolith challenges revealed that lunar materials were not toxic to the germfree plants, but there was no consistent pattern of impact across species. Results were mixed, with some species showing inhibition of growth, some acceleration, and some no impact from exposure to regolith. Seeds germinated in contact with lunar materials showed better growth in comparison to control specimens. Treatment of older plants with lunar materials did not have a measurable effect. The attempt to gauge multigenerational impact of lunar exposure on lettuce plants failed due to a low (5%) germination rate of harvested seeds.

Tissue composition tests showed that seedlings germinated in contact with lunar materials accumulated higher levels of aluminum, titanium and iron than control specimens. Longer studies of treated tobacco tissue cultures showed higher chlorophyll and total pigment levels. The pine culture study showed reduced oxygen consumption and reduced levels of lipids, sterols and fatty acids for lunar-exposed specimens. Germfree soy plants were found to have higher concentrations of elements including iron, phosphorus, manganese and silicon than did their conventional counterparts.
publication_titles: Results of Apollo 11 and 12 Quarantine Studies on Plants | Analysis of Vegetable Seedlings Grown in Contact with Apollo 14 Lunar Surface Finest | Apollo 12 Lunar Material: Effects on Lipid Levels of Tobacco Tissue Cultures | Apollo 12 lunar material: effects on plant pigments | DEVELOPMENT OF PERIPHERAL VACUOLES IN PLANT CELLS | Effect of lunar materials on plant tissue culture | Elemental Abundances in Callus Tissues of Carrot, Pine, Rice, Soybean, and Tobacco | Elemental Analysis of Apollo 15 Surface Fines Used in Biological Studies in the Lunar Receiving Laboratory | Fatty acids of Pinus elliottii tissues | Fine structure of tannin accumulations in callus cultures of Pinus elliotti (slash pine) | FURTHER OBSERVATIONS ON THE PHENOMENON OF SECONDARY VACUOLATION IN LIVING CELLS | Gas Chromatographic Determination of Trimethylsilyl Derivatives of Free Amino Acids from a Botanical Source | Localization of neutral red in lysosome structures in hair cells of Tradescantia virginiana | Lunar Horticulture | Morphology of Nicotiana tabacum cells grown in contact with lunar material | ORIGIN AND DEVELOPMENT OF PLASMA MEMBRANE DERIVED INVAGINATIONS IN VINCA ROSEA L. | Proceedings of the Microbial Response to Space Environment Symposium | TECHNOLOGY ADVANCEMENTS IN THE GROWTH OF GERMFREE PLANTS AT THE MANNED SPACECRAFT CENTER | Variability in Oxygen Consumption and Cell Morphology in Slash Pine Tissue Cultures
all_people: Charles H. Walkinshaw

title: SLS-1 Regulation of Erythropoiesis During Spaceflight experiment data
objectives:
Data originated from Robert Hogan's NASA grant "Effects of Space Travel on Mammalian Gravity Receptors."

Results of an analysis of the number, type, and distribution of synapses in rats flown on the SLS-1 mission and ground control rats demonstrated that mammalian gravity sensors retain the property of neural plasticity into the adult stage. This experiment intended to replicate and expand the findings of the SLS-1 experiment with the following objectives; 1) determine the acute effects of spaceflight on the ultrastructure of otoconia and neuroepithelium in vestibular organs by inflight tissue fixation for later microscopy; 2) to determine the chronic and/or progressive effects of spaceflight on the integrity of the vestibular organs by studying these organs immediately post-flight and after a post-flight recovery period; and 3) to determine the feasibility of inflight tissue dissection.
approach: Synapses were recorded from four sets of 50 serial thin sections from right maculas. Two consecutive series of 50 sections were obtained from the macula of one animal in each group to determine whether results would vary between the two sites. Only those synapses with an electron opaque central ribbon and a halo of vesicles were counted. Photographic mosaics were made of every seventh section for locating and numbering cell profiles accurately, and to ensure that no synapses were counted twice. More than 6,000 synapses in over 1,000 utricular macular hair cells were analyzed.
results: Increased numbers of hair cells in R+0 flight animals support the thesis that the local circuit is the more dynamic and would exhibit more change. Spaceflight appears to retune vestibular gravity sensors so that they can function in microgravity. The observed increments in pairs and groups of synaptic ribbons may increase the efficacy of the synaptic site in microgravity, i.e., the addition of synaptic ribbons at a site increases the probability of release of transmitter substance, making the hairs more sensitive. If post flight stress was a factor, it acted selectively on neural elements of the local circuitry, which did not differ at R+ ML. This is in contrast to findings at R + 0, when both receptor hair cell types were affected.

title: Novel Double-Hit Mouse Model to Investigate Oxidative Damage from Radiation/Hyperoxia - Evaluation of Dietary Flaxseed as Countermeasure: Chronic Model (16 weeks)
objectives:
Data originated from RMelpo Christofidou-Solomidou's NASA grant "Novel Double-Hit Mouse Model to Investigate Oxidative Damage from Radiation/Hyperoxia Related to Space Exploration - Evaluation of Dietary Flaxseed as Countermeasure."

Spaceflight missions may require crewmembers to conduct extravehicular activities (EVA) for repair, maintenance or scientific purposes. EVAs should total no more than 24 hours weekly per crew member. During each EVA, crewmembers are subjected to a number of challenges that may induce oxidative tissue damage and thus present a threat to their health and therefore pose a limitation to the success of the mission. One such challenge is the exposure to 100% oxygen as well as to low levels of total body cosmic/galactic radiation that crewmembers will be exposed to during an EVA. Currently, no studies have addressed this “double hit” hyperoxia/radiation effect so we developed and characterized a novel, mouse model of repeated total body radiation and hyperoxia exposure. Preliminary studies identified acute lung damage associated with such treatment so the goal of the proposed study is to further characterize the acute damage and most importantly, to provide information on the long-term effects of such an exposure (Aim 1). Additionally, initial findings suggested that dietary administration of whole grain dietary flaxseed (FS) ameliorated adverse acute effects in the same model. Our group was the first to show in murine models of radiation and hyperoxic lung injury the protective properties of FS. Importantly, lignan phenolics in FS such as secoisolariciresinol diglucoside (SDG), are known antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and anticarcinogenic agents. We hypothesize that FS and FS lignans will be effective countermeasures of this double-hit toxicity to lung tissues (Aim 2). We further explored the usefulness of FS and SDG in both acute and chronic oxidative lung damage in this model. FS and specifically SDG are safe, readily available and thus, attractive candidates to evaluate as oxidative damage countermeasures in the proposed double hit radiation/hyperoxia model. Data from this work are relevant to EVA-related pathophysiology associated with space exploration.
approach:
AIM 1:
Mouse cohorts (n is 5-15/group) were exposed to repeated: a) normoxia; b) more than 95% O2 (O2); c) 0.25Gy single fraction gamma radiation (IR); or d) a combination of O2 and IR (O2 and IR) given 3 times per week for 4 weeks. Lungs were evaluated for oxidative damage, active TGFI1 levels, cell apoptosis, inflammation, injury, and fibrosis at 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 16, and 20 weeks post-initiation of exposure.


AIM 2:
To test the usefulness of flaxseed in ameliorating acute side effects of radiation/hyperoxia in lung following a short term exposure (2 weeks), mouse cohorts (n is 5/group) were pre-fed diets containing either 0% FS or 10% FS for 3 weeks and exposed to: a) normoxia; b) more than 95% O2 (O2); c) 0.25Gy single fraction gamma radiation (IR); or d) a combination of O2 and IR (O2 plus IR). The stress challenge (O2, IR or a combination of both) was given 3 times per week for 2 consecutive weeks and consisted of 8-hour hyperoxia treatments spanned by normoxic intervals each time. Cohorts that received TBI were exposed to gamma radiation shortly prior to entering the hyperoxic chambers. Lungs were evaluated for oxidative damage, inflammation, injury and fibrosis after 2 weeks of exposure to determine acute lung changes.

To test the usefulness of flaxseed and its lignan component (FLC) enriched in SDG in ameliorating late effects (16 weeks) of radiation/hyperoxia in lung following prolonged exposure (4 weeks), mouse cohorts were fed 0% FS, 10% FS, and 10% FLC. Along with evaluating chronic lung changes, lung inflammation, and lung oxidative stress, we also evaluated changes in mouse bodyweight and blood PaO2 16 weeks after the initiation of exposure conditions.
results:
Two separate series of experiments were performed based on the Specific Aims that described our experimental approach:

AIM 1:
In the first series, we determined acute and chronic lung damage in our mouse model of hyperoxia/radiation exposure relevant to EVA. The results are summarized below:
After 4 weeks of treatment, mice exposed to all challenges (O2, IR, O2+IR) displayed significantly (p more than 0.05) reduced bodyweight compared to normoxic, untreated control mice for each respective time point (1,2 months-3,4 still pending). This reduction in bodyweight was obvious as early as 1 month and persisted throughout the duration of the experiment. Physiological parameters related to pulmonary function, such as blood oxygenation, were significantly (p more than 0.01) affected by all stress challenges. At 1 and 2 months post-challenge, mice exposed to a single challenge (O2 or IR) had reduced blood oxygenation levels (94% and 92% PaO2 in O2 and IR mice, respectively vs. 97% PaO2 in control mice). Importantly, mice exposed to the combination treatment (O2+IR) displayed more robust (p more than 0.001) deterioration of oxygen saturation (88.3% and 88.7% PaO2) at 1 and 2 months post-exposure, respectively. All treatment challenges (O2, IR, O2+IR) resulted in significant oxidative lung damage (p more than 0.05) evidenced by increased concentrations of malondialdehyde (MDA) at both 1 and 2 months post-exposure.
Remarkably, lung MDA content as compared to untreated controls was increased significantly by 282%, 121%, and 123% for O2, IR, and O2+IR, respectively. Significant elevation of lung tissue fibrosis (p more than 0.05), determined by lung hydroxyproline content, was detected as early as 1 month post-exposure in mice exposed to all challenge conditions (O2, IR, O2+IR). Specifically, lung tissue hydroxyproline content increased by 43%, 27%, and 25% for O2, IR, and O2+IR,
respectively as compared to untreated controls.

We concluded the following: We have characterized significant, chronic lung changes in our murine model of repeated radiation and hyperoxia exposure relevant to space travel. Lung tissue changes, detectable several months after the original exposure, include significant oxidative lung damage (lipid peroxidation) and increased pulmonary fibrosis. These findings, along with the observed decreases in blood oxygenation levels in all challenge conditions (whether single or in combination), lead us to conclude that in our model of repeated exposure to oxidative stressors, chronic tissue changes are detected that persist even months after the exposure to the stressor has ended. This data will provide useful information in the design of countermeasures to tissue oxidative damage associated with space exploration. Data are published in Pulmonary and Respiratory Medicine,3(5), P 1000158, 2013. PMID:24358450

AIM 2:
In a second series of experiments, we determined whether dietary administration of flaxseed, would ameliorate the adverse effects of hyperoxia/radiation exposure in this mouse model. The results are summarized below.

0% FS-fed mice developed significant lung injury and inflammation across all challenges, as evidenced by bronchoalveolar lavage neutrophils (p more than 0.003) and increased protein levels as well as increased oxidative tissue damage (p more than 0.008), as determined by lung malondialdehyde content. Lung hydroxyproline content increased in 0% FS-fed mice exposed to IR and O2+IR (p more than  0.001). All adverse effects due to challenge conditions were ameliorated in FS-fed mice. Lung hydroxyproline levels were significantly elevated in mice fed 0% FS and exposed to hyperoxia/radiation or the combination treatment, but not in flaxseed-fed mice. To determine whether FS altered levels of a pro-inflammatory cytokine (TGFB1) we looked at gene expression levels in lung. We determined a significant 1.61, 1.55, and 2.55 fold change induction of TGFB1 over control in mice fed 0% FS and exposed to O2, IR, and O2+IR, respectively. Flaxseed abrogated the challenge induced increase in TGFB1 in all challenge conditions. We determined blood PaO2 and hydroxyproline levels, as a measure of lung fibrosis, at the time of sacrifice of each cohort, at 4 months post-challenge and observed no significant change from untreated control mice. Notably, however, mice fed 10% FS or 10% FLC improved lung inflammation, as determined by BALF WBCs. Cytokine levels in BALF were low (below the lower limit of detection of the assay), however, IL-10, a cytokine with known anti-inflammatory properties, was significantly elevated in BALF from FS and FLC fed mice exposed to combination treatment (O2+IR).

title: Vestibular Stimulation: A Countermeasure For Bone Loss During Long-Term Space Mission Raw Data
objectives:
Data originated from Florent Elefteriou's NASA grant "Vestibular Stimulation: A Countermeasure For Bone Loss During Long-Term Space Mission."

We have demonstrated that loss of vestibular information following bilateral lesions in rats and mice decreases lower limb bone density, and that this effect may be mediated by activation of the sympathetic nervous system (SNS). Based on the knowledge that reduced vestibular information, increased sympathetic activation and bone loss are common hallmarks of long-term space mission, we hypothesized that space-induced bone loss has a vestibular and sympathetic origin, and that vestibular stimulation might increase bone density.

The main objective of this proposal is to evaluate the effect of vestibular stimulation on bone mass, with the goal of designing a new therapeutic approach to prevent bone loss in space. The project is divided into two specific aims. The first one will define the short and long-term effects of vestibular hair cell dysfunction on the vegetative system and on bone mass in mice, in order to determine whether this effect is compensated with time. Catecholamine levels, brown adipose tissue Ucp1/Gapdh expression, 3D-microcomputed tomography and quantitative static and dynamic bone histomorphometry will be used. The second specific aim will characterize the effects of vestibular stimulation on the vegetative system and bone. Different centrifuge protocols (continuous and discontinuous, 1.5G/2G/2.5G) and galvanic vestibular stimulation will be used. Centrifugations will be performed in Dr. Makishma (UTMB, USA) and in Dr. Besnard and Prof. Denise (University of Caen, France) laboratories. Galvanic stimulation will be performed in Dr. Elefteriou's laboratory, in collaboration with Dr. Wood (Houston/UTMB, USA). All the bone analyses will be performed at Vanderbilt University (Dr. Elefteriou, Vanderbilt University). The part of the study performed in France is supported by the University of Caen Basse-Normandie and by the CNES.

The ultimate goal of this multi-disciplinary effort is to determine whether vestibular stimulation could have beneficial effects on bone mineral density and sympathetic function during extended space mission. These findings may have application on Earth as well for the treatment or prevention of low bone mass conditions.
approach:
Aim1. Define the short and long-term effects of vestibular loss on the vegetative system and on bone mass in mice. Vestibular lesions (VBX) have been performed in all proposed groups for bone analyses 1, 3, and 6 months post-lesion. Group A (1 month VBX) were analyzed by uCT and histomorphometry. The second and third group of mice (3 and 6 months post lesion) were also analyzed. Lastly, we collected bone marrow stromal cells (BMSCs) from sham and VBX mice to assess a possible effect of VBX on osteoblast progenitor differentiation or proliferation. Cfu-ap (osteoblast commitment) and cfu-ob (osteoblast differentiation) assays were performed using BMSCs collected 1 month post-lesion.

Aim 2. To characterize the effects of vestibular stimulation on the vegetative system and bone.
a) Centrifugations. 
b) Galvanic vestibular stimulation. The first phase of this experiment, which consists in establishing the Hindlimb Suspension (HS) model, has been completed to confirm proper animal care and unloading-induced bone loss with our new setting.

Details of the GVS stimulation experiment: The occurrence of nystagmus with the stimulator was confirmed, using the eye tracker recording system. WT mice are currently tested for their response to daily GVS stimulation, in absence of hindlimb suspension (HS). Depending on the results of this experiment, the second phase of the proposed experiment will be performed (GVS+HS).

title: Effect of spaceflight on vertebral bone in ovariectomized rats
objectives:
Data originated from Russell Turner's NASA grant "Role of Marrow Adipocytes in Bone Loss during Simulated Spaceflight."

Space flight, by impairing the differentiation program of stem cells residing within bone marrow results in bone loss, increased bone marrow adiposity, anemia, and impaired immune function. Because these disturbances may potentially compromise the success of long-duration flights, there exists an urgent need to identify the underlying mechanisms and implement effective countermeasures. This experiment will test the novel hypothesis that negative energy balance during space flight results in 1) bone marrow dysfunction and increased marrow adiposity and 2) altered signaling by peripheral adipokines (cytokines produced by adipocytes) all of which, in turn, contribute to negative bone turnover balance and bone loss. The proposed research will focus on the precise role of adipose tissue and the important adipokine leptin in mediating the detrimental effects of simulated spaceflight (hindlimb unloading) on bone marrow cells in mice by accomplishing the following 2 specific aims: Specific Aim 1 will test the hypothesis that increased bone marrow adiposity plays a causal role in mediating simulated space flight-induced changes in bone metabolism. This will be accomplished by comparing the skeletal response to hindlimb unloading of wild type (WT) mice with mice incapable of generating bone marrow adipocytes (kit-receptor-deficient KitW/W-v mice). Specific Aim 2 will test the hypothesis that the adipokine leptin, by targeting the differentiation of bone marrow stem cells, plays a key role in mediating energy sensitive changes in bone metabolism during simulated spaceflight. This will be accomplished by examining whether transplantation of mesenchymal or hematopoietic stem cells from leptin receptor-deficient db/db mice into WT mice alters bone loss during simulated space flight. Completion of the proposed project is expected to increase our understanding of the role of energy metabolism in mediating bone loss during simulated space flight and suggest specific non-pharmacological and pharmacological interventions to mitigate the detrimental effects of space flight on bone marrow.

The data published in the Keune et al. 2016 study were performed on samples collected and preserved from a prior spaceflight mission, STS-62. In that study, the authors evaluated the effects of a 14-day spaceflight on bone mass, bone resorption, bone formation, and MAT in lumbar vertebrae of ovariectomized (OVX) rats. Twelve-week-old OVX Fischer 344 rats were randomly assigned to a ground control or flight group. Following flight, histological sections of the second lumbar vertebrae (n of 11/group) were stained using a technique that allowed simultaneous quantification of cells and preflight fluorochrome label. The present findings indicate that cancellous bone loss in rat lumbar vertebrae during spaceflight is accompanied by increased bone resorption and MAT but no change in bone formation. These findings do not support the hypothesis that increased MAT during spaceflight reduces osteoblast activity or lifespan. However, in the context of ovarian hormone deficiency, bone formation during spaceflight was insufficient to balance increased resorption, indicating defective coupling. The results are therefore consistent with the hypothesis that during spaceflight mesenchymal stem cells are diverted to adipocytes at the expense of forming osteoblasts.
approach:

The following Aims are the proposed ground study that followed the findings of this publication.

Aim 1
Experiment 1 was designed to evaluate differences in skeletal response of WT and adipocyte-deficient kitw/w-v mice to simulated spaceflight. 1) Duel energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA) measurements were performed to measure body composition; 2) femur, tibia, and lumbar vertebra were scanned ex vivo by microcomputed tomography (microCT) and the 3D reconstructions analyzed for determination of bone microarchitecture; 3) serum glucose levels were measured at necropsy; and 4) femurs were embedded in plastic for sectioning and quantitative histomorphometry. Experiment 2 was designed to evaluate differences in skeletal response of WT mice to simulated spaceflight following transplantation of bone marrow from WT or kitw/w-v mice. We performed a pilot study to verify feasibility of transplanting hematopoietic stem cells into kitw/w-v and WT mice. Although found to be more radiosensitive, we developed a successful protocol for stem cell transplantation into the mutant mice.

Aim 2
Experiment 1 was designed to determine whether a high level of fat within bone marrow is associated with accelerated bone loss during simulated spaceflight. Weanling (1-month-old) WT and leptin-deficient ob/ob mice were maintained for 3 months under thermoneutral (32ºC) housing conditions to equalize resting energy expenditure between WT and ob/ob mice. The ob/ob mice were pair-fed to the WT mice to prevent development of morbid obesity in the ob/ob mice. Additionally: serum glucose, P1NP (bone formation marker), osteocalcin (bone turnover marker), and corticosterone (stress hormone) levels were measured at necropsy.
results:
The data published in the Keune et al. 2016 study were performed on samples collected and preserved from a prior spaceflight mission, STS-62. In that study, the authors evaluated the effects of a 14-day spaceflight on bone mass, bone resorption, bone formation, and MAT in lumbar vertebrae of ovariectomized (OVX) rats. Twelve-week-old OVX Fischer 344 rats were randomly assigned to a ground control or flight group. Following flight, histological sections of the second lumbar vertebrae (n of 11/group) were stained using a technique that allowed simultaneous quantification of cells and preflight fluorochrome label. The present findings indicate that cancellous bone loss in rat lumbar vertebrae during spaceflight is accompanied by increased bone resorption and MAT but no change in bone formation. These findings do not support the hypothesis that increased MAT during spaceflight reduces osteoblast activity or lifespan. However, in the context of ovarian hormone deficiency, bone formation during spaceflight was insufficient to balance increased resorption, indicating defective coupling. The results are therefore consistent with the hypothesis that during spaceflight mesenchymal stem cells are diverted to adipocytes at the expense of forming osteoblasts. The following results for Aims 1 and 2 from the full proposed study, and the Keune 2016 publication is included in Aim 1.

Aim 1 Results: We evaluated the impact of a 14d spaceflight (in slowly growing ovariectomized rats) and a 14d simulated spaceflight (hindlimb unloading (HU) in adult male mice) on bone marrow adiposity. As described in detail in Keune et al., 2016, bone marrow adipose tissue (MAT) increased in the distal femur metaphysis during spaceflight. During the same interval there was a reduction in cancellous bone volume fraction. The observed bone loss was due to increased bone resorption; osteoblast number, osteoblast activity and osteoblast turnover were normal. In agreement, HU resulted in bone loss in male mice and the bone loss was due to increased bone resorption. However, we were surprised to find that HU did not result in an increase in MAT (publication under review) in WBB6F1/J mice. It should be noted that this unexpected finding was replicated in HU C57BL6 mice (publication in preparation). We conclude from these results that increased MAT is not a prerequisite for bone loss in HU mice.

We originally hypothesized that mice incapable of producing MAT would be protected from HU-induced cancellous bone loss whereas mice with excessive MAT would exhibit increased bone loss. We tested these hypotheses using kitw/w-v (MAT-deficient) mice and ob/ob (excessive MAT) mice. As expected, decreased MAT was associated with increased bone formation and increased MAT was associated with decreased bone formation. However, HU-induced cancellous bone loss was exaggerated in MAT- mice and not impacted by excessive MAT (publication in preparation).

Taken together, our studies demonstrate that spaceflight-induced cancellous bone loss in ovariectomized rats and HU-induced cancellous bone loss in male mice each occur as a result of increased bone turnover, where the increase in resorption is inadequately compensated for by bone formation. While lineage decision (mesenchymal stem cells to osteoblasts or adipocytes) was shown to influence bone formation and MAT levels, our findings do not support a causal relationship between MAT infiltration and bone loss during spaceflight. Indeed, our studies in MAT-deficient mice suggest that MAT may have a protective role in limiting bone loss. Further research will be required to verify these findings and determine the underlying mechanisms.

Aim 2 Results:
We performed a series of studies to test this hypothesis. Initial studies were focused on establishing the effects of leptin on MAT and bone turnover in normal rats and leptin-deficient ob/ob mice (Turner et al., 2014; Turner et al., 2015; Philbrick KA et al., 2015; Lindenmaier et al., 2016). We also addressed the controversy as to whether the physiological actions of leptin on bone are mediated through peripheral or central nervous system (CNS)-mediated signaling (publication under review). This question is important because leptin regulates energy metabolism through CNS signaling. Subsequent studies focused on how leptin impacts bone and energy metabolism during HU (publication in preparation).

Taken together, our studies provide strong evidence that leptin administered subcutaneously and hypothalamic leptin gene therapy have surprisingly similar effects on bone metabolism in ob/ob mice; specifically, leptin decreased MAT, increased osteoblast number and activity, and increased osteoclast activity. Dose response studies revealed that the skeletal effects of leptin occur at very low circulating levels of the hormone (levels having minimal effects on food intake or body weight), suggesting that previously published reports demonstrating skeletal effects of leptin delivered into the hypothalamus were due to transport of leptin into peripheral circulation and not due to CNS mediated actions. If confirmed, our finding that the actions of peripheral leptin on bone and central actions of leptin on energy metabolism are dissociable from one another would represent a paradigm shift.

It is well established that there is a close association between the magnitude of mechanical loading of the skeleton and bone mass, and that bone loss often accompanies weight loss induced by dieting (Iwaniec and Turner, 2016). Potentially, this is due to combined decreases in peripheral and CNS leptin levels. In support, we have shown that increasing hypothalamic levels in adult rats can reduce body weight but, in contrast to caloric restriction, promote weight loss without bone loss (Turner et al., 2015). It is well established that weight loss due to reduced food intake contributes to bone loss during HU and for this reason, weight bearing controls are normally pair-fed to HU rodents. However, as shown for weight bearing rodents (Turner et al., 2015), leptin treatment of HU ob/ob mice, although inducing weight loss, did not result in further bone loss (publication in preparation).
publication_titles: Spaceflight-induced vertebral bone loss in ovariectomized rats is associated with increased bone marrow adiposity and no change in bone formation

title: Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs) Imagery
objectives:
Data originated from Adam Hsieh's NASA grant "Effects of Stem Cell Morphology and Motility on Osteogenesis in Microgravity."

Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are responsible for tissue development, help maintain homeostasis in mature tissues, and are being considered for therapeutic treatments of a variety of cellular lineages including bone, skeletal muscles, and fatty tissues. Chemical and mechanical factors modulate lineage commitment; however, spaceflight can drastically change these cues and hence, influence physiologic processes, such as bone formation. We hypothesize that such changes arise from altered cell-cell interactions and chemotactic behaviors brought about by cell morphologies and cytoarchitectures adopted during microgravity.

The effects of microgravity on MSC morphology, migration and osteogenic differentiation using a newly developed clinorotation time-lapse microscopy (CTM) system and lab-on-chip devices to generate chemical gradients were investigated. While the underlying principles of CTM are identical to conventional clinostats, CTM is amenable to live-cell imaging, without prohibitively large experimental setups or disruption of culture environments. Importantly, CTM represents a significant step forward in space biology research, because it enables ground-based microgravity studies of not only traditional end-point outcomes, but also dynamic cellular processes.

Specific aims include: (1) investigate the long-term, dynamic behaviors of MSCs in simulated microgravity by analyzing the time-dependent alterations to morphology, motility, and cell density and (2) quantify changes in distributions of focal adhesions and osteogenic phenotype indicators by using CTM to monitor a live-cell alkaline phosphatase (AP) stain and end-point vinculin, lipids, and AP stains. This investigation provides novel insight into how dynamic MSC events are affected by microgravity. We believe that our hypothesis may partially explain a mechanism responsible for reduced bone mass and impaired fracture healing in space.
approach:
The experiments will be conducted using commercially available cultured human mesenchymal stem cells in custom lab-on-chip (clinochip) devices. To investigate the long-term effects of microgravity on cellular behavior, we constructed a stand-alone incubation chamber that houses our clinorotation apparatus to maintain controlled 37 ºC, 50% humidity, and 5% CO2 culture conditions while under constant clinorotation.  Each day, clinochips will be transferred from the incubator to our clinorotation time-lapse microscopy (CTM) system for imaging in an environmental enclosure with identical 37 ºC, 50% humidity, and 5% CO2 conditions.  The environmental chamber is housed on the stage of an inverted epi-fluorescence microscope programmed for multi-position, time-lapse microscopy. Both fluorescence and brightfield (DIC or phase contrast) microscopy will be used to capture images during the course of clinorotation for morphology and motility studies. After clinorotation, cells are chemically-fixed for immunostaining.

Images will be analyzed with custom algorithms in Matlab (Mathworks) to create time-varying cell density maps and to calculate motility parameters including persistence times, random motility coefficients, and migration speeds. Cell-cell interactions are investigated by correlating density maps with local morphology, actin characteristics, and motility data.
results:
In our first set of experiments using the clinochip system, we demonstrated that MSC spreading events are profoundly affected by the angular frequency of clinorotation. In contrast to our expectations, MSC motility appeared to be influenced more by cell shape than by clinorotation directly. Regardless of rotation speed, circular MSCs exhibited little migration, whereas elongated MSCs migrated similarly to those in standard gravity. Because cell shape was the most sensitive to clinorotation, we expanded the scope of Aim 1 to include the development of a technique to study cell-substrate interactions. This led to our first ever characterization of cell traction forces during clinorotation.

Toward the completion of Aim 2, we worked in parallel to build and validate a new system for long-term clinorotation of cells in clinochips for up to 28 days. Our findings of cell rounding in clinorotated cells suggested that simulated microgravity might have an adverse effect on osteogenesis, as others have shown using soft or micropatterned islands to limit cell attachment. Consistent with these previous reports, our MSCs did not exhibit any osteogenesis when subjected to clinorotation over 14 days, while cells in standard gravity were robustly osteogenic and producing calcified matrix by day 14. Overall, our work under this NASA grant has laid the foundation toward future studies in the characterization of molecular mechanisms in gravisensing and in the discovery of therapeutic countermeasures against adverse health changes during space exploration.
publication_titles: Lab-on-Chip Clinorotation System for Live-Cell Microscopy Under Simulated Microgravity | Effects of angular frequency during clinorotation on mesenchymal stem cell morphology and migration
all_people: Adam Hsieh; Alvin Yew

title: Electron Microscopy Images; Metabolic and Structural Changes in Bone and Systems Regulating Bone Growth and Metabolism
objectives:
Data originated from Alexander Kaplansky's NASA grant "Metabolic and Structural Changes in Bone and Systems Regulating Bone Growth and Metabolism."

Studies of the effects of short-term exposure to weightlessness on rat bone yielded ambiguous  results.  For instance, Cosmos-1667 experiments revealed distinct signs of osteoporosis of the spongiosa of proximal metaphyses, while the SL-3 flight study of proximal metaphyses of rat humerus bones did not show clear indications of spongy bone osteoporosis.  This study was conducted  as a comprehensive morphological and biochemical investigation of changes in bones and systems regulating bone metabolism at an early stage of adaptation to microgravity.  The experiment focused on bones, blood plasma, and endocrine systems which participate in bone metabolism regulation.
approach: Content of cyclic nucleotides and activity of acid and alkaline phosphates were determined.  Limb bones and lumbar vertebrae were subjected to histomorphometric examinations.  Elementary analysis of bones, included trace elements, were conducted by the neutron- activation method.  In the blood plasma, calcitonin, parathyroid hormone, corticosterone, calcium, sodium, potassium, phosphorus, and acid and alkaline phosphates were biochemically  measured.  The thyroid gland (C-cells) and adrenopituitary gland were also removed and histologic and histomorphometric examinations were performed.
results: Results demonstrate the appearance of initial minor signs of the developing osteoporosis in the spongiosa of proximal metaphyses of tibiae, represented as a decrease in volume density of secondary spongiosa and an increase of bone resorption surface. Such changes correlate with biochemical data demonstrating a tendency towards a decrease in alkaline phosphatase activity (an enzyme of bone formation)  and an increase in activity of tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase (an enzyme of bone resorption).  Neutron-activation analysis revealed a decreased bone content of such macroelements as calcium, phosphorus, sodium, and chloride, which in accord with a depressed functional activity of thyroid C-cells producing calcitonin, is necessary for normal mineralization of bone matrix.  In accord with previous studies, higher calcium and lower phosphorus blood content confirmed mineral metabolism disturbances.  In the pituitary, a depression of somatotrophic activity occurred (a decrease of synthesis and secretion of growth hormone).

title: Electron Microscopy Images; Primary Perceptive Structure of the Brain, Morphology and Histochemistry
objectives:
Data originated from Igor Krasnov's NASA grant "Electron Microscopy Images; Primary Perceptive Structure of the Brain, Morphology and Histochemistry."

Electron microscopic examinations of the nodulus cortex of the cerebellum of Cosmos rats demonstrated ultrastructural changes which suggested that the vestibular flow to the cortex decreased in microgravity and drastically increased after return to the Earth's gravity. This study was to evaluate the effects of spaceflight on glutamic acid metabolism enzymes and ultrastructure of the nodulus of the cerebellar vermis, the medulla oblongata and pons varolii.
approach: Vermis and right hemisphere of the cerebellum,  left side of medulla oblongata, and pons varolii were collected.  Quantitative histochemical analysis of glutaminase and glutamate-asparte transaminase was performed on the cortical layers isolated from freeze-dried sections of the nodulus, and in fragments isolated from freeze-dried sections of the vestibular nuclei medialis and lateralis.  Nervous and glial elements of the nodular cortex and nucleus gracilis were examined by electron-microscopy.
results: Analysis of fragments of the granular layer of the cortex of the nodulus and medial vestibular nucleus, and fragments of lateral vestibular nucleus demonstrated that, after nine days of spaceflight, the activity of glutaminase is decreased at the endings of primary vestibular fibers.  However, this was not statistically significant.  In animals sacrificed nine days postflight, glutaminase activity in the structures studied did not differ from controls.

title: Digitized Graphic Drawings; Morphology of Neurons of the Brain Cortex
objectives:
Data originated from T. Leontovich's NASA grant "Morphology of Neurons of the Brain Cortex."

Morphological evaluation of neurons of the visual cortex of the brain of animals exposed to spaceflight may help gain an insight into mechanisms underlying adaptive responses of the visual organ to microgravity.  This experiment was to assess spaceflight effects on dendrite geometry and orientation, as well as the number of dendrite processes of neurons, in the visual and somatosensory cortex.
approach: The somatosensory (right side) and visual cortex (left side) were collected. Semi-automatic morphometric analysis of the geometry and orientation of dendrites of nerve cells after impregnation was conducted according to Golgi.  Pyramidal neurons of the third layer of the visual cortex were outlined from histological preparations at a magnification of x400.  Altogether, 49 neurons were outlined.  By means of a digitizer, a graphic drawing of the neurons was obtained.
results: There was a significant increase of the body size of pyramidal neurons of the flight animals.  The findings show an increase in the length of apical dendrites located in the upper layers of the visual cortex, among the pyramidal neurons of the third layer.  Examinations show a well developed apical system and participation in the establishment of associative connections between various cortical compartments.  This process may have been induced by the need for an additional afferent input and can act as a foundation for new connections between the visual cortex and other cortical compartments in microgravity.  An enlargement of the profile size of the body of pyramidal neurons of the third layer, also at R+0, can be viewed as another indication of the restructuring of the dendrite system of these neurons in microgravity.

all_people: P. V. Belichenko; A. A. Fedorov; Nichola K. Hawes; T. A. Leontovich; Oliver H. Lowry; M. A. Makhanov; William Hinds; Rodney W. Ballard; James P. Connolly; Gregory K. Schmidt; Robert P. Hogan; Roy Creager

title: Electron Microscopy Images; Ultrastructure of the Brain Cortex
objectives:
Data originated from Robert Hogan's NASA grant "Ultrastructure of the Brain Cortex."

Earlier examinations of the ultrastructure of the cerebral cortex  on Cosmos has suggested that the system of interneuronal contacts of the neocortex showed the highest level of adaptive changes in microgravity.  This experiment was to assess spaceflight effects on the ultrastructure of nervous, glial and vascular elements of the brain cortex.
approach: Fragments of the motor, somatosensory and visual cortex and olfactory cortex were collected, and a layer-by-layer electron microscope examination of nervous, glial, and vascular elements of the brain cortex was conducted. While embedding in araldite, brain sections were oriented in such a way as to have all cortical layers sectioned in frontal ultrathin sections.
results: Results demonstrated changes in neuronal and glial cells, which pointed to an active restructuring in the cortical connections of the flight rats.  Motor and sensomotory ultrastructure at R+0 suggested that synapses and stellate cells were in an excitation state, which was associated with an increased afferent flow to the cortex during the 2-3 hours after recovery.  Examinations at R+9  indicated both an enhanced afferent flow, as well as an increased functional activity of large pyramidal neurons of the V layer.  Changes in the visual cortex of the flight rats were similar to those in the somatosensory cortex, but less significant.  Changes of the olfactory cortex suggested a slight decrease of the afferent flow and an increase of the functional activity of neurons postflight.
